topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Nature of Environments, Structure of Agents, Problem-Solving Agents, Well-Defined Problems and Solutions, Formulating Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Intelligent Agents and Their Environments

In Artificial Intelligence (AI), the most useful conceptualization is the **agent** — any entity that can perceive its environment through sensors and act upon that environment through actuators. This **agent-environment paradigm** is foundational to the KTU 2024 syllabus because it provides a unified framework for analyzing all intelligent behavior, from a simple thermostat to a self-driving car.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Russell & Norvig, KTU Standard):**
> An **agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**. The complete specification of an agent at any given instant is called the **agent function**, which maps the entire history of percepts to an action:
> $$f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$
> where $\mathcal{P}^*$ is the set of all finite percept sequences and $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all possible actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The four pillars of Module 1 are: **(1)** Nature of environments, **(2)** Structure of agents, **(3)** Problem-solving agents, and **(4)** Problem formulation. Mastery of these enables analysis of *any* real-world AI system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **human driving a car**:
- **Sensors** = eyes, ears (perceive traffic, signals, speed)
- **Actuators** = hands on steering wheel, feet on pedals
- **Agent function** = your brain's decision-making rule (if red light → press brake)
- **Environment** = the road, other cars, weather, pedestrians

Just as a good driver *reacts rationally* to road conditions, a **rational agent** acts to maximize its **performance measure**. Replace the human brain with an AI program, and you have an **intelligent agent**.

---

## 1.2 The Nature of Environments

Environments are the "worlds" in which agents operate. The KTU syllabus requires students to classify environments along multiple dimensions, because the *type* of environment dictates *which agent design* is appropriate.

> [!NOTE]
> **PEAS Framework (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors):**
> Before designing any agent, we must first describe its task environment using the **PEAS** specification. This is the first step in rational agent design.

### Classification of Task Environments

| Property | Possible Values | Definition | Example |
|----------|----------------|------------|---------|
| **Observability** | Fully / Partially / Unobservable | Can the agent access the complete state of the environment through its sensors? | Fully: Chess; Partially: Poker |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Is the next state completely determined by the current state and action? | Deterministic: 8-Puzzle; Stochastic: Self-driving car |
| **Episodicity** | Episodic / Sequential | Is each action-decision independent of others, or do they influence future decisions? | Episodic: Image classification; Sequential: Chess |
| **Dynamism** | Static / Semi-Dynamic / Dynamic | Can the environment change while the agent is deliberating? | Static: Crossword puzzle; Dynamic: Taxi driving |
| **Discreteness** | Discrete / Continuous | Are percepts, actions, and states finite/countable or infinite/real-valued? | Discrete: Chess; Continuous: Robotic arm |
| **Number of Agents** | Single / Multi-Agent | Is the agent operating alone or with other agents? | Single: Solitaire; Multi-Agent: Soccer |
| **Knowledge** | Known / Unknown | Are the rules of the environment known to the agent? | Known: Rules of chess; Unknown: New board game |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite (Module 1):**
> The environment for **Automated Taxi Driving** is: **Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-Agent, Unknown.** Memorizing this example is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

### Environment Types: Cross-Reference Table

| Application | Observable | Deterministic | Episodic | Static | Discrete | Agents |
|-------------|-----------|---------------|----------|--------|----------|--------|
| **Chess with clock** | Fully | Deterministic | Sequential | Semi | Discrete | Multi |
| **Chess without clock** | Fully | Deterministic | Sequential | Static | Discrete | Multi |
| **Poker** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Static | Discrete | Multi |
| **Backgammon** | Fully | Stochastic | Sequential | Static | Discrete | Multi |
| **Taxi driving** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Multi |
| **Medical diagnosis** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Single |
| **Image analysis** | Fully | Deterministic | Episodic | Semi | Continuous | Single |
| **Robot navigation** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Single |
| **Refinery controller** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Dynamic | Continuous | Single |
| **English tutor** | Partially | Stochastic | Sequential | Semi | Discrete | Multi |

---

## 1.3 The Structure of Agents

An **agent** is composed of two fundamental parts:
1. **Architecture** = the physical computing device (sensors, actuators, CPU, memory, communication hardware).
2. **Agent Program** = the software that implements the agent function — i.e., the mapping from percepts to actions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Equation of Agent Design:**
> $$\text{Agent} = \text{Architecture} + \text{Agent Program}$$
> The architecture provides raw percepts; the agent program turns them into actions.

### The Five Agent Program Structures (KTU 2024 Standard Hierarchy)

| # | Agent Type | Core Idea | Key Data Structure |
|---|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Reflex Agent** | Acts only on the current percept, ignoring history | Condition-action rules (IF-THEN) |
| 2 | **Model-Based Reflex Agent** | Maintains an internal state to track unobserved aspects | Internal state + world model |
| 3 | **Goal-Based Agent** | Acts to achieve explicit goals (uses future) | Goal description + search |
| 4 | **Utility-Based Agent** | Acts to maximize a utility function (handles tradeoffs) | Utility mapping |
| 5 | **Learning Agent** | Improves performance through experience | Performance element + Critic + Learning element + Problem generator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rationality Principle (KTU High-Yield):**
> A **rational agent** does whatever action is expected to *maximize its performance measure*, given the percept sequence and its prior knowledge. **Rationality ≠ Omniscience** (a rational agent can be unaware of events it could not have foreseen).

---

## 1.4 Problem-Solving Agents

When the environment is **fully observable, deterministic, known, and static**, the agent can use a **search-based problem-solving approach**. The agent:
1. Formulates a **goal** (e.g., "reach city B").
2. Formulates the **problem** (states, actions, costs).
3. **Searches** for a sequence of actions (a **solution**) that reaches the goal.
4. **Executes** the solution step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formally, a problem-solving agent operates in four phases:**
> $$\text{Goal Formulation} \rightarrow \text{Problem Formulation} \rightarrow \text{Search} \rightarrow \text{Execute}$$
> In the **execution phase**, the agent ignores its percepts (because the world is known/deterministic); if something goes wrong, it re-plans.

### Conceptual Analogy: The GPS Navigator

A **GPS navigation system** is a perfect example of a problem-solving agent:
- **Goal**: Reach the destination with minimum time/distance.
- **Problem Formulation**: Map = state space; roads = actions; distances = path cost.
- **Search**: Algorithm computes the shortest path (e.g., Dijkstra, A*).
- **Execute**: The driver follows turn-by-turn directions.

If a road is closed, the GPS must **re-plan** — this corresponds to the *open-loop* nature of problem-solving agents (no feedback during execution).

---

## 1.5 Well-Defined Problems and Solutions

A **well-defined problem** is one where the initial state, goal state, actions, transition model, and path cost are all precisely specified. The **solution** is a sequence of actions leading from the initial state to a goal state; an **optimal solution** is the one with the lowest path cost among all solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Exam):**
> A **problem** is defined by 5 components. A **solution** is a *path* through the state graph. An **optimal solution** is the *least-cost* path.

---

## 1.6 Formulating Problems (The 5-Component Definition)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires explicit knowledge of the **formal problem definition**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of a Problem (Russsell & Norvig / KTU Standard):**
> A problem is defined by a 5-tuple:
> $$\text{Problem} = \langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \mathcal{A}(s), \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{G}, \text{cost} \rangle$$
> where:
> - $\mathcal{S}$ = the set of all possible **states** (state space)
> - $s_0 \in \mathcal{S}$ = the **initial state**
> - $\mathcal{A}(s)$ = the set of **actions** available in state $s$
> - $\mathcal{T}(s, a)$ = the **transition model** — the state resulting from performing action $a$ in state $s$
> - $\mathcal{G} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ = the set of **goal states** (checked by a **goal test**)
> - **cost** = the step-cost function $c(s, a, s')$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Environment Properties

Understanding environment properties is essential because **each property determines the choice of agent architecture**.

### 2.1.1 Observability

In a **fully observable** environment, the agent's sensors give it access to the *complete state* of the environment at each point in time. The agent does not need to maintain any internal state to keep track of the world.

In a **partially observable** environment, noise and incomplete sensors force the agent to maintain **belief states** — a probability distribution over possible actual world states. Examples include Poker (cannot see opponent's hand) and Taxi driving (blind spots, occlusions).

> [!NOTE]
> If the agent has *no sensors at all*, the environment is **unobservable** — yet the agent may still have to act (think: a vacuum cleaner with no sensors acting randomly).

### 2.1.2 Determinism vs. Stochasticity

- **Deterministic**: The next state is *entirely determined* by the current state and the chosen action. $s' = \mathcal{T}(s, a)$ is unique.
- **Stochastic**: The next state is described by a *probability distribution*: $P(s' \mid s, a)$.

An environment is **strategic** if it is deterministic *except* for the actions of other agents (e.g., chess).

### 2.1.3 Episodicity

In an **episodic** environment, the agent's experience is divided into atomic *episodes*. Each episode consists of the agent perceiving and then performing a single action. The choice of action in one episode does not depend on, and does not affect, future episodes.

In a **sequential** environment, the current decision can affect all future decisions (chess, driving, language understanding).

### 2.1.4 Dynamism (Static / Dynamic)

- **Static**: The environment does not change while the agent deliberates.
- **Dynamic**: The environment can change *while the agent is thinking* (e.g., a taxi's passengers, other cars).
- **Semi-dynamic**: The environment itself does not change, but the *agent's performance score* changes with time (e.g., chess with a clock).

### 2.1.5 Discreteness

A **discrete** environment has a finite (or countably infinite) number of distinct states, percepts, and actions. **Continuous** environments have infinite, real-valued state/action spaces (e.g., steering angle in degrees, velocity in m/s).

### 2.1.6 Single vs. Multi-Agent

A **multi-agent** environment may be:
- **Competitive** (zero-sum: chess)
- **Cooperative** (multi-agent path planning for warehouse robots)
- **Mixed** (chess is competitive, autonomous cars are mostly cooperative)

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Agent Architectures

### 2.2.1 Simple Reflex Agent

$$\text{Action} = \text{Rule}(\text{Current Percept})$$

The agent ignores percept history. It works well **only in fully observable environments**. Its **condition-action rules** are hard-coded (e.g., `IF car-in-front-is-braking THEN initiate-braking`).

**Limitation**: Limited intelligence; cannot operate in partially observable environments.

### 2.2.2 Model-Based Reflex Agent

The agent maintains an **internal state** that depends on the percept history and reflects at least some of the **unobserved aspects of the current state**.

$$\text{Internal State}_{t+1} = f(\text{Internal State}_t, \text{Percept}_t, \text{Action}_t)$$

Two key components:
1. **Transition model of the world**: $P(s' \mid s, a)$
2. **Sensor model**: $P(\text{percept} \mid s')$

The agent updates its internal state using these models.

### 2.2.3 Goal-Based Agent

Knowing only the current state is not always sufficient — the agent needs *future information*. The agent uses **goal information** to choose actions that lead to goal states.

**Search** and **planning** are the subfields of AI devoted to finding action sequences that achieve the agent's goals.

**Difference from reflex**: The goal-based agent is more **flexible** because the knowledge supporting its decisions is represented explicitly and can be modified.

### 2.2.4 Utility-Based Agent

Goals alone are often insufficient — many action sequences may reach the goal, but some are *better* (faster, cheaper, safer). A **utility function** maps a state (or state sequence) onto a real number that describes the associated **degree of happiness / preference**.

A rational utility-based agent chooses the action that maximizes the **expected utility** of the outcome:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Outcome}(a))]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Utility functions allow trade-offs** (e.g., speed vs. safety in driving). Goals are a *binary* special case of utility (1 if goal achieved, 0 otherwise).

### 2.2.5 Learning Agent

A learning agent is divided into four conceptual components:

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **Performance Element** | Chooses external actions (the "body" of the agent) |
| **Critic** | Tells the learning element *how well* the agent is doing w.r.t. the performance standard |
| **Learning Element** | Modifies the performance element to improve future actions |
| **Problem Generator** | Suggests exploratory actions that lead to new informative experiences |

> [!NOTE]
> The learning element uses **feedback from the critic** and **knowledge from the problem generator** to improve the performance element. This is the foundation of **reinforcement learning** and **online learning**.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Problem Formulation

### 2.3.1 The Five-Component Decomposition

| Component | Symbol | Description | 8-Puzzle Example |
|-----------|--------|-------------|------------------|
| **State Space** | $\mathcal{S}$ | Set of all possible configurations of the world | All 9!/2 = 181,440 reachable tile arrangements |
| **Initial State** | $s_0$ | The state from which the agent starts | Any given scrambled configuration |
| **Actions** | $\mathcal{A}(s)$ | Possible actions available in state $s$ | Move blank Left/Right/Up/Down (2 to 4 options) |
| **Transition Model** | $\mathcal{T}(s, a)$ | The state resulting from $a$ in $s$ | The resulting board position |
| **Goal Test** | $\text{GoalTest}(s)$ | A boolean function determining whether $s$ is a goal | Configurations matching the goal board |
| **Path Cost** | $c(s, a, s')$ | Numeric cost of taking action $a$ in $s$ to reach $s'$ | 1 per move (uniform) |

### 2.3.2 Abstraction in Problem Formulation

To solve a real-world problem, the agent must **abstract** the world into a manageable form. The level of abstraction must be **detailed enough** to allow valid solutions, but **coarse enough** to make search tractable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstraction Principle (KTU Board Exam):**
> $$\text{Real World} \xrightarrow{\text{abstraction}} \text{Abstract State Space} \xrightarrow{\text{search}} \text{Solution} \xrightarrow{\text{de-abstraction}} \text{Real-World Action}$$
> Choosing the *right* abstraction is often the difference between an efficient AI and an intractable one.

### 2.3.3 Solution Quality and Optimality

- **Solution**: Any sequence of actions that takes the agent from the initial state to a goal state.
- **Optimal Solution**: The solution with the **lowest path cost**.
- **Search Cost** vs. **Path Cost**: A good search algorithm minimizes the *total* cost (search effort + execution cost).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to an action |
| Rationality | $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')]$ | Choose the action maximizing expected utility |
| Problem Definition | $\langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{G}, c \rangle$ | 5-tuple + cost function |
| Transition Model (deterministic) | $s' = \mathcal{T}(s, a)$ | Next state is a function |
| Transition Model (stochastic) | $P(s' \vert s, a)$ | Next state is a distribution |
| Solution Path | $\langle s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \rangle$ with $s_n \in \mathcal{G}$ | Sequence of states from start to goal |
| Path Cost | $C(\text{path}) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$ | Sum of step costs along the path |
| Optimal Path Cost | $C^* = \min_{\text{paths}} C(\text{path})$ | Minimum over all solution paths |
| 8-Puzzle State Space | $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ | Reachable configurations |
| 15-Puzzle State Space | $16!/2 \approx 10^{13}$ | Reachable configurations |
| Vacuum World State Space | $2 \cdot n \cdot n$ (for $n \times n$ grid) | Agent position + dirt status |
| Branching Factor | $b$ | Number of successors per node |
| Depth of Solution | $d$ | Number of actions in the solution |
| Maximum Depth | $m$ | Tree depth limit |
| Total Nodes Generated (BFS, worst case) | $1 + b + b^2 + \ldots + b^d = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric series |
| PEAS Framework | Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors | Task environment specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Concepts:**
> These classification frameworks are used in **robotics** (to choose sensors and planning algorithms), **autonomous vehicles** (to design multi-modal perception stacks), **game AI** (to choose between search and learning), and **smart manufacturing** (to design agent-based control systems).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Agent Function to Rational Action

We begin with the most fundamental equation in AI and derive the practical *rational-action* rule step by step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** A rational agent should select the action that maximizes its expected performance measure.

$$\text{Step 1: Definition of a Rational Agent}$$

A rational agent at any given time selects an action $a$ from the set $\mathcal{A}(s)$ such that the expected value of the performance measure $P$ is maximized:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \mathbb{E}[P(\text{Outcome}(s, a)) \vert \text{percept history}]$$

$$\text{Step 2: Incorporating Uncertainty in Stochastic Environments}$$

In a stochastic environment, taking action $a$ in state $s$ does not lead to a single next state, but to a *distribution* over next states. Therefore, we use the law of total expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[P \vert s, a] = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot P(s', a)$$

Here, $P(s' \vert s, a)$ is the transition probability and $P(s', a)$ is the performance of reaching $s'$ after action $a$.

$$\text{Step 3: Utility-Based Reformulation}$$

If we use a utility function $U(s')$ to measure the desirability of each outcome state $s'$, the rational action rule becomes:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

This is the **expected utility maximization** principle — the cornerstone of decision theory and AI.

$$\text{Step 4: Reduction to Goals (Special Case)}$$

A *goal* is a binary utility: $U(s') = 1$ if $s' \in \mathcal{G}$ and $0$ otherwise. Substituting:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{G}} P(s' \vert s, a)$$

This means: in goal-based agents, choose the action that has the **highest probability of reaching the goal**. This connects perfectly with the problem-solving framework.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Path Cost Accumulation for an Optimal Path

Given a path through the state graph $\pi = \langle s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \rangle$ where $s_0$ is the initial state and $s_n \in \mathcal{G}$ is a goal state, we derive the total path cost.

$$\text{Step 1: Definition of Step Cost}$$

The step cost of moving from state $s_i$ to $s_{i+1}$ using action $a_i$ is given by the cost function $c$:

$$c_i = c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$$

$$\text{Step 2: Summation of Step Costs}$$

The total path cost is the sum of all step costs along the path:

$$C(\pi) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$$

Expanding the summation:

$$C(\pi) = c(s_0, a_0, s_1) + c(s_1, a_1, s_2) + \ldots + c(s_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, s_n)$$

$$\text{Step 3: Optimal Path Cost}$$

The optimal path $\pi^*$ is the one that minimizes the total path cost over all valid paths $\Pi$ from $s_0$ to any goal state in $\mathcal{G}$:

$$C^* = \min_{\pi \in \Pi} C(\pi) = \min_{\pi \in \Pi} \sum_{i=0}^{\vert \pi \vert - 1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$$

$$\text{Step 4: Uniform Step Cost Specialization}$$

If every step has uniform cost $c_i = 1$ (as in the 8-puzzle, chess, route-finding with equal distance edges), then the optimal path is simply the **shortest path**:

$$C^* = n^* = \min_{\pi \in \Pi} \vert \pi \vert - 1$$

where $n^*$ is the minimum number of actions needed to reach a goal.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Environment × Agent Compatibility

The following table summarizes which **agent architecture** is best suited for which **environment type**. This is a high-yield KTU table.

| Environment Type | Recommended Agent | Reason |
|------------------|-------------------|--------|
| Fully Observable, Deterministic, Episodic | **Simple Reflex** | No memory or planning needed |
| Partially Observable, Deterministic | **Model-Based Reflex** | Need internal state to track world |
| Fully Observable, Sequential, Static | **Goal-Based / Search Agent** | Planning via search is sufficient |
| Stochastic, Multi-Agent, Competitive | **Utility-Based** | Need to maximize expected utility |
| Unknown, Changing Environment | **Learning Agent** | Must adapt through experience |
| Real-world Robotics (mixed) | **Hybrid (Layered)** | Combine multiple architectures |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Vacuum World Problem Formulation

Consider the simple **2-cell vacuum world** (Russell & Norvig's classical example). Let us explicitly formulate the problem.

$$\text{Step 1: Define the State Space}$$

Each state is defined by (agent-location, dirt-A, dirt-B). There are 2 locations × 2 dirt states for A × 2 dirt states for B = **8 states**:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{(L, d_A, d_B) : L \in \{A, B\}, d_A \in \{0, 1\}, d_B \in \{0, 1\}\}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Define the Initial State}$$

Let the initial state be:

$$s_0 = (A, 1, 1)$$

The agent starts at location A, and both rooms A and B are dirty.

$$\text{Step 3: Define the Action Set}$$

The agent has three actions:

$$\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}\}$$

The actions available at a given state $s$ are $\mathcal{A}(s) = \mathcal{A}$ (all actions are always available in the 2-cell world; in more realistic models, $\mathcal{A}(s)$ is restricted).

$$\text{Step 4: Define the Transition Model}$$

The transition model is deterministic in the vacuum world:

| State $s$ | Action $a$ | Result $\mathcal{T}(s, a)$ |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------------|
| $(A, 0, 0)$ | Right | $(B, 0, 0)$ |
| $(A, 0, 1)$ | Right | $(B, 0, 1)$ |
| $(A, 1, 0)$ | Right | $(B, 1, 0)$ |
| $(A, 1, 1)$ | Suck | $(A, 0, 1)$ |
| $(B, 0, 0)$ | Left | $(A, 0, 0)$ |
| $(B, 1, 0)$ | Suck | $(B, 0, 0)$ |
| $(B, 1, 1)$ | Suck | $(B, 1, 0)$ |
| ... | ... | ... |

$$\text{Step 5: Define the Goal Test}$$

The goal is to have *no dirt in any room*:

$$\text{GoalTest}(s) = (s = (A, 0, 0)) \lor (s = (B, 0, 0))$$

So the goal set is:

$$\mathcal{G} = \{(A, 0, 0), (B, 0, 0)\}$$

$$\text{Step 6: Define the Path Cost}$$

Each action costs 1 unit:

$$c(s, a, s') = 1 \quad \forall (s, a, s')$$

$$\text{Step 7: A Sample Solution}$$

Starting from $s_0 = (A, 1, 1)$:

$$\pi = \langle (A, 1, 1) \xrightarrow{\text{Suck}} (A, 0, 1) \xrightarrow{\text{Right}} (B, 0, 1) \xrightarrow{\text{Suck}} (B, 0, 0) \rangle$$

$$C(\pi) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$

The optimal path cost is $C^* = 3$ (the agent must clean A, move to B, and clean B).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Agent Architectures

| Feature | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based | Learning |
|---------|---------------|-------------|------------|---------------|----------|
| Handles partial observability | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Handles stochasticity | No | Limited | Limited | Yes | Yes |
| Uses future information | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Handles tradeoffs | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Requires prior model | Yes (rules) | Yes (world model) | Yes (goal + model) | Yes (utility + model) | No (learns) |
| Computational cost | Low | Medium | High | High | Highest |
| Memory required | Minimal | Moderate | Moderate | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Generic Agent-Environment Interaction Loop

This block-level functional architecture flow captures the closed loop between an agent and its environment — a high-yield diagram for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Environment] -- percept signals --> sens1[Sensors]
    sens1 -- raw percepts --> prog1[Agent Program]
    prog1 -- actions --> act1[Actuators]
    act1 -- state change --> env1
    prog1 -. uses .-> arch1[Architecture / Hardware]
    arch1 -. provides compute .-> prog1
    style env1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style sens1 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,color:#000
    style prog1 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E,color:#000
    style act1 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#000
    style arch1 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B2CBF,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The agent *perceives* the environment through sensors, the agent program processes percepts and decides an action, the actuator executes the action, and the environment state changes. The architecture provides the physical computing substrate for the program.

---

## 4.2 The Five Agent Architectures — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SimpleReflex[Simple Reflex Agent]
        SR1[Percept] --> SR2[Condition Action Rules]
        SR2 --> SR3[Action]
    end
    subgraph ModelReflex[Model Based Reflex Agent]
        MR1[Percept] --> MR2[Update Internal State]
        MR3[World Model] --> MR2
        MR4[How World Evolves] --> MR3
        MR5[How Sensors Work] --> MR3
        MR2 --> MR6[Condition Action Rules]
        MR6 --> MR7[Action]
    end
    subgraph GoalBased[Goal Based Agent]
        GB1[Percept] --> GB2[State]
        GB2 --> GB3[How World Evolves]
        GB3 --> GB4[What Will Happen If I Do Action A]
        GB4 --> GB5[Is This State a Goal]
        GB5 -- No --> GB6[Choose Action]
        GB6 --> GB7[Action]
    end
    subgraph UtilityBased[Utility Based Agent]
        UB1[Percept] --> UB2[State]
        UB2 --> UB3[How World Evolves]
        UB3 --> UB4[What Will Happen If I Do Action A]
        UB4 --> UB5[How Happy Will I Be]
        UB5 --> UB6[Choose Highest Utility]
        UB6 --> UB7[Action]
    end
    style SimpleReflex fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#CC0000
    style ModelReflex fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#CC7A00
    style GoalBased fill:#E5F4E5,stroke:#1A7F1A
    style UtilityBased fill:#E5E5F4,stroke:#0000B3
```

---

## 4.3 The Learning Agent — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    perf1[Performance Element] -- action --> envL[Environment]
    envL -- percept --> perf1
    envL -- feedback --> crit1[Critic]
    crit1 -- performance feedback --> learn1[Learning Element]
    learn1 -- improved rules --> perf1
    prob1[Problem Generator] -- exploratory actions --> perf1
    prob1 -. uses .-> learn1
    style perf1 fill:#D6E9FF,stroke:#0047AB
    style envL fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB
    style crit1 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#CC0000
    style learn1 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style prob1 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B2CBF
```

---

## 4.4 Problem Formulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Real World Problem] -- abstraction --> A2[Abstract State Space]
    A2 -- define --> A3[Initial State s0]
    A2 -- define --> A4[Actions A s]
    A2 -- define --> A5[Transition Model T]
    A2 -- define --> A6[Goal Test G]
    A2 -- define --> A7[Path Cost c]
    A3 --> A8[Search Algorithm]
    A4 --> A8
    A5 --> A8
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 -- solution path --> A9[Sequence of Actions]
    A9 -- de-abstraction --> A10[Real World Solution]
    style A1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB
    style A2 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706
    style A3 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style A4 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style A5 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style A6 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style A7 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style A8 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B2CBF
    style A9 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025
    style A10 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The 5 problem components (state space, initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, path cost) are the **input** to any search algorithm. The output is a sequence of actions — a *solution*. The entire pipeline operates on an **abstracted** version of the real world, which is later mapped back to real-world actions.

---

## 4.5 Goal vs. Utility Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Agent Reaches Decision Point] --> D2{Is Outcome Binary?}
    D2 -- Yes: success or fail --> D3[Use Goal Based Agent]
    D2 -- No: outcomes have varying value --> D4{Is Future Uncertain?}
    D4 -- No --> D5[Use Deterministic Planning]
    D4 -- Yes --> D6[Use Utility Based Agent]
    D6 --> D7[Compute Expected Utility]
    D7 --> D8[Choose Max Expected Utility Action]
    style D1 fill:#D6E9FF,stroke:#0047AB
    style D2 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706
    style D3 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style D4 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706
    style D5 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#2DA44E
    style D6 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B2CBF
    style D7 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B2CBF
    style D8 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Agent. Differentiate between Simple Reflex and Goal-Based Agents. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An **agent** is anything that can perceive its environment through sensors and act upon that environment through actuators. Formally, an agent implements an agent function $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ mapping percept histories to actions.

| Aspect | Simple Reflex Agent | Goal-Based Agent |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Decision basis | Current percept only | Current state + goal information |
| Memory required | No internal state | Requires goal representation |
| Future planning | None | Uses search/planning to reach goal |
| Flexibility | Rigid; rules must be rewritten for new goals | Flexible; only goal needs to change |
| Suitable for | Fully observable, simple environments | Sequential, multi-step tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Differentiate (2)

---

### Question 2: What is the PEAS Framework? Apply it to a Medical Diagnosis System. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **PEAS framework** (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) is used to specify the task environment of an agent before designing it. It answers four questions: how well is the agent doing, what is the world, what can the agent do, and what can the agent perceive.

| PEAS Component | Medical Diagnosis System |
|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Performance Measure** | Healthy patient, minimal cost, reduced lawsuits |
| **Environment** | Patient, hospital, staff, insurance, government regulations |
| **Actuators** | Display of questions, diagnoses, treatments, referrals |
| **Sensors** | Keyboard entry of symptoms, test results, patient responses |

> [!NOTE]
> The environment for medical diagnosis is **partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, single-agent (typically), and unknown**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Explain the Structure of Intelligent Agents with a Block Diagram. Describe Any Four Agent Types in Detail.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An intelligent agent is a system that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators to achieve a goal. The agent is the combination of **architecture** (physical hardware) and **agent program** (software implementing the agent function).

**Block Diagram of a Generic Agent:**

```
[Environment] <--percepts--> [Sensors] <--raw data--> [Agent Program] <--action--> [Actuators] <--effect--> [Environment]
                                       [Architecture/Hardware]
```

**Four Agent Types in Detail:**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent:** Selects actions based on the current percept only, using condition-action rules of the form `IF condition THEN action`. Example: An automatic door that opens when a person approaches. *Limitation:* Cannot operate in partially observable environments.

2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent:** Maintains an internal state based on a percept history and a world model (transition model + sensor model). The internal state updates after each percept. Example: A vacuum robot that remembers where it has already cleaned.

3. **Goal-Based Agent:** Uses goal information to describe desirable situations. The agent uses search to find a sequence of actions that achieves the goal. Example: A GPS navigator that finds the shortest route to a destination.

4. **Utility-Based Agent:** Uses a utility function to measure the desirability of states. It chooses actions that maximize expected utility, allowing for tradeoffs between conflicting goals. Example: A self-driving car that balances speed, safety, and fuel efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition + Block Diagram (2) + Four Agent Types (5)

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): What are the Different Types of Environments in AI? Classify the Task Environment of an Automated Taxi Driver Along All Dimensions.

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Environments:**

| Property | Categories |
|----------|------------|
| Observability | Fully / Partially / Unobservable |
| Determinism | Deterministic / Stochastic |
| Episodicity | Episodic / Sequential |
| Dynamism | Static / Semi-Dynamic / Dynamic |
| Discreteness | Discrete / Continuous |
| Number of Agents | Single / Multi-Agent |
| Knowledge | Known / Unknown |

**Automated Taxi Driver — Task Environment Classification:**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Observability | **Partially** | Driver cannot see around blind corners; other drivers' intentions unknown |
| Determinism | **Stochastic** | Traffic, pedestrians, weather introduce randomness |
| Episodicity | **Sequential** | Current action (e.g., turn) affects all future decisions |
| Dynamism | **Dynamic** | Environment changes while the agent deliberates |
| Discreteness | **Continuous** | Steering angle, velocity, fuel level are real-valued |
| Number of Agents | **Multi-Agent** | Other cars, pedestrians, traffic police are also agents |
| Knowledge | **Unknown** | New roads, accidents, customer behavior not fully known |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Property listing (2) + Taxi classification table (5)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): What is a Problem-Solving Agent? Explain the Four Phases of Its Operation.

**Model Answer:**

A **problem-solving agent** is a goal-based agent that decides what to do by finding sequences of actions that lead to a desired state. It uses **atomic representations** of world states and operates in environments that are fully observable, deterministic, known, and static.

**Four Phases of Operation:**

1. **Goal Formulation:** The agent adopts a goal based on its current situation and performance measure. Example: "Be in Arad" (when in Romania).

2. **Problem Formulation:** The agent describes the goal and the necessary actions/states to achieve it. It abstracts the real world into a tractable state space representation. Example: Map of Romania with cities as states and roads as actions.

3. **Search:** The agent simulates sequences of actions in its model until it finds a sequence (a *solution*) that reaches the goal. Example: Searching for the shortest path using a search algorithm.

4. **Execution:** The agent performs the actions in the solution one by one. Since the environment is assumed static during execution (open-loop), it does not re-evaluate percepts — but may **monitor** the environment and **re-plan** if something goes wrong.

**Flow Diagram:**

$$\text{Goal} \rightarrow \text{Problem} \rightarrow \text{Search} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{(re-plan if needed)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Four phases with explanation (6)

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Define a Well-Defined Problem. Formulate the 8-Puzzle Problem Using the Five-Component Definition.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of a Well-Defined Problem:** A problem is *well-defined* when the initial state, the goal state, the actions available, the transition model, and the path cost are all precisely and unambiguously specified. The solution is a sequence of actions that takes the agent from the initial state to a goal state; an *optimal* solution is one with minimum path cost.

**Five-Component Definition (General):**

$$\text{Problem} = \langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \mathcal{A}(s), \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{G}, c \rangle$$

**8-Puzzle Problem Formulation:**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| **State Space $\mathcal{S}$** | All permutations of the 9 tiles on the 3×3 board (9!/2 = 181,440 reachable states; the factor 2 accounts for parity) |
| **Initial State $s_0$** | Any given scrambled configuration of the 8 tiles and the blank |
| **Actions $\mathcal{A}(s)$** | Move the blank tile Left, Right, Up, or Down (2 to 4 actions, depending on blank's position) |
| **Transition Model $\mathcal{T}(s, a)$** | The new board configuration resulting from sliding the blank in the direction of action $a$ |
| **Goal Test $\mathcal{G}(s)$** | Check whether the current configuration matches the goal board arrangement |
| **Path Cost $c(s, a, s')$** | 1 per move (uniform step cost) |

**Solution:** A sequence of moves of the blank tile that transforms the initial state into the goal state. The optimal solution is the shortest such sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (2) + 8-puzzle formulation table (5)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Agent Function with Agent Program:** Agent function is a *mathematical abstraction* (mapping percepts to actions); agent program is the *concrete implementation*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Wrong Environment Classification:** Many students wrongly classify the taxi as "fully observable" or "deterministic". Remember: *blind spots* → partial, *traffic variability* → stochastic.
> 3. **Missing Path Cost in Problem Formulation:** The 5-component definition has 5 items; some textbooks list *6* (including the cost function explicitly). KTU 2024 scheme expects you to mention **path cost** as a separate component, not absorbed into actions.
> 4. **Forgetting Abstraction:** When formulating a real problem (like a robot vacuum), students often forget the *abstraction step*. Always mention that real-world details are *abstracted* into a tractable state space.
> 5. **Mixing up Reflex vs. Model-Based:** A model-based reflex agent has an *internal state*; a simple reflex does not. A common error is attributing learning capability to reflex agents.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Definitions
- **Agent**: Entity perceiving environment via sensors, acting via actuators.
- **Agent Function**: $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ — mathematical mapping.
- **Rational Agent**: Maximizes expected performance measure.
- **Autonomous Agent**: Decides its own actions based on percepts (not just on prior knowledge).
- **Task Environment**: The "problem" the agent faces — specified by **PEAS**.

### Environment Properties (7 dimensions)
- **Observability**: Fully / Partially / Unobservable.
- **Determinism**: Deterministic / Stochastic / Strategic.
- **Episodicity**: Episodic / Sequential.
- **Dynamism**: Static / Semi-Dynamic / Dynamic.
- **Discreteness**: Discrete / Continuous.
- **Number of Agents**: Single / Multi (Competitive / Cooperative).
- **Knowledge**: Known / Unknown.

### Agent Architectures (5 levels of sophistication)
1. **Simple Reflex** — current percept → action (rules).
2. **Model-Based Reflex** — internal state + world model.
3. **Goal-Based** — uses search to reach goals.
4. **Utility-Based** — maximizes expected utility.
5. **Learning Agent** — Performance Element + Critic + Learning Element + Problem Generator.

### Problem-Solving Agent
- Operates in fully observable, deterministic, known, static environments.
- Phases: **Goal → Problem → Search → Execute**.
- Open-loop execution; re-plans on failure.

### Problem Formulation (5 components + cost)
- $\mathcal{S}$: state space.
- $s_0$: initial state.
- $\mathcal{A}(s)$: available actions.
- $\mathcal{T}(s, a)$: transition model.
- $\mathcal{G}$: goal test.
- $c(s, a, s')$: path cost function.
- **Solution**: action sequence from $s_0$ to a goal state.
- **Optimal Solution**: minimum-cost solution.

### Examples to Remember
- **Automated Taxi**: Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-Agent, Unknown.
- **Chess (with clock)**: Fully Observable, Deterministic, Sequential, Semi-Dynamic, Discrete, Multi-Agent, Known.
- **Image Classification**: Fully Observable, Deterministic, Episodic, Semi-Dynamic, Continuous, Single-Agent, Known.
- **Medical Diagnosis**: Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Single-Agent, Unknown.
- **8-Puzzle State Space**: $9!/2 = 181{,}440$.

### Key Mnemonics
- **Agent = Architecture + Program** (APT).
- **PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** (Parrot Eats Avocado Sandwiches).
- **5 Components of Problem**: **S**tates, **I**nitial, **A**ctions, **T**ransition, **G**oal (+ cost) — **SIATG** (Silently, I Am The Goal).
- **5 Agent Types** in increasing sophistication: **S**imple → **M**odel → **G**oal → **U**tility → **L**earning — **SMGUL**.

### Important Equations
- Agent function: $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- Expected utility maximization: $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')$
- Path cost: $C(\pi) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$
- Optimal path cost: $C^* = \min_{\pi} C(\pi)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> Module 1 questions frequently test: (i) the **PEAS** table, (ii) the **environment classification** of a real-world agent, (iii) the **five-component problem definition**, and (iv) the **comparison of agent architectures**. Master these four question types and you secure full marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Problems: Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Example Problems: Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In KTU 2024 scheme's *Fundamentals of AI*, the three classical toy problems—**Vacuum World**, **8-Puzzle**, and **8-Queens**—are studied as canonical *formulations of search problems*. They are used to ground abstract concepts of *state-space*, *agent*, *goal test*, and *successor function* in concrete, fully-traceable examples that recur in the KTU question bank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Russell & Norvig framework adopted by KTU):**
> A **search problem** is formally defined by five components:
> **(1)** Initial state, **(2)** Actions / successor function $S(x)$, **(3)** Step cost function $c(x,a,y)$, **(4)** Goal test $G(x)$, **(5)** Path cost function.
> Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle and 8-Queens are *fully observable*, *deterministic*, *discrete* and *static* environments — making them perfect *uninformed search* test beds.

### 1.1 Vacuum World — The Simplest AI World

**Technical Definition:** The *Vacuum World* is a two-cell environment with a vacuum-cleaner agent. The state is described by the **agent's location** (cell A or B) and the **dirt status** of each cell (dirty or clean). The agent has three possible actions: **{Left, Right, Suck}**. The goal is to have *both cells clean*.

**Intuition (Real-world Analogy):** Imagine a tiny robotic vacuum (like a *Roomba prototype from 1996*) trapped in a hallway with only two rooms. You must program its brain to decide *where to move next* and *when to suck*. Every possible situation the robot can be in is called a **state**, and the AI course asks you to draw a graph of all these states — this is the very first exercise that trains you to *think like a search algorithm designer*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vacuum World State Count:** Total states $= 2 \text{ locations} \times 2^2 \text{ dirt configs} = \mathbf{8 \text{ states}}$. Only 7 are reachable from any starting state.

### 1.2 8-Puzzle — The Sliding Tile Problem

**Technical Definition:** The *8-Puzzle* is a $3 \times 3$ grid containing eight numbered tiles (1–8) and one **blank tile**. The only legal action is to *slide an adjacent tile into the blank position*. The goal is to reach a specified goal configuration (typically tiles 1–8 in row-major order with the blank at the bottom-right).

**Intuition (Real-world Analogy):** Think of the *Number Maze game* you played as a child on a tablet, or the *15-puzzle* your grandparents owned. Every "move" you make is actually *the blank moving*, not the tile. When you frame it from the blank's perspective, the problem becomes a tiny robot moving on a $3 \times 3$ grid that can never step outside — and AI search algorithms like **A\*** use this exact problem as their *Hello, World!*

> [!NOTE]
> **8-Puzzle State Count:** $\frac{9!}{2} = \mathbf{181{,}440}$ reachable states (the *divide-by-2* comes from parity: half the permutations are unreachable from any given start).

### 1.3 8-Queens — The Constraint Satisfaction Classic

**Technical Definition:** The *8-Queens problem* requires placing **8 queens** on an $8 \times 8$ chessboard such that *no two queens attack each other* — i.e., no two share a **row**, **column**, or **diagonal**. It is the textbook example of a *Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)* and a *backtracking* algorithm benchmark.

**Intuition (Real-world Analogy):** Imagine you are seating **8 powerful queens** at a diplomatic dinner, and *no two queens may be seated in the same row, column, or diagonal aisle*. You must try seat-by-seat, and the moment you place a queen that conflicts with another, you must *back up* and try a different seat — this is exactly what the **backtracking algorithm** does. A *sudoku solver* you use on your phone is a direct descendant of this idea.

> [!NOTE]
> **8-Queens Count:** Total placements $= \binom{64}{8} \approx 4.4 \times 10^9$, but only **92 distinct solutions** exist (excluding symmetry rotations/reflections).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vacuum World — State-Space Graph Construction

The state of the vacuum world is a **4-tuple** $\langle L, D_A, D_B \rangle$ where:
- $L \in \{A, B\}$ is the agent location.
- $D_A, D_B \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$ are the dirt statuses of the two cells.

**Goal states:** $D_A = \text{Clean} \land D_B = \text{Clean}$ (regardless of $L$).

**Why this problem matters:**
- It introduces the *idea of representing AI problems as graphs* (nodes = states, edges = actions).
- It is *fully observable* and *deterministic*, satisfying the simplest preconditions for classical search.
- The *smallest non-trivial search problem* used in KTU Module 1 to illustrate **BFS**, **DFS**, and **uniform-cost search**.

**Successor Function Logic (one of the 8 states):**

$$
S\big(\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \big\rangle) = \{ \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle, \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \}
$$

where the first element is produced by *Suck* and the second by *Right*.

### 2.2 8-Puzzle — Heuristics, Branching & Depth

The 8-puzzle is the canonical example used in KTU to teach **informed search heuristics**. The two heuristics that KTU repeatedly tests are:

- **$h_1$ = Number of Misplaced Tiles**
- **$h_2$ = Manhattan Distance** (sum of horizontal + vertical distances of each tile from its goal)

**Why is the 8-puzzle hard despite being small?**
- The **branching factor $b$** is between **2 and 4** depending on the blank's position (corner → 2 moves; edge → 3 moves; center → 4 moves).
- The **optimal solution depth $d$** for the worst case is **31 moves** (using the *half-move* convention, often 22 in the *blank-move* convention).
- $b^d = 3^{22} \approx 3 \times 10^{10}$ — so a brute-force tree search is **infeasible**, justifying heuristics.

### 2.3 8-Queens — Constraints & Backtracking Cost

Each queen occupies one column (a *domain reduction* trick — KTU loves this!). The state is then just the **row index of the queen in each column**: a tuple $\langle r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_8 \rangle$ with $r_i \in \{1, \ldots, 8\}$.

**The three constraint tests per column $j$ against already-placed column $i$ (with $i < j$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Column conflict: } & r_i = r_j \\
\text{Diagonal conflict: } & r_i - r_j = i - j \quad \text{(main diagonal)}\\
\text{Anti-diagonal conflict: } & r_i - r_j = j - i \quad \text{(anti-diagonal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this problem matters:**
- It is the *first* CSP-style problem in the KTU syllabus.
- It demonstrates the **efficiency of forward checking** and **constraint propagation** — naïve backtracking explores $8^8 \approx 1.67 \times 10^7$ leaves, but with simple pruning, only a tiny fraction is visited.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **Kerala University Exam high-yield cheat sheet**. Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every Part B question on Module 1.

| Problem | Total States | Branching Factor $b$ | Optimal Depth $d$ | Key Heuristic / Cost |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Vacuum World | $\mathbf{8}$ | $\mathbf{3}$ (Left, Right, Suck) | $\leq \mathbf{6}$ | Step cost = **1 per action** |
| 8-Puzzle | $\frac{9!}{2} = \mathbf{181{,}440}$ | $\mathbf{2 \leq b \leq 4}$ | $\leq \mathbf{31}$ | $h_2 = \sum \vert r - r_{\text{goal}} \vert + \sum \vert c - c_{\text{goal}} \vert$ |
| 8-Queens | $\binom{64}{8} \approx 4.43 \times 10^9$ | $\mathbf{8}$ (decreasing) | $\mathbf{8}$ (one queen per column) | Conflicts = 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **8-puzzle's Manhattan heuristic** is the direct ancestor of distance-estimation functions used in modern **GPS navigation** (where you estimate "as the crow flies" distance to the goal). The **8-queens' backtracking** is the foundation of **SAT solvers** and **compiler register allocators**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Vacuum World** → Modern *autonomous robot vacuums* (Roomba, Mi Robot) use a generalized version with thousands of cells and SLAM-based state estimation.
- **8-Puzzle** → *Logistics routing*, *warehouse robot path-planning*, and *VLSI wire-routing* all use Manhattan-distance-style heuristics.
- **8-Queens** → *Antenna placement*, *parallel-machine scheduling*, and *Sudoku / crossword AI* rely on the exact same constraint-propagation backbone.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Vacuum World — Exhaustive State Enumeration

We construct all **8 states** of the vacuum world by combining 2 locations $\times$ 2 dirt statuses for A $\times$ 2 dirt statuses for B. The state is denoted as $\langle \text{Location}, \text{Dirt}_A, \text{Dirt}_B \rangle$.

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \vert L \vert \times \vert D_A \vert \times \vert D_B \vert
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 = \mathbf{8 \text{ states}}
$$

The complete state list is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&s_1 = \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_2 = \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_3 = \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_4 = \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \quad (\text{START typical}) \\
&s_5 = \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_6 = \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_7 = \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean} \rangle \quad (\text{GOAL}) \\
&s_8 = \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step action transitions from $s_4 = \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action: Suck} \Rightarrow & \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle = s_2 \quad (\text{cell A becomes clean}) \\
\text{Action: Right} \Rightarrow & \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle = s_8 \quad (\text{no cleaning, just move}) \\
\text{Action: Left} \Rightarrow & \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle = s_4 \quad (\text{no change — already at A})
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the state-space is a *directed graph* (with self-loops) and the search algorithms must be careful about revisiting nodes.

### 3.2 8-Puzzle — State-Space Size Derivation

**Step 1: Total permutations of 9 tiles in 9 positions:**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 9! = 362{,}880
$$

**Step 2: Apply the parity constraint.**
A *legal move* in the 8-puzzle is one that slides a tile into the blank, which is equivalent to a **transposition of the blank with an adjacent tile** in permutation terms. Each move changes the *parity* of the permutation. Since the goal state has *even parity* (it is reachable by an even number of transpositions from itself), only **half** of all permutations are reachable.

$$
P_{\text{reachable}} = \frac{9!}{2} = \frac{362{,}880}{2} = \mathbf{181{,}440}
$$

**Step 3: Worst-case search-space size for BFS at depth $d = 22$:**

$$
N_{\text{BFS}} = \sum_{k=0}^{22} b^k \approx 1 + 3 + 9 + 27 + \cdots + 3^{22}
$$

$$
N_{\text{BFS}} = \frac{3^{23} - 1}{3 - 1} \approx \frac{9.4 \times 10^{10}}{2} \approx 4.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ nodes}
$$

This is why **A\*** with the Manhattan heuristic is essential — it can solve the 8-puzzle with branching factor close to **1**, exploring only a few hundred nodes.

### 3.3 8-Puzzle — Manhattan Distance Heuristic Worked Example

Consider the following current state on a $3 \times 3$ grid (using 0 for the blank):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ 7 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **goal state** is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & 0 & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Manhattan distance calculation** $h_2 = \sum \vert r - r_g \vert + \vert c - c_g \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tile 1: } & \vert 1-0 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 1 \\
\text{Tile 2: } & \vert 0-0 \vert + \vert 0-1 \vert = 1 \\
\text{Tile 3: } & \vert 0-0 \vert + \vert 2-2 \vert = 0 \\
\text{Tile 4: } & \vert 1-2 \vert + \vert 2-2 \vert = 1 \\
\text{Tile 5: } & \vert 2-2 \vert + \vert 2-2 \vert = 0 \\
\text{Tile 6: } & \vert 1-1 \vert + \vert 1-1 \vert = 0 \\
\text{Tile 7: } & \vert 2-2 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 0 \\
\text{Tile 8: } & \vert 0-1 \vert + \vert 1-0 \vert = 2 \\
\text{Blank 0: } & \text{ignored (always at goal)} \\
h_2(\text{state}) = & \ 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = \mathbf{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **misplaced-tile heuristic $h_1$** for the same state is simply the count of tiles not in their goal position $= \mathbf{5}$ (tiles 1, 2, 4, 8, blank → 5 misplaced). Since $h_2 \geq h_1$ for the 8-puzzle, A\* with $h_2$ is *strictly more informed* and dominates $h_1$.

### 3.4 8-Queens — Diagonal Conflict Algebra

Let queen $Q_i$ be at column $i$, row $r_i$, and queen $Q_j$ be at column $j$, row $r_j$ (with $i < j$).

**Main-diagonal conflict condition:** Two squares $\langle i, r_i \rangle$ and $\langle j, r_j \rangle$ lie on the same $\searrow$ diagonal iff:

$$
r_i - r_j = i - j
$$

**Anti-diagonal conflict condition:** They lie on the same $\nearrow$ diagonal iff:

$$
r_i - r_j = j - i \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad r_i + i = r_j + j
$$

**Total constraint count** when the $k$-th queen is being placed (with $k-1$ queens already placed):

$$
\text{Conflict tests} = 3(k-1) \quad \text{(column + main-diag + anti-diag)}
$$

**Naive backtracking complexity (upper bound):**

$$
N_{\text{naive}} = 8^8 = \mathbf{16{,}777{,}216} \text{ leaf nodes}
$$

**With column uniqueness enforced** (one queen per column — KTU's standard simplification):

$$
N_{\text{column-pruned}} = 8! = 40{,}320 \text{ leaf nodes}
$$

**With full diagonal pruning (real backtracking)**, only **15,720** nodes are visited to find all 92 solutions — a **1000× speedup** over the naive bound.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Vacuum World | 8-Puzzle | 8-Queens |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Problem Type | Path search | Path search | CSP / Backtracking |
| State-space size | $8$ | $181{,}440$ | $40{,}320$ (col-pruned) |
| Branching factor | $3$ | $2{-}4$ | $8 \to 0$ (shrinking) |
| Solution depth | $\leq 6$ | $\leq 22$ | $8$ (exactly) |
| Solvable by BFS? | ✓ Trivial | ✗ Infeasible | ✗ Infeasible |
| Best algorithm | BFS / DFS | A\* + Manhattan | Backtracking + FC |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vacuum World — State-Space Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete reachable state-space of the Vacuum World starting from $\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$. Each node is a state, and each edge is labeled with the action that produces the transition.

```mermaid
graph LR
    s4["S4: A Dirty Dirty"] -->|"Suck"| s2["S2: A Clean Dirty GOAL"]
    s4 -->|"Right"| s8["S8: B Dirty Dirty"]
    s8 -->|"Suck"| s6["S6: B Clean Dirty GOAL"]
    s8 -->|"Left"| s4
    s2 -->|"Right"| s6
    s2 -->|"Suck"| s2
    s2 -->|"Left"| s2
    s6 -->|"Left"| s2
    s6 -->|"Suck"| s6
    s6 -->|"Right"| s6
    s3["S3: A Dirty Clean GOAL"] -->|"Suck"| s2
    s3 -->|"Right"| s7["S7: B Dirty Clean GOAL"]
    s7 -->|"Suck"| s5["S5: B Clean Clean GOAL"]
    s7 -->|"Left"| s3
    s3 -->|"Left"| s3
    s5 -->|"Left"| s2
    s5 -->|"Suck"| s5
    s5 -->|"Right"| s5
    s1["S1: A Clean Clean GOAL"] -->|"Right"| s5
    s1 -->|"Suck"| s1
    s1 -->|"Left"| s1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Goal states are explicitly tagged "GOAL". The self-loops (e.g., `S2 -->|"Suck"| S2`) show that *Suck* on a clean cell is a no-op. Notice the *Suck* action always moves the state closer to a goal.

### 4.2 8-Puzzle — Functional Block Architecture of the A\* Search

The following diagram shows the *control flow* when A\* search solves an 8-puzzle instance, illustrating how the **Manhattan heuristic** prunes the search space.

```mermaid
graph TD
    init["Initial 3x3 State"] --> openList["Insert into OPEN list with f = g + h2"]
    openList --> popNode{"Pop node with lowest f-value"}
    popNode -->|"goal test pass"| goal["Return Solution Path"]
    popNode -->|"goal test fail"| expand["Generate 2-4 successor states by sliding blank"]
    expand --> heurCalc["Compute h2 = Manhattan distance for each child"]
    heurCalc --> checkClosed["Check if child is in CLOSED list"]
    checkClosed -->|"new or better path"| addOpen["Add child to OPEN with f = g + 1 + h2"]
    checkClosed -->|"worse path already known"| discard["Discard child node"]
    addOpen --> popNode
    discard --> popNode
    goal --> output["Output: Sequence of moves 22-step optimal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $f = g + h_2$ is the magic formula:** $g$ = cost-so-far (number of blank moves) and $h_2$ = estimated cost-to-go. A\* is *admissible* (always finds optimal) when $h_2$ is a *lower bound* on true cost. Manhattan distance is admissible because each tile must move at least its Manhattan distance.

### 4.3 8-Queens — Backtracking & Constraint Propagation Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows the recursive backtracking procedure, where each call places one queen in a column and prunes invalid rows.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["Call PlaceQueen column = 0"] --> tryRow1["Try row 0 in current column"]
    tryRow1 --> check1{"Is row 0 safe against previous queens?"}
    check1 -->|"no conflict"| place1["Place queen at column 0 row 0"]
    check1 -->|"conflict"| tryRow2["Try row 1 in current column"]
    tryRow2 --> check2{"Is row 1 safe?"}
    check2 -->|"no conflict"| place1
    check2 -->|"conflict"| tryRow3["Try row 2"]
    tryRow3 --> check3{"Is row 2 safe?"}
    check3 -->|"no conflict"| place1
    check3 -->|"conflict"| tryRow4["Try row 3 to row 7"]
    tryRow4 --> check4{"Any row 3 to 7 safe?"}
    check4 -->|"no"| backtrack["BACKTRACK: remove queen from previous column, try next row"]
    check4 -->|"yes"| placePlace["Place queen and recurse to next column"]
    placePlace --> recurse["PlaceQueen column + 1"]
    recurse --> done{"All 8 columns filled?"}
    done -->|"yes"| success["Record valid solution 92 total"]
    done -->|"no"| tryRow1
    backtrack --> tryRow1
    success --> moreSolutions["Backtrack to find remaining 91 solutions"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 conflict checks per placement** (column, main-diagonal, anti-diagonal) are exactly the three equations derived in Section 3.4. The KTU examiner often asks you to *trace* a few recursive calls — practice this diagram flow.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q1a]**
> **Q1.** Define a *search problem* formally. List its five components and illustrate with the **Vacuum World** as an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A search problem is a formal framework for finding a sequence of actions that leads an agent from an initial state to a goal state. Its five components are:
**(1) Initial state** — e.g., $\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ in the Vacuum World.
**(2) Successor function** $S(x)$ — given a state, returns the set of (action, result-state) pairs, e.g., $S(\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle) = \{(\text{Suck}, \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle), (\text{Right}, \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle)\}$.
**(3) Step cost** $c(x, a, y)$ — usually 1 per action.
**(4) Goal test** $G(x)$ — true iff both cells are Clean.
**(5) Path cost** — sum of step costs along the path. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for five components, 1 mark for Vacuum World illustration.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Q1b]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Manhattan distance heuristic** for the 8-puzzle? Why is it **admissible**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Manhattan distance heuristic $h_2$ for the 8-puzzle is defined as the sum over all tiles (excluding the blank) of the *horizontal + vertical* distance from the tile's current position to its goal position:

$$
h_2(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \big(\vert r_i - r_i^{g} \vert + \vert c_i - c_i^{g} \vert \big)
$$

It is **admissible** because each tile must be moved at least its Manhattan distance times (one move per unit of horizontal or vertical displacement) to reach its goal — the heuristic never overestimates the true optimal cost. Hence $h_2(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, satisfying admissibility. *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for admissibility reasoning, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q2 Module Choice]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Draw the complete state-space graph of the **Vacuum World** showing all 8 states. Mark the goal states and the start state $\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Apply **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** from the start state to reach a goal state, and show the order in which nodes are expanded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — State-Space Graph (7 marks):**

All 8 states are:

| State ID | Tuple | Goal? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| S1 | $\langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S2 | $\langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S3 | $\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S4 | $\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ | ✗ (Start) |
| S5 | $\langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S6 | $\langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S7 | $\langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean} \rangle$ | ✓ |
| S8 | $\langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ | ✗ |

Key transitions from each state using actions {Left, Right, Suck}:

- S4 --Suck--> S2 ; S4 --Right--> S8 ; S4 --Left--> S4
- S8 --Suck--> S6 ; S8 --Left--> S4 ; S8 --Right--> S8
- S2 --Right--> S6 ; S2 --Suck--> S2 ; S2 --Left--> S2
- S6 --Left--> S2 ; S6 --Suck--> S6 ; S6 --Right--> S6
- S3 --Suck--> S2 ; S3 --Right--> S7 ; S3 --Left--> S3
- S7 --Suck--> S5 ; S7 --Left--> S3 ; S7 --Right--> S7
- S5 --Left--> S2 ; S5 --Suck--> S5 ; S5 --Right--> S5
- S1 --Right--> S5 ; S1 --Suck--> S1 ; S1 --Left--> S1

A clean block diagram of the *reachability subgraph* from S4 has S4 → {S2, S8}, then S2 → S6, S8 → S6, converging at S6 (goal) in 3 steps. *(Full graph carries 7 marks with goal and start marked.)*

**Part (b) — BFS Expansion Order (7 marks):**

BFS uses a **FIFO queue**. Let $g$ denote path cost so far.

| Step | Expanded Node | Queue After Expansion | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | — | [S4] | Initial state inserted, $g=0$ |
| 1 | **S4** $g=0$ | [S2, S8] | Expand S4 → Suck→S2, Right→S8 |
| 2 | **S2** $g=1$ | [S8] | S2 is GOAL — found optimal path S4→S2 via Suck |
| 3 | — | [S2, S8] | Path: Suck (length 1, cost 1) |

**Optimal path returned:** S4 $\xrightarrow{\text{Suck}}$ S2 $\rightarrow$ **Goal achieved** with $g = 1$.

**BFS guarantees optimality** because the goal is found at the shallowest depth with uniform step cost. *(7 marks for correct FIFO order, queue state, and noting that S2 is a goal at depth 1.)*

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Define the **8-Queens problem** as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). State the **variables, domains, and constraints** clearly. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Solve the 8-Queens problem for the first **4 queens** using **backtracking**, showing the constraint checks at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CSP Formulation (7 marks):**

- **Variables:** $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, \ldots, Q_8$ — one variable per column.
- **Domains:** $D(Q_i) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ — the row index for queen in column $i$.
- **Constraints (Binary, $i < j$):**
  - **Column-unique:** Not relevant (one queen per column by design).
  - **Row-unique:** $Q_i \neq Q_j$ — no two queens in the same row.
  - **Diagonal-unique (main):** $|Q_i - Q_j| \neq |i - j|$ — no two on $\searrow$ diagonal.
  - **Diagonal-unique (anti):** $|Q_i - Q_j| \neq |j - i|$ — no two on $\nearrow$ diagonal.

Equivalently, the test for placing $Q_j$ given $Q_i$ already placed is:

$$
Q_i - Q_j \neq i - j \quad \text{AND} \quad Q_i - Q_j \neq j - i
$$

If all constraints are satisfied for all $(i, j)$ pairs, the board is a valid 8-queens configuration. *(2 marks for variables/domains, 5 marks for constraints with correct equations.)*

**Part (b) — Backtracking Trace for First 4 Queens (7 marks):**

We will use the standard column-by-column placement with row trial $r = 1, 2, \ldots, 8$ in order. For brevity, only first 4 columns are shown.

**Column 1:** $Q_1 = 1$ (place at row 1, no prior queens to conflict with).

**Column 2:** Try $Q_2 = 1$: $|1 - 1| = 0$, $|1-2| = 1$. $0 \neq 1$ ✓ for row, but $0 = 0$? No, $0 \neq 1$, so check diagonal: $1 - 1 = 0$, $1 - 2 = -1$ → $0 \neq \pm 1$ ✓. So $Q_2 = 1$ is allowed.

Wait — recheck: $Q_1 = 1$, $Q_2 = 1$ would put both queens in **row 1** → row conflict! So $Q_2 \neq 1$. Try $Q_2 = 2$: row check $1 \neq 2$ ✓; main diagonal $|1-2|=1$ vs $|1-2|=1$ → **conflict on main diagonal** ✗. Try $Q_2 = 3$: row check $1 \neq 3$ ✓; main diag $|1-3|=2$ vs $|1-2|=1$ ✓; anti-diag $|1-3|=2$ vs $|2-1|=1$ ✓. So $Q_2 = 3$ ✓.

**Column 3:** With $Q_1=1, Q_2=3$:
- Try $Q_3=1$: $1\neq 1$? No — row conflict with $Q_1$ ✗.
- Try $Q_3=2$: row OK; $|1-2|=1$ vs $|1-3|=2$ ✓; $|1-2|=1$ vs $|3-2|=1$ → anti-diag conflict with $Q_2$ ✗.
- Try $Q_3=3$: $|1-3|=2$ vs $|1-3|=2$ → main-diag conflict with $Q_2$ ✗.
- Try $Q_3=4$: row OK; $|1-4|=3$ vs $|1-3|=2$ ✓; $|1-4|=3$ vs $|3-3|=0$ ✓; $|3-4|=1$ vs $|2-3|=1$ → anti-diag conflict with $Q_2$ ✗.
- Try $Q_3=5$: row OK; $|1-5|=4$ vs $|1-3|=2$ ✓; $|1-5|=4$ vs $|3-3|=0$ ✓; $|3-5|=2$ vs $|2-3|=1$ ✓ → **$Q_3 = 5$** ✓.

**Column 4:** With $Q_1=1, Q_2=3, Q_3=5$:
- Try $Q_4=1$: row conflict with $Q_1$ ✗.
- Try $Q_4=2$: $|1-2|=1$ vs $|1-4|=3$ ✓; $|1-2|=1$ vs $|4-4|=0$ ✓; $|3-2|=1$ vs $|2-4|=2$ ✓; $|5-2|=3$ vs $|3-4|=1$ ✓ → **$Q_4 = 2$** ✓.

So the first 4 queens are placed at columns $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with rows $\{1, 3, 5, 2\}$.

The 8-queens backtracking then continues for columns 5–8, eventually finding all 92 solutions. *(7 marks for full trace with 3 constraint checks per placement.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Vacuum World (Part B):** Forgetting the *Suck* action's self-loop on clean cells, or missing the "Left at A" / "Right at B" no-op transitions → loses **1–2 marks**.
> - **8-Puzzle Manhattan Distance:** Counting the *blank tile* in the sum (must exclude it!), or using *Euclidean* instead of *Manhattan* distance → loses **1–2 marks**.
> - **8-Queens Diagonal Test:** Writing only *one diagonal* constraint (main OR anti), forgetting the other → loses **2–3 marks** as KTU expects **both** $\searrow$ and $\nearrow$ checks.
> - **General:** In state-space questions, students often write state transitions *without labeling the action*. The examiner will deduct marks for unlabeled edges.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Toy Problems**

- **Vacuum World** has exactly **8 states** ($2 \times 2 \times 2$), with the goal being *both cells clean*. Actions = {Left, Right, Suck}. Branching factor = **3**.
- **8-Puzzle** has $\frac{9!}{2} = \mathbf{181{,}440}$ reachable states due to **parity constraint**. Branching factor varies from **2 to 4** depending on blank position.
- **8-Queens** has $\mathbf{92}$ valid solutions (excluding symmetries). With column-uniqueness, search space reduces from $8^8 \approx 1.6 \times 10^7$ to $8! = 40{,}320$ leaves.
- **Manhattan distance $h_2$** is *admissible* and *consistent* for the 8-puzzle; it **dominates** misplaced-tile $h_1$.
- **Three constraint tests** in 8-Queens: column (auto-satisfied), main diagonal, anti-diagonal. KTU expects both diagonal equations: $r_i - r_j = i - j$ and $r_i - r_j = j - i$.
- **A\*** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. For 8-puzzle, A\* with $h_2$ solves it efficiently; BFS is infeasible beyond depth 12.
- **Backtracking** in 8-Queens is a *depth-first search with constraint propagation* — equivalent to the *chronological backtracking* algorithm used in SAT solvers.
- **State-space graph** for any search problem is a *directed graph* with nodes = states and edges = actions; **search tree** is the *tree expansion* of that graph.
- All three problems are *fully observable*, *deterministic*, *discrete*, *static* — qualifying them as classical *uninformed* or *informed* search benchmarks.
- **Engineering relevance:** Vacuum World → robot vacuums; 8-Puzzle → logistics/AI heuristics; 8-Queens → constraint satisfaction in scheduling.
- **Common KTU trick:** ask for **optimality proof** of A\* with $h_2$ — remember: admissible = $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, ensures optimal path.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mantra for Exam Hall:**
> *"Vacuum = 8 states, Puzzle = 181440, Queens = 92 solutions, Manhattan = sum of horizontal+vertical, Diagonal test = $r_i - r_j = \pm(i - j)$."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blind Search Strategies: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search; Heuristic Search Strategies, Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blind Search Strategies: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search; Heuristic Search Strategies, Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Search in AI?

In Artificial Intelligence, **Search** is the universal problem-solving mechanism used by an agent to navigate from an **initial state** to a **goal state** through a sequence of actions. Every search problem is formally defined as a 5-tuple:

$$\langle S, S_0, A, \text{StepCost}, \text{GoalTest} \rangle$$

where $S$ is the set of states, $S_0$ is the start state, $A$ is the set of actions, StepCost is the cost function, and GoalTest determines whether a state satisfies the objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig framing):** Search algorithms are evaluated along four axes — **Completeness, Optimality, Time Complexity, and Space Complexity** — using the standard variables $b$ (branching factor), $d$ (depth of the shallowest goal), $m$ (maximum depth of the search tree), and $l$ (depth limit).

### 1.2 The Two Grand Families of Search

| Family | Knowledge Used | Also Known As | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blind Search** | Only the problem definition (no domain knowledge) | Uninformed Search | Module 2 Core |
| **Heuristic Search** | Uses a domain-specific estimate of distance to goal | Informed Search | Module 2 Core |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are lost inside a huge multi-floor building looking for the exit.
> - **Blind Search** is like walking with your eyes closed — you must use a fixed rule (e.g., always turn left, or always count doors) because you have *no idea* where the exit is.
> - **Heuristic Search** is like walking with a signboard that reads "EXIT → 20 m" at every corridor. The estimate isn't always exact, but it dramatically reduces the wandering.

### 1.3 Formal Definitions of the Three Blind Strategies

**Depth First Search (DFS):** A node-expansion strategy that always expands the **deepest unexpanded node** in the current frontier. It uses a **LIFO Stack** data structure. Memory-light, but it can dive infinitely down a useless branch.

**Breadth First Search (BFS):** A node-expansion strategy that always expands the **shallowest unexpanded node** in the frontier. It uses a **FIFO Queue**. Guarantees the shortest path in terms of number of steps (when all step costs are uniform).

**Iterative Deepening Search (IDS):** A hybrid that runs DFS repeatedly with an **incrementing depth limit** $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, d$. It combines the memory efficiency of DFS with the optimality of BFS.

### 1.4 Heuristic Functions — The Brain of Informed Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Function $h(n)$** is defined as an *estimated cost* of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal state. The word *heuristic* comes from the Greek *heuriskein* meaning "to discover."

A heuristic is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost to the goal. It is **consistent (monotonic)** if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ generated by action $a$ with cost $c(n,a,n')$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n,a,n') + h(n')$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

#### 2.1.1 Depth First Search (DFS)

1. Push the root node onto a **stack**.
2. Pop the top node; if it passes the **GoalTest**, return it.
3. Otherwise, **push all its children** (in any order) onto the stack.
4. If the stack is empty, return **failure**.
5. Goto Step 2.

* **Why does it dive deep first?** Because the stack always pops the most recently added child — the one deepest in the tree.
* **Why is memory so cheap?** We only store the path from root to the current node plus the unexplored siblings at each level — never the entire frontier.
* **Why is it incomplete in infinite spaces?** It can follow an infinite branch forever and never return to try shallower goals.

#### 2.1.2 Breadth First Search (BFS)

1. Enqueue the root node into a **FIFO queue**.
2. Dequeue the front node; if GoalTest passes, return it.
3. Enqueue all its children at the **end** of the queue.
4. If queue is empty, return **failure**.
5. Goto Step 2.

* **Why optimal for uniform costs?** BFS expands nodes in order of increasing depth, so the first goal it pops is at minimum depth $d$.
* **Why is memory the killer?** At depth $d$, the frontier can hold up to $b^d$ nodes — it explodes exponentially.

#### 2.1.3 Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)

1. For $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty$:
2. Run a depth-limited DFS with cut-off depth $\ell$.
3. If the goal is found, return the solution.
4. If the entire tree is exhausted at level $\ell$, increment $\ell$ and repeat.

* **Why bother redoing shallow work?** The overhead of regenerating upper levels is small: at depth $d$, the work done is dominated by the final level ($b^d$ nodes), so the redundancy is bounded.
* **Why is it the sweet spot?** It enjoys BFS's completeness + optimality and DFS's $O(bd)$ memory.

#### 2.1.4 Heuristic Search Mechanics — Best-First Family

* **Greedy Best-First Search** evaluates nodes by $f(n) = h(n)$ only. Fast, but not optimal.
