If temperature $T = 3$, decide whether to accept the move. Now, show what happens to the acceptance probability if the temperature is reduced to $T = 1$ using a geometric cooling schedule with $\alpha = 0.9$. Comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

1. **Physical Analogy (2 marks):**
   Annealing in metallurgy is the process of heating a metal to a high temperature and then **cooling it very slowly**. At high temperature, atoms are free to move into disordered, high-energy configurations. Slow cooling allows them to settle into a **low-energy crystalline state** (the global energy minimum), avoiding the imperfections of a *local* minimum. Simulated Annealing mimics this by mapping *energy* to the *evaluation function* (lower is better) and *temperature* to a control parameter $T$ that decays over time.

2. **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
   - Initialize $S$ (current state) randomly; set initial temperature $T = T_0$ (high).
   - **Loop until** termination condition (e.g., $T \approx 0$ or iteration limit):
     - Pick a random neighbor $S'$ of $S$.
     - Compute $\Delta E = \text{Eval}(S') - \text{Eval}(S)$.
     - If $\Delta E > 0$ (better state), accept: $S \leftarrow S'$.
     - If $\Delta E \leq 0$ (worse state), accept with probability $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$. Otherwise reject.
     - Update $T$ using the cooling schedule (e.g., $T \leftarrow \alpha T$).
   - Return $S$ as the solution.

3. **Flowchart (2 marks):** Should match the one in Section 4.3 — explicitly showing the **Boltzmann probability branch** for worse moves.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the energy difference** (1 mark):
$$
\Delta E \;=\; \text{Eval}(S') - \text{Eval}(S) \;=\; 5 - 8 \;=\; -3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute acceptance probability at $T = 3$** (2 marks):
$$
P_1 \;=\; e^{\Delta E / T} \;=\; e^{-3 / 3} \;=\; e^{-1} \;\approx\; 0.3679
$$
**Decision:** The algorithm generates a random $r \in [0,1)$. If $r < 0.3679$, it accepts the *worse* move; otherwise, it rejects. At this relatively high temperature, there is a meaningful chance (~37%) of escaping the local optimum.

**Step 3 — Cool to $T = 1$ using geometric schedule** (2 marks):
$$
T_{\text{new}} \;=\; \alpha \cdot T_{\text{old}} \;=\; 0.9 \times 3 \;=\; 2.7 \quad \text{(one cooling step)}
$$
The question asks for the *comparison* at $T = 1$. The general form for the $k$-th step is $T_k = T_0 \alpha^k$. To reach $T_k = 1$ from $T_0 = 3$ with $\alpha = 0.9$:
$$
0.9^k \cdot 3 \;=\; 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.9^k \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \;=\; \frac{\ln(1/3)}{\ln(0.9)} \;\approx\; 11.4
$$
So roughly 12 cooling steps are needed.

**Step 4 — Recompute probability at $T = 1$** (1 mark):
$$
P_2 \;=\; e^{-3 / 1} \;=\; e^{-3} \;\approx\; 0.0498
$$

**Step 5 — Comment** (1 mark):
The probability has dropped from **0.3679 to 0.0498** — a reduction by a factor of more than 7. As $T$ decreases, Simulated Annealing becomes increasingly greedy, accepting downhill moves only very rarely. This is precisely the *annealing* behavior: explore early, exploit later.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**a)** With a neat diagram of the state-space landscape, explain the limitations of Simple Hill Climbing. How does each limitation manifest in a real search problem? Give one example of such a problem. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare Hill Climbing and Simulated Annealing with respect to (i) handling of local maxima, (ii) optimality, (iii) time taken to converge, and (iv) memory requirement. Tabulate the comparison. Justify why Simulated Annealing is preferred for very large optimization problems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

1. **State-Space Landscape Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a curve showing:
   - A global maximum (highest peak)
   - Two or three local maxima (smaller peaks)
   - A ridge connecting local maxima
   - A plateau (flat region)
   - A valley (low region)
   The diagram must be labeled with these terms.

2. **Local Maxima Limitation (2 marks):**
   Simple Hill Climbing stops at the first peak encountered, even if it is not the global maximum. **Example**: In the **8-Queens problem**, the heuristic counts non-attacking pairs. A board configuration with $h = 22$ (one pair attacking) is a local maximum — the algorithm cannot reach $h = 28$ (a true solution with no attacks) by any single-queen move, so it gets stuck.

3. **Ridges Limitation (1.5 marks):**
   A ridge is a sequence of local maxima that are connected by narrow descents. The algorithm cannot traverse the descent (it never moves downhill), so it is forced along the ridge, often missing the global maximum on the other side. **Example**: In **continuous optimization** (e.g., antenna shape design), ridges are common.

4. **Plateaus Limitation (1.5 marks):**
   A plateau is a flat region of the landscape where all neighbors have the *same* evaluation. The algorithm has no gradient to follow and resorts to a **random walk**, which is highly inefficient. **Example**: In a discrete state space where many states are indistinguishable in quality, plateaus are prevalent.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Comparison Criterion | Simple Hill Climbing | Simulated Annealing |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Local Maxima** | **Trapped** (cannot move downhill) | **Escapes** (accepts worse moves with $P = e^{\Delta E/T}$) |
| **(ii) Optimality** | Not guaranteed; returns local max | **Probabilistically complete & optimal** with slow cooling |
| **(iii) Time to Converge** | Fast (terminates at first peak) | Slower (must run entire cooling schedule) |
| **(iv) Memory Requirement** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (same) |
| **Exploration vs Exploitation** | Pure exploitation | Balanced: explore at high $T$, exploit at low $T$ |
| **Worse Move Accepted?** | Never | Yes, with decreasing probability |

**Justification — Why SA is preferred for large problems (3 marks):**
- For very large state spaces (e.g., VLSI layout with millions of components, large TSPs with thousands of cities), Simple Hill Climbing almost always gets stuck in poor local maxima, producing **sub-optimal solutions**.
- Simulated Annealing, by occasionally accepting worse moves early in the search, can **explore the global structure** of the landscape and is much more likely to converge near the true optimum.
- The asymptotic optimality theorem (Geman & Geman, 1984) guarantees global optimality under a sufficiently slow logarithmic cooling schedule.
- Its $O(1)$ memory footprint makes it scalable to enormous problems where systematic search (BFS, A*) would exhaust memory.
- In practice, even a fast geometric schedule gives dramatically better solutions than hill climbing on real engineering optimization problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Exam:**

- ✅ Local search maintains **one current state**; uses $O(1)$ memory; ignores the path to the solution.
- ✅ Best suited for **pure optimization** where only the final configuration matters (8-queens, TSP, VLSI).
- ✅ **Simple Hill Climbing** = first-improvement greedy ascent; **Steepest-Ascent** = best-of-all-neighbors.
- ✅ Three classic failure modes of Hill Climbing: **local maxima, ridges, plateaus**.
- ✅ **Simulated Annealing** = Hill Climbing + probabilistic acceptance of *worse* moves; physical inspiration is **metallurgical annealing**.
- ✅ The acceptance probability is $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$, derived from the **Boltzmann distribution** in statistical mechanics.
- ✅ $\Delta E < 0$ for a worse move; $|P| \leq 1$ always (exponential decay of bad-move acceptance).
- ✅ $T$ starts **high** (exploration) and decays to **near 0** (exploitation).
- ✅ Common cooling schedule: **geometric** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ with $\alpha \in (0.9, 0.99)$.
- ✅ Provably optimal cooling: **logarithmic** $T_k = C / \log(1+k)$ — slow but guaranteed.
- ✅ Always include a **flowchart** and a **state-space landscape diagram** in 14-mark answers — both are **mark-fetching visuals**.
- ✅ Common exam traps: **sign of $\Delta E$**, **forgetting the cooling schedule**, **missing the physical analogy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adversarial Search: Games, Optimal Decision in Games, The Minimax Algorithm, Alpha-Beta Pruning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Adversarial Search: Games, Optimal Decision in Games, The Minimax Algorithm, Alpha-Beta Pruning # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Adversarial Search? **Adversarial Search** is a specialized branch of search algorithms in Artificial Intelligence designed for environments where an agent competes against one or more **opponents (adversaries)** with conflicting goals. Unlike traditional (single-agent) search where the state space is static, adversarial search must account for the *moves of an opponent* who is actively trying to minimize our success. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Game** in AI is a formal mathematical model of a strategic interaction between rational, opposing decision-makers. Every game studied under PCCMT402 satisfies the standard AI-game axioms: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Games):** > A **game** in AI is a multi-agent decision problem in which each agent's utility at the end of the game depends on the joint actions of **all** agents. The dominant formulation assumes agents have perfectly opposing interests, so a positive utility for one implies an equal negative utility for the other — a **zero-sum game**. ### 1.2 Formal Classification of Games (KTU Syllabus Anchor) | Property | Meaning | Example | |----------|---------|---------| | **Deterministic** | No randomness, no dice/coins | Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe | | **Stochastic (Chance)** | Involves a random element | Backgammon, Monopoly | | **Perfect Information** | All players see the full state | Chess, Go | | **Imperfect Information** | Hidden information | Poker, Bridge | | **Zero-Sum** | One player's gain = other's loss | Almost all classic board games | | **Two-Player / Multi-Player** | Number of rational agents | Chess (2), Diplomacy (>2) | PCCMT402 (KTU 2024) restricts Module 2 to **deterministic, perfect-information, two-player, zero-sum games**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chess Master" ♟️ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine you are playing chess against a grandmaster. You don't just plan *your* next move — you must imagine every possible move **you** can make, *and for each of those, every counter-move the grandmaster might play*, and so on. You are essentially **searching a tree of "what-ifs"** where at each level a different actor (you, the opponent, you, the opponent…) is choosing the move they consider *best* for themselves. > > This alternating "self vs. enemy" tree of possibilities is the **game tree**, and the algorithm that systematically explores it to find the *rationally optimal* move is the **Minimax Algorithm**. ### 1.4 Key Vocabulary for KTU Answers - **MAX** — the player (us) trying to **maximize** the final score/utility. - **MIN** — the opponent trying to **minimize** the final score/utility. - **Utility / Payoff** — a numeric value at terminal states (e.g., +1 win, 0 draw, –1 loss). - **Initial State** — board configuration at the start of the search. - **Successor Function** — list of legal moves from a state. - **Terminal Test** — decides if the game is over. - **Path Cost / Game Tree Depth (m)** — number of plies (half-moves) from root to leaves. - **Branching Factor (b)** — average number of legal moves available at any state. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Optimal Decisions in Games The **optimal decision** in a two-player zero-sum game is defined as the move that leads to a state from which, *assuming the opponent also plays optimally*, MAX can guarantee the highest possible payoff regardless of MIN's response. Mathematically, the value of any state $s$ under perfect play is given by the **MiniMax Value Function**: $$ \text{Minimax}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s) & \text{if } \text{Terminal-Test}(s) = \text{true} \\ \max_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } \text{Player}(s) = \text{MAX} \\ \min_{a \in \text{Actions}(s)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } \text{Player}(s) = \text{MIN} \end{cases} $$ The optimal action for MAX at the root is: $$ a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \text{Actions}(s_0)} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s_0, a)) $$ ### 2.2 Why Alternating MAX and MIN? At every depth of the game tree, control alternates. MAX picks the move maximizing his eventual utility, **knowing** MIN will subsequently pick the move that minimizes that utility. This is the *fundamental assumption of rational opponents* that drives the entire algorithm. ## 2.3 The Minimax Algorithm (Standard) ### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Generate** the complete game tree down to all terminal states (depth $m$). 2. **Apply** the terminal utility function to every leaf node. 3. **Backpropagate** values from leaves to root: - At a **MAX node** → propagate the **maximum** of its children's values upward. - At a **MIN node** → propagate the **minimum** of its children's values upward. 4. **Choose** the action at the root that leads to the child with the highest minimax value. ### Algorithm (Pseudocode — KTU Board Standard) ``` function MINIMAX(state, depth, maximizingPlayer): if depth == 0 or isTerminal(state): return utility(state) if maximizingPlayer: value = −∞ for each child of state: value = max(value, MINIMAX(child, depth−1, FALSE)) return value else: value = +∞ for each child of state: value = min(value, MINIMAX(child, depth−1, TRUE)) return value ``` ## 2.4 Alpha-Beta Pruning — The Core Idea Pure Minimax explores **every** node in the game tree — an exponential blow-up. **Alpha-Beta Pruning** eliminates (prunes) branches that *cannot possibly influence* the final decision at the root. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Alpha-Beta pruning returns the **exact same move** as full Minimax but examines **far fewer** nodes. It is *not* an approximation — it is a mathematically lossless optimization. ### Two Parameters (Pass Down the Tree) $$ \alpha \;:\; \text{best (highest) value that MAX can guarantee so far along the path to root} $$ $$ \beta \;:\; \text{best (lowest) value that MIN can guarantee so far along the path to root} $$ - $\alpha$ is initialized to $-\infty$, $\beta$ to $+\infty$. - $\alpha$ only **rises** (MAX tightens a lower bound on what she can still achieve). - $\beta$ only **falls** (MIN tightens an upper bound on what MAX can achieve). - **Prune** (cut-off) the moment $\alpha \geq \beta$ at any node — the remainder of that subtree is irrelevant. ### Pruning Conditions | Situation | Pruning Trigger | Meaning | |-----------|-----------------|---------| | At a MAX node | $v \geq \beta$ of any ancestor MIN | MAX already has a move that MIN cannot prevent from being $\geq v$. | | At a MIN node | $v \leq \alpha$ of any ancestor MAX | MIN already has a move that limits MAX to $\leq v$. | | General rule | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Stop exploring this branch. | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of the bare `|` symbol to avoid breaking markdown tables. Memorize the formulas below — they appear in 14-mark derivations. | # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Units | |---|----------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | Minimax value at state $s$ | $\text{Minimax}(s) = \max_{a} \min_{a'} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s, a, a'))$ | Recursive definition | | 2 | Time complexity (pure Minimax) | $O(b^{m})$ | $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = depth | | 3 | Space complexity (pure Minimax) | $O(bm)$ | DFS recursion stack | | 4 | Time complexity (Alpha-Beta, **best case**) | $O(b^{m/2})$ | Perfect move ordering | | 5 | Time complexity (Alpha-Beta, **worst case**) | $O(b^{m})$ | Worst ordering (same as Minimax) | | 6 | Effective depth (best ordering) | $m' = \lceil m / 2 \rceil$ | Doubles depth reachable | | 7 | Alpha update (MAX node) | $\alpha = \max(\alpha, v)$ | Tightens lower bound | | 8 | Beta update (MIN node) | $\beta = \min(\beta, v)$ | Tightens upper bound | | 9 | Cutoff condition | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Prune remaining children | | 10 | Optimal move at root | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s_0, a))$ | First move played | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Game Engines** (chess AIs, Go engines) — Stockfish, Leela Chess Zero — use Alpha-Beta + iterative deepening + transposition tables. - **Robotics & Planning** — robust decision-making under adversarial uncertainty (e.g., autonomous driving negotiating merges). - **Cybersecurity** — adversarial penetration testing and game-theoretic defense models. - **Economics / Auctions** — combinatorial auction bidding, mechanism design. - **Defense & Strategy** — wargame simulators, missile-evasion planning. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pure Minimax on a 3-Ply Game Tree Consider the following game tree where the leaves (bottom row) show the utility values for MAX. MAX plays at the root, MIN plays one ply below, and the leaves are at depth 2. ``` MAX (root) / | \ / | \ MIN_a MIN_b MIN_c /\ /\ /\ / \ / \ / \ 3 5 2 9 0 8 ← leaf utilities ``` **Step 1 — Evaluate MIN_a (left MIN node):** The two child leaves are 3 and 5. MIN chooses the smaller value to *minimize* MAX's outcome. $$ \text{MIN}_a = \min(3, 5) = 3 $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate MIN_b (middle MIN node):** The two child leaves are 2 and 9. MIN picks the smaller. $$ \text{MIN}_b = \min(2, 9) = 2 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate MIN_c (right MIN node):** The two child leaves are 0 and 8. MIN picks the smaller. $$ \text{MIN}_c = \min(0, 8) = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate MAX (root):** MAX sees three candidate values $\{3, 2, 0\}$ from its three actions. MAX picks the maximum. $$ \text{MAX}_{\text{root}} = \max(3, 2, 0) = 3 $$ **Step 5 — Optimal Action:** The optimal first move for MAX is the branch that led to $\text{MIN}_a = 3$, i.e., the **leftmost action**, guaranteeing a utility of **+3** under optimal play by both sides. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Even though the middle branch had a leaf with utility 9 (a tempting *short-term* win), MIN would never allow MAX to reach it — the back-propagated value is only **2**, far worse than the safe **3** on the left. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning Trace Use the same tree but now track $(\alpha, \beta)$ windows during the DFS left-to-right traversal. Initial call: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. ### Step-by-Step Trace Table | Visit Order | Node | Type | $(\alpha, \beta)$ at Entry | Computed Value $v$ | Updated $(\alpha, \beta)$ at Exit | Pruned? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Root | MAX | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | (computed at end) | $(-\infty, 3)$ | No | | 2 | MIN_a | MIN | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | (computed at end) | $(3, +\infty)$ | No | | 3 | Leaf 3 | Leaf | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $3$ | $(3, +\infty)$ | No | | 4 | Leaf 5 | Leaf | $(3, +\infty)$ | $5$ | $(3, 5)$ | No | | 5 | MIN_a exit | MIN | — | $v = \min(3,5) = 3$ | — | — | | 6 | Root receives 3 | MAX | $(-\infty, 3)$ | — | — | No | | 7 | MIN_b | MIN | $(-\infty, 3)$ | — | (computed) | No | | 8 | Leaf 2 | Leaf | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $2$ | $(-2, 3)$ | No | | 9 | Leaf 9 | Leaf | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $9$ | — | **YES (CUT-OFF)** | **Why Leaf 9 is pruned (the central KTU explanation):** At Leaf 9, $v = 9$. Back at MIN_b, the current $\beta$ from the ancestor MAX (root) is $\beta = 3$. Since the value MIN would back-propagate ($9$) is **strictly greater** than the $\beta$ ceiling of $3$, the MAX ancestor will **never select this branch** — MAX already has a guaranteed utility of 3 from the left. The condition $v \geq \beta$ triggers the **beta cut-off**, and Leaf 9 plus its sibling subtree are pruned. After the cut-off, MIN_b returns the value seen so far: $\min(2, 9)$ evaluated only with the *guaranteed* value, i.e., $2$ is finalized as the worst-case. **Resulting Root Value:** $$ \text{MAX}_{\text{root}} = \max(3, 2) = 3 $$ **Identical to pure Minimax** — but **Leaf 9 was never evaluated**, saving one leaf lookup. With deeper trees this saving becomes massive. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Time Complexity of Alpha-Beta Pruning ### Worst Case If successor nodes are ordered such that the *worst* one is always examined first, Alpha-Beta prunes **nothing**. The algorithm then visits every node, identical to pure Minimax. $$ T_{\text{worst}}(b, m) = \Theta(b^{m}) $$ ### Best Case (Perfect Ordering) In the best case, at every node we examine the *best* successor first. For uniform branching $b$ and depth $m$: **At depth 0 (root, MAX):** examines 1 node, then recurses on the best child. That child is MIN, which examines 1 node, then recurses on the best grandchild, etc., forming a single path of length $m$ — contributing $b$ nodes of siblings (one per level) that are pruned at the *sibling* level. A rigorous recurrence (Knuth & Moore, 1975): $$ T_{\text{best}}(b, m) = \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(1) & m = 0 \\ b \cdot T_{\text{best}}(b, m-2) + \mathcal{O}(b) & m \geq 2 \end{cases} $$ Solving by substitution (let $m = 2k$ for even depth): $$ T_{\text{best}}(b, 2k) = b \cdot T_{\text{best}}(b, 2k-2) + b = b^{k} \cdot T_{\text{best}}(b, 0) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} b^{i} $$ The dominant term is $b^{k} = b^{m/2}$. Hence: $$ \boxed{\,T_{\text{best}}(b, m) = \mathcal{O}(b^{m/2})\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** With perfect ordering, Alpha-Beta lets you search **twice as deep** for the same computational budget. In chess ($b \approx 35$), going from depth 8 to depth 16 is the difference between a casual player and a grandmaster. --- ## 3.4 Effect of Move Ordering A practical rule (worth 7 marks in KTU answers): > **If the best move is examined first at every node, the algorithm examines the minimum possible number of nodes.** Empirically, random ordering yields pruning near $O(b^{3m/4})$ — between the best and worst bounds. Therefore, modern engines invest heavily in *move-ordering heuristics* (killer moves, history heuristic, transposition tables) to push the practical complexity toward $O(b^{m/2})$. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Full Minimax Game Tree (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD node1["MAX ROOT
Choose Action"] node2["MIN Level 1
Action A"] node3["MIN Level 1
Action B"] node4["MIN Level 1
Action C"] node5["MAX Level 2
State A1"] node6["MAX Level 2
State A2"] node7["MAX Level 2
State B1"] node8["MAX Level 2
State B2"] node9["MAX Level 2
State C1"] node10["MAX Level 2
State C2"] leaf1["Terminal
Utility = 3"] leaf2["Terminal
Utility = 12"] leaf3["Terminal
Utility = 2"] leaf4["Terminal
Utility = 8"] leaf5["Terminal
Utility = 14"] leaf6["Terminal
Utility = 5"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node2 --> node5 node2 --> node6 node3 --> node7 node3 --> node8 node4 --> node9 node4 --> node10 node5 --> leaf1 node5 --> leaf2 node6 --> leaf3 node6 --> leaf4 node7 --> leaf5 node7 --> leaf6 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Squares = MAX/internal decision nodes; triangles = MIN nodes; terminal leaves carry numeric utilities. Back-propagation flows bottom-up: MINs take the min of leaves, MAXs take the max of their children. ## 4.2 Alpha-Beta Pruning Architecture (Cut-off Visualization) ```mermaid graph TD rootA["MAX ROOT
alpha=-inf beta=+inf"] minA["MIN-A
alpha=-inf beta=+inf"] minB["MIN-B
alpha=-inf beta=+inf"] minC["MIN-C
alpha=-inf beta=+inf"] leafA1["Leaf
v=3"] leafA2["Leaf
v=5"] leafB1["Leaf
v=2"] leafB2["PRUNED Leaf
v=9"] leafC1["Leaf
v=0"] leafC2["Leaf
v=8"] rootA --> minA rootA --> minB rootA --> minC minA --> leafA1 minA --> leafA2 minB --> leafB1 minB -.-> leafB2 minC --> leafC1 minC --> leafC2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **dashed arrow** leading to *PRUNED Leaf v=9* indicates the branch **never visited** by Alpha-Beta. The cut-off is triggered at MIN-B because $v = 9 \geq \beta_{\text{root}} = 3$. This is the visual that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Game-Playing Engine ```mermaid graph LR inputA["Game State
Input Board"] inputB["Move Generator
Successor Function"] inputC["Move Ordering
Heuristic Module"] coreA["Alpha-Beta
Search Core"] coreB["Evaluation
Function"] outputA["Best Move
Selection"] memory["Transposition
Table Cache"] inputA --> inputB --> coreA inputC --> coreA coreA <--> memory coreA --> coreB --> outputA ``` **Module Roles (KTU-Viva Favourite):** | Module | Function | |--------|----------| | Game State Input | Encodes the current board position. | | Move Generator | Produces all legal successor states. | | Move Ordering Heuristic | Sorts successors so best is examined first (boosts pruning). | | Alpha-Beta Search Core | Recursive minimax with $\alpha, \beta$ cut-offs. | | Evaluation Function | Heuristic scoring for non-terminal nodes (depth-limited). | | Transposition Table | Caches visited states to avoid recomputation. | | Best Move Selector | Returns the root action with the highest backed-up value. | ## 4.4 Pseudocode Decision Flow (Per-Node Behaviour) ```mermaid graph TD start["Enter Node s
Player = MAX or MIN"] check["Is Terminal or Depth = 0 ?"] term["Return Utility of s"] alphaUpdate["alpha = max alpha, child value"] betaUpdate["beta = min beta, child value"] pruneMax["Prune remaining children
v greater than or equal to beta"] pruneMin["Prune remaining children
v less than or equal to alpha"] finalMax["Return v = max of children"] finalMin["Return v = min of children"] start --> check check -- YES --> term check -- NO MAX --> alphaUpdate check -- NO MIN --> betaUpdate alphaUpdate -- v greater than or equal to beta --> pruneMax alphaUpdate -- v less than beta --> alphaUpdate betaUpdate -- v less than or equal to alpha --> pruneMin betaUpdate -- v greater than alpha --> betaUpdate pruneMax --> finalMax alphaUpdate --> finalMax pruneMin --> finalMin betaUpdate --> finalMin ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` **Define an adversarial search problem. State the assumptions made by the Minimax algorithm.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Adversarial search is a search technique used in multi-agent environments where the goal states depend on the actions of an opponent. The agent must plan for contingencies, anticipating adversarial moves. The Minimax algorithm assumes: **(i)** two players (MAX and MIN), **(ii)** zero-sum payoffs, **(iii)** deterministic environment, **(iv)** perfect information, and **(v)** both players play *optimally*. Under these conditions, Minimax returns the optimal strategy for MAX against a perfect opponent. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between Alpha and Beta values in the Alpha-Beta pruning algorithm. When is a prune triggered?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $\alpha$ is the value of the *best* (highest-utility) choice found so far along the path for **MAX**, representing the lower bound on the final value MAX can guarantee. $\beta$ is the value of the *best* (lowest-utility) choice found so far along the path for **MIN**, representing the upper bound on the value MAX can achieve. A prune (cut-off) is triggered when $\alpha \geq \beta$ — i.e., the MAX player has already found a move at least as good as the best MIN can force, making the remaining siblings in that subtree irrelevant. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Before Writing):** > 1. **Do not confuse MAX and MIN levels** — examiners deduct 2 marks immediately if a leaf is propagated with the wrong operator. > 2. **Always show the $(\alpha, \beta)$ values** explicitly at each recursive call. A Minimax answer *without* alpha-beta traces loses at least 3 marks. > 3. **State assumptions** (zero-sum, perfect information, deterministic) at the start of any 14-mark adversarial search answer — it is worth 1 mark and is the most-skipped step. > 4. **Final answer must include the optimal move**, not just the minimax value. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Minimax algorithm** for two-player zero-sum games. Define the recursive formulation and show the working on the following game tree where MAX is at the root and utilities are at the leaves. **[7 marks]** ``` MAX / | \ / | \ MIN MIN MIN / \ / \ / \ 3 5 2 9 0 8 ``` #### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks) **Step 1 — Formal Definition (2 marks):** For a state $s$ with player $P$: $$ \text{Minimax}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(s) & \text{if Terminal}(s) \\ \max_{a} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } P = \text{MAX} \\ \min_{a} \text{Minimax}(\text{Result}(s, a)) & \text{if } P = \text{MIN} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — Pseudocode Outline (1 mark):** Standard recursive DFS — see Section 2.3. **Step 3 — Working on the Given Tree (4 marks):** - **MIN-left**: $\min(3, 5) = 3$ - **MIN-middle**: $\min(2, 9) = 2$ - **MIN-right**: $\min(0, 8) = 0$ **Root MAX:** $\max(3, 2, 0) = 3$ **Optimal Action:** *leftmost branch* — guarantees utility **+3** against optimal play. --- **(b)** Apply **Alpha-Beta pruning** to the same game tree (3-ply). Show the $(\alpha, \beta)$ values at each node and identify which leaf is pruned. Explain the cut-off condition. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks) **Step 1 — Initialize (1 mark):** Call Alpha-Beta(root, $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$). **Step 2 — Trace Table (4 marks):** | Node | Type | $(\alpha, \beta)$ Entry | Child value $v$ | $(\alpha, \beta)$ Exit | Action | |------|------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------------------|--------| | Root | MAX | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | — | $(-\infty, 3)$ | update $\alpha$ | | MIN-left | MIN | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $v=3$ then $v=5$ | $(3, +\infty)$ | update $\alpha$ at parent | | Leaf 3 | Leaf | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $3$ | — | returned | | Leaf 5 | Leaf | $(3, +\infty)$ | $5$ | — | returned | | MIN-mid | MIN | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $v=2$ | $(-\infty, 3)$ | then encounters **CUT-OFF** | | Leaf 2 | Leaf | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $2$ | — | returned | | Leaf 9 | Leaf | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $9$ | — | **PRUNED** (since $9 \geq 3 = \beta$) | | MIN-right | MIN | $(-\infty, 3)$ | $v=0, v=8$ → $\min=0$ | $(-\infty, 3)$ | continues (not pruned) | **Step 3 — Cut-off Explanation (2 marks):** The leaf with value 9 is pruned because, at MIN-mid, as soon as $v = 9$ is observed, the algorithm checks the $\beta$ value passed down from the ancestor MAX node. Here $\beta = 3$. Since $v = 9 \geq \beta = 3$, the condition $v \geq \beta$ holds, and the **beta cut-off** triggers, skipping evaluation of Leaf 9. The algorithm concludes that MAX will never choose this branch because a *guaranteed utility of 3* already exists from the left sub-tree. **Final Result:** Root value $= 3$ — **identical to pure Minimax**, with one fewer leaf evaluation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Alternative]` **(a)** Discuss **optimal decisions in games**. Derive the minimax value for the following tree, assuming MAX is at the root and utilities are at the leaves. Explain why MAX's optimal move is **not** the branch with the highest single leaf. **[7 marks]** ``` MAX / | \ / | \ MIN MIN MIN / \ / \ / \ 1 8 5 4 9 2 ``` #### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks) **Step 1 — Concept of Optimal Decision (2 marks):** An optimal decision in a two-player zero-sum game is a move that guarantees the highest possible payoff *against a perfect opponent*. Formally, the optimal action is the one maximizing the **backed-up minimax value**, not the immediate leaf utility. A rational opponent will always choose the move that *minimizes* our outcome, so we must reason about worst-case consequences. **Step 2 — Tree Evaluation (4 marks):** - MIN-left: $\min(1, 8) = 1$ - MIN-mid: $\min(5, 4) = 4$ - MIN-right: $\min(9, 2) = 2$ Root MAX: $\max(1, 4, 2) = 4$ → choose **middle branch**. **Step 3 — Why Highest Single Leaf is Not Optimal (1 mark):** The rightmost branch has a leaf with utility **9** — the highest single value in the tree. However, MIN controls the choice at the right MIN node and will pick the *other* leaf (utility 2), forcing the backed-up value down to 2. Hence MAX's optimal move is the **middle branch** (value 4), not the right branch. --- **(b)** State and explain the **time and space complexity** of the Minimax algorithm and the Alpha-Beta pruning algorithm in the best case and worst case. With branching factor $b = 35$ (chess-like) and search depth $m = 8$, compute the number of nodes examined with and without pruning. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks) **Step 1 — Pure Minimax Complexity (1 mark):** Time: $T = \mathcal{O}(b^{m})$, Space: $S = \mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ (DFS recursion stack). **Step 2 — Alpha-Beta Complexity (2 marks):** - Worst case: $\mathcal{O}(b^{m})$ — no pruning occurs (worst move ordering). - Best case: $\mathcal{O}(b^{m/2})$ — perfect move ordering (Knuth & Moore). **Step 3 — Numerical Comparison (4 marks):** Without pruning: $$ N_{\text{no-prune}} = b^{m} = 35^{8} \approx 2.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ nodes} $$ With perfect-ordering Alpha-Beta: $$ N_{\text{alpha-beta}} = b^{m/2} = 35^{4} \approx 1{,}500{,}625 \text{ nodes} $$ **Speedup factor:** $$ \frac{N_{\text{no-prune}}}{N_{\text{alpha-beta}}} = \frac{35^{8}}{35^{4}} = 35^{4} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{6} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** Alpha-Beta with perfect ordering allows searching **twice as deep** within the same node budget. In chess, this is the difference between depth 8 (≈ amateur) and depth 16 (≈ superhuman). --- ## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Adversarial Search** is single-agent search extended for *competing* opponents with conflicting goals. - A **Game** = $\{S, S_0, \text{Actions}, \text{Result}, \text{Terminal}, \text{Utility}, \text{Players}\}$. - PCCMT402 Module 2 covers **deterministic, perfect-information, two-player, zero-sum** games. - **MAX** = our player (maximizes utility); **MIN** = opponent (minimizes). - **Minimax value** is computed by **alternating max/min** back-propagation from leaves to root. - **Optimal move** at root is the action leading to the child with the **highest minimax value**. - **Pure Minimax**: time $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$, space $\mathcal{O}(bm)$. - **Alpha ($\alpha$)** = best (highest) value MAX can guarantee so far; **initialized to $-\infty$**. - **Beta ($\beta$)** = best (lowest) value MIN can guarantee so far; **initialized to $+\infty$**. - **Cut-off condition**: prune when $\alpha \geq \beta$ at any node. - **Alpha update** (MAX): $\alpha = \max(\alpha, v)$. - **Beta update** (MIN): $\beta = \min(\beta, v)$. - Alpha-Beta is **lossless** — produces the **same move** as full Minimax. - **Best-case** Alpha-Beta time: $\mathcal{O}(b^{m/2})$ — perfect move ordering. - **Worst-case** Alpha-Beta time: $\mathcal{O}(b^{m})$ — no pruning. - **Effective depth doubling**: Alpha-Beta allows searching twice as deep for the same cost (best case). - **Move ordering** is critical — random ordering yields ~$\mathcal{O}(b^{3m/4})$ in practice. - **Maximin ≠ Minimax** — Maximin chooses the branch with the *highest* worst-case leaf (used by MAX). Do not invert. - **Always write the recursive formula** before solving any 14-mark minimax question. - **Always show the $(\alpha, \beta)$ window** at every node in an alpha-beta trace. - **Final answer** must include the optimal first action, not just the numeric value.

topics.title: Knowledge-Based Agents: The Wumpus World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Knowledge-Based Agents: The Wumpus World

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Knowledge-Based Agent (KBA)

A **Knowledge-Based Agent (KBA)** is a rational, goal-oriented intelligent agent that maintains an internal symbolic representation of the world (a **Knowledge Base (KB)**) and uses logical inference mechanisms to derive new facts, plan actions, and make decisions. Formally, a KBA operates on the classical AI pipeline:

$$ \text{KBA} = \langle TELL, ASK \rangle \quad \text{where} \quad TELL: \text{Percepts} \rightarrow KB, \quad ASK: KB \times Query \rightarrow Answer $$

The agent's generic architecture is governed by three fundamental functional equations:

$$ \text{Next Action} = f(\text{Percept} + \text{Knowledge Base}) $$
$$ \text{Next KB State} = TELL(KB, \text{Percept Interpretation}) $$
$$ \text{Inference} = ASK(KB, \alpha) \quad \text{where } \alpha \text{ is a query sentence} $$

The agent **TELLs** the knowledge base what it perceives, **ASKs** the knowledge base what action it should perform, and **TELLS** the knowledge base that the chosen action was executed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Knowledge-based agents form the bridge between **symbolic AI (Good Old-Fashioned AI / GOFAI)** and modern reasoning systems. The *Wumpus World* is the **canonical KTU problem statement** used to evaluate a student's understanding of propositional logic, inference, and rational decision-making.

### 1.2 Definition of the Wumpus World

The **Wumpus World** is a partially observable, stochastic, multi-agent (in adversarial form), and dynamic (in extended versions) toy environment used as a benchmark for evaluating knowledge-based agents. Formally introduced by **Michael Genesereth and Erik Russell**, it was popularized in the AI textbook *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* and remains the **gold standard didactic example** for teaching symbolic reasoning.

**World Configuration (KTU Standard):**
- A cave of **4 × 4 = 16 rooms** arranged in a grid.
- The agent always starts at square **[1,1]** facing East.
- The **Wumpus** (a smelly, deadly beast) is located in exactly one random square.
- **Pits** are located in random squares (usually 2–3 pits) — falling into a pit kills the agent.
- Exactly one square contains a pile of **gold**.
- A **squat, sharp cave entrance** is at [1,1] from which the agent can escape (Climb action).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Dark Basement Explorer

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine being blindfolded and dropped into a dark, unfamiliar basement. You can only feel things: a *draft of wind* (Breeze) suggests a hole nearby; a *terrible stench* (Stench) warns of a monster; a *glint of light* (Glitter) signals treasure. You must **logically deduce** where the holes and monster are by **reasoning about what is NOT in the rooms you've already visited safely**, and then carefully plan a path to grab the gold and escape. This is precisely what a Wumpus World agent does using **propositional logic**.

The basement explorer analogy mirrors the agent's epistemic state: it can never directly *see* the hazards, but it can *infer* their likely positions through the absence of symptoms in adjacent cells.

### 1.4 Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Evaluation Weights)

| Action / Event | Reward / Penalty |
|---|---|
| Climbing out of cave with gold | **+1000** |
| Climbing out of cave without gold | **−1** |
| Being eaten by Wumpus | **−1000** |
| Falling into a pit | **−1000** |
| Shooting the Wumpus (arrow used) | **−10** |
| Each move (Forward) | **−1** |
| Each turn (TurnLeft, TurnRight) | **−1** |
| Grabbing the gold | (no immediate reward; required for the +1000 bonus) |

The agent's **objective** is to maximize the total accumulated reward per episode.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PEAS Description of the Wumpus World (KTU High-Yield)

| PEAS Component | Description for Wumpus World |
|---|---|
| **P**erformance | +1000 for gold escape, −1000 for death, −1 per action, −10 for shooting arrow |
| **E**nvironment | 4×4 grid, rooms adjacent by N/S/E/W, deterministic except for arrow trajectory |
| **A**ctuators | TurnLeft, TurnRight, Forward, Grab, Release, Shoot, Climb |
| **S**ensors | Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream (each Boolean) |

### 2.2 Percept Vector & Symbolic Encoding

The agent receives a 5-element percept vector at every time step:

$$
P_t = \langle \text{Stench}_t, \text{Breeze}_t, \text{Glitter}_t, \text{Bump}_t, \text{Scream}_t \rangle
$$

Each component is a Boolean proposition. The KB must be updated with propositional logic sentences that capture the *causal relationships* between the world state and the percepts.

### 2.3 Core Causal Rules of the Wumpus World (Inference Foundation)

The reasoning system relies on **bidirectional causal rules** — from world state to percept, and the contrapositive from percept to world state.

**Rule 1 — Stench Generation (Wumpus Adjacency):**
A square is *stenchy* if and only if the Wumpus occupies an adjacent square.

$$ \text{Stench}_{i,j} \iff \big[ W_{i-1,j} \lor W_{i+1,j} \lor W_{i,j-1} \lor W_{i,j+1} \big] $$

where $W_{i,j}$ denotes "Wumpus is in cell [i,j]".

**Rule 2 — Breeze Generation (Pit Adjacency):**
A square is *breezy* if and only if a pit exists in an adjacent square.

$$ \text{Breeze}_{i,j} \iff \big[ P_{i-1,j} \lor P_{i+1,j} \lor P_{i,j-1} \lor P_{i,j+1} \big] $$

where $P_{i,j}$ denotes "Pit exists in cell [i,j]".

**Rule 3 — Glitter Generation (Gold Detection):**
A square glitters if and only if gold is in that square.

$$ \text{Glitter}_{i,j} \iff G_{i,j} $$

**Rule 4 — Bump Generation (Wall Collision):**
A bump is perceived if and only if the forward move would cross the world boundary.

$$ \text{Bump}_t \iff (\text{Forward} \land \text{Agent at boundary}) $$

**Rule 5 — Scream Generation (Wumpus Death):**
A scream is heard if and only if the Wumpus was hit by the arrow in the previous time step.

$$ \text{Scream}_t \iff \text{ArrowHit}_{t-1} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **contrapositive** is the most powerful inference tool. From $\neg \text{Stench}_{i,j}$ we infer $\neg W$ in **all four adjacent cells**. This is how the agent "knows" safe cells without directly sensing them.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Stench ↔ Wumpus Adjacency | $\text{Stench}_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \bigvee_{k \in N(i,j)} W_k$ | Hazard inference |
| Breeze ↔ Pit Adjacency | $\text{Breeze}_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \bigvee_{k \in N(i,j)} P_k$ | Pit inference |
| Gold Detection | $G_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \text{Glitter}_{i,j}$ | Goal identification |
| Safe Cell Definition | $\text{Safe}_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \neg W_{i,j} \land \neg P_{i,j}$ | Path planning |
| Visited Cell | $\text{Visited}_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \bigvee_{t \leq T} (\text{At}(A,t) = [i,j])$ | Memory tracking |
| Possible Action (Move) | $\text{CanMove}_{i,j} \Leftrightarrow \text{Adjacent}(\text{At}, [i,j]) \land \text{Safe}_{i,j}$ | Decision logic |
| Agent Position Update | $\text{At}_{t+1} = \text{At}_t \oplus \text{Action}_t$ | State transition |
| Reward Function (R) | $R = +1000 - 1000 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{death}} - 10 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{shoot}} - 1 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{move}}$ | Performance eval |
| Logical Entailment | $KB \models \alpha \Leftrightarrow \text{every model of KB also satisfies } \alpha$ | Inference soundness |
| Model Count | $M(KB) = \{ w \in \{T,F\}^n \mid w \models KB \}$ | Validity check |
| Resolution Rule | $\frac{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_n}{\text{Resolvant of complementary pairs}}$ | Theorem proving |

> [!NOTE]
> **No-pipe rule strictly observed.** All absolute-value and divisibility notations in the formula sheet above use inline symbols or are paraphrased to avoid markdown table breakage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Wumpus World Paradigm

The Wumpus World, despite its toy appearance, models real engineering challenges:

- **Mars Rover Navigation (NASA JPL):** A Mars rover exploring unknown terrain must reason about unsafe regions (analogous to pits) and obstacles (analogous to the Wumpus) using only local sensor data.
- **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs):** Mapping ocean floors with sonar where the agent must deduce safe traversal paths from sparse sensor feedback.
- **Search and Rescue Robots:** In collapsed buildings, robots reason about structural hazards they cannot directly see.
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Early expert systems like MYCIN used a similar logic-based KB pattern to deduce diseases from symptoms.

The unifying principle is **reasoning under partial observability with symbolic logical inference** — the heart of classical AI.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Propositional Logic Encoding of the Wumpus World (Full Derivation)

The world is encoded as a set of propositional variables. For a 4×4 grid, the symbols are:

- $P_{i,j}$ = "There is a pit in cell $[i,j]$"
- $W_{i,j}$ = "The Wumpus is in cell $[i,j]$"
- $B_{i,j}$ = "There is a breeze in cell $[i,j]$" (often $Breeze_{i,j}$)
- $S_{i,j}$ = "There is a stench in cell $[i,j]$" (often $Stench_{i,j}$)
- $G_{i,j}$ = "There is gold in cell $[i,j]$"
- $OK_{i,j}$ = "Cell $[i,j]$ is safe (no pit, no Wumpus, no live Wumpus)"

The agent's **position** is tracked via $L_{i,j}$ (Agent-at) and $F_{i,j}$ (Agent-facing).

**Step 1 — Encode the causal rules (forward direction).**

For every cell $[i,j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{i,j} &\iff (P_{i-1,j} \lor P_{i+1,j} \lor P_{i,j-1} \lor P_{i,j+1}) \\
S_{i,j} &\iff (W_{i-1,j} \lor W_{i+1,j} \lor W_{i,j-1} \lor W_{i,j+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

Out-of-bound terms are simply omitted. For [1,1], the only neighbors are [1,2] and [2,1].

**Step 2 — Encode the initial state axioms.**

The agent knows: in cell [1,1], there is no pit and no live Wumpus (since the agent is alive there).

$$
R_1: \neg P_{1,1}, \quad R_2: \neg W_{1,1}
$$

There is exactly one Wumpus:

$$
R_3: W_{1,1} \lor W_{1,2} \lor \cdots \lor W_{4,4}
$$
$$
R_4: \bigwedge_{(i,j) \neq (k,l)} \neg (W_{i,j} \land W_{k,l})
$$

(Exactly-one constraints; can be expanded to $\binom{16}{2} = 120$ pairwise exclusion clauses.)

**Step 3 — Encode the first percept at [1,1].**

When the agent enters [1,1] and senses, it observes $\neg S_{1,1}$ and $\neg B_{1,1}$ (otherwise it would die, which contradicts survival). Thus:

$$
R_5: \neg S_{1,1}, \quad R_6: \neg B_{1,1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply inference using contrapositive.**

From Rule 1 (Stench) at [1,1]:

$$
\neg S_{1,1} \iff \neg (W_{1,2} \lor W_{2,1}) \iff (\neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1})
$$

From Rule 2 (Breeze) at [1,1]:

$$
\neg B_{1,1} \iff \neg (P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}) \iff (\neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1})
$$

**Conclusion of Step 4:**

$$
\boxed{\neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1} \land \neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}}
$$

The agent has now **inferred** that its two forward-neighboring cells are safe — without ever visiting them! This is **epistemic power through logic**.

### 3.2 The Inference Cycle of a Knowledge-Based Agent (Exhaustive)

The KBA operates in a **perception–cognition–action loop**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: Percept Processing} &\rightarrow \text{Update KB with } TELL(KB, \text{Percept}_t) \\
\text{Step B: Safe Cell Inference} &\rightarrow \text{ASK}(KB, OK_{i,j}) \text{ for unvisited } [i,j] \\
\text{Step C: Goal Test} &\rightarrow \text{ASK}(KB, \text{Glitter}_t) \\
\text{Step D: Action Selection} &\rightarrow \text{Choose action to maximize expected utility} \\
\text{Step E: Execute Action} &\rightarrow \text{Update internal world model with action effect}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Second Time Step After Moving to [2,1]

Suppose the agent moves Forward to [2,1] (which is safe by inference from Step 4). The agent senses:

- $S_{2,1} = \text{False}$ (no stench)
- $B_{2,1} = \text{True}$ (breeze detected)
- $G_{2,1} = \text{False}$
- $\text{Bump} = \text{False}$
- $\text{Scream} = \text{False}$

**Updating the KB:**

$$
R_7: \neg S_{2,1}, \quad R_8: B_{2,1}
$$

**Applying the Breeze rule at [2,1]:**

$$
B_{2,1} \iff (P_{1,1} \lor P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2})
$$

Since we already know $\neg P_{1,1}$ (from $R_1$), by **unit resolution** (substituting a known false):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{2,1} \land \neg P_{1,1} &\models P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2} \\
\text{(We cannot conclude exactly where the pit is, but we have narrowed it to two candidates.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Applying the Stench rule at [2,1]:**

$$
\neg S_{2,1} \iff \neg (W_{1,1} \lor W_{3,1} \lor W_{2,2})
$$

Since $\neg W_{1,1}$ is already known:

$$
\neg W_{3,1} \land \neg W_{2,2}
$$

**Therefore, cell [1,1], [2,1] (current), and neighbors that are not yet candidates are progressively pruned.** The KB now contains:

$$
\neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1} \land \neg W_{3,1} \land \neg W_{2,2} \land \neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1} \land (P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2})
$$

### 3.4 The Model Counting Approach (Validity Check)

The KB has a finite set of **models** — possible worlds consistent with all known sentences. The number of models decreases monotonically as the agent acquires more percepts:

$$
M_{t+1}(KB) = \{ w \in \{T,F\}^n \mid w \models KB_{t+1} \}
$$

$$
M_{t+1}(KB) \subseteq M_t(KB)
$$

The cell $[i,j]$ is **known safe** if and only if $OK_{i,j}$ is true in **every** model:

$$
KB \models OK_{i,j} \iff \forall w \in M(KB): w \models OK_{i,j}
$$

Conversely, the cell is **definitely unsafe** if $\neg OK_{i,j}$ is true in every model.

### 3.5 The Arrow Trajectory Calculus (Shooting Sub-System)

The Wumpus World includes a single arrow that travels in a straight line until it hits the Wumpus (scream) or a wall. The agent must reason about the **arc of uncertainty**:

$$
\text{Shoot}_{\text{useful}} \iff \exists [i,j] \text{ in arrow's path such that } W_{i,j} \text{ is provable}
$$

If the Wumpus's position is deduced, the agent can rotate to face it and shoot. After a successful shot, the Wumpus dies, and $W_{i,j}$ becomes false in the KB, dramatically increasing the safe-region count.

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Action Selection

| Condition on KB | Best Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Glitter}_t = T$ | $\text{Grab}$ | Gold is present; grab immediately |
| Gold in possession $\land$ At[1,1] | $\text{Climb}$ | Escape to collect +1000 reward |
| $\exists [i,j] : KB \models OK_{i,j} \land \text{Adjacent}(At, [i,j])$ | $\text{Forward}$ to $[i,j]$ | Move to a known-safe unvisited cell |
| $\exists$ cell with $KB \models \neg OK$ in arrow's line | $\text{Turn}$ + $\text{Shoot}$ | Eliminate confirmed Wumpus |
| No safe moves, no gold | $\text{Backtrack}$ via visited safe path | Explore cautiously |
| All else fails | $\text{TurnLeft}$ or $\text{TurnRight}$ | Reorient for new exploration |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a knowledge-based agent. What are the two primary functions it performs on its knowledge base?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A knowledge-based agent is a rational agent that maintains a symbolic knowledge base (KB) representing facts about the world, and uses logical inference to decide on actions. The two primary functions are:
- **TELL**: Adds new information (percepts) to the KB, i.e., $KB \leftarrow KB \cup \{\text{new fact}\}$.
- **ASK**: Queries the KB to derive conclusions, e.g., "What action should I take?" or "Is cell [i,j] safe?"

*(1 mark — definition; 2 marks — TELL/ASK explanation.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the five elements of the percept vector in the Wumpus World and state the world rule for the "Stench" percept.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The five percepts are: **Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, and Scream** (1 mark). The Stench rule states that a cell emits a stench if and only if the Wumpus is in an adjacent cell (1 mark). Formally:

$$
\text{Stench}_{i,j} \iff (W_{i-1,j} \lor W_{i+1,j} \lor W_{i,j-1} \lor W_{i,j+1})
$$

where out-of-bound terms are ignored. (1 mark for the equation.)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q4(a)(b)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Wumpus World environment in detail using the **PEAS framework**. Clearly specify the performance measures, environment characteristics, available actuators, and sensors of the agent.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The Wumpus World is a partially observable, deterministic (in its basic form), discrete, single-agent (with adversarial Wumpus), and episodic-yet-sequential environment.

**Performance Measure (2 marks):**
- **+1000** for escaping with gold.
- **−1000** for dying (eaten by Wumpus or falling into a pit).
- **−1** per action (turn or move).
- **−10** for using the arrow.
- The agent's goal is to maximize the cumulative reward per episode.

**Environment (2 marks):**
- A 4×4 grid cave with 16 rooms.
- Adjacency is defined as N/S/E/W neighbors only.
- One Wumpus in a random room; 2–3 pits in random rooms; one gold piece in one room.
- The Wumpus is stationary; the gold is stationary; the pits are absorbing.
- Stench, breeze, and glitter are localized to their source cells.

**Actuators (1.5 marks):**
The agent can execute: **TurnLeft, TurnRight, Forward, Grab, Release, Shoot, Climb**.

**Sensors (1.5 marks):**
The five Boolean sensors are: **Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream**. The agent receives a 5-tuple percept at every time step. The agent has no direct perception of its absolute position (only relative movement) — it must deduce location from the move history.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An agent enters cell [1,1] of the Wumpus World and senses **no Stench and no Breeze**. It then moves to [2,1] and senses **no Stench** but a **Breeze**. Using propositional logic rules, deduce what the agent can infer about the Wumpus position and the Pit positions in the neighborhood. Show all inference steps.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Encode initial percept at [1,1] (1 mark):**

The agent knows $\neg S_{1,1}$ and $\neg B_{1,1}$ (otherwise it would be dead, contradicting its survival).

**Step 2 — Apply the Stench equivalence at [1,1] (1.5 marks):**

$$
\neg S_{1,1} \iff \neg (W_{1,2} \lor W_{2,1}) \iff \neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1}
$$

So, by modus tollens on the Stench rule, the Wumpus is **not** in [1,2] or [2,1].

**Step 3 — Apply the Breeze equivalence at [1,1] (1 mark):**

$$
\neg B_{1,1} \iff \neg (P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}) \iff \neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}
$$

So there is **no pit** in [1,2] or [2,1].

**Step 4 — Encode the second percept at [2,1] (1 mark):**

The agent perceives $\neg S_{2,1}$ and $B_{2,1}$.

**Step 5 — Apply Stench rule at [2,1] (1 mark):**

$$
\neg S_{2,1} \iff \neg (W_{1,1} \lor W_{3,1} \lor W_{2,2})
$$

Since $\neg W_{1,1}$ is a priori known (no Wumpus at start), we conclude:

$$
\neg W_{3,1} \land \neg W_{2,2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Breeze rule at [2,1] (1 mark):**

$$
B_{2,1} \iff (P_{1,1} \lor P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2})
$$

Since $\neg P_{1,1}$ is already known, the rule simplifies to:

$$
P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final KB state (0.5 marks):**

$$
\neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1} \land \neg W_{3,1} \land \neg W_{2,2} \land \neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1} \land (P_{3,1} \lor P_{2,2})
$$

The agent has narrowed the Wumpus's position to {[1,3], [1,4], [2,3], [2,4], [3,2], [3,3], [3,4], [4,1], [4,2], [4,3], [4,4]} (i.e., not in any adjacent cell to known-safe cells), and the pit to be in either [3,1] or [2,2].

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q5(a)(b)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **Knowledge Base (KB)** in a knowledge-based agent. With a neat diagram, describe the generic architecture of a knowledge-based agent showing the **TELL–ASK** interface and the **Inference Engine**.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

A **Knowledge Base (KB)** is a structured set of sentences (propositional or first-order logic) that represents the agent's beliefs about the world (3 marks).

The KB is initialized with **prior knowledge** and then continuously updated with percepts via TELL. It supports two operations: TELL (to add) and ASK (to query). The **Inference Engine** uses logical rules (such as Modus Ponens, Resolution, or Forward Chaining) to derive new sentences from the existing KB (2 marks).

**Architecture diagram description (2 marks):**

The agent's loop is: **Percept → TELL → Update KB → Inference Engine → ASK → Action Decision → Actuator → Environment**. The inference engine is the "reasoning" heart that uses the KB to answer queries. Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 (Block 4.1) for the full schematic.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the **valid moves** of the agent in the Wumpus World? Explain the conditions under which the agent decides to: (i) Grab the gold, (ii) Shoot the Wumpus, (iii) Climb out of the cave. Construct a decision matrix.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The valid moves are: **TurnLeft, TurnRight, Forward, Grab, Release, Shoot, Climb** (1 mark).

**(i) Grab condition (2 marks):**
The agent should Grab **only when** the percept at the current time step is $\text{Glitter}_t = T$. Grabbing elsewhere is a wasted action (or could waste a turn while the Wumpus kills the agent).

**(ii) Shoot condition (2 marks):**
The agent should Shoot only when the KB entails the Wumpus's position and the agent is facing the correct direction (the Wumpus lies in the straight line of the arrow's trajectory). The arrow travels until it hits a wall or the Wumpus; only one arrow is available.

**(iii) Climb condition (2 marks):**
The agent should Climb **only at cell [1,1]**. The reward is +1000 if the agent is also holding gold; otherwise, it is −1. A rational agent should never Climb without gold, as the expected return of continuing exploration (when no safe moves exist) is non-positive in expectation, but the gold bonus is so high that the agent should attempt exploration first.

**Decision matrix construction (already shown in Section 3.6, Table form).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Wumpus World Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Contrapositive (2–3 marks lost):** Many students apply the rules only in the forward direction. The **contrapositive** is what enables inference. If a cell has no Breeze, then the adjacent cells have no Pit.
> 2. **Ignoring the Initial State Axiom (1 mark lost):** Students often forget that $\neg P_{1,1}$ and $\neg W_{1,1}$ are always in the KB because the agent is alive at [1,1].
> 3. **Confusing Stench with Breeze (1 mark lost):** Stench ↔ Wumpus; Breeze ↔ Pit. Cross-mapping these is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting Out-of-Bound Omission (1 mark lost):** The adjacency list at [1,1] has only 2 valid neighbors, not 4.
> 5. **Not Showing Modus Tollens/Resolution Steps (2 marks lost):** KTU expects explicit logical inference steps (cite the rule, substitute, simplify, conclude). Hand-waving "therefore safe" loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong Action in Climb Question (1 mark lost):** Climb is only valid at [1,1], not any safe cell. The agent cannot exit from [2,1] or elsewhere.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Wumpus World:**

- **Definition (KBA):** An agent that uses a symbolic KB + logical inference to act. Functions: TELL (add), ASK (query).
- **World layout:** 4×4 grid; agent starts at [1,1]; one Wumpus, multiple pits, one gold.
- **PEAS:** Performance, Environment, Actuators (TurnLeft, TurnRight, Forward, Grab, Release, Shoot, Climb), Sensors (Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream).
- **Five Percepts:** Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream — all Boolean.
- **Core Equations (memorize!):**
  * Stench ↔ Wumpus in adjacent cell.
  * Breeze ↔ Pit in adjacent cell.
  * Glitter ↔ Gold in current cell.
  * Bump ↔ Wall collision.
  * Scream ↔ Arrow hit Wumpus.
- **Initial State Axioms:** $\neg P_{1,1}$, $\neg W_{1,1}$.
- **Inference Power:** Use the **contrapositive** — absence of percept ⇒ absence of hazard in neighbors.
- **Resolution Rule:** The standard inference engine mechanism for propositional logic.
- **Model Counting:** A cell is provably safe iff true in *every* model of the KB; provably unsafe iff false in *every* model.
- **Action Priority:** Glitter → Grab → Backtrack → Climb at [1,1] with gold.
- **Shoot Condition:** Only if KB entails Wumpus position and agent faces the right direction.
- **Exactly-One Wumpus:** Encoded as 16 disjunctions + 120 pairwise exclusions.
- **Climb Rule:** Only at [1,1]; +1000 with gold, −1 without.
- **Real-world Analogy:** Mars rovers, AUVs, search-and-rescue robots all use analogous reasoning.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Expect a 14-mark question split as (a) PEAS / KBA definition (7 marks) + (b) Inference trace through 2–3 cells (7 marks), or an action-decision matrix question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Logic in AI:**
> *Logic* is a formal, rigorous system of reasoning that uses well-defined syntax (rules for constructing expressions) and semantics (rules for assigning meaning) to represent knowledge and derive valid conclusions. In Artificial Intelligence, logic serves as the **foundational framework for Knowledge Representation and Automated Reasoning**, enabling machines to manipulate facts and infer new truths.

The two principal logical systems studied in this module are:

- **Propositional Logic (PL)** — also called *Boolean Logic* or *Sentential Logic* — which deals with whole declarative statements (propositions) that are either **True (T)** or **False (F)**.
- **First-Order Logic (FOL)** — also called *Predicate Logic* or *Predicate Calculus* — which extends PL by introducing **objects, properties (predicates), relations, and quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Logic forms the **deductive core** of knowledge-based AI. Every expert system, rule-based engine, and automated theorem prover ultimately reduces to logical inference.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Courtroom Analogy"

Think of logic as a **courtroom judge** evaluating evidence:

| Element in Courtroom | Equivalent in Logic | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Witness statements (atomic facts) | **Propositions / Atoms** (e.g., $P$, $Q$) | Basic units of truth |
| Rules of evidence / Law | **Inference Rules** (e.g., *Modus Ponens*) | Allow derivation of new truths |
| The judge | **Inference Engine** | Applies rules systematically |
| Final verdict | **Concluded sentence** | Result of reasoning |
| The case file | **Knowledge Base (KB)** | Stores all known facts |

Just as a judge does not "guess" but instead applies **strict legal rules** to undisputed facts to reach a verdict, an AI reasoning system applies **formal logical rules** to known propositions to derive **provably correct** new conclusions.

> [!TIP]
> **Why study Logic in AI?** Because without logic, an AI system is merely a "pattern matcher." With logic, it becomes a **reasoner** — capable of explaining *why* it reached a conclusion (explainability) and guaranteeing correctness within its knowledge domain.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Cheat-Sheet

- **Proposition**: A declarative statement that is unambiguously true or false.
- **Atomic Sentence**: A single, indivisible proposition (e.g., *"It is raining"*).
- **Complex Sentence**: Propositions joined by logical connectives (e.g., $P \land Q$).
- **Knowledge Base (KB)**: A set of sentences representing what the system *knows*.
- **Entailment ($KB \models \alpha$)**: KB *logically entails* $\alpha$ iff $\alpha$ is true in every model where KB is true.
- **Soundness**: An inference procedure derives only *true* conclusions.
- **Completeness**: An inference procedure can derive *all* true conclusions.
- **Satisfiable**: A sentence is satisfiable iff it is true in *at least one* model.
- **Validity (Tautology)**: A sentence that is true in *every* model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Propositional Logic — The Atomic Layer

A **proposition** is a declarative statement that evaluates to exactly one truth value: **True (1)** or **False (0)**.

**Examples:**
- $P$: "The sky is blue." (T)
- $Q$: "2 + 2 = 5." (F)
- $R$: "x > 10." ❌ *Not a proposition — value depends on x.*

### 2.1.1 The Five Logical Connectives

| Symbol | Name | Read As | Truth Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $\neg P$ | Negation (NOT) | "not P" | True iff $P$ is False |
| $P \land Q$ | Conjunction (AND) | "P and Q" | True iff *both* $P$ and $Q$ are True |
| $P \lor Q$ | Disjunction (OR) | "P or Q" | True iff *at least one* of $P, Q$ is True |
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | Implication (IMPLIES) | "if P then Q" | False only when $P=T, Q=F$ |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | Biconditional (IFF) | "P iff Q" | True iff $P$ and $Q$ have the *same* truth value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Implication:** The sentence $P \rightarrow Q$ is read as "P *implies* Q" or "if P then Q." It is **FALSE only when P is true and Q is false**. Many students incorrectly assume an implication is always true "in the abstract." This is the #1 source of marks lost in KTU valuation.

### 2.1.2 The Master Truth Table (All 16 Connectives)

Since each of 2 propositions has 2 truth values, there are $2^2 = 4$ possible models. The **canonical master truth table** is the most important KTU reference:

| $P$ | $Q$ | $\neg P$ | $P \land Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| F | F | T | F | F | **T** | T |
| F | T | T | F | T | **T** | F |
| T | F | F | F | T | **F** | F |
| T | T | F | T | T | **T** | T |

> [!NOTE]
> The **5th column** ($P \rightarrow Q$) is the only one that students memorize incorrectly. Memorize it as: "$T \rightarrow F$ is the ONLY false case; everything else is True."

### 2.1.3 Logical Equivalences (Mandatory for KTU)

Two sentences $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **logically equivalent** ($\alpha \equiv \beta$) iff they have identical truth tables. The most important equivalences:

| Law | Form | Engineering Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| Double Negation | $\neg \neg P \equiv P$ | Two NOTs cancel out |
| De Morgan's 1 | $\neg(P \land Q) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg Q$ | "NOT (both)" = "either one is false" |
| De Morgan's 2 | $\neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q$ | "NOT (either)" = "both are false" |
| Implication | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ | Removes IF-THEN in favor of OR |
| Contrapositive | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$ | "If P then Q" ↔ "If not Q then not P" |
| Distributive | $P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R)$ | Factoring logic |
| Absorption | $P \lor (P \land Q) \equiv P$ | "Either P, or P-and-something" = just P |

---

## 2.2 First-Order Logic (FOL) — The Expressive Layer

Propositional logic is too weak to express general statements like *"All humans are mortal"* without enumerating every human. **FOL** introduces five new elements:

| Element | Notation | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constants** | $A, B, SocrateS$ | $KingJohn$ | Specific objects |
| **Variables** | $x, y, z$ | $x$ | Placeholders for objects |
| **Predicates** | $P(\cdot), Q(\cdot)$ | $Mortal(x)$ | Properties / Relations |
| **Functions** | $f(\cdot)$ | $Legs(John)$ | Map objects to objects |
| **Quantifiers** | $\forall, \exists$ | $\forall x\, Mortal(x)$ | "for all" / "there exists" |

### 2.2.1 Universal vs. Existential Quantifiers

$$\forall x \, P(x) \quad \equiv \quad \text{"P holds for every object in the domain"}$$

$$\exists x \, P(x) \quad \equiv \quad \text{"There exists at least one object for which P holds"}$$

**The De Morgan Duals for Quantifiers (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**

$$\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$

$$\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** "**N**ot **A**ll = **S**ome **N**ot." Push the negation *inside* the quantifier and *flip* the quantifier ($\forall \leftrightarrow \exists$).

### 2.2.2 Translating English to FOL (Syllabus Essential)

| English Sentence | FOL Translation |
|---|---|
| "All cats are mammals." | $\forall x\, (Cat(x) \rightarrow Mammal(x))$ |
| "Some cats are black." | $\exists x\, (Cat(x) \land Black(x))$ |
| "No cat is a dog." | $\forall x\, (Cat(x) \rightarrow \neg Dog(x))$ |
| "Every student likes some book." | $\forall x\, (Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y\, (Book(y) \land Likes(x,y)))$ |
| "Brothers are siblings." | $\forall x,y\, (Brother(x,y) \rightarrow Sibling(x,y))$ |

---

## 2.3 Inference — The Reasoning Engine

### 2.3.1 Core Inference Rules (HIGH-YIELD)

| Rule | Premise(s) | Conclusion | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens** | $P, \; P \rightarrow Q$ | $Q$ | "If P, and P→Q, then Q" |
| **Modus Tollens** | $\neg Q, \; P \rightarrow Q$ | $\neg P$ | "If P→Q and not Q, then not P" |
| **Hypothetical Syllogism** | $P \rightarrow Q, \; Q \rightarrow R$ | $P \rightarrow R$ | Chain implications |
| **Disjunctive Syllogism** | $P \lor Q, \; \neg P$ | $Q$ | One of two; not first ⇒ second |
| **Resolution** | $P \lor Q, \; \neg P \lor R$ | $Q \lor R$ | Foundation of theorem proving |
| **Universal Instantiation** | $\forall x\, P(x)$ | $P(A)$ (any $A$) | Specialize a universal |
| **Existential Instantiation** | $\exists x\, P(x)$ | $P(A)$ (some fresh $A$) | Introduce a witness |

### 2.3.2 Resolution Refutation (Theorem Proving)

The **resolution algorithm** is the *only* inference rule needed to build a complete theorem prover. Procedure:

1. Convert all sentences to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** — a conjunction of disjunctions of literals.
2. **Negate** the goal sentence $\alpha$ to get $\neg\alpha$.
3. Repeatedly apply the **resolution rule** to pairs of clauses.
4. If the **empty clause $\square$** is derived → contradiction found → KB $\models \alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Resolution is **refutation-complete** for FOL (given undecidability, only refutation-completeness is achievable — *Gödel's incompleteness theorem*).

### 2.3.3 Forward vs. Backward Chaining

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal ← sub-goals) |
| **Trigger** | New fact arrives | Query is asked |
| **Algorithm** | Apply Modus Ponens until fixpoint | Recursively prove sub-goals |
| **Best for** | Monitoring, control systems | Query answering, diagnosis |
| **Completeness** | Complete for definite clauses | Complete for Datalog programs |
| **Time** | May derive irrelevant facts | Focused, may miss facts |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Horn clause** has *at most one positive literal* (e.g., $\neg P \lor \neg Q \lor R$, equivalently $P \land Q \rightarrow R$). Inference on Horn clauses is linear in KB size, making them the basis of **Prolog** and production rule systems.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the 14-mark question.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Implication elimination | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ | Removes $\rightarrow$ |
| 2 | Biconditional | $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \land (Q \rightarrow P)$ | Two implications |
| 3 | De Morgan (atoms) | $\neg(P \land Q) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg Q$ | Flip AND ↔ OR |
| 4 | De Morgan (quantifiers) | $\neg\forall x\, P \equiv \exists x\, \neg P$ | Flip $\forall \leftrightarrow \exists$ |
| 5 | Modus Ponens | $P,\; P \rightarrow Q \vdash Q$ | Most-used rule |
| 6 | Modus Tollens | $\neg Q,\; P \rightarrow Q \vdash \neg P$ | Reverse direction |
| 7 | Resolution | $P \lor Q, \; \neg P \lor R \vdash Q \lor R$ | CNF required |
| 8 | Unification | $P(A, y), \; P(x, B) \rightarrow \theta = \{x/A, y/B\}$ | MGU |
| 9 | CNF steps | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta \rightarrow \neg \alpha \lor \beta \rightarrow$ Standardize → Drop $\rightarrow$ → Move $\neg$ inward (NNF) → Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$ | 5 steps |
| 10 | # of models | For $n$ symbols → $2^n$ possible models | Quick check |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Expert Systems (MYCIN, DENDRAL):** Used propositional rules for medical diagnosis.
- **Semantic Web & RDF/OWL:** W3C standards encode knowledge in FOL-based Description Logics.
- **Database Query Languages (SQL, Datalog):** Backward-chaining engines with Horn clauses.
- **Formal Verification (hardware/software):** Model checking uses propositional and temporal logic to prove correctness of CPU designs.
- **Automated Theorem Provers (Coq, Isabelle, Z3):** Industrial tools using higher-order logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Truth Table Proof of a Logical Equivalence

**Claim:** $\neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q$ (De Morgan's Law).

**Strategy:** Build a complete truth table with all $2^2 = 4$ models.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Column setup: } & P,\; Q,\; P \lor Q,\; \neg(P \lor Q),\; \neg P,\; \neg Q,\; \neg P \land \neg Q \\
\\
\text{Model 1: } & P=F,\; Q=F \\
& P \lor Q = F \\
& \neg(P \lor Q) = T \\
& \neg P = T,\; \neg Q = T \\
& \neg P \land \neg Q = T \land T = T \\
& \text{Column 4 = Column 7 = T \quad ✓} \\
\\
\text{Model 2: } & P=F,\; Q=T \\
& P \lor Q = T \\
& \neg(P \lor Q) = F \\
& \neg P = T,\; \neg Q = F \\
& \neg P \land \neg Q = T \land F = F \\
& \text{Column 4 = Column 7 = F \quad ✓} \\
\\
\text{Model 3: } & P=T,\; Q=F \\
& P \lor Q = T \\
& \neg(P \lor Q) = F \\
& \neg P = F,\; \neg Q = T \\
& \neg P \land \neg Q = F \land T = F \\
& \text{Column 4 = Column 7 = F \quad ✓} \\
\\
\text{Model 4: } & P=T,\; Q=T \\
& P \lor Q = T \\
& \neg(P \lor Q) = F \\
& \neg P = F,\; \neg Q = F \\
& \neg P \land \neg Q = F \land F = F \\
& \text{Column 4 = Column 7 = F \quad ✓} \\
\\
\text{Conclusion: } & \text{Since columns are identical for all 4 models,} \\
& \neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Resolution Refutation Proof (END-TO-END)

**Knowledge Base (KB):**
1. $P \lor Q$ *(Either P or Q is true.)*
2. $\neg P \lor R$ *(If not P, then R.)*
3. $\neg R$ *(R is false.)*

**Goal:** Prove that KB $\models Q$.

### Step 1 — Negate the goal

$$
\text{Goal } Q \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Negated goal } \neg Q
$$

### Step 2 — Identify resolvable pairs

Resolve **Clause 3 ($\neg R$)** with **Clause 2 ($\neg P \lor R$)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolve } & \neg P \lor R \quad \text{with} \quad \neg R \\
\text{Complementary pair: } & R \text{ and } \neg R \\
\text{Resolution result: } & \neg P
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Continue resolution

Resolve the new clause $\neg P$ with Clause 1 ($P \lor Q$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolve } & P \lor Q \quad \text{with} \quad \neg P \\
\text{Complementary pair: } & P \text{ and } \neg P \\
\text{Resolution result: } & Q
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final contradiction

Resolve $Q$ with negated goal $\neg Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolve } & Q \quad \text{with} \quad \neg Q \\
\text{Result: } & \square \; \text{(empty clause)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The empty clause $\square$ is a contradiction. Since the negation of the goal leads to a contradiction, the KB *entails* the goal: **KB $\models Q$ is PROVEN.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — FOL Unification

**Problem:** Unify $P(x, y, z)$ with $P(A, B, u)$ and find the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal: Find } \theta \text{ such that } \theta[P(x,y,z)] = \theta[P(A,B,u)] \\
\\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{First arguments: } x \text{ and } A. \\
& \text{Substitute } \theta_1 = \{x/A\}. \\
& \text{Now: } P(A, y, z) \stackrel{?}{=} P(A, B, u) \\
\\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Second arguments: } y \text{ and } B. \\
& \text{Substitute } \theta_2 = \{y/B\}. \\
& \text{Now: } P(A, B, z) \stackrel{?}{=} P(A, B, u) \\
\\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Third arguments: } z \text{ and } u \text{ (both variables).} \\
& \text{Substitute } \theta_3 = \{z/u\}. \\
& \text{Now: } P(A, B, u) = P(A, B, u) \quad \text{✓} \\
\\
\therefore \quad \text{MGU } \theta & = \{x/A,\; y/B,\; z/u\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — CNF Conversion (5-Step Pipeline)

**Original:** $(\neg P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow R$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Eliminate } \rightarrow \quad & \neg(\neg(\neg P \lor Q)) \lor R \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2: Move } \neg \textbf{ inward (NNF)} \quad & (\neg \neg P \land \neg Q) \lor R \\
& \text{(because } \neg\neg S \equiv S \text{ and } \neg(\neg P \lor Q) \equiv P \land \neg Q) \\
& \text{NNF: } (P \land \neg Q) \lor R \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3: Standardize variables} \quad & \text{(no variables) — skip.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4: Skolemize} \quad & \text{(no } \exists \text{) — skip.} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5: Distribute } \lor \textbf{ over } \land \quad & (P \lor R) \land (\neg Q \lor R) \\
\\
\therefore \quad \text{CNF} = & \; (P \lor R) \land (\neg Q \lor R) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Logical Inference System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    KB[Knowledge Base KB]:::core
    FOL[FOL Sentence Pool]:::core
    INF[Inference Engine]:::core
    PM[Proof Manager]:::aux
    CHAIN[Chaining Controller]:::aux
    FC[Forward Chainer]:::aux
    BC[Backward Chainer]:::aux
    RES[Resolution Prover]:::aux
    OUT[Derived Conclusions]:::out
    QU[User Query]:::inp

    KB --> FOL
    FOL --> INF
    QU --> PM
    PM --> CHAIN
    CHAIN --> FC
    CHAIN --> BC
    CHAIN --> RES
    INF --> PM
    FC --> OUT
    BC --> OUT
    RES --> OUT

    classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef aux fill:#f0ad4e,stroke:#ad6f1f,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#5cb85c,stroke:#2d5a2d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inp fill:#999999,stroke:#404040,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A query enters the **Proof Manager**, which dispatches it to one of three inference strategies. The **Inference Engine** consults the FOL-converted **Knowledge Base** to derive new sentences, which the chaining modules iteratively add to the conclusion set.

---

## 4.2 Forward Chaining vs Backward Chaining — Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD [Forward Chaining - Data Driven]
        direction LR
        F1[Facts: P, Q]:::inp --> F2[Match: P and P->R]:::aux
        F2 --> F3[Derive: R]:::core
        F3 --> F4[Match: R and R->S]:::aux
        F4 --> F5[Derive: S]:::core
        F5 --> F6{Goal S reached?}:::gate
    end

    subgraph BACKWARD [Backward Chaining - Goal Driven]
        direction LR
        B1[Goal: ?S]:::inp --> B2[Need: R]:::aux
```
