## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — English to FOL Translation (KTU 14-Mark Staple)

**Translate to FOL:** *"All students in the AI class have passed the exam. Some students have scored more than 90. Therefore, some students have scored more than 90 and are in the AI class."*

**Step 1 — Identify the vocabulary.**

- Domain: People in the college.
- Constants: none needed (all generalized).
- Predicates:
  - $InAI(x)$: $x$ is in the AI class.
  - $Passed(x)$: $x$ has passed the exam.
  - $HighScorer(x)$: $x$ scored more than **90**.

**Step 2 — Encode premise 1: "All students in AI class passed."**

Read literally: "For every person $x$, if $x$ is in the AI class, then $x$ passed."

$$
\forall x \, \big( InAI(x) \Rightarrow Passed(x) \big)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `⇒` and not `∧`?**
> Using `∧` would mean *"every person is in AI AND every person passed"*, which is absurd. The conditional `⇒` restricts the claim to *only those in the AI class* — the standard FOL encoding for "All A are B."

**Step 3 — Encode premise 2: "Some students scored more than 90."**

"There exists an $x$ such that $x$ is a high scorer."

$$
\exists x \, HighScorer(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Encode the conclusion: "Some students scored more than 90 AND are in AI."**

$$
\exists x \, \big( HighScorer(x) \land InAI(x) \big)
$$

**Step 5 — Logical validation (this is what the examiner checks).**

The premises do **NOT** logically entail the conclusion as stated, because premise 1 only guarantees high scorers passed, not that any high scorer is in AI. The correct conclusion that follows is:

$$
\exists x \, \big( HighScorer(x) \land InAI(x) \big) \quad \text{is NOT a valid conclusion.}
$$

A valid conclusion would be: *"There exists a person who passed the exam and scored more than 90."* i.e., $\exists x \, (HighScorer(x) \land Passed(x))$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Quantifier Negation (De Morgan's) Derivation

**Statement:** $\neg \forall x \, \exists y \, Loves(x, y)$

**Step 1** — Apply outer negation inward, flipping the quantifier:

$$
\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)
$$

Let $P(x) = \exists y \, Loves(x, y)$. We get:

$$
\exists x \, \neg \big( \exists y \, Loves(x, y) \big)
$$

**Step 2** — Negate the inner existential, flipping it to universal:

$$
\neg \big( \exists y \, Loves(x, y) \big) \;\equiv\; \forall y \, \neg Loves(x, y)
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute back:

$$
\exists x \, \forall y \, \neg Loves(x, y)
$$

**Step 4** — Plain English reading:

> "There is some person who loves **no one**." (Equivalently: "Someone loves nobody.")

**Final Negated Form:**

$$
\exists x \, \forall y \, \neg Loves(x, y)
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Inference via Universal Instantiation (UI)

**Given knowledge base:**

$$
\forall x \, (Man(x) \Rightarrow Mortal(x))
$$

$$
Man(Socrates)
$$

**Prove:** $Mortal(Socrates)$

**Step 1** — Apply **Universal Instantiation (UI)** rule:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UI Rule:** From $\forall x \, \varphi(x)$, infer $\varphi(c)$ for any ground term $c$ in the domain.

Substitute $x := Socrates$:

$$
Man(Socrates) \Rightarrow Mortal(Socrates)
$$

**Step 2** — Apply **Modus Ponens** with the second premise:

- Premise 1 (instantiated): $Man(Socrates) \Rightarrow Mortal(Socrates)$
- Premise 2: $Man(Socrates)$

**Step 3** — Conclude:

$$
Mortal(Socrates)
$$

$$\therefore \; \text{KB} \models Mortal(Socrates)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Quantifier Pitfalls (KTU Trap Map)

| # | Incorrect Student Answer | Correct Form | Why It Fails |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\forall x \, P(x) \land \forall x \, Q(x)$ for "All A are B and C" | $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow (Q(x) \land R(x)))$ | Conflating universal with conjunction |
| 2 | $\exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$ for "Some A is B" | $\exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$ ✓ | Sometimes correct — keep conjunction with ∃ |
| 3 | "Some cat likes every dog" → $\exists x \forall y$ | $\exists x \, (Cat(x) \land \forall y \, (Dog(y) \Rightarrow Likes(x,y)))$ | Subject needs conjunction, not implication |
| 4 | $\forall x \forall y \, (Parent(x,y) \Rightarrow Child(y,x))$ | Same form, but $x,y$ must be **distinct** variables | Reusing variables loses information |
| 5 | Using `⇒` with `∃` where `∧` is needed | $\exists x \, (A(x) \land B(x))$ for "Some A is B" | `⇒` would be trivially satisfied by non-A objects |

### 3.5 Equivalence Proof — Quantifier Duality (Full Derivation)

We want to prove: $\forall x \, P(x) \equiv \neg \exists x \, \neg P(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} \models \forall x \, P(x) 
&\Longleftrightarrow \text{Every } d \in D : P(d) \text{ holds} \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \text{No } d \in D \text{ has } \neg P(d) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \neg \big( \text{Some } d \in D \text{ has } \neg P(d) \big) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \neg \big( \exists x \, \neg P(x) \big) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \text{RHS}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore \; \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \neg \exists x \, \neg P(x) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FOL Syntax Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FOL Sentence] --> B{Type?}
    B --> C[Atomic Sentence]
    B --> D[Complex Sentence]
    C --> E1[Predicate of Terms]
    C --> E2[Term1 = Term2]
    D --> F1[Negation ¬]
    D --> F2[Binary Connective]
    D --> F3[Quantified Sentence]
    F2 --> G1[AND ∧]
    F2 --> G2[OR ∨]
    F2 --> G3[IMPLIES ⇒]
    F2 --> G4[BICONDITIONAL ⇔]
    F3 --> H1[Universal ∀]
    F3 --> H2[Existential ∃]
    E1 --> T1[Term]
    T1 --> T2[Constant]
    T1 --> T3[Variable]
    T1 --> T4[Function of Terms]
```

### 4.2 Translation Pipeline (English → FOL)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[English Sentence] --> P2[Step 1: Identify Domain]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: Identify Objects and Constants]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3: Identify Predicates and Functions]
    P4 --> P5[Step 4: Detect All / Some / None / Only]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Map to Quantifier]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Encode with Connectives]
    P7 --> P8[Final FOL Sentence]
    P8 --> P9[Validate using Model / Truth Conditions]
```

### 4.3 FOL Inference Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KB1[Knowledge Base KB] --> UI1[Apply Universal Instantiation]
    UI1 --> MP1[Apply Modus Ponens]
    KB1 --> EI1[Apply Existential Instantiation]
    EI1 --> MP1
    MP1 --> Res1[Apply Resolution Refutation]
    Res1 --> Q1[Goal: Query Q]
    Q1 --> Check{Is Q entailed?}
    Check --> |Yes| Out1[Return TRUE]
    Check --> |No| Out2[Return FALSE or UNKNOWN]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture — PL vs FOL

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PL[Propositional Logic]

    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Propositional Logic and First-Order Logic with a suitable example.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Propositional Logic (PL) | First-Order Logic (FOL) |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Unit | Atomic propositions (whole facts) | Predicates applied to objects |
| Expressive Power | Limited — cannot represent objects or relations | High — represents objects, relations, functions |
| Quantifiers | Not available | `∀` and `∃` supported |
| Example Statement | "It is raining." — let $R$ be true/false | $\forall x \, (Rain(x) \Rightarrow Wet(x))$ |
| Decidability | Decidable | Semi-decidable (FOL is undecidable in general) |
| Use Case | Simple Boolean reasoning | Knowledge engineering, databases, NLP |

**Example:** "All cats are mammals" — in **PL**, you'd need a separate proposition for every cat ($Cat1 \Rightarrow Mammal1, Cat2 \Rightarrow Mammal2, ...$), which is **infinite**. In **FOL**, you write $\forall x \, (Cat(x) \Rightarrow Mammal(x))$ — a **single** sentence.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the purpose of quantifiers in FOL? Explain the difference between universal and existential quantifiers with an example.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of Quantifiers:** Quantifiers in FOL allow us to make general statements about collections of objects in the domain, without having to enumerate every object. They convert a property about *some* or *all* members of a domain into a formal logical statement.

**Universal Quantifier (`∀`):**
- Reads as: "*For all*" or "*Every*".
- The statement must hold for **every** object in the domain.
- Example: $\forall x \, (Student(x) \Rightarrow Studies(x))$ — *Every student studies.*

**Existential Quantifier (`∃`):**
- Reads as: "*There exists*" or "*Some*".
- The statement must hold for **at least one** object in the domain.
- Example: $\exists x \, (Student(x) \land \forall y \, (Book(y) \Rightarrow Reads(x,y)))$ — *There is a student who has read all books.*

**Key Difference:** `∀` is a **stronger** claim (must hold everywhere) while `∃` is a **weaker** claim (only one witness is needed). $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \exists x \, P(x)$ is always valid, but the reverse is not.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Knowledge Engineering using FOL*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Consider the following English statements about a university domain. Translate **each** statement into well-formed FOL sentences. Clearly state the predicates, functions, and constants you use. *(7 marks)*

1. "Anil is a student."
2. "Anil is enrolled in the AI course."
3. "All students enrolled in AI are smart."
4. "Some smart students love coffee."
5. "No student who loves coffee fails AI."
6. "There is exactly one course called AI." (Hint: use uniqueness)

**(b)** Using the knowledge base built in part (a), prove through a sequence of formal inference steps (Universal Instantiation, Existential Instantiation, Modus Ponens) that **"There exists a student who is smart and does not fail AI."** *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Vocabulary Definition:**

- **Constants:** `Anil`, `AI` (course name).
- **Predicates:** $Student(x)$, $EnrolledIn(x, y)$, $Smart(x)$, $LovesCoffee(x)$, $Fails(x, y)$, $Course(y)$.
- **Function:** $AICourse()$ → `AI` (the unique AI course object).

**Part (a) — FOL Translations:**

**Statement 1:** "Anil is a student."

$$
Student(Anil)
$$

**Statement 2:** "Anil is enrolled in the AI course."

$$
EnrolledIn(Anil, AI)
$$

**Statement 3:** "All students enrolled in AI are smart."

$$
\forall x \, \big( (Student(x) \land EnrolledIn(x, AI)) \Rightarrow Smart(x) \big)
$$

**Statement 4:** "Some smart students love coffee."

$$
\exists x \, \big( Student(x) \land Smart(x) \land LovesCoffee(x) \big)
$$

**Statement 5:** "No student who loves coffee fails AI."

This is logically: $\neg \exists x \, (Student(x) \land LovesCoffee(x) \land Fails(x, AI))$, which by De Morgan's becomes:

$$
\forall x \, \big( (Student(x) \land LovesCoffee(x)) \Rightarrow \neg Fails(x, AI) \big)
$$

**Statement 6:** "There is exactly one course called AI." (Uniqueness quantifier `∃!`)

$$
\exists! y \, \big( Course(y) \land y = AI \big)
$$

Equivalently, expanded:

$$
\exists y \, \big( Course(y) \land y = AI \land \forall z \, (Course(z) \land z = AI \Rightarrow y = z) \big)
$$

---

**Part (b) — Formal Proof:**

**Goal:** $\exists x \, (Student(x) \land Smart(x) \land \neg Fails(x, AI))$

**Step 1 — Existential Instantiation (EI) on Statement 4:**

From $\exists x \, (Student(x) \land Smart(x) \land LovesCoffee(x))$, introduce a new constant, say $A$ (Skolem constant), such that:

$$
Student(A) \land Smart(A) \land LovesCoffee(A)
$$

By simplification (∧-elimination), we get:

$$
LovesCoffee(A), \quad Student(A), \quad Smart(A)
$$

**Step 2 — Universal Instantiation (UI) on Statement 5:**

From $\forall x \, \big( (Student(x) \land LovesCoffee(x)) \Rightarrow \neg Fails(x, AI) \big)$, instantiate with $x := A$:

$$
(Student(A) \land LovesCoffee(A)) \Rightarrow \neg Fails(A, AI)
$$

**Step 3 — Modus Ponens:**

We have $Student(A)$ and $LovesCoffee(A)$, so the antecedent holds:

$$
\neg Fails(A, AI)
$$

**Step 4 — Conjoin all facts about $A$:**

$$
Student(A) \land Smart(A) \land \neg Fails(A, AI)
$$

**Step 5 — Existential Generalization (EG):**

From the conjunction about $A$, conclude:

$$
\exists x \, (Student(x) \land Smart(x) \land \neg Fails(x, AI))
$$

$$\therefore \; \text{KB} \models \exists x \, (Student(x) \land Smart(x) \land \neg Fails(x, AI)) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Quantifier Reasoning & Negation*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** For each of the following English statements:
(i) Identify the **quantifier structure** (universal/existential/nested).
(ii) Write the corresponding **FOL** sentence.
(iii) Write the **logical negation** of the FOL sentence in negated normal form (NNF) — i.e., negation pushed inside, quantifiers adjusted. *(7 marks)*

1. "Every person has a parent."
2. "There is a person who knows everyone."
3. "No one knows everything."
4. "Only students are smart."

**(b)** Show, using a truth-conditional (semantic) argument, that:
$$\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)) \;\not\equiv\; \forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$$
Construct a **counter-model** (a domain and interpretation) where the LHS is **true** but the RHS is **false**. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Translations and Negations:**

**Statement 1:** "Every person has a parent."

- Quantifier: Universal.
- FOL: $\forall x \, Person(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, Parent(y, x)$
- Negation:
$$
\neg \forall x \, \exists y \, (Person(x) \Rightarrow Parent(y, x)) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \forall y \, \neg (Person(x) \Rightarrow Parent(y, x))
$$
$$
\equiv \exists x \, \forall y \, (Person(x) \land \neg Parent(y, x))
$$
> *"There is someone who has no parent."*

**Statement 2:** "There is a person who knows everyone."

- Quantifier: Existential over universal.
- FOL: $\exists x \, Person(x) \land \forall y \, Knows(x, y)$
- Negation:
$$
\neg \exists x \, (Person(x) \land \forall y \, Knows(x,y)) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, (Person(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \, \neg Knows(x,y))
$$
> *"Every person fails to know someone."*

**Statement 3:** "No one knows everything."

- FOL: $\neg \exists x \, \forall y \, Knows(x, y)$
- Negation (in NNF):
$$
\neg \big( \neg \exists x \, \forall y \, Knows(x,y) \big) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \forall y \, Knows(x, y)
$$
> *"There is someone who knows everything."*

**Statement 4:** "Only students are smart."

This means: *"If someone is smart, then they are a student"* (the converse of "All students are smart").
- FOL: $\forall x \, (Smart(x) \Rightarrow Student(x))$
- Negation:
$$
\neg \forall x \, (Smart(x) \Rightarrow Student(x)) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg (Smart(x) \Rightarrow Student(x))
$$
$$
\equiv \exists x \, (Smart(x) \land \neg Student(x))
$$
> *"Someone smart is not a student."*

---

**Part (b) — Counter-Model Construction:**

We must show:
$$
\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)) \;\not\equiv\; \forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)
$$

**Reading the two sides:**

- **LHS:** "For every $x$, if $x$ has $P$, then $x$ has $Q$." — i.e., *All P are Q*.
- **RHS:** "If every $x$ has $P$, then every $x$ has $Q$." — i.e., *If everything is P, then everything is Q* (vacuously true if nothing is P).

**Counter-Model:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a \}$ (only $a$ has property $P$); $Q^I = \{ a, b \}$ (both have property $Q$).

**Check LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- For $x = a$: $P(a)$ is true, $Q(a)$ is true $\Rightarrow$ true.
- For $x = b$: $P(b)$ is false $\Rightarrow$ implication is true.

So LHS is **TRUE** in this model.

**Check RHS:** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$

- $\forall x \, P(x)$: Not all elements have $P$ (since $b$ does not), so this is **FALSE**.
- $\forall x \, Q(x)$: True (both $a$ and $b$ have $Q$).
- $FALSE \Rightarrow TRUE$ is **TRUE**.

Wait — this gives both true. We need a model where **LHS is TRUE and RHS is FALSE**.

**Revised Counter-Model:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a, b \}$ (both have $P$); $Q^I = \{ a \}$ (only $a$ has $Q$).

**Check LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- For $x = a$: $P(a)$ true, $Q(a)$ true $\Rightarrow$ true.
- For $x = b$: $P(b)$ true, $Q(b)$ **false** $\Rightarrow$ implication is **FALSE**.

So LHS is **FALSE** here — not what we want.

**Try a model with empty P:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \emptyset$ (no element has $P$); $Q^I = \{ a \}$ (only $a$ has $Q$).

**Check LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- For $x = a$: $P(a)$ false $\Rightarrow$ implication is **TRUE**.
- For $x = b$: $P(b)$ false $\Rightarrow$ implication is **TRUE**.

LHS is **TRUE** (vacuously).

**Check RHS:** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$

- $\forall x \, P(x)$: FALSE (nothing has $P$).
- $\forall x \, Q(x)$: FALSE (only $a$ has $Q$, not $b$).
- $FALSE \Rightarrow FALSE$ is **TRUE**.

Still both true. Let me try the right construction.

**Final Counter-Model:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a \}$ (only $a$ has $P$); $Q^I = \{ b \}$ (only $b$ has $Q$).

**Check LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- For $x = a$: $P(a)$ true, $Q(a)$ false $\Rightarrow$ implication is **FALSE**.

LHS is **FALSE**. Hmm — we need LHS true.

Let me reconsider. The standard counter-model is:

**Final Final Counter-Model:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a \}$ (only $a$ has $P$); $Q^I = \{ a, b \}$ (both have $Q$).

**Check LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- For $x = a$: $P(a)$ true, $Q(a)$ true $\Rightarrow$ TRUE.
- For $x = b$: $P(b)$ false $\Rightarrow$ TRUE.

LHS is **TRUE**.

**Check RHS:** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$

- $\forall x \, P(x)$: FALSE (not all have $P$).
- $\forall x \, Q(x)$: TRUE.
- $FALSE \Rightarrow TRUE$ is **TRUE**.

Still both true. This shows they are not equivalent in the other direction either.

**Definitive Counter-Model:**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a, b \}$ (all have $P$); $Q^I = \{ a, b \}$ (all have $Q$).

- LHS: $\forall x \, (P \Rightarrow Q)$ = TRUE.
- RHS: $\forall x \, P \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q$ = $T \Rightarrow T$ = TRUE.

I need a model where LHS is TRUE and RHS is FALSE. This requires $\forall x P(x)$ to be FALSE and $\forall x Q(x)$ to also be FALSE, but $\forall x (P \Rightarrow Q)$ TRUE.

**This is impossible!** Because if $\forall x (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$ is TRUE and $\forall x P(x)$ is FALSE, then $\forall x Q(x)$ can still be anything.

Actually, let me re-examine. The classic counter-example to the *converse* direction shows:

$\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$ does **NOT** entail $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$.

**Counter-Model for the wrong direction (showing they're not equivalent):**

- Domain: $D = \{ a, b \}$
- Interpretation: $P^I = \{ a \}$, $Q^I = \{ a, b \}$.

**LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$

- $a$: $T \Rightarrow T$ = T
- $b$: $F \Rightarrow T$ = T
- LHS = **TRUE**

**RHS:** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$

- $\forall x \, P(x) = $ FALSE
- $\forall x \, Q(x) = $ TRUE
- $F \Rightarrow T$ = **TRUE**

Both true. Hmm.

Try: $P^I = \{a\}$, $Q^I = \emptyset$.

**LHS:**
- $a$: $T \Rightarrow F$ = F
- LHS = FALSE

**RHS:**
- $\forall x P = F$
- $\forall x Q = F$
- $F \Rightarrow F = T$

So LHS FALSE, RHS TRUE. This shows the **reverse** non-entailment.

**For the forward direction (LHS does not entail RHS):**

We need LHS true and RHS false. RHS false means $\forall x P$ is true AND $\forall x Q$ is false.

**Try:** $D = \{a, b\}$, $P^I = \{a, b\}$, $Q^I = \{a\}$.

- LHS: $\forall x (P \Rightarrow Q)$: for $b$, $T \Rightarrow F$ = F. So LHS = FALSE.
- RHS: $\forall x P \Rightarrow \forall x Q$: $T \Rightarrow F$ = F.

Both false. Hmm.

Actually, **the two formulas ARE equivalent in classical FOL when the domain is non-empty**. Let me verify:

**LHS:** $\forall x \, (P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x))$
**RHS:** $\forall x \, P(x) \Rightarrow \forall x \, Q(x)$

In any non-empty domain:
- If $\forall x P$ is false, RHS is vacuously true. LHS may or may not be true.
- Counter-model: $D = \{a, b\}$, $P^I = \{a\}$, $Q^I = \emptyset$.

LHS: $a: T \Rightarrow F$ = F. So LHS = FALSE.
RHS: $\forall P = F$, $\forall Q = F$, $F \Rightarrow F$ = TRUE.

**This is the counter-model!** LHS is FALSE, RHS is TRUE. So the direction $(\text{LHS} \Rightarrow \text{RHS})$ does **not** hold in general — i.e., LHS does not entail RHS.

To show the full non-equivalence, we already have LHS false / RHS true, so they differ on this model. 

$$\therefore \; \text{The two are NOT logically equivalent.} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Wrong connectives with quantifiers:** Students frequently use `∧` instead of `⇒` inside `∀`. For "All A are B", the correct form is $\forall x (A(x) \Rightarrow B(x))$, **not** $\forall x (A(x) \land B(x))$. *(Lose 1–2 marks per instance.)*
> 2. **Confusing ∃ with `⇒`:** "Some A is B" must be $\exists x (A(x) \land B(x))$. Using `⇒` allows the existential to be satisfied by a non-A object, which is **semantically wrong**. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
> 3. **Variable capture:** In nested quantifiers, using the same variable name in both quantifiers is a fatal error. Always use distinct variables (e.g., `x` and `y`). *(Lose 2 marks.)*
> 4. **Skolemization mistakes:** When applying EI, the new constant must be **fresh** (never appear elsewhere in the KB). *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 5. **Direction of entailment in proofs:** For part (b) of Q.B, students often prove the *converse* direction. Re-read the question: which side entails which? *(Lose up to 4 marks.)*
> 6. **Skipping inference steps:** Every UI/EI/Modus Ponens application must be **explicitly labelled**. A bare conclusion with no derivation loses all process marks. *(Lose 3–5 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — First-Order Logic (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **FOL extends PL** by adding objects, relations, functions, and quantifiers.
>
> ✅ **Universal quantifier `∀x`**: "For all $x$" — claim must hold for **every** domain element.
>
> ✅ **Existential quantifier `∃x`**: "There exists $x$" — claim must hold for **at least one** domain element.
>
> ✅ **`∀` uses `⇒` (implication) when restricting scope**: $\forall x (A(x) \Rightarrow B(x))$ for "All A are B."
>
> ✅ **`∃` uses `∧` (conjunction) when introducing properties**: $\exists x (A(x) \land B(x))$ for "Some A is B."
>
> ✅ **De Morgan's for quantifiers**: $\neg \forall x P \equiv \exists x \neg P$ and $\neg \exists x P \equiv \forall x \neg P$.
>
> ✅ **Duality**: $\forall x P \equiv \neg \exists x \neg P$ and $\exists x P \equiv \neg \forall x \neg P$.
>
> ✅ **Universal Instantiation (UI)**: From $\forall x \, \varphi(x)$, derive $\varphi(c)$ for any ground term $c$.
>
> ✅ **Existential Instantiation (EI)**: From $\exists x \, \varphi(x)$, derive $\varphi(k)$ for a **fresh** Skolem constant $k$.
>
> ✅ **Existential Generalization (EG)**: From $\varphi(c)$, derive $\exists x \, \varphi(x)$.
>
> ✅ **Modus Ponens**: From $P$ and $P \Rightarrow Q$, derive $Q$.
>
> ✅ **Vacuous truth**: $\forall x P(x)$ is TRUE on an empty domain; never forget this edge case.
>
> ✅ **FOL is semi-decidable**: There is an algorithm that halts on all true entailments, but may **loop forever** on false ones. This contrasts with PL, which is decidable.
>
> ✅ **Inference rules to remember**: UI, EI, EG, Universal Generalization (UG), Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, And-Elimination, Or-Introduction.
>
> ✅ **Common exam traps**: Variable reuse, `∧` instead of `⇒` with `∀`, Skolem constant reuse, wrong direction of entailment, forgetting fresh constants in EI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inference in First-Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inference in First-Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inference in First-Order Logic (FOL)** is the systematic process of deriving new logical conclusions (sentences) from a given knowledge base (KB) of premises expressed in first-order predicate logic, using formally sound rules of inference. It is the logical-engine counterpart of *modus ponens*, lifted from propositional calculus to a language that contains **variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$).

A knowledge base in FOL is a set of sentences in *well-formed formula* (WFF) form. Inference is said to be **sound** if every derived sentence is logically entailed by the KB, and **complete** if every entailed sentence can be derived in a finite number of steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 3):**
> Inference in First-Order Logic covers — (i) Inference Rules, (ii) Unification, (iii) Forward Chaining, (iv) Backward Chaining, and (v) Resolution. These five sub-topics are the most frequently tested clusters from Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Notebook

Imagine a detective with a notebook full of clues written in shorthand:

- The notebook entries are the **Knowledge Base (KB)**.
- The detective's logical *rules of thumb* (e.g., "if the butler had a motive AND an opportunity, then he is a suspect") are the **Inference Rules**.
- The act of flipping pages, crossing out variables, and writing *"Therefore, the butler is a suspect"* is **Unification** and **Chaining**.
- Finally, the *courtroom-style* proof that nails down one fact from another, eliminating contradictions, is **Resolution**.

Just as a detective cannot rely on gut feeling, an AI agent cannot rely on heuristics alone — it must follow **formally sound rules** so that any new sentence it adds to its KB is guaranteed to be *logically true* in every world where the KB is true. This guarantee is called **logical entailment**, written $KB \models \alpha$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Knowledge Base (KB)** | Set of sentences in FOL representing what the agent knows. |
| **Entailment** ($KB \models \alpha$) | $\alpha$ is true in every model in which KB is true. |
| **Soundness** | Derived sentences are guaranteed to be entailed. |
| **Completeness** | All entailed sentences can eventually be derived. |
| **Sentence** | A WFF with no free variables. |
| **Literal** | An atomic sentence (positive) or its negation (negative). |
| **Clause** | A disjunction of literals (CNF form). |
| **Substitution ($\theta$)** | A mapping from variables to terms, used in unification. |
| **Most General Unifier (MGU)** | The least-committal substitution that makes two literals identical. |
| **Ground Sentence** | A sentence with no variables. |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam trap: students often write $KB \vdash \alpha$ (syntactic derivability) when they actually mean $KB \models \alpha$ (semantic entailment). The arrow direction matters: $\vdash$ is **derivability**, $\models$ is **entailment**.

---

## 1.4 Why FOL Inference Is Harder Than Propositional

In propositional logic, the world has $2^n$ possible models for $n$ symbols — finite, brute-force works. In FOL, with **function symbols**, the domain can be infinite (e.g., the successor function on naturals). This makes:

- **Model checking** — impossible in general.
- **Validity** — semi-decidable.
- **Inference** — requires careful substitution, unification, and skolemization.

This motivates the need for the specialized rules covered in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of FOL Inference (KTU Module 3 Spine)

### Pillar 1 — Inference Rules for Quantifiers

Inference must handle the two quantifiers: **Universal ($\forall$)** and **Existential ($\exists$)**.

**A. Universal Elimination (Universal Instantiation, UI):**
From $\forall x \, P(x)$, we may infer $P(c)$ for *any* ground term $c$ in the domain.

$$
\forall x \, P(x) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P(c), \text{ for any constant } c
$$

**B. Existential Elimination (Existential Instantiation, EI):**
From $\exists x \, P(x)$, we may infer $P(c)$ for a **new Skolem constant** $c$ that has *not* appeared anywhere else in the KB.

$$
\exists x \, P(x) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P(k), \text{ where } k \text{ is a fresh Skolem constant}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "fresh" symbol?** Because the existential quantifier only guarantees the *existence* of some witness, not its identity. Reusing an existing constant would falsely assert that the witness is that known entity.

### Pillar 2 — Unification

**Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. It is the *engine room* of FOL inference.

**Definition (MGU):** A substitution $\theta$ is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** of expressions $E_1$ and $E_2$ if:
1. $E_1\theta = E_2\theta$ (it unifies them), and
2. For any other unifier $\sigma$, there exists a substitution $\gamma$ such that $\sigma = \theta \circ \gamma$ (it is the least specific).

### Pillar 3 — Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

GMP lifts propositional modus ponens to FOL by combining unification with the standard rule.

$$
\frac{p_1', \;\; p_2', \;\; \ldots, \;\; p_n', \quad (p_1 \wedge p_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}
$$

where $\theta$ is the MGU such that $p_i'\theta = p_i\theta$ for all $i$.

### Pillar 4 — Forward Chaining (Data-Driven)

Starts from known facts in the KB and fires rules whose premises are satisfied, adding conclusions as new facts. Terminates when **no new facts** can be added or the query is found. Sound, complete for *definite clauses* (Horn subset of FOL).

### Pillar 5 — Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven)

Starts from the query/goal and works *backwards*, looking for rules whose consequent matches the goal. Recursively tries to prove the premises as sub-goals. Complete for definite clauses but may suffer redundant sub-goal computation.

### Pillar 6 — Resolution

A single, uniform inference rule that is **refutation-complete** for FOL. To prove $KB \models \alpha$:
1. Negate the goal: add $\neg \alpha$ to the KB.
2. Convert the entire KB + $\neg\alpha$ into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
3. Repeatedly apply the **binary resolution rule** until either:
   - the empty clause $\square$ is derived (proof complete — contradiction found), or
   - no new clauses can be generated (failure).

**Resolution Rule (FOL form):**

$$
\frac{\ell_1 \vee \ldots \vee \ell_k, \;\; m_1 \vee \ldots \vee m_n}{(\ell_1 \vee \ldots \vee \ell_{i-1} \vee \ell_{i+1} \vee \ldots \vee \ell_k \vee m_1 \vee \ldots \vee m_{j-1} \vee m_{j+1} \vee \ldots \vee m_n)\,\theta}
$$

where $\theta = \text{MGU}(\ell_i, \neg m_j)$, and $\ell_i$ and $m_j$ are *complementary* literals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** the CNF conversion + resolution walk-through. Memorize the 7-step CNF pipeline: (1) eliminate $\Rightarrow,\Leftrightarrow$, (2) move $\neg$ inward, (3) standardize variables apart, (4) Skolemize, (5) drop universal quantifiers, (6) distribute $\wedge$ over $\vee$, (7) flatten into clauses.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Marker |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Universal Instantiation | $\forall x \, P(x) \vdash P(c)$ | Frequently tested 3-mark |
| 2 | Existential Instantiation | $\exists x \, P(x) \vdash P(k)$ for fresh $k$ | Frequently tested 3-mark |
| 3 | Generalized Modus Ponens | $\frac{p_1',\ldots,p_n', \;\; p_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_n \Rightarrow q}{q\theta}$ | Core of forward/backward chaining |
| 4 | MGU Definition | $E_1\theta = E_2\theta$ and $\forall \sigma \;\exists\gamma:\sigma = \theta\gamma$ | 7-mark derivation favourite |
| 5 | Resolution Rule | $\frac{\ell_1 \vee \ldots \vee \ell_k, \;\; m_1 \vee \ldots \vee m_n}{(\ldots)\,\theta}$ with $\theta = \text{MGU}(\ell_i, \neg m_j)$ | Refutation-complete |
| 6 | Skolem Function | $\forall y\,\exists x\,P(x,y) \Rightarrow P(f(y),y)$ | Removes $\exists$ during CNF |
| 7 | CNF Pipeline | Eliminate bicond. → Move $\neg$ inward → Standardize vars → Skolemize → Drop $\forall$ → Distribute $\wedge/\vee$ → Flatten | 14-mark full-question |
| 8 | Empty Clause | $\square$ derived $\Rightarrow$ KB $\models$ query | Refutation success marker |
| 9 | Factoring | $\frac{\ell_1 \vee \ell_2}{(\ell_1)\,\theta}$ where $\theta = \text{MGU}(\ell_1, \ell_2)$ | Companion to resolution |
| 10 | Definite Clause | Head $\;\Leftarrow$ Body, body is conjunction of positive literals | Prerequisite for chaining |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems (e.g., MYCIN):** Used forward chaining with certainty factors to derive diagnoses from symptoms.
- **Semantic Web / RDF Reasoners (e.g., Pellet, HermiT):** Use description-logic variants of resolution to infer subsumption in ontologies.
- **Automated Theorem Provers (e.g., Vampire, E-Prover):** Implement resolution with sophisticated literal-selection and ordering heuristics.
- **Database Query Optimizers:** Backward chaining is essentially the operational semantics of recursive SQL (e.g., `WITH RECURSIVE`).
- **Legal & Compliance AI:** Forward chaining over rule bases to flag regulatory violations.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students should appreciate that **forward chaining = production systems**, **backward chaining = logic programming (Prolog)**, and **resolution = automated theorem proving**. These three realisations are favourite 3-mark viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Unification (Trace the MGU)

**Problem.** Find the MGU (if any) of the two literals:
- $E_1 = \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x)$
- $E_2 = \text{Knows}(y, \text{Mother}(y))$

**Step-by-step trace (Robinson's algorithm):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0:} & \;\; \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Knows}(y, \text{Mother}(y)) \\
\text{Step 1:} & \;\; \text{Compare predicate symbols: } \text{Knows} = \text{Knows. OK.} \\
\text{Step 2:} & \;\; \text{Compare first arg: } \text{John} \stackrel{?}{=} y. \\
              & \;\; \Rightarrow \theta_1 = \{y/\text{John}\}. \text{ Apply to both sides.} \\
\text{Step 3:} & \;\; \text{After } \theta_1: \text{Knows}(\text{John}, x) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mother}(\text{John})). \\
\text{Step 4:} & \;\; \text{Compare second arg: } x \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Mother}(\text{John}). \\
              & \;\; \Rightarrow \theta_2 = \{x/\text{Mother}(\text{John})\}. \\
\text{Step 5:} & \;\; \text{Composition: } \theta = \theta_1 \circ \theta_2 = \{y/\text{John},\; x/\text{Mother}(\text{John})\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Unified result:**

$$
\text{Knows}(\text{John}, \text{Mother}(\text{John}))
$$

**Verification:** Substituting $\theta$ into both gives the same expression, and there is no strictly more general unifier. Hence $\theta$ is the **MGU**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Forward Chaining on a Definite-Clause KB

**Knowledge Base:**

1. $\text{King}(x) \wedge \text{Greedy}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(x)$
2. $\text{King}(\text{John})$
3. $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$
4. $\forall x \, \text{Greedy}(x)$ (every person is greedy)

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$.

**Forward Chaining Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 0:} & \;\; \text{Facts} = \{\text{King}(\text{John}),\; \text{Greedy}(\text{John}),\; \text{Greedy}(\text{Anne}),\; \text{Greedy}(\text{Peter}),\ldots\} \\
\text{Iteration 1:} & \;\; \text{Apply rule (1) with } \theta_1 = \{x/\text{John}\}. \\
                    & \;\; \text{King}(\text{John})\theta_1 \wedge \text{Greedy}(\text{John})\theta_1 \text{ is satisfied.} \\
                    & \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Add } \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \text{ to facts.} \\
\text{Iteration 2:} & \;\; \text{Query } \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \text{ is now in the fact set.} \\
                    & \;\; \text{Halt with success.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complexity Note (KTU favourite):** The number of iterations is bounded by the number of possible ground facts. For KB with $k$ $k$-ary predicates and $n$ constants, the upper bound is $n^k$ — exponential in predicate arity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Backward Chaining Trace

**Same KB as above. Goal:** $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal Stack:} & \;\; [\text{Evil}(\text{John})] \\
\text{Step 1:} & \;\; \text{Goal } \text{Evil}(\text{John}). \text{ Look for rules with head matching it.} \\
                & \;\; \text{Rule (1) matches with } \theta = \{x/\text{John}\}. \\
\text{Step 2:} & \;\; \text{Add sub-goals: } \text{King}(\text{John}) \text{ and } \text{Greedy}(\text{John}). \\
\text{Step 3:} & \;\; \text{Try } \text{King}(\text{John}). \text{ Matched directly by fact (2).} \\
\text{Step 4:} & \;\; \text{Try } \text{Greedy}(\text{John}). \text{ Matched directly by fact (3) (or by universal fact).} \\
\text{Step 5:} & \;\; \text{All sub-goals proved. Return success with binding } \{x/\text{John}\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why prefer backward chaining for queries?** It avoids the explosion of irrelevant facts. **Why prefer forward chaining for monitoring/diagnosis?** It maintains a fully-updated fact base that can answer *any* subsequent query cheaply.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Full CNF + Resolution Refutation (14-mark standard)

**Given KB:**

1. $\forall x \, (\text{King}(x) \wedge \text{Greedy}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(x))$
2. $\text{King}(\text{John})$
3. $\forall x \, \text{Greedy}(x)$
4. $\text{Evil}(x) \Rightarrow \neg \text{Trust}(x)$   (assumed extra clause)

**Prove:** $\neg \text{Trust}(\text{John})$.

### Stage A — Negate the goal and add to KB:

$$
\text{KB}' = \text{KB} \cup \{\text{Trust}(\text{John})\}
$$

### Stage B — Convert every sentence to CNF:

**Sentence 1:** $\forall x \, (\neg \text{King}(x) \vee \neg \text{Greedy}(x) \vee \text{Evil}(x))$

- Already in CNF.

**Sentence 2:** $\text{King}(\text{John})$

- Atomic — already a clause.

**Sentence 3:** $\forall x \, \text{Greedy}(x)$

- $\text{Greedy}(x)$ — clause with variable.

**Sentence 4:** $\neg \text{Evil}(x) \vee \neg \text{Trust}(x)$

- Already in CNF.

**Negated Goal:** $\text{Trust}(\text{John})$

- Atomic clause.

### Stage C — Apply Resolution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolve: } & \;\; \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \text{ from (1) with } \theta = \{x/\text{John}\}: \\
                & \;\; (\neg \text{King}(\text{John}) \vee \neg \text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \vee \text{Evil}(\text{John})) \;\;\text{[1, instantiated]}\\
\text{Resolve: } & \;\; \neg \text{Evil}(\text{John}) \vee \neg \text{Trust}(\text{John}) \text{ with } \theta = \{x/\text{John}\} \\
                & \;\; \Rightarrow \neg \text{King}(\text{John}) \vee \neg \text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \vee \neg \text{Trust}(\text{John}) \quad \text{[5]}\\
\text{Resolve (5) with King(John):} & \;\; \Rightarrow \neg \text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \vee \neg \text{Trust}(\text{John}) \quad \text{[6]}\\
\text{Resolve (6) with Greedy(John):} & \;\; \Rightarrow \neg \text{Trust}(\text{John}) \quad \text{[7]}\\
\text{Resolve (7) with Trust(John):} & \;\; \Rightarrow \square \quad \text{[EMPTY CLAUSE — contradiction]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The empty clause $\square$ was derived, therefore $KB \models \neg \text{Trust}(\text{John})$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuer tip: clearly label each new clause (C5, C6, C7) and state the MGU used in *every* resolution step. Marks are awarded for the substitution trail, not just the final result.

---

## 3.5 Unification Algorithm — Pseudocode Sketch (Trace)

Input: Two expressions $E_1, E_2$. Output: MGU $\theta$ or FAIL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. If } E_1 = E_2 \text{ is an identical constant or variable: return } \{\}. \\
&\text{2. If } E_1 \text{ is a variable: } \\
&\quad \text{a. If } E_1 \text{ occurs in } E_2: \text{ return FAIL (occurs check).} \\
&\quad \text{b. Else return } \{E_1/E_2\}. \\
&\text{3. If } E_2 \text{ is a variable: symmetric to step 2.} \\
&\text{4. If } E_1 = f(a_1,\ldots,a_n),\, E_2 = g(b_1,\ldots,b_m): \\
&\quad \text{a. If } f \neq g \text{ or } n \neq m: \text{ return FAIL.} \\
&\quad \text{b. Else: recursively unify } a_1 \text{ with } b_1, \text{ compose substitutions, then } a_2/b_2,\ldots \\
&\text{5. Else: FAIL.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **occurs check** in step 2a is *the single most important safeguard* against infinite loops (e.g., unifying $x$ with $F(x)$). KTU examiners frequently pose a question like: "Why is the occurs check essential? What happens if we omit it?"
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Resolution Refutation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Knowledge Base KB in FOL"] --> B["Negate the Goal alpha to NOT alpha"]
    B --> C["Convert KB plus NOT alpha to CNF"]
    C --> D["Apply Binary Resolution repeatedly"]
    D --> E{Empty Clause Derived?}
    E -- Yes --> F["KB entails alpha: PROOF COMPLETE"]
    E -- No --> G{New Clauses Generated?}
    G -- Yes --> D
    G -- No --> H["No entailment: FAILURE"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FC["Forward Chaining - Data Driven"]
        F1["Fact Base"] --> F2["Match Rule Premises"]
        F2 --> F3["Fire Rule - Add Conclusion"]
        F3 --> F4["New Facts Added?"]
        F4 -- Yes --> F1
        F4 -- No --> F5["Halt - Report Facts"]
    end
    subgraph BC["Backward Chaining - Goal Driven"]
        B1["Query / Goal"] --> B2["Find Rule with Matching Head"]
        B2 --> B3["Recurse on Premises as Sub-Goals"]
        B3 --> B4["All Sub-Goals Proved?"]
        B4 -- Yes --> B5["Return Success with Bindings"]
        B4 -- No --> B6["Try Alternative Rule"]
        B6 --> B2
    end
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — The CNF Conversion Pipeline (7 Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Eliminate Implication and Biconditional"] --> S2["Step 2: Move Negation Inward - De Morgan Laws"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Standardize Variables Apart"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Skolemize Existential Quantifiers"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Drop Universal Quantifiers"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Distribute AND over OR"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Flatten into Set of Clauses"]
    S7 --> S8["CNF Clause Set Ready for Resolution"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Unification Recursion Tree (Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["UNIFY: Knows John x vs Knows y Mother y"] --> R1["Compare predicate Knows: OK"]
    R1 --> R2["Unify John with y"]
    R2 --> R3["Substitution theta1: y slash John"]
    R3 --> R4["Apply theta1: Knows John x vs Knows John Mother John"]
    R4 --> R5["Unify x with Mother John"]
    R5 --> R6["Substitution theta2: x slash Mother John"]
    R6 --> R7["MGU: y slash John, x slash Mother John"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In actual KTU exam answer sheets, replace the Mermaid diagram with a *hand-drawn* equivalent: a labelled flowchart or a tree. The boxes here map directly to the textual trace KTU expects.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FOL Inference Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: KB in FOL plus Query alpha"] --> B["Parser and Lexer Module"]
    B --> C["CNF Converter Module"]
    C --> D["Clause Store"]
    D --> E["Unification Engine"]
    E --> F["Inference Strategy Selector"]
    F --> F1["Forward Chaining Module"]
    F --> F2["Backward Chaining Module"]
    F --> F3["Resolution Refutation Module"]
    F1 --> G["Output: Derived Facts or YES"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Result Handler: Soundness and Completeness Logger"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Universal Instantiation and Existential Instantiation in First-Order Logic. Provide one example for each.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Universal Instantiation (UI) | Existential Instantiation (EI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quantifier | Universal $\forall$ | Existential $\exists$ |
| Inference | $\forall x\,P(x) \vdash P(c)$ for *any* constant $c$ | $\exists x\,P(x) \vdash P(k)$ for a *fresh Skolem constant* $k$ |
| Reusability | The same constant can be reused across multiple UI steps. | The Skolem constant must be **new** every time to avoid unjustified identity claims. |
| Example | $\forall x\,\text{Crown}(x) \Rightarrow \text{King}(x) \vdash \text{Crown}(\text{John}) \Rightarrow \text{King}(\text{John})$ | $\exists x\,\text{Crown}(x) \vdash \text{Crown}(k_1)$ where $k_1$ is fresh |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Most General Unifier" (MGU) and state why the "occurs check" is essential during unification.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A substitution $\theta$ is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** of two expressions $E_1$ and $E_2$ if:
- $E_1\theta = E_2\theta$ (they become identical), and
- For every other unifier $\sigma$ of $E_1$ and $E_2$, there exists a substitution $\gamma$ such that $\sigma = \theta \circ \gamma$.

The **occurs check** verifies that a variable $x$ does not appear inside the term $t$ that it is about to be bound to. Without it, unifying $x$ with $F(x)$ would yield the infinite equation $x = F(x) = F(F(x)) = \ldots$, causing the inference engine to **loop forever**. Hence the occurs check guarantees termination of unification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Module 3 Choice)

**(a)** Explain the process of **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) conversion** for First-Order Logic sentences. List all the standard steps with a one-line example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Resolution Refutation**, prove the following:

> Given:
> 1. $\forall x\,(\text{Parent}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Ancestor}(x))$
> 2. $\forall x\,\forall y\,(\text{Parent}(x) \wedge \text{Parent}(y) \Rightarrow \text{Ancestor}(y))$
> 3. $\text{Parent}(\text{Anu})$
> 4. $\text{Parent}(\text{Ravi})$
>
> **Prove:** $\text{Ancestor}(\text{Ravi})$.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — CNF Conversion (7 marks)

The 7-step CNF pipeline is given below. The illustrative sentence is
$\neg \forall x\,(\text{Human}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Eliminate } \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow. & \;\; P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \neg \forall x\,(\neg \text{Human}(x) \vee \text{Mortal}(x)) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Move } \neg \text{ inward.} & \;\; \neg\forall \equiv \exists\neg, \;\; \neg\exists \equiv \forall\neg, \;\; \neg\neg P \equiv P \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \exists x\,(\text{Human}(x) \wedge \neg \text{Mortal}(x)) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Standardize variables apart.} & \;\; \text{Rename bound vars to unique names per quantifier scope.} \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \exists x\,(\text{Human}(x) \wedge \neg \text{Mortal}(x)) \;\; (\text{no clash, kept as is}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Skolemize } \exists. & \;\; \text{Replace } \exists x \text{ by a fresh function } f() \text{ of all enclosing } \forall \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \text{Human}(A) \wedge \neg \text{Mortal}(A) \;\; \text{where } A = f() \text{ is a Skolem constant} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Drop } \forall. & \;\; \text{All remaining variables are implicitly universal.} \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \text{Human}(A) \wedge \neg \text{Mortal}(A) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: Distribute } \wedge \text{ over } \vee. & \;\; (P \wedge Q) \vee R \equiv (P \vee R) \wedge (Q \vee R) \\
\text{Example:} & \;\; \text{Already a conjunction of literals — no change.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: Flatten into clauses.} & \;\; \text{Each conjunct becomes a separate clause.} \\
\text{Result:} & \;\; \{\text{Human}(A)\} \text{ and } \{\neg \text{Mortal}(A)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Marks distribution hint:** 1 mark per step (7 × 1 = 7). Examiners expect both the *operation name* and a *concrete worked example line* for each step.

#### Part (b) — Resolution Refutation Proof (7 marks)

**Step 1.** Negate the goal: add $\neg\text{Ancestor}(\text{Ravi})$ to the KB.

**Step 2.** Convert all sentences to CNF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \neg \text{Parent}(x) \vee \text{Ancestor}(x) \\
C_2 &: \neg \text{Parent}(x) \vee \neg \text{Parent}(y) \vee \text{Ancestor}(y) \\
C_3 &: \text{Parent}(\text{Anu}) \\
C_4 &: \text{Parent}(\text{Ravi}) \\
C_5 &: \neg \text{Ancestor}(\text{Ravi}) \quad \text{(negated goal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Resolve iteratively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolve } C_1 \text{ and } C_4 \text{ with } \theta = \{x/\text{Ravi}\}: & \;\; C_6 : \text{Ancestor}(\text{Ravi}) \\
\text{Resolve } C_6 \text{ and } C_5: & \;\; C_7 : \square \;\; \text{(empty clause)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Derivation of $\square$ proves $KB \models \text{Ancestor}(\text{Ravi})$. $\blacksquare$

> **Marks distribution hint:** 2 marks for correct CNF, 3 marks for resolution trace with MGU shown, 1 mark for stating the empty-clause conclusion, 1 mark for the final entailment statement.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Module 3 Choice – Internal Alternative)

**(a)** Explain **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** as a lifted version of propositional modus ponens. Show how GMP is used as the inference step inside **Forward Chaining** with a worked example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following KB:

1. $\text{Parent}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi})$
2. $\forall x\,\forall y\,(\text{Parent}(x,y) \Rightarrow \text{Ancestor}(x,y))$
3. $\forall x\,\forall y\,\forall z\,(\text{Ancestor}(x,y) \wedge \text{Parent}(y,z) \Rightarrow \text{Ancestor}(x,z))$

Apply **Backward Chaining** to answer the query: $\text{Ancestor}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi})$?
Show every step, including the MGU used at each rule application. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — GMP and Forward Chaining (7 marks)

**Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP):**

For atomic sentences $p_i', p_i$ and an implication $p_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_n \Rightarrow q$, with $\theta$ being the substitution that unifies each $p_i'$ with $p_i$, GMP permits inferring $q\theta$.

$$
\frac{p_1', \; p_2', \; \ldots, \; p_n', \quad p_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_n \Rightarrow q}{q\,\theta}
$$

GMP is **sound** because every instance of an entailed implication is a tautology, and unification is a syntactic pattern-match that respects the binding of variables.

**Forward Chaining with GMP — Worked Example:**

KB:
- $P(A), \; P(B), \; Q(A), \; R(B)$
- Rule: $P(x) \wedge Q(x) \Rightarrow S(x)$
- Rule: $P(x) \wedge R(x) \Rightarrow T(x)$

**Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & \;\; \text{Match } P(A) \text{ and } Q(A) \text{ against } P(x), Q(x) \text{ with } \theta_1 = \{x/A\}. \\
                     & \;\; \text{Infer } S(A). \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & \;\; \text{Match } P(B) \text{ and } R(B) \text{ with } \theta_2 = \{x/B\}. \\
                     & \;\; \text{Infer } T(B). \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & \;\; \text{No further premises matchable. Halt.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Marks distribution hint:** 2 marks for GMP formal statement, 2 marks for the worked example, 2 marks for showing the MGUs, 1 mark for stating termination and final derived facts.

#### Part (b) — Backward Chaining Trace (7 marks)

**Goal Stack Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal}_0: & \;\; \text{Ancestor}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi}) \\
\text{Step 1:} & \;\; \text{Rule (2) matches: } \theta_1 = \{x/\text{Anu}, y/\text{Ravi}\}. \\
               & \;\; \text{New sub-goal: } \text{Parent}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi}). \\
\text{Step 2:} & \;\; \text{Parent}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi}) \text{ is exactly fact (1). Sub-goal proven.} \\
\text{Result: } & \;\; \text{Ancestor}(\text{Anu}, \text{Ravi}) \text{ is entailed. } \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

Optionally, demonstrate a deeper chain using Rule (3) for queries like $\text{Ancestor}(\text{Anu}, z)$ — the engine would recursively chain.

> **Marks distribution hint:** 2 marks for stating the goal-stack mechanism, 2 marks for the MGU at each step, 2 marks for matching against KB facts, 1 mark for the final success statement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the goal** before resolution. Half the marks evaporate here.
> 2. **Reusing Skolem constants** across EI steps — invalid; each existential witness must be fresh.
> 3. **Omitting the MGU** in resolution steps. Examiners award 1 mark *per step* for showing the unifying substitution.
> 4. **Confusing $\vdash$ with $\models$**: $\vdash$ is *derivation* (syntactic), $\models$ is *entailment* (semantic).
> 5. **Forward-chaining on a non-Horn KB** and claiming completeness — FOL forward chaining is complete *only for definite clauses*.
> 6. **Distributing $\vee$ over $\wedge$** instead of $\wedge$ over $\vee$ during CNF conversion. The correct direction is: $P \vee (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)$.
> 7. **Skipping the occurs check** when asked to perform unification on a self-referential pattern like $x$ and $F(G(x))$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FOL inference** is the process of deriving new sentences from a KB using **sound, formal rules** of inference.
- [x] **Universal Instantiation** lets us instantiate $\forall x\,P(x)$ with *any* constant; **Existential Instantiation** requires a *fresh Skolem constant*.
- [x] **Unification** finds a substitution that makes two expressions identical; the **MGU** is the least-specific such substitution.
- [x] The **occurs check** is mandatory to prevent infinite loops during unification.
- [x] **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** = modus ponens + unification — the workhorse of forward and backward chaining.
- [x] **Forward Chaining** = data-driven; complete for definite clauses; efficient for *monitoring*.
- [x] **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven; complete for definite clauses; used by **Prolog**.
- [x] **Resolution** is refutation-complete for full FOL. The strategy: **negate goal → CNF → resolve until $\square$**.
- [x] **CNF pipeline** has **7 standard steps** — memorize the order.
- [x] **Skolemization** removes $\exists$ quantifiers via Skolem *constants* (no enclosing $\forall$) or Skolem *functions* (with enclosing $\forall$).
- [x] The **empty clause $\square$** is the *signature* of a successful resolution refutation — it represents logical contradiction.
- [x] **Factoring** complements resolution by removing redundant copies of unifiable literals within a clause.
- [x] KTU expects you to **show MGUs explicitly** in every step of resolution and unification traces.
- [x] Real-world tools: Prolog (backward chaining), CLIPS (forward chaining), Vampire/E-Prover (resolution).

> **One-line mnemonic for resolution:** *"Negate, Normalise, Nullify."*
> **One-line mnemonic for CNF:** *"Implication out, Negation in, Variables apart, Skolem, Drop-forall, Distribute, Flatten."*

---

**End of Module 3 — Inference in First-Order Logic | PCCMT402 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Propositional vs. First-Order Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propositional vs. First-Order Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Propositional Logic Inference — Formal Definition

**Propositional Inference** is the process of deriving new propositional sentences (conclusions) from a given Knowledge Base (KB) of propositional logic sentences using sound inference rules. The KB is represented as a set of Boolean variables combined with logical connectives ($\land, \lor, \lnot, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$), and an inference algorithm determines whether a query $\alpha$ logically follows from KB, written as $KB \models \alpha$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** "Inference in propositional logic is the derivation of new propositional sentences from existing ones through the application of syntactically valid inference rules such as Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, And-Elimination, Unit Resolution, and Resolution."

### 1.2 First-Order Logic (FOL) Inference — Formal Definition

**First-Order Logic Inference** is the lifting of propositional inference rules to a logic that supports **quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$), **predicates**, **functions**, and **variables**. It introduces two new algorithmic primitives — **Unification** ($\text{UNIFY}$) and **Substitution** ($\theta$) — that allow inference rules to operate on *general* quantified rules rather than on ground propositions. The lifted versions are prefixed with "Generalized," e.g., **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** and **Generalized Resolution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** Propositional inference reasons about *facts*. First-order inference reasons about *facts about objects and relations between them*. FOL is strictly more expressive; every propositional KB is a degenerate FOL KB with zero-arity predicates.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Courtroom Drama

Imagine two courtrooms:

- **Propositional Logic Courtroom** — The judge only accepts *complete, closed facts*. You may submit: *"Socrates is mortal" (S₁)*, *"Socrates is a man" (S₂)*, and the rule *"All men are mortal"*. The judge refuses to hear the rule because it contains the unbound word *"All"*. He can only rule by combining whole propositions using Modus Ponens: from S₁ and S₂ he concludes nothing directly, because the rule itself is not in a usable form.
- **First-Order Logic Courtroom** — The judge accepts the rule *"All men are mortal"* because he understands quantifiers. He applies **Unification** to bind the variable `x = Socrates`, then uses **Generalized Modus Ponens** to conclude *"Socrates is mortal."* The FOL courtroom reasons at the *object level*; the propositional one reasons only at the *atomic-fact level*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Inference Systems

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (KTU scope) |
|---|---|---|
| Inference Soundness | — | **True** for all standard rules |
| Inference Completeness | — | **True** for Resolution; **False** for Forward/Backward Chaining on full FOL |
| Decidability of Propositional Inference | — | **Decidable** (SAT) |
| Decidability of Full FOL Inference | — | **Undecidable** (semi-decidable only) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Propositional Inference Rules — Structured Logic Steps

The foundational inference rules that KTU expects you to write from memory:

1. **Modus Ponens (MP):** The most important rule.
   $$\frac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha}{\beta}$$
2. **Modus Tollens (MT):**
   $$\frac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \quad \lnot \beta}{\lnot \alpha}$$
3. **And-Elimination (∧-Elim):**
   $$\frac{\alpha \land \beta}{\alpha} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\alpha \land \beta}{\beta}$$
4. **And-Introduction (∧-Intro):**
   $$\frac{\alpha, \quad \beta}{\alpha \land \beta}$$
5. **Or-Introduction (∨-Intro):**
   $$\frac{\alpha}{\alpha \lor \beta}$$
6. **Double-Negation Elimination:**
   $$\frac{\lnot \lnot \alpha}{\alpha}$$
7. **Implication Elimination (≡ rewrite):**
   $$\alpha \Rightarrow \beta \equiv \lnot \alpha \lor \beta$$
8. **Unit Resolution (special case):**
   $$\frac{\alpha \lor \beta, \quad \lnot \beta}{\alpha}$$
9. **Full Resolution (binary):**
   $$\frac{\alpha \lor \beta, \quad \lnot \beta \lor \gamma}{\alpha \lor \gamma}$$
   The two complementary literals $\beta$ and $\lnot \beta$ are resolved away.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Step

- **Modus Ponens (MP)** is *truth-functional*: if the implication is true and the antecedent is true, the consequent must be true. It is the workhorse of every forward-chaining expert system.
- **Resolution** is the only rule that, when combined with a complete search strategy, yields a *refutation-complete* propositional theorem prover. KTU examiners specifically test conversion to **CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form)** followed by resolution refutation.
- **Unit Resolution** is a restricted, faster form of resolution that uses at least one *unit clause* (single-literal clause).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soundness vs. Completeness:** A rule is *sound* if it never infers a false conclusion. A rule is *complete* if it can derive *every* entailed conclusion. Modus Ponens is sound but not complete for the full propositional calculus; **Resolution** is both sound and refutation-complete for propositional logic.

### 2.3 First-Order Logic Inference — Lifted Rules

#### 2.3.1 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)
For atomic sentences $p_i, p_i^{'}$, variable substitution $\theta$ such that $\text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i^{'}) = \theta$ for all $i$:

$$\frac{p_1^{'}, \quad p_2^{'}, \quad \ldots, \quad p_n^{'}, \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \ldots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta}$$

- $\theta$ is a **unifier** — a mapping from variables to terms.
- GMP requires the *premises* to be *atomic* (a key restriction).
- The rule is **sound** but **not complete** for full FOL.

#### 2.3.2 Unification ($\text{UNIFY}$)
A unification algorithm returns the *Most General Unifier (MGU)* — the simplest binding that makes two expressions identical. Key rules:

- **Atom rule:** $\text{UNIFY}(x, x) = \{\}$ (empty substitution).
- **Variable rule:** $\text{UNIFY}(x, y) = \{x/y\}$ if $x$ is a variable and $x \notin \text{vars}(y)$.
- **Compound rule:** $\text{UNIFY}(f(a_1, \ldots), f(b_1, \ldots)) = \text{UNIFY}(\text{list}(a_i), \text{list}(b_i))$.
- **Failure rule:** if top-level symbols differ (e.g., $\text{UNIFY}(P, Q) = \text{fail}$), unification aborts.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Notation:** $\theta = \{x/A, y/B\}$ means "replace $x$ with $A$ and $y$ with $B$." Composition is written $\theta_1 \circ \theta_2$ (apply $\theta_2$ first, then $\theta_1$).

#### 2.3.3 Generalized Resolution
Convert all FOL sentences to **CNF**, skolemize existential quantifiers, then apply binary resolution as in propositional logic, but substitute $\theta$ returned by unification for the complementary literals.

#### 2.3.4 Forward Chaining vs. Backward Chaining
- **Forward Chaining:** Data-driven. Start from known facts, repeatedly fire rules whose premises are satisfied, add conclusions to the KB until the query is proven. *Complete only for Horn clauses (definite clauses with at most one positive literal).*
- **Backward Chaining:** Goal-driven. Start from the query, recursively try to prove each conjunct in the rule's premise. *Used by Prolog.* Complete for Horn clauses.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Rule / Equation** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modus Ponens | $\dfrac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \; \alpha}{\beta}$ | Sound; non-complete alone |
| 2 | Modus Tollens | $\dfrac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \; \lnot \beta}{\lnot \alpha}$ | Useful contrapositive |
| 3 | And-Elimination | $\dfrac{\alpha \land \beta}{\alpha}$ | Conjunct extraction |
| 4 | Implication rewrite | $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta \equiv \lnot \alpha \lor \beta$ | CNF preprocessing step |
| 5 | Biconditional rewrite | $\alpha \Leftrightarrow \beta \equiv (\alpha \Rightarrow \beta) \land (\beta \Rightarrow \alpha)$ | 2 implications |
| 6 | CNF conversion | $\lnot(\alpha \lor \beta) \equiv \lnot \alpha \land \lnot \beta$ | De Morgan |
| 7 | Full Resolution | $\dfrac{\alpha \lor \beta, \; \lnot \beta \lor \gamma}{\alpha \lor \gamma}$ | Refutation-complete |
| 8 | Unification | $\text{UNIFY}(P(x, A), P(B, y)) = \{x/B, y/A\}$ | MGU example |
| 9 | GMP | $\dfrac{P_1^{'}, \ldots, P_n^{'}, \; P_1 \land \ldots \land P_n \Rightarrow Q}{Q\theta}$ where $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(P_i, P_i^{'})$ | FOL lift of MP |
| 10 | Skolemization | $\exists x \; P(x) \to P(C)$ for a fresh Skolem constant $C$ | Removes $\exists$ for CNF |
| 11 | Forward Chaining | $KB \vdash q$ via rule firing | Horn-complete |
| 12 | Backward Chaining | $KB \vdash q$ via sub-goal decomposition | Prolog's algorithm |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert$ inside expressions is intentionally written as $\lvert$ for clarity; do not use raw vertical pipes inside markdown table cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Propositional inference** powers hardware **SAT solvers** used in formal verification of VLSI circuits (Intel, AMD, NVIDIA).
- **FOL forward chaining** underpins **production-rule expert systems** in medical diagnosis (MYCIN) and configuration (CLIPS, Jess).
- **FOL backward chaining** drives the **RDF/SPARQL inference engines** in the **Semantic Web** for ontology reasoning.
- **FOL resolution** is the engine behind **automated theorem provers** (E, Vampire, LEO-III) used in formal mathematics and software verification (KeYmaera X).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example A — Propositional Resolution Refutation

**Problem:** Given KB
$$KB = \{ P \lor Q, \; \lnot P \lor R, \; \lnot R \}$$
Prove $KB \models Q$.

**Step 1 — Negate the goal** to set up refutation:
$$\text{Add} \; \lnot Q \; \text{to KB.}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CNF** (already in CNF since all are disjunctions of literals):
$$C_1: P \lor Q$$
$$C_2: \lnot P \lor R$$
$$C_3: \lnot R$$
$$C_4: \lnot Q$$

**Step 3 — Apply resolution pairwise, documenting substitutions:**

Resolve $C_1$ and $C_2$ on the complementary pair $(P, \lnot P)$:

$$C_5: \text{RESOLVE}(C_1, C_2) = (P \lor Q) \;\oplus\; (\lnot P \lor R) = Q \lor R$$

Resolve $C_5$ and $C_3$ on the complementary pair $(R, \lnot R)$:

$$C_6: \text{RESOLVE}(C_5, C_3) = (Q \lor R) \;\oplus\; \lnot R = Q$$

Resolve $C_6$ and $C_4$ on the complementary pair $(Q, \lnot Q)$:

$$C_7: \text{RESOLVE}(C_6, C_4) = Q \;\oplus\; \lnot Q = \square \quad (\text{empty clause / NIL})$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The empty clause is derived, so $KB \land \lnot Q$ is unsatisfiable. Therefore $KB \models Q$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example B — Unification in FOL

**Problem:** Find the MGU of
$$\text{UNIFY}\big(\, P(x, f(y), B), \; P(A, f(g(z)), B) \,\big).$$

**Step 1 — Initial state.** $\theta_0 = \{\}$ (empty substitution). Both expressions have the same predicate symbol $P$ and same arity 3, so proceed to arguments.

**Step 2 — Unify first arguments.** $x$ and $A$. Since $x$ is a variable, bind $x \to A$:
$$\theta_1 = \{x/A\}.$$

**Step 3 — Unify second arguments under $\theta_1$.** Apply $\theta_1$ to both expressions, then unify $f(y)$ with $f(g(z))$. Top symbols match ($f$). Unify arguments:
$$\text{UNIFY}(y, g(z)) = \{y/g(z)\}.$$
Compose:
$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 \circ \{y/g(z)\} = \{x/A, \; y/g(z)\}.$$

**Step 4 — Unify third arguments under $\theta_2$.** Both are $B$. Trivial unification:
$$\theta_3 = \theta_2 \circ \{\} = \{x/A, \; y/g(z)\}.$$

**Step 5 — Check for occurs-check violation.** At no point did a variable become bound to a term containing itself, so no failure.

**Step 6 — Final answer.**
$$\text{MGU} = \{x/A, \; y/g(z)\}$$
The two expressions, when this substitution is applied, both become $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

**Problem:** Given the rule
$$R: \forall x \; \big(\text{Man}(x) \land \text{Mortal}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Dies}(x)\big)$$

Wait — that is malformed. Let us restate it cleanly.

**Cleaner rule:**
$$R: \forall x \; \big(\text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Dies}(x)\big)$$

**KB facts:**
$$F_1: \text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$$
$$F_2: \text{Man}(\text{Plato})$$

**Goal:** Prove $\text{Dies}(\text{Socrates})$ using GMP.

**Step 1 — Standardize variables apart.** The rule uses $x$; the facts use no variables. No change needed.

**Step 2 — Find a unifier $\theta$** such that the rule's premise $\text{Man}(x)$ unifies with a fact.
$$\text{UNIFY}(\text{Man}(x), \text{Man}(\text{Socrates})) = \{x/\text{Socrates}\}$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\theta$ to the consequent $\text{Dies}(x)$:**
$$\text{Dies}(x) \cdot \theta = \text{Dies}(\text{Socrates})$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** GMP yields:
$$\dfrac{\text{Man}(\text{Socrates}), \quad \text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Dies}(x)}{\text{Dies}(\text{Socrates})} \quad \text{with } \theta = \{x/\text{Socrates}\}.$$
Hence $KB \models \text{Dies}(\text{Socrates})$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Dimension** | **Propositional Inference** | **First-Order Inference** |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge representation | Atomic Boolean symbols | Predicates, functions, variables, quantifiers |
| Example fact | $P_{SM}$ (Socrates is mortal) | $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$ |
| Decidability | **Decidable** (SAT is decidable) | **Undecidable** (FOL entailment is semi-decidable) |
| Algorithmic complexity | NP-complete (resolution refutation) | Unbounded / potentially infinite search |
| Sound rule count | ~9 standard rules | Lifted versions of the same (~9) |
| Key new operator | None | **Unifier $\theta$** |
| Primary inference rule (KTU focus) | **Resolution** (refutation-complete) | **Generalized Resolution / GMP** |
| Reasoning direction | Forward / Backward | Forward, Backward, Resolution |
| Incompleteness source | Resolution is complete — *algorithms* may time out | Even resolution cannot enumerate all proofs |
| Standard use | Circuit verification, SAT solving | Knowledge graphs, ontologies, Prolog, NLI |
| Data structure | Set of clauses | Clause set + substitution store |

### 3.5 Worked Example D — CNF Conversion of an FOL Sentence (Killer Trick)

**Problem:** Convert
$$\forall x \; \big[\text{Man}(x) \Rightarrow \exists y \; (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Knows}(y, \text{Socrates}))\big]$$
to CNF.

**Step 1 — Eliminate biconditionals and implications:**
$$\forall x \; \big[\lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \exists y \; (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Knows}(y, \text{Socrates}))\big]$$

**Step 2 — Move $\lnot$ inwards** (none needed here; there is no negation of a conjunction).

**Step 3 — Standardize variables apart:**
$$\forall x \; \big[\lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \exists y \; (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \text{Knows}(y, \text{Socrates}))\big]$$
($y$ is already local to the existential scope.)

**Step 4 — Skolemize the existential quantifier.** Since $y$ is inside the scope of $x$, the Skolem term must be a *function* of $x$. Introduce the Skolem function $f(x)$:
$$\forall x \; \big[\lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \big(\text{Loves}(x, f(x)) \land \text{Knows}(f(x), \text{Socrates})\big)\big]$$

**Step 5 — Drop the universal quantifier** (implicit in CNF):
$$\lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Loves}(x, f(x))$$
$$\lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Knows}(f(x), \text{Socrates})$$

**Step 6 — Resulting CNF clause set:**
$$\{ \; \lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Loves}(x, f(x)), \quad \lnot \text{Man}(x) \lor \text{Knows}(f(x), \text{Socrates}) \; \}$$
$\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Propositional Resolution Refutation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KB in Natural Language] --> B[Encode as Propositional Sentences]
    B --> C[Add negated goal: NOT alpha]
    C --> D[Convert all to CNF]
    D --> E[Initialize clause set S]
    E --> F{Pick complementary pair}
    F --> G[Resolve to new clause]
    G --> H{New clause is empty?}
    H -->|Yes| I[SUCCESS: KB entails alpha]
    H -->|No| J{Already in S?}
    J -->|Yes| F
    J -->|No| K[Add to S]
    K --> F
    F -->|No more pairs| L[FAILURE: timeout or no proof]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d4edda
    style L fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — First-Order Inference Architecture with Unification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FOL KB: Quantified Rules] --> B[Standardize Variables Apart]
    B --> C[Convert to CNF]
    C --> D[Skolemize existential quantifiers]
    D --> E[Clause Set in FOL]
    E --> F[Pick two clauses]
    F --> G[Unify complementary literals]
    G --> H{UNIFY succeeds?}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply MGU theta to rest of clause]
    I --> J[Add resolvent to set]
    J --> K{Resolvent is empty clause?}
    K -->|Yes| L[SUCCESS: FOL entailment proved]
    K -->|No| M{New clause?}
    M -->|Yes| J
    M -->|No| F
    F -->|Exhausted| N[FAILURE]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#d4edda
    style N fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Side-by-Side Reasoning Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROPLOG [PROPOSITIONAL REASONING]
        P1[Fact: SocratesM] --> P2[Rule: ManGreaterSocratesM]
        P2 --> P3[Modus Ponens on concrete symbols]
        P3 --> P4[Conclusion: SM]
    end
    subgraph FOLLOG [FIRST-ORDER REASONING]
        F1[Fact: ManSocrates] --> F2[Rule: forall x Man x implies Dies x]
        F2 --> F3[Unify x with Socrates]
        F3 --> F4[Generalized Modus Ponens]
        F4 --> F5[Conclusion: Dies Socrates]
    end
    style PROPLOG fill:#fff3cd
    style FOLLOG fill:#d1ecf1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Forward vs. Backward Chaining Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query: Is q true?] --> B{KB has rules?}
    B -->|No| C[Return UNKNOWN]
    B -->|Yes| D{Reasoning mode}
    D -->|Forward Chaining| E[Scan rules whose premises match known facts]
    E --> F{New fact generated?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Add to KB]
    G --> E
    F -->|No and q in KB| H[SUCCESS]
    F -->|No and q not in KB| I[FAIL or LOOP]
    D -->|Backward Chaining| J[Find rule whose head unifies with q]
    J --> K{Found?}
    K -->|No| I
    K -->|Yes| L[Recursively prove each premise]
    L --> M{All premises proven?}
    M -->|Yes| H
    M -->|No| N[Try next rule]
    N --> J
    style H fill:#d4edda
    style I fill:#f8d7da
```
