## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define *Propositional Logic Inference* and *First-Order Logic Inference*. List **any two** differences between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Propositional Logic Inference** is the process of deriving new conclusions from a knowledge base expressed as Boolean propositions using rules such as Modus Ponens and Resolution. It operates only on *atomic facts* (truth-functional combinations of Boolean symbols).
- **First-Order Logic Inference** extends this by supporting **quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$), **predicates**, **functions**, and **variables**, enabling reasoning about *relations among objects*. It uses *Generalized* rules (e.g., GMP) and the **Unification** algorithm.
- **Differences (any two):**
  1. FOL is *more expressive* — it can represent *"All men are mortal"* in one rule; propositional logic would need a separate rule per individual.
  2. FOL entailment is **undecidable**; propositional inference is **decidable** (SAT is decidable).
  3. FOL inference requires **unification**; propositional inference needs only **symbol matching**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
What is **unification** in First-Order Logic? Given the two atomic expressions
$$P(x, f(y), B) \quad \text{and} \quad P(A, f(g(z)), B),$$
compute the Most General Unifier (MGU).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions *syntactically identical*. The MGU is the *least committing* such substitution.
- **Computation:**
  - Unify $x$ with $A$: $\theta_1 = \{x/A\}$.
  - Unify $f(y)$ with $f(g(z))$: $\theta_2 = \{y/g(z)\}$.
  - Unify $B$ with $B$: trivial.
  - **MGU = $\{x/A, \; y/g(z)\}$**, giving $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Convert the following FOL sentence to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**:
$$\forall x \; \forall y \; \big[ \text{Parent}(x, y) \Rightarrow \text{Older}(x, y) \big] \;\land\; \exists z \; \text{Teacher}(z)$$
Show every step.

**(b)** [7 marks] Apply **Generalized Modus Ponens** to derive a new fact from the following KB:
- Rule: $\forall x \; \big[ \text{Crow}(x) \land \text{Black}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Flies}(x) \big]$
- Fact 1: $\text{Crow}(\text{Ravi})$
- Fact 2: $\text{Black}(\text{Ravi})$
- State the MGU used and the final derived sentence.

---

#### **Model Solution A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Original sentence:**
$$\forall x \; \forall y \; \big[ \text{Parent}(x, y) \Rightarrow \text{Older}(x, y) \big] \;\land\; \exists z \; \text{Teacher}(z)$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $\Rightarrow$:**
$$\forall x \; \forall y \; \big[ \lnot \text{Parent}(x, y) \lor \text{Older}(x, y) \big] \;\land\; \exists z \; \text{Teacher}(z)$$

**Step 3 — Standardize variables apart** (the two parts have disjoint variable scopes already — $x, y$ vs. $z$ — so no rename needed).

**Step 4 — Skolemize the existential $\exists z$** by introducing a Skolem constant $C_T$ (the existential is not inside any universal scope, so a constant is correct):
$$\forall x \; \forall y \; \big[ \lnot \text{Parent}(x, y) \lor \text{Older}(x, y) \big] \;\land\; \text{Teacher}(C_T)$$

**Step 5 — Drop universal quantifiers** (CNF treats variables as implicitly universally quantified):
$$\big[ \lnot \text{Parent}(x, y) \lor \text{Older}(x, y) \big] \;\land\; \text{Teacher}(C_T)$$

**Step 6 — Final CNF clause set:**
$$\Big\{ \; \lnot \text{Parent}(x, y) \lor \text{Older}(x, y), \quad \text{Teacher}(C_T) \; \Big\}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the rule structure.** The rule has the form
$$P_1(x) \land P_2(x) \Rightarrow Q(x) \quad \text{where} \quad P_1 = \text{Crow}, \; P_2 = \text{Black}, \; Q = \text{Flies}.$$

**Step 2 — Compute unifiers between rule premises and KB facts.**

Unify $\text{Crow}(x)$ with $\text{Crow}(\text{Ravi})$:
$$\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Ravi}\}.$$

Unify $\text{Black}(x)$ under $\theta_1$ with $\text{Black}(\text{Ravi})$:
$$\theta_2 = \theta_1 \circ \{x/\text{Ravi}\} = \{x/\text{Ravi}\} \quad (\text{already consistent}).$$

**Step 3 — Final MGU = $\{x/\text{Ravi}\}$.**

**Step 4 — Apply GMP with this MGU to the consequent $\text{Flies}(x)$:**
$$\text{Flies}(x) \cdot \theta = \text{Flies}(\text{Ravi}).$$

**Step 5 — Formal GMP application:**
$$\dfrac{\text{Crow}(\text{Ravi}), \quad \text{Black}(\text{Ravi}), \quad \big[\text{Crow}(x) \land \text{Black}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Flies}(x)\big]}{\text{Flies}(\text{Ravi})} \quad \theta = \{x/\text{Ravi}\}.$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** The KB now contains the new derived fact $\text{Flies}(\text{Ravi})$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Given the propositional KB
$$KB = \{ P \lor Q, \; \lnot P \lor R, \; \lnot P \lor S, \; \lnot R, \; \lnot S \}$$
use **resolution refutation** to prove that $KB \models P$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Compare **Forward Chaining** and **Backward Chaining** in First-Order Logic. Mention two advantages and one limitation of each.

---

#### **Model Solution B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Negate the goal.** Add $\lnot P$ to the KB.

**Step 2 — CNF clause set:**
$$C_1: P \lor Q$$
$$C_2: \lnot P \lor R$$
$$C_3: \lnot P \lor S$$
$$C_4: \lnot R$$
$$C_5: \lnot S$$
$$C_6: \lnot P \quad \text{(negated goal)}$$

**Step 3 — Resolve $C_2$ with $C_4$ on $R$ and $\lnot R$:**
$$C_7: \text{RESOLVE}(C_2, C_4) = \lnot P.$$

**Step 4 — Resolve $C_3$ with $C_5$ on $S$ and $\lnot S$:**
$$C_8: \text{RESOLVE}(C_3, C_5) = \lnot P.$$

**Step 5 — Resolve $C_1$ with $C_6$ on $P$ and $\lnot P$:**
$$C_9: \text{RESOLVE}(C_1, C_6) = Q.$$

**Step 6 — Resolve $C_9$ with $C_7$ on $P$ (note: $Q$ does not directly contradict $\lnot P$; we must look for complementary pairs).**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often pair $Q$ with $\lnot P$ — but they are *not complementary literals*. Complementary means *same predicate, opposite sign*. Here we must re-resolve using $C_6$ and $C_7$ or $C_8$.

**Correct continuation — Step 6 (revised):** Resolve $C_7$ ($\lnot P$) with $C_1$ ($P \lor Q$) on $P, \lnot P$:
$$C_{10}: Q.$$

Resolve $C_8$ ($\lnot P$) with $C_1$ again:
$$C_{11}: Q.$$

**Better approach — resolve the negated goal $\lnot P$ with $C_1$ directly:**
$$C_{10}: \text{RESOLVE}(C_1, C_6) = Q.$$

But we have no $\lnot Q$ in the KB, so we cannot close. We need a stronger route.

**Correct closure route:** Resolve $C_1$ with $C_7$ (which is a *new* $\lnot P$ derived):
$$C_{10}: \text{RESOLVE}(C_1, C_7) = (P \lor Q) \;\oplus\; \lnot P = Q.$$

Hmm — we still do not have $\lnot Q$. Let us re-examine: we need to derive $P$ *directly* from $\lnot P$.

**Correct closure:** Resolve $C_6$ ($\lnot P$) with $C_2$ ($\lnot P \lor R$) — no complementary pair (both negative on $P$). Resolve $C_6$ with $C_3$ — no. The closure requires resolving $\lnot P$ with a clause containing $P$. Only $C_1$ has $P$. So we get $Q$, and we need $\lnot Q$ to close — but it is not in the KB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Recheck the problem.** With $KB$ as given, $KB \models P$ does **not** actually hold, because $P \lor Q$ allows $Q$ true and $P$ false. **We must reject the question as stated** OR assume the KB is $\{ P \lor Q, \; \lnot P \lor R, \; \lnot P \lor S, \; \lnot R, \; \lnot S, \; \lnot Q \}$ — a typical KTU question.

**Corrected KB for closure:**
$$KB = \{ P \lor Q, \; \lnot P \lor R, \; \lnot P \lor S, \; \lnot R, \; \lnot S, \; \lnot Q \}$$
**Negated goal:** add $P$.

- $C_1: P \lor Q$, $C_2: \lnot P \lor R$, $C_3: \lnot P \lor S$, $C_4: \lnot R$, $C_5: \lnot S$, $C_6: \lnot Q$, $C_7: P$ (negated goal).

Resolve $C_1$ and $C_6$: complementary pair $(Q, \lnot Q)$
$$C_8: P.$$

Resolve $C_7$ and $C_8$: complementary pair $(P, P)$ — *not complementary, both positive!* So this is not a valid resolution. We need $\lnot P$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** To prove $KB \models P$ by refutation, you negate the goal to $\lnot P$, not $P$. Always negate the goal you want to prove.

**Fully corrected refutation (goal: prove $P$):**

- $KB$ clauses: $C_1: P \lor Q$, $C_2: \lnot P \lor R$, $C_3: \lnot P \lor S$, $C_4: \lnot R$, $C_5: \lnot S$, $C_6: \lnot Q$.
- **Negate goal:** $C_7: \lnot P$.
- Resolve $C_1$ and $C_6$ on $(Q, \lnot Q)$: $C_8: P$.
- Resolve $C_7$ and $C_8$ on $(P, \lnot P)$: $C_9: \square$ (empty clause). ✓
- $KB \models P$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** The empty clause is derived; $KB \land \lnot P$ is unsatisfiable; therefore $KB \models P$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Comparison table.**

| **Criterion** | **Forward Chaining** | **Backward Chaining** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal → sub-goals) |
| Trigger | New fact added to KB | Query posed to system |
| Algorithm | Repeatedly fire rules whose premises are satisfied | Recursively decompose rule head into premises |
| Completeness | Complete **only for Horn clauses** | Complete **only for Horn clauses** |
| Typical use | Monitoring, real-time control | Query answering, Prolog |
| Efficiency | May derive many *irrelevant* facts | Focused on proving the specific goal |
| Implementation ease | Simple, fits production systems | Slightly more complex (recursive search) |
| Termination | May not terminate on cyclic KB | May loop on recursive rules |

**Forward Chaining — 2 advantages + 1 limitation:**
- *Adv 1:* Naturally suited for **real-time, event-driven** systems (e.g., sensor networks).
- *Adv 2:* All consequences of the KB are eventually derivable — useful for **monitoring** tasks.
- *Limitation:* Generates *irrelevant* facts not needed for the current query (wasted computation).

**Backward Chaining — 2 advantages + 1 limitation:**
- *Adv 1:* Focused search — only explores sub-goals relevant to the query.
- *Adv 2:* Direct support for *explanation* facilities (the proof tree is the explanation).
- *Limitation:* Cannot derive *all* facts of a cyclic KB; may re-explore the same sub-goal without memoization.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the goal in resolution refutation.** Students add the goal instead of $\lnot \text{goal}$.
> 2. **Skipping CNF conversion steps** (implication elimination, De Morgan, Skolemization). Each omitted step = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Confusing unification with pattern matching.** Unification returns a *substitution* $\theta$; pattern matching is unification in one direction.
> 4. **Missing the variable-standardization step** before GMP — variables in different rules must be renamed to be distinct.
> 5. **Incorrect Skolemization depth** — existential variables inside the scope of universals become *Skolem functions* of those universals, not constants. ($f(x)$ vs. $C$).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Propositional inference** works on **atomic Boolean symbols**; **FOL inference** works on **predicates, variables, and quantifiers**.
- The two main propositional rules are **Modus Ponens** and **Resolution**; **Resolution is refutation-complete**.
- FOL lifts these to **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** and **Generalized Resolution**, both of which use the **unifier $\theta$** computed by the **Unification algorithm**.
- **Unification** returns the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**, found by recursively matching terms and binding variables (with the *occurs-check* to prevent infinite loops).
- **CNF conversion** is the mandatory preprocessing for resolution: (i) eliminate $\Leftrightarrow$; (ii) eliminate $\Rightarrow$; (iii) push $\lnot$ inwards using De Morgan; (iv) standardize variables apart; (v) **Skolemize** existentials; (vi) drop universals; (vii) distribute $\lor$ over $\land$.
- **Forward chaining** = data-driven; **backward chaining** = goal-driven; both are **complete only for Horn clauses**.
- Propositional inference is **decidable** (SAT is decidable); full FOL inference is **undecidable** but **semi-decidable**.
- The **empty clause** $\square$ in a resolution refutation proves the goal; the search continues until either $\square$ is found (success) or no new clauses can be derived (failure / timeout).
- **Soundness** is preserved by *every* standard rule; **completeness** is reserved for **Resolution** in propositional logic and **Generalized Resolution** in FOL.
- **Real-world engines** to remember: SAT solvers (propositional), Prolog / Datalog / Clingo (FOL backward chaining), E / Vampire (FOL resolution).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unification — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution** $\theta$ (theta) that makes two first-order logic (FOL) expressions syntactically identical. The output, when it exists, is called the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** — the *least committing* substitution that still aligns the two expressions.

Formally, a substitution $\theta$ **unifies** two logical expressions $p$ and $q$ if and only if:
$$p\theta = q\theta$$

where $p\theta$ denotes the result of applying substitution $\theta$ to expression $p$.

A substitution is a mapping from variables to terms, written as:
$$\theta = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \dots, x_n/t_n\}$$

The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the unification solution that imposes the fewest constraints, leaving as many variables ungrounded as possible. If $s$ is an MGU of $p$ and $q$, then any other unifier $\theta'$ can be expressed as $s$ followed by some additional substitution: $\theta' = s \circ \phi$.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Puzzle Fit" Analogy:** Think of unification as fitting two jigsaw puzzle pieces together. Each piece has slots (variables) and pegs (constants/functions). You keep adjusting the slots until the *shapes* match perfectly. The MGU is the *minimal* set of slot adjustments — you don't want to lock in pegs unnecessarily, because that would over-constrain the puzzle and break its reuse elsewhere.

A real-world analogy: consider two job descriptions:
- Description A: "Hire a **developer** who knows **Python**"
- Description B: "Hire a **programmer** who knows **Java**"

To unify them, we map `developer → programmer` and `Python → Java`. The unifier is `{x/programmer, y/Java}`. Now both expressions read: "Hire a programmer who knows Java."

---

## 1.2 Lifting — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lifting** is the technique of taking an inference rule that works at the **propositional level** and promoting (lifting) it to operate over **first-order logic** with quantified variables, by incorporating unification as the variable-matching engine.

The classic example is **Lifting Modus Ponens** to obtain the **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)**:

**Propositional Modus Ponens:**
$$P, \quad P \Rightarrow Q \quad \vdash \quad Q$$

**Lifted (Generalized) Modus Ponens:**
$$\frac{p_1', \; p_2', \; \dots, \; p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{\theta = \text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i'), \text{ then conclude } q\theta}$$

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Template Stamp" Analogy:** A propositional rule is like a *concrete rubber stamp* that prints one fixed phrase. A lifted rule is a *rubber stamp with adjustable sliders* — the sliders are positioned automatically by unification, allowing the same stamp to print infinitely many specific sentences. **Lifting = making rules parametric; Unification = setting the parameters.**

---

## 1.3 Forward Chaining — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Chaining** is a **data-driven** (bottom-up) inference algorithm used with definite-clause knowledge bases. It starts from the **known facts** in the KB and repeatedly applies rules whose premises (antecedents) are satisfied, deriving new facts until either the query is proven or no new facts emerge (fixed point reached).

It is the logical dual of **backward chaining** (goal-driven, top-down). Forward chaining is sound and complete for definite-clause KBs.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Domino Chain" Analogy:** Imagine a row of dominos. You push the first one (the known fact). Each domino that falls (each rule whose conditions are met) knocks over the next. Forward chaining is exactly this — facts cause rules to fire, and the *conclusions of those rules become new facts that can trigger other rules*. The chain keeps propagating until the goal domino falls (query proven) or all reachable dominos are toppled (fixed point).

A practical analogy: in a medical diagnosis expert system, if the system knows the patient has *fever*, *cough*, and *loss of smell*, forward chaining will trigger rules like:
- (Fever ∧ Cough ∧ LossOfSmell) ⇒ Suspect(COVID-19)
- Suspect(COVID-19) ⇒ Recommend(RT-PCR-Test)

… automatically, without the user asking a question first.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unification Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unification algorithm operates recursively on the *structure* of the two expressions being unified. It applies the rules in a fixed priority order until either a substitution is found or failure is detected.

**UNIFY$(\alpha, \beta, \theta)$** — pseudocode logic:

| Step | Condition | Action |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | $\theta$ = failure | Return failure (propagate) |
| 2 | $\alpha = \beta$ (identical) | Return $\theta$ unchanged |
| 3 | $\alpha$ is a variable $v$ | Call UNIFY-VAR$(v, \beta, \theta)$ |
| 4 | $\beta$ is a variable $v$ | Call UNIFY-VAR$(v, \alpha, \theta)$ |
| 5 | Both are compound with same predicate/op | Unify each argument pair, threading $\theta$ |
| 6 | Both are lists | Unify first elements, then recursively unify the rest |
| 7 | Otherwise | Return failure |

**UNIFY-VAR$(v, x, \theta)$** — the variable-handling sub-routine:

1. If $\{v/val\} \in \theta$: recursively UNIFY$(val, x, \theta)$ (use existing binding)
2. Else if $\{x/val\} \in \theta$: recursively UNIFY$(v, val, \theta)$ (use existing binding of $x$)
3. **Occur-Check:** If $v$ occurs anywhere inside $x$, return **failure** (prevents infinite terms like $X = f(X)$)
4. Otherwise, add $\{v/x\}$ to $\theta$ and return updated $\theta$

> [!NOTE]
> **Occur-Check — Why it matters:** Without the occur-check, we could produce nonsensical substitutions like $\{X/f(X)\}$, which when applied creates an infinite regress $f(f(f(\dots)))$. Most efficient Prolog systems actually *omit* the occur-check for speed (soundness sacrifice).

---

## 2.2 Worked Examples of Unification

### Example A: Simple Unification
Unify $P(x, f(y))$ and $P(a, f(b))$.

$$\theta_1 = \{x/a\} \quad \text{(from first arg)}$$

Apply $\theta_1$ to second expression: $P(a, f(b))$.
Unify $f(y)$ and $f(b)$: $\theta_2 = \{y/b\}$.

Final unifier: $\theta = \{x/a, y/b\}$.

### Example B: Failure Due to Function Symbol Clash
Unify $P(x, f(x))$ and $P(a, f(b))$.

Unify $x$ and $a$: $\theta_1 = \{x/a\}$.
Apply $\theta_1$: unify $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ → unify $a$ and $b$ → **failure** (distinct constants).

Result: **No unifier exists.**

### Example C: Occur-Check Failure
Unify $P(x, f(x))$ and $P(a, f(b))$.

After $\theta_1 = \{x/a\}$, we need to unify $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, which fails as above.

### Example D: Nested Compound Expression
Unify $\text{Knows}(\text{John}, x)$ and $\text{Knows}(y, \text{Jane})$.

Unify first args: $\text{John} = y \Rightarrow \theta_1 = \{y/\text{John}\}$.
Apply: unify second args: $x$ and $\text{Jane} \Rightarrow \theta_2 = \{x/\text{Jane}\}$.

Final: $\theta = \{y/\text{John}, x/\text{Jane}\}$.

---

## 2.3 The Generalized Modus Ponens (Lifted Inference)

> [!NOTE]
> **GMP** is the lifted version of Modus Ponens. Given atomic sentences $p_1', p_2', \dots, p_n'$ and an implication $(p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)$, GMP finds the most general unifier $\theta$ such that $\text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i')$ succeeds for all $i$. The conclusion is $q\theta$.

**Formal statement:**
$$\frac{p_1', \; p_2', \; \dots, \; p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{q\theta} \quad \text{where } \theta = \text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i') \text{ for all } i$$

**Example application:**
- Premise facts: $\text{American}(\text{West})$, $\text{Weapon}(\text{M1})$, $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, \text{M1}, \text{Nono})$
- Rule: $\text{American}(x) \land \text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(x, y, z) \Rightarrow \text{Hostile}(z)$
- $\theta = \{x/\text{West}, y/\text{M1}, z/\text{Nono}\}$
- **Conclusion:** $\text{Hostile}(\text{Nono})$

---

## 2.4 Forward Chaining Algorithm — Detailed Logic

**FORWARD-CHAINING(KB, q)** — determines if a query $q$ is entailed:

1. Initialize the **count** of each rule's premises (how many are still unsatisfied)
2. Maintain a working list of **known facts** (initially the facts in the KB)
3. Repeat until query is proven or no new facts are derivable:
   - For each rule $R$ whose premise count is 0 (all antecedents satisfied):
     - Compute MGU $\theta$ that unifies the antecedents with known facts
     - Apply $\theta$ to the consequent to obtain a new fact
     - If the new fact is the query → return success
     - Else, add the new fact to the known-facts set
   - **Increment-safe propagation:** When a new fact is added, decrement the premise count of any rule that contains that fact
4. If no new facts and query not proven → return **failure**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key invariant:** A rule is *triggered* (allowed to fire) **only when all its premises have been unified with known facts**. This avoids redundant partial work.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Key Condition |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| Substitution $\theta$ | $\theta = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \dots, x_n/t_n\}$ | Each $t_i$ is a term (var, const, or function) |
| Unification | $p\theta = q\theta$ | Both sides must be syntactically identical |
| Composition $\theta \circ \sigma$ | Apply $\sigma$ first, then $\theta$ | $\text{SUBST}(\theta, \text{SUBST}(\sigma, p))$ |
| MGU Property | If $s$ is MGU, then any other unifier $\theta = s \circ \phi$ for some $\phi$ | $s$ is *least committing* |
| Occur-Check | $X$ cannot be unified with $f(\dots, X, \dots)$ | Prevents infinite terms |
| Lifted Modus Ponens | $q\theta$ where $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i')$ | All $p_i$ must unify simultaneously |
| Forward Chaining | Starts from facts, applies rules bottom-up | Complete for definite-clause KBs |
| Fixed Point | Reached when no new facts derivable | Algorithm halts with success or failure |
| Complexity (naive) | $O(p \cdot n)$ where $p$ = premises, $n$ = facts | Optimization via index structures |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Expert Systems** | Medical diagnosis, fault detection in industrial systems |
| **Semantic Web / RDF** | OWL reasoners (e.g., HermiT, Pellet) use forward chaining for ABox entailment |
| **Database Systems** | Recursive query processing (Datalog engines like LogicBlox) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion detection rules in SIEM systems (e.g., Splunk, ArcSight) |
| **Smart Manufacturing** | IoT rule engines for predictive maintenance |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Semantic role labeling, information extraction pipelines |
| **Automated Theorem Proving** | Resolution-based provers (Vampire, E-prover) extend these ideas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Forward chaining is the workhorse of **production rule systems** because it is *trigger-based* — perfect for real-time streaming event data. The cost is that many irrelevant conclusions may be derived, which is why *indexing* of rules and *retraction mechanisms* (truth maintenance) are critical in deployed systems.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Unification Walkthrough — The Crime Knowledge Base

We will unify complex first-order expressions step-by-step to demonstrate the algorithm.

### Problem: Unify $\text{Knows}(\text{John}, x)$ with $\text{Knows}(y, \text{Mary})$

**Initial state:** $\theta_0 = \{\}$ (empty substitution)

**Step 1:** Apply rule 5 (compound expressions with same predicate $\text{Knows}$). Recurse into the argument lists.

$$\text{UNIFY}(\text{John}, \; y, \; \text{UNIFY}(\text{Knows}, \text{Knows}, \theta_0))$$

**Step 2:** The predicates $\text{Knows}$ and $\text{Knows}$ are identical. Return $\theta_0$ unchanged. Now:

$$\text{UNIFY}(\text{John}, y, \{\})$$

**Step 3:** $\text{John}$ is not a variable. $y$ is a variable. Apply rule 4 → call UNIFY-VAR$(y, \text{John}, \{\})$.

**Step 4:** Inside UNIFY-VAR$(y, \text{John}, \{\})$:
- $y$ has no existing binding in $\{\}$
- $\text{John}$ has no existing binding in $\{\}$
- **Occur-check:** Does $y$ occur in $\text{John}$? No (constants are atomic).
- Add $\{y/\text{John}\}$ to $\theta$.

$$\theta_1 = \{y/\text{John}\}$$

**Step 5:** Return to the original recursion. Now unify the second arguments: $x$ and $\text{Mary}$ with $\theta_1$.

$$\text{UNIFY}(x, \text{Mary}, \{y/\text{John}\})$$

**Step 6:** $x$ is a variable, $\text{Mary}$ is a constant. Apply rule 3 → call UNIFY-VAR$(x, \text{Mary}, \{y/\text{John}\})$.

**Step 7:** Inside UNIFY-VAR$(x, \text{Mary}, \{y/\text{John}\})$:
- $x$ has no existing binding
- $\text{Mary}$ has no existing binding
- Occur-check passes
- Add $\{x/\text{Mary}\}$ to $\theta$

$$\theta_2 = \{y/\text{John}, \; x/\text{Mary}\}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\theta = \{y/\text{John}, \; x/\text{Mary}\}}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Unification Walkthrough — Nested Structure with Recursion

### Problem: Unify $P(f(x, g(y)), z)$ with $P(f(h(a), g(b)), c)$

**Step 1:** Predicates $P$ match. Unify argument lists recursively.

$$\text{UNIFY}(f(x, g(y)), \; f(h(a), g(b)), \; \{\})$$

**Step 2:** Function symbols $f$ match. Unify their arguments pairwise.

$$\text{UNIFY}(x, h(a), \; \text{UNIFY}(g(y), g(b), \{\}))$$

**Step 3:** Unify $g(y)$ and $g(b)$:
- Function symbols $g$ match
- Unify arguments: $y$ and $b$

$$\theta_1 = \{y/b\}$$

**Step 4:** Now unify $x$ and $h(a)$ with $\theta_1$:
- $x$ is a variable, $h(a)$ is a compound term
- Occur-check: does $x$ occur in $h(a)$? No
- Add binding

$$\theta_2 = \{y/b, \; x/h(a)\}$$

**Step 5:** Now unify $z$ and $c$ with $\theta_2$:
- $z$ is a variable, $c$ is a constant
- Add binding

$$\boxed{\theta = \{y/b, \; x/h(a), \; z/c\}}$$

**Verification:** Apply $\theta$ to both sides:
- LHS: $P(f(h(a), g(b)), c)$ ✓
- RHS: $P(f(h(a), g(b)), c)$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Detailed Unification Walkthrough — Failure Case

### Problem: Unify $Q(x, f(x))$ with $Q(a, f(b))$

**Step 1:** Predicates $Q$ match. Unify argument lists.

$$\text{UNIFY}(x, a, \; \text{UNIFY}(f(x), f(b), \{\}))$$

**Step 2:** Unify $x$ and $a$:
- $x$ is a variable
- Occur-check passes (no $x$ in $a$)
- Add binding

$$\theta_1 = \{x/a\}$$

**Step 3:** Now unify $f(x)$ and $f(b)$ with $\theta_1$ applied. Since $x \to a$:

$$\text{UNIFY}(f(a), f(b), \{x/a\})$$

**Step 4:** Function symbols $f$ match. Unify arguments:

$$\text{UNIFY}(a, b, \{x/a\})$$

**Step 5:** $a$ and $b$ are both constants. They are **not identical**. Return **failure**.

$$\boxed{\theta = \text{FAILURE}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Complexity & Properties

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Sound? | Complete? | Use Case |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|--------|-----------|----------|
| Unification | $O(n)$ per pair (n = size of terms) | $O(n)$ | Yes (with occur-check) | Yes | Variable matching |
| GMP (Lifted MP) | $O(n \cdot k)$ where k = # premises | $O(n)$ | Yes | Yes (definite clauses) | Single-step inference |
| Forward Chaining (naive) | $O(p \cdot n)$ per iteration | $O(n + r)$ | Yes | Yes | Derive all consequences |
| Backward Chaining | Exponential worst case | $O(d)$ depth | Yes | Yes | Targeted query proving |
| Rete Algorithm | $O(1)$ per fact insertion (amortized) | $O(R \cdot P)$ where P = patterns | Yes | Yes | Production systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The naive forward chaining algorithm has poor worst-case performance because it may repeatedly try to unify facts with rule premises. The **Rete algorithm** (Forgy, 1979) used in CLIPS, Jess, and Drools solves this by building a *discrimination network* that caches partial matches.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Forward Chaining is Complete for Definite Clauses

**Theorem:** Forward chaining on a finite definite-clause knowledge base will reach a **fixed point** in finite time.

**Proof sketch:**

1. A definite-clause KB contains only facts and Horn clauses of the form $A \Rightarrow B$ (single positive literal consequent).
2. There are finitely many atomic sentences that can be constructed from a finite set of predicate symbols, constants, and functions (Herbrand base is finite for function-free FOL).
3. Each application of forward chaining either:
   - Derives a *new* fact (strictly grows the fact set), or
   - Derives a *fact already known* (no growth).
4. Since the fact set is bounded by the Herbrand base, it can grow only finitely many times.
5. Therefore, the algorithm **must terminate** at a fixed point.
6. By the definition of entailment, if $q$ is entailed by the KB, then $q$ must be derivable by some chain of forward applications → **completeness**.

$$\blacksquare$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Unification Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: UNIFY p, q, theta] --> B{theta = FAILURE?}
    B -->|YES| Z[Return FAILURE]
    B -->|NO| C{p = q literally?}
    C -->|YES| Y[Return theta unchanged]
    C -->|NO| D{p is VARIABLE?}
    D -->|YES| E[Call UNIFY-VAR p, q, theta]
    D -->|NO| F{q is VARIABLE?}
    F -->|YES| G[Call UNIFY-VAR q, p, theta]
    F -->|NO| H{Both COMPOUND with same predicate?}
    H -->|YES| I[theta1 = UNIFY OP p, OP q, theta]
    I --> J[theta2 = UNIFY ARGS p, ARGS q, theta1]
    J --> K[Return theta2]
    H -->|NO| L{Both are LISTS?}
    L -->|YES| M[theta3 = UNIFY FIRST p, FIRST q, theta]
    M --> N[theta4 = UNIFY REST p, REST q, theta3]
    N --> O[Return theta4]
    L -->|NO| P[Return FAILURE]

    E --> E1{v has binding in theta?}
    E1 -->|YES| E2[Recurse: UNIFY val, q, theta]
    E1 -->|NO| E3{Occur-Check FAIL?}
    E3 -->|YES| Z
    E3 -->|NO| E4[Add v, q to theta]
    E4 --> E5[Return theta updated]

    G --> G1{q has binding in theta?}
    G1 -->|YES| G2[Recurse: UNIFY p, val, theta]
    G1 -->|NO| G3{Occur-Check FAIL?}
    G3 -->|YES| Z
    G3 -->|NO| G4[Add q, p to theta]
    G4 --> G5[Return theta updated]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Forward Chaining — Data-Driven Inference Flow

```mermaid

    INIT --> F1[Known Facts: F1, F2, F3]
```
