# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Unification
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define unification in first-order logic. What is a Most General Unifier (MGU)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions $p$ and $q$ syntactically identical, i.e., $p\theta = q\theta$. **(1 mark)**

The substitution $\theta$ is a mapping of variables to terms, e.g., $\theta = \{x/a, y/f(b)\}$. **(1 mark)**

The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the unification substitution that is *least committing* — it leaves as many variables ungrounded as possible, while still aligning the two expressions. Any other unifier can be obtained by composing the MGU with some additional substitution. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: Lifting Concept
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q:** What is meant by *lifting* in the context of inference rules? Give one example of a propositional rule and its lifted counterpart.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lifting is the technique of taking a propositional inference rule and promoting it to operate over first-order logic, using unification to match variable patterns. **(1 mark)**

Example — **Propositional Modus Ponens:** From $P$ and $P \Rightarrow Q$, infer $Q$. **(1 mark)**

**Lifted (Generalized Modus Ponens):** From $P(a)$, $P(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)$, and $\theta = \{x/a\}$, infer $Q(a)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Unification and Forward Chaining Computation

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** Unify the following pairs of first-order logic expressions. Show all substitution steps clearly. If unification fails, state the reason. **(7 marks)**

- (i) $\text{Parent}(x, \text{John})$ and $\text{Parent}(\text{Bill}, y)$ **(3 marks)**
- (ii) $\text{Loves}(x, f(x))$ and $\text{Loves}(\text{Mary}, f(\text{Sam}))$ **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Unify** $\text{Parent}(x, \text{John})$ and $\text{Parent}(\text{Bill}, y)$

- Step 1: Predicates `Parent` match. Unify arguments. **(0.5 marks)**
- Step 2: Unify $x$ with $\text{Bill}$ → $x$ is a variable, no occur-check failure → $\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Bill}\}$. **(1 mark)**
- Step 3: Apply $\theta_1$, then unify $\text{John}$ with $y$ → $y$ is a variable → $\theta_2 = \{x/\text{Bill}, y/\text{John}\}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Final MGU:** $\theta = \{x/\text{Bill}, y/\text{John}\}$. **(0.5 marks)**

**(ii) Unify** $\text{Loves}(x, f(x))$ and $\text{Loves}(\text{Mary}, f(\text{Sam}))$

- Step 1: Predicates `Loves` match. Unify arguments. **(0.5 marks)**
- Step 2: Unify $x$ with $\text{Mary}$ → $x$ is a variable → $\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Mary}\}$. **(1 mark)**
- Step 3: Apply $\theta_1$ to the second expression: $f(\text{Mary})$. Now unify $f(\text{Mary})$ with $f(\text{Sam})$. Function symbols $f$ match. **(1 mark)**
- Step 4: Unify $\text{Mary}$ with $\text{Sam}$. Both are distinct constants. **No unifier exists.** **(1 mark)**
- **Final result: FAILURE** (constants Mary and Sam do not match). **(0.5 marks)**

---

**Q (b)** Consider the following knowledge base. Use **forward chaining** to prove that $\text{Criminal}(\text{West})$ is entailed. Show all intermediate facts derived and the unifications used. **(7 marks)**

**Facts:**
1. $\text{American}(\text{West})$
2. $\text{Weapon}(\text{M1})$
3. $\text{Enemy}(\text{Nono}, \text{America})$
4. $\text{Owns}(\text{Nono}, \text{M1})$

**Rules:**
- R1: $\text{Enemy}(x, \text{America}) \Rightarrow \text{Hostile}(x)$
- R2: $\text{Missile}(x) \land \text{Owns}(\text{Nono}, x) \Rightarrow \text{Sells}(\text{West}, x, \text{Nono})$
- R3: $\text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z) \land \text{Hostile}(z) \Rightarrow \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$
- R4: $\text{Missile}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Weapon}(x)$ *(additional rule)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Iteration 1: Scan rules for applicable ones.**

- **R1:** Premise $\text{Enemy}(x, \text{America})$ unified with fact 3: $\text{Enemy}(\text{Nono}, \text{America})$.
  - $\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Nono}\}$
  - Consequent: $\text{Hostile}(\text{Nono})$
  - **New fact (NF1):** $\text{Hostile}(\text{Nono})$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Iteration 2: Re-scan rules with updated fact set.**

- **R4:** Premise $\text{Missile}(x)$ — *no matching fact* (we don't have a Missile fact).
  - R4 cannot fire.

- **R2:** Premise $\text{Missile}(x)$ — *no matching fact*. R2 cannot fire.

- **R3:** Premise requires $\text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z) \land \text{Hostile}(z)$.
  - $\text{Weapon}(y)$ unifies with fact 2: $\text{Weapon}(\text{M1})$ → $\{y/\text{M1}\}$
  - $\text{Hostile}(z)$ unifies with NF1: $\text{Hostile}(\text{Nono})$ → $\{z/\text{Nono}\}$
  - Missing premise: $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z) = \text{Sells}(\text{West}, \text{M1}, \text{Nono})$ — **NOT yet known**.

- No new rule fires in iteration 2 with just the current facts. **(1 mark)**

**Wait — we need Missile.** The KB as given does not directly state `Missile(M1)`. Let us assume R4 was mis-stated and the actual KB includes a fact `Missile(M1)`. With that assumption:

**Revised Iteration 2 (assuming fact 5: Missile(M1)):**

- **R4:** $\text{Missile}(x)$ unified with $\text{Missile}(\text{M1})$ → $\theta = \{x/\text{M1}\}$. Consequent: $\text{Weapon}(\text{M1})$. Already known. Skip.

- **R2:** $\text{Missile}(x) \land \text{Owns}(\text{Nono}, x)$ unified with $\text{Missile}(\text{M1})$ and $\text{Owns}(\text{Nono}, \text{M1})$ → $\theta = \{x/\text{M1}\}$. **(1.5 marks)**
  - Consequent: $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, \text{M1}, \text{Nono})$
  - **New fact (NF2):** $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, \text{M1}, \text{Nono})$ **(1 mark)**

**Iteration 3: With NF2 and NF1, re-evaluate R3.**

- **R3:** $\text{Weapon}(y) \land \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z) \land \text{Hostile}(z)$:
  - $\text{Weapon}(y)$ with $\text{Weapon}(\text{M1})$ → $\{y/\text{M1}\}$
  - $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z)$ with $\text{Sells}(\text{West}, \text{M1}, \text{Nono})$ → $\{y/\text{M1}, z/\text{Nono}\}$
  - $\text{Hostile}(z)$ with $\text{Hostile}(\text{Nono})$ → consistent
  - $\theta = \{y/\text{M1}, z/\text{Nono}\}$
  - Consequent: $\text{Criminal}(\text{West})$
  - **Query proven!** **(2 marks)**

**Fixed point reached. Final entailment:** $\text{KB} \models \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Lifting and Generalized Modus Ponens

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** Explain the *Lifting Modus Ponens* rule. Apply it to derive the conclusion from the following: **(7 marks)**

- Premise 1: $\text{Parent}(x, \text{John}), \text{Parent}(x, y) \Rightarrow \text{Ancestor}(x, y)$
- Premise 2 (fact): $\text{Parent}(\text{Alice}, \text{John})$

State the substitution $\theta$ and the derived conclusion.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Lifting Modus Ponens (Generalized Modus Ponens):** Given atomic premises $p_1', p_2', \dots, p_n'$ and a rule $p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \Rightarrow q$, find an MGU $\theta$ such that UNIFY$(p_i, p_i')$ succeeds for all $i$. The conclusion is $q\theta$. **(2 marks)**

**Application:**

- $p_1 = \text{Parent}(x, \text{John})$
- $p_1' = \text{Parent}(\text{Alice}, \text{John})$
- UNIFY: $x$ unifies with $\text{Alice}$ → $\theta_1 = \{x/\text{Alice}\}$ **(1.5 marks)**
- Apply $\theta_1$ to $q = \text{Ancestor}(x, y)$:
  - $q\theta_1 = \text{Ancestor}(\text{Alice}, y)$ **(1 mark)**

**However**, the rule has another premise $\text{Parent}(x, y)$ that must also be unified with a known fact. We need a fact matching $\text{Parent}(\text{Alice}, y)$.

- If the KB also contains $\text{Parent}(\text{Alice}, \text{Bob})$:
  - UNIFY $\text{Parent}(x, y)$ with $\text{Parent}(\text{Alice}, \text{Bob})$ → $\theta_2 = \{x/\text{Alice}, y/\text{Bob}\}$
  - **Combined MGU:** $\theta = \{x/\text{Alice}, y/\text{Bob}\}$ **(1.5 marks)**
  - **Conclusion:** $\text{Ancestor}(\text{Alice}, \text{Bob})$ **(1 mark)**

---

**Q (b)** Explain the **forward chaining algorithm** with a suitable block diagram. Discuss its **soundness, completeness, and complexity** characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Forward Chaining Algorithm — Stepwise Operation:** **(2 marks)**

1. Initialize known-facts set $K$ with all ground facts in the KB.
2. For each rule $R_i$ in the rule base:
   - Count the number of premises that are *not yet* matched against $K$.
3. Loop:
   - Pick a rule whose premise count = 0.
   - Compute the MGU $\theta$ between the rule's antecedents and known facts.
   - Apply $\theta$ to the consequent to derive a new fact $F$.
   - If $F$ = query → **succeed**.
   - Else, add $F$ to $K$ and update premise counts of all rules.
4. Terminate when $K$ stops growing (fixed point) → **fail** if query not proven.

**Block Diagram (text representation):** **(2 marks)**

```
[Knowledge Base] --> [Initialize Working Memory of Facts]
        |
        v
[Match Rule Premises against Facts via Unification]
        |
        v
[Activate Rules with All Premises Satisfied --> Agenda]
        |
        v
[Conflict Resolution --> Pick One Rule]
        |
        v
[Apply MGU to Consequent --> New Fact]
        |
        v
[Add to Working Memory / Check Query] --> Loop
```

**Properties:** **(3 marks)**

- **Soundness:** Every fact derived by forward chaining is logically entailed by the KB. ✓
- **Completeness:** If a query is entailed, forward chaining will derive it (for definite-clause KBs). ✓
- **Complexity:** Naive implementation is $O(R \cdot P \cdot N)$ where $R$ = rules, $P$ = premises per rule, $N$ = facts. Optimized via the **Rete algorithm** (Forgy 1979) which achieves $O(1)$ amortized per fact insertion.
- **Termination:** Guaranteed for finite, function-free definite-clause KBs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the occur-check:** Students often unify $X$ with $f(X)$ and produce nonsensical infinite terms. *Always* explicitly check for variable occurrence in the term.
> 2. **Confusing MGU with any unifier:** The MGU is the *most general* — if a more specific unifier is given (e.g., $\{x/\text{John}, y/\text{Bill}\}$ when $\{x/\text{John}\}$ suffices), partial credit is lost.
> 3. **Order of application in forward chaining:** Students often apply rules in *textual* order rather than *iterative* order. Mark scheme requires explicit iteration boundaries.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply substitution to the consequent:** When computing the MGU, students find $\theta$ correctly but then forget to apply it to $q$ before concluding. This is a **2-mark deduction** in typical KTU valuation.
> 5. **Mixing propositional and FOL rules:** Forward chaining is defined for *first-order definite clauses*. Applying it naively to propositional KBs without noting this distinction loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Unification, Lifting, Forward Chaining)**

- [ ] **Unification** finds substitution $\theta$ such that $p\theta = q\theta$. The **MGU** is the least committing unifier.
- [ ] **UNIFY algorithm** is recursive, processes compound terms depth-first, applies predicates matching before arguments.
- [ ] **UNIFY-VAR** must perform the **occur-check** to prevent infinite terms like $X = f(X)$.
- [ ] **Lifting Modus Ponens (GMP)** = propositional Modus Ponens + unification to handle quantified variables.
- [ ] **Forward Chaining** is data-driven (bottom-up); derives new facts from rules whose premises are satisfied.
- [ ] **Forward Chaining is sound and complete** for definite-clause (Horn) KBs.
- [ ] **Fixed point** is reached when no new facts can be derived — algorithm terminates.
- [ ] **Rete algorithm** is the production-grade optimization for forward chaining (used in CLIPS, Jess, Drools).
- [ ] **Complexities to remember:** Unification $O(n)$, naive forward chaining $O(RPN)$, Rete $O(1)$ amortized.
- [ ] **Application domains:** Expert systems, semantic web (OWL), Datalog, SIEM, IoT rule engines.
- [ ] **Common exam trap:** Always show *intermediate substitution composition* — e.g., $\theta_1 \circ \theta_2$ explicitly.
- [ ] **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $p\theta = q\theta$ (unification condition)
  - $\theta = \{x_1/t_1, \dots, x_n/t_n\}$ (substitution form)
  - GMP conclusion: $q\theta$ where $\theta = \text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i')$ for all $i$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backward Chaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backward Chaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Backward Chaining** is a *goal-driven* (or *data-sparse*) inference strategy used in knowledge-based systems, expert systems, and logic programming languages such as **PROLOG**. The reasoning engine starts from a hypothesized conclusion (the **goal**) and works *backwards* through the knowledge base, recursively decomposing the goal into sub-goals until the sub-goals match known facts in the working memory.

Formally, if a rule is of the form:

$$ \text{IF } P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \text{ THEN } Q $$

Forward chaining fires the rule when $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ are known and concludes $Q$.
**Backward chaining, in contrast, starts with the query $Q$ (the goal) and attempts to prove each antecedent $P_i$ recursively, terminating only when every sub-goal is unified with a verifiable fact.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Backward chaining is classified under *Inference Mechanisms* in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. Students must clearly distinguish it from **Forward Chaining** and understand its role in **PROLOG**, **Expert Systems (e.g., MYCIN, CLIPS)**, and **Definite Clause Logic**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective" Approach

Imagine a detective at a crime scene. Instead of randomly examining every piece of evidence (forward chaining), the detective begins with a **suspect** (the goal/hypothesis) and asks:

> *"What would it take to prove this suspect guilty?"*

- To prove guilt, witness testimony is required → sub-goal #1
- To obtain testimony, locate the witness → sub-goal #2
- To locate the witness, check the registry → factual check

The detective chains **backwards** from hypothesis to evidence. Each step is a *rule*; each terminal check is a *fact*. This is exactly how backward chaining operates inside an inference engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - **Forward Chaining** = *"Given what I know, what can I conclude?"* → Reasoning from data to conclusion.
> - **Backward Chaining** = *"To prove this conclusion, what must I verify?"* → Reasoning from conclusion to data.

---

## 1.3 Salient Features of Backward Chaining

- **Goal-Directed:** Begins execution from a user-supplied query/goal.
- **Depth-First Search (DFS):** The inference engine traverses the rule tree in a depth-first manner along the leftmost branch first.
- **Efficient for Small Knowledge Bases:** Avoids firing irrelevant rules, saving computational cost.
- **Uses Modus Tollens (Reverse Modus Ponens):** Validates antecedents to support consequents.
- **Expressive in PROLOG:** PROLOG's built-in resolution engine is essentially an optimized backward chaining mechanism.

| Property | Backward Chaining | Forward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Conclusion → Premises | Premises → Conclusion |
| **Control** | Goal-driven | Data-driven |
| **Search Strategy** | Depth-First (DFS) | Breadth-First (BFS) |
| **Used In** | PROLOG, MYCIN, DENDRAL | CLIPS, Production Systems |
| **Best For** | Diagnostic / Question-Answering | Monitoring / Control Systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The backward chaining algorithm can be broken down into a structured pipeline:

1. **Goal Insertion:** Place the queried conclusion $G$ on top of the goal stack.
2. **Rule Matching:** Search the knowledge base for any rule whose head (consequent) unifies with the current goal $G$. A rule of the form $P_1 \land P_2 \rightarrow G$ is selected.
3. **Sub-Goal Generation:** Push all antecedents ($P_1, P_2, \dots$) onto the goal stack in reverse order (so $P_1$ is on top).
4. **Fact Verification:** Pop the top sub-goal. If it matches a known fact in the working memory, mark it as **PROVED** and remove it.
5. **Recursive Decomposition:** If the popped sub-goal is *not* a known fact, recursively apply steps 2–4 to it.
6. **Backtracking:** If a sub-goal cannot be proved under the current rule choice, **backtrack** and try a different rule whose head unifies with the goal.
7. **Termination:** The original goal $G$ is **PROVED** only when *all* sub-goals in its dependency tree are resolved as facts. Otherwise, $G$ is reported as **FAILED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Algorithm:** Backward chaining minimizes redundant rule firings by ignoring any rule whose consequent does not contribute to the current goal. This is the *logical opposite* of forward chaining, which fires *every* rule whose premises are satisfied.

---

## 2.2 Logical Foundations — Modus Tollens & Definite Clauses

The logical validity of backward chaining rests on the **deduction theorem** and the use of **Horn Clauses** (rules with at most one positive literal).

A definite clause is written as:

$$ A \leftarrow B_1 \land B_2 \land \dots \land B_n $$

where $A$ is the head (goal) and $B_i$ are the body (sub-goals).

To prove $A$, the engine must prove every $B_i$. This is the **recursive definition of logical entailment**:

$$ KB \models A \iff \text{for every rule } (A \leftarrow B_1 \land \dots \land B_n) \in KB,\ KB \models B_1 \land \dots \land B_n $$

The reverse modus ponens inference rule is:

$$ \frac{A \leftarrow B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n \quad\quad B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n}{A} $$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Element** | **Symbol / Form** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Definite Clause | $A \leftarrow B_1 \land B_2$ | "To prove $A$, prove $B_1$ and $B_2$" |
| Fact | $A.$ | "A is unconditionally true" |
| Goal | $?- A$ | PROLOG query asking "Is A provable?" |
| Unification | $\theta = \text{mgu}(A, B)$ | Most General Unifier of $A$ and $B$ |
| Inference Rule | $\frac{A \leftarrow B,\ B}{A}$ | Reverse Modus Ponens |
| Search Strategy | DFS with Backtracking | PROLOG's default strategy |
| Time Complexity | $O(b^d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of proof tree |
| Space Complexity | $O(d)$ | Linear in proof depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Backward chaining underpins real-time **medical diagnosis systems** (e.g., asking the patient a minimal set of symptoms to confirm a disease hypothesis) and **automated theorem provers** used in formal hardware verification at companies like Intel and AMD.

---

## 2.4 Worked Trace — The Classic "Animal Identification" Knowledge Base

Consider the following rule set:

| Rule | Form |
|---|---|
| R1 | `mammal(X) :- has_hair(X).` |
| R2 | `mammal(X) :- gives_milk(X).` |
| R3 | `bird(X) :- has_feathers(X).` |
| R4 | `canary(X) :- bird(X), lays_eggs(X), has_eggs(X).` |
| R5 | `ostrich(X) :- bird(X), long_neck(X).` |
| R6 | `flies(X) :- canary(X).` |
| R7 | `flies(X) :- ostrich(X).` |
| Facts | `lays_eggs(canary). has_eggs(canary). long_neck(ostrich).` |
| Query | `?- flies(canary).` |

**Trace (DFS with backtracking):**

1. Goal stack: `[flies(canary)]`
2. Match `flies(canary)` with R6 head → push sub-goals `canary(canary)`, then `lays_eggs(canary)`, then `has_eggs(canary)`.
3. Verify `lays_eggs(canary)` against facts → **TRUE**.
4. Verify `has_eggs(canary)` against facts → **TRUE**.
5. Prove `canary(canary)` via R4 → requires `bird(canary)` → via R3 → requires `has_feathers(canary)` → **NOT IN FACTS** → backtrack.
6. Try alternative rule for `canary` — none exists.
7. Backtrack further and try R7: `flies(X) :- ostrich(X).` — fails since `canary ≠ ostrich`.
8. Original goal `flies(canary)` → **FAILED**.

This trace demonstrates **depth-first backtracking**, the defining behaviour of PROLOG-style backward chaining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Medical Diagnosis (KTU Classic)

**Knowledge Base:**

```
R1: fever(X) :- infection(X).
R2: infection(X) :- cough(X), fatigue(X).
R3: pneumonia(X) :- infection(X), chest_pain(X).
R4: typhoid(X) :- fever(X), abdominal_pain(X).
Fact 1: cough(john).
Fact 2: fatigue(john).
Fact 3: chest_pain(john).
```

**Query:** `?- pneumonia(john).`

### Step-by-Step Proof Tree Construction

**Step 1 — Goal Insertion.**
Place the top-level query on the goal stack:
$$ G_0 = \text{pneumonia(john)} $$

**Step 2 — Rule Matching for $G_0$.**
Scan the knowledge base for any rule whose head unifies with `pneumonia(john)`. Only R3 qualifies:
$$ \text{pneumonia(X)} \leftarrow \text{infection(X)} \land \text{chest\_pain(X)} $$
With unifier $\theta_1 = \{X / \text{john}\}$.

**Step 3 — Sub-Goal Generation.**
Push the body of R3 onto the stack (in order):
$$ G_1 = \text{infection(john)},\quad G_2 = \text{chest\_pain(john)} $$

**Step 4 — Verify $G_2$ (chest_pain(john)).**
Direct match with Fact 3.
$$ \text{chest\_pain(john)} \rightarrow \text{TRUE} \quad \blacksquare $$

**Step 5 — Verify $G_1$ (infection(john)).**
No direct fact. Match with R2:
$$ \text{infection(X)} \leftarrow \text{cough(X)} \land \text{fatigue(X)} $$
With unifier $\theta_2 = \{X / \text{john}\}$.

**Step 6 — Sub-Goals from R2:**
$$ G_3 = \text{cough(john)},\quad G_4 = \text{fatigue(john)} $$

**Step 7 — Verify $G_3$.**
Match with Fact 1.
$$ \text{cough(john)} \rightarrow \text{TRUE} \quad \blacksquare $$

**Step 8 — Verify $G_4$.**
Match with Fact 2.
$$ \text{fatigue(john)} \rightarrow \text{TRUE} \quad \blacksquare $$

**Step 9 — Back-Propagation of Truth Values.**
Since all leaves of the proof tree are TRUE, every internal node becomes TRUE.

**Step 10 — Final Conclusion.**
$$ KB \models \text{pneumonia(john)} \rightarrow \textbf{TRUE} \quad \blacksquare $$

### Proof Tree (ASCII)

```
?- pneumonia(john)
        |
   [R3] matches
        |
   infection(john)   chest_pain(john)  [Fact 3] ✓
        |
   [R2] matches
        |
   cough(john) [Fact 1] ✓    fatigue(john) [Fact 2] ✓
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Incompleteness via Backward Chaining

This example demonstrates a **failure trace** (essential for KTU problems).

**Knowledge Base:**

```
R1: bird(tweety).
R2: flies(X) :- bird(X), not_penguin(X).
Fact 1: bird(tweety).
```

**Query:** `?- flies(tweety).`

**Step 1:** Goal = `flies(tweety)`.
**Step 2:** Match with R2 head.
**Step 3:** Sub-goals: `bird(tweety)` and `not_penguin(tweety)`.
**Step 4:** `bird(tweety)` → matches Fact 1 → TRUE.
**Step 5:** `not_penguin(tweety)` → no rule defines this predicate and no fact states it.
**Step 6:** System performs **Closed-World Assumption (CWA)** → assumes `not_penguin(tweety)` is TRUE by negation as failure.
**Step 7:** Final result:
$$ KB \models \text{flies(tweety)} \rightarrow \textbf{TRUE} \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The *Closed-World Assumption* is implicit in PROLOG. Anything not provable is assumed false. This is the philosophical cornerstone of backward chaining in logic programming.

---

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation

For a knowledge base with $r$ rules and a proof tree of depth $d$ and branching factor $b$:

$$ T(n) = b \cdot T(n-1) + O(1) $$

Solving this recurrence using the **Master Theorem** (Case 2: linear recursion with constant work):

$$ T(n) = O(b^d) $$

Space complexity is dominated by the recursion stack, which holds at most $d$ goals simultaneously:

$$ S(n) = O(d) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The exponential time complexity $O(b^d)$ is why **cycle detection** and **iterative deepening (IDDFS)** are sometimes layered on top of naive backward chaining to prevent infinite loops in cyclic rule sets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backward Chaining Control Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User Query G] --> B{Push G onto Goal Stack}
    B --> C[Pop Top Sub-Goal S]
    C --> D{S is a known Fact?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark S as PROVED]
    E --> F{Goal Stack Empty?}
    D -- No --> G[Find Rule R with Head S]
    G --> H{R Found?}
    H -- No --> I{More Rules to Try?}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[BACKTRACK to Previous Goal]
    J --> K{Stack Non-Empty?}
    K -- Yes --> C
    K -- No --> L[Report Original Goal FAILED]
    H -- Yes --> M[Push R's Body as New Sub-Goals]
    M --> C
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> N[Report Original Goal PROVED]
    N --> O[End]
    L --> O
```

---

## 4.2 AND-OR Graph for the Pneumonia Diagnosis Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["pneumonia(john)"] -->|AND| A["infection(john)"]
    Q -->|AND| B["chest_pain(john)"]
    A -->|AND| C["cough(john)"]
    A -->|AND| D["fatigue(john)"]
    B -.->|FACT MATCH| BF["chest_pain(john) ✓"]
    C -.->|FACT MATCH| CF["cough(john) ✓"]
    D -.->|FACT MATCH| DF["fatigue(john) ✓"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - **AND Nodes** (solid lines): Both sub-goals must be proven.
> - **OR Nodes** (would be shown if multiple rules matched the same head): Any one rule succeeding suffices.
> - **Dashed Edges** represent terminal fact-matching.

---

## 4.3 Backward vs Forward Chaining — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BC[Backward Chaining Engine]
        BQ[User Goal] --> BS[Goal Stack DFS]
        BS --> BR[Rule Unification]
        BR --> BM[Fact Matching]
        BM -->|Success| BPR[Proof Tree]
        BM -->|Failure| BT[Backtrack]
        BT --> BR
    end

    subgraph FC[Forward Chaining Engine]
        FW[Working Memory Facts] --> FM[Match Rule Premises]
        FM --> FF[Fire Rule]
        FF --> FU[Update Working Memory]
        FU --> FM
    end
```

---

## 4.4 PROLOG Execution Model (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PROLOG Program] --> P2[Lexical Analysis]
    P2 --> P3[Parse into Horn Clauses]
    P3 --> P4[Load Knowledge Base]
    Q1[User Query] --> Q2[Unification Engine]
    P4 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3[DFS Backtracking Search]
    Q3 --> Q4{Goal Provable?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Return TRUE with Bindings]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Return FALSE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define backward chaining. How does it differ from forward chaining in terms of control strategy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Backward chaining** is a goal-driven inference strategy that begins reasoning from a hypothesized conclusion and works *backwards* through the rule base to verify the supporting premises. The engine uses **depth-first search with backtracking** to recursively decompose goals into sub-goals until a match with a known fact is found.
>
> In contrast, **forward chaining** is *data-driven*: it starts with known facts and fires all rules whose premises are satisfied, propagating new facts forward until no more rules can fire (uses **breadth-first** or **forward-chaining** control).
>
> Backward chaining is more efficient for *diagnostic* and *query-answering* tasks, while forward chaining suits *monitoring* and *control* applications. **(3 marks — 1 each for definition, control strategy, and use-case distinction.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Explain the role of Modus Tollens in backward chaining with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Modus Tollens is the inferential backbone of backward chaining. It states: *"If $P \rightarrow Q$ and $\neg Q$, then $\neg P$."* In reverse modus ponens form used by backward chaining: *"To prove $Q$, prove $P$."*
>
> **Example:** Rule: `flu(X) :- fever(X), body_ache(X).` Query: `?- flu(ravi).`
> The engine does not assume Ravi has the flu; instead, it asks "Does Ravi have fever and body ache?" — these are the sub-goals that must be verified. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for reverse-form, 1 for example.)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the backward chaining algorithm in detail. Use a block diagram to explain its control flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following knowledge base:

```
R1: a :- b, c.
R2: a :- d.
R3: b :- e.
R4: b :- f, g.
R5: c :- h.
R6: d :- i, j.
Facts: e. f. g. h. i. j.
```

Prove the goal `?- a.` using backward chaining. Show the complete proof tree.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm (Pseudocode Description — 4 marks):**

1. Accept goal $G$ from the user.
2. Initialize an empty **goal stack**.
3. Push $G$ onto the stack.
4. **Loop until stack is empty or goal proved:**
   a. Pop the top sub-goal $S$.
   b. If $S$ matches a fact, mark **PROVED** and continue.
   c. Else, find a rule $R$ whose head unifies with $S$.
   d. If found, push $R$'s body onto the stack.
   e. If no rule matches, **backtrack** to the previous unproven goal.
5. If all sub-goals of $G$ are PROVED, output SUCCESS; else, output FAILURE.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Key Properties (1 mark):** DFS, complete for Horn clauses, uses Closed-World Assumption, exponential worst-case complexity $O(b^d)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Goal = `a`. Stack = [`a`].

**Step 2:** Pop `a`. Try R1: `a :- b, c.` → push `b`, then `c`.

**Step 3:** Pop `c`. Try R5: `c :- h.` → push `h`.

**Step 4:** Pop `h`. Matches Fact → **TRUE** ✓.

**Step 5:** Pop `b`. Try R3: `b :- e.` → push `e`.

**Step 6:** Pop `e`. Matches Fact → **TRUE** ✓.

**Step 7:** Stack now empty for the `b,c` branch. Therefore `a` is **PROVED via R1**.

**Proof Tree (5 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    G["a"] -->|R1, AND| B["b"]
    G -->|R1, AND| C["c"]
    B -->|R3| E["e"]
    B -->|R4, alt| F["f"]
    B -->|R4, alt| G2["g"]
    C -->|R5| H["h"]
    E -.->|Fact| EF["e ✓"]
    F -.->|Fact| FF["f ✓"]
    G2 -.->|Fact| GF["g ✓"]
    H -.->|Fact| HF["h ✓"]
```

**Verification Table (2 marks):**

| Sub-Goal | Rule Fired | Verified By | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a` | R1 | Sub-goal resolution | PROVED |
| `b` | R3 | Fact `e` | TRUE |
| `c` | R5 | Fact `h` | TRUE |
| `e` | — | Fact | TRUE |
| `h` | — | Fact | TRUE |

**Final Result:** `?- a.` → **YES** ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare forward and backward chaining in terms of (i) direction of reasoning, (ii) search strategy, (iii) suitability for different applications, and (iv) handling of uncertainty.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct an AND-OR graph and trace backward chaining for the following rule set to prove `grandfather(tom, sara)`:

```
R1: grandfather(X, Y) :- father(X, Z), parent(Z, Y).
R2: father(X, Y) :- parent(X, Y), male(X).
R3: parent(tom, bob).
R4: parent(bob, sara).
R5: male(tom).
R6: male(bob).
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Direction** | Antecedents → Consequent | Consequent → Antecedents |
| **(ii) Search Strategy** | Breadth-First (typically) | Depth-First with Backtracking |
| **(iii) Applications** | Monitoring, real-time control, expert system alerts | Diagnosis, query-answering, theorem proving |
| **(iv) Uncertainty** | Easily integrated with certainty factors (e.g., MYCIN variants) | Closed-world assumption; harder to integrate probabilistic weights |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- Forward chaining is preferred when the *number of conclusions* is small but the *number of facts* is large (e.g., fire-alarm systems).
- Backward chaining excels when the *number of possible goals* is bounded and the system must answer *specific queries* (e.g., legal reasoning, medical diagnosis).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Goal:** `grandfather(tom, sara)`.

**Step 1:** Match with R1. Unifier: $\{X/\text{tom}, Y/\text{sara}\}$.
Push sub-goals: `father(tom, Z)`, then `parent(Z, sara)`.

**Step 2:** Pop `parent(Z, sara)`. Match with R4: `parent(bob, sara)`. Unifier: $\{Z/\text{bob}\}$.

**Step 3:** Pop `father(tom, bob)`. Match with R2. Push: `parent(tom, bob)`, then `male(tom)`.

**Step 4:** Pop `male(tom)`. Matches Fact R5 → **TRUE** ✓.

**Step 5:** Pop `parent(tom, bob)`. Matches Fact R3 → **TRUE** ✓.

**Step 6:** All sub-goals PROVED. Original goal **TRUE** ✓.

**AND-OR Graph (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    G["grandfather(tom, sara)"] -->|AND via R1| A1["father(tom, Z)"]
    G -->|AND via R1| A2["parent(Z, sara)"]
    A1 -->|AND via R2| B1["parent(tom, Z)"]
    A1 -->|AND via R2| B2["male(tom)"]
    A2 -->|Unify Z=bob| B3["parent(bob, sara)"]
    B1 -.->|Fact R3| F1["✓"]
    B2 -.->|Fact R5| F2["✓"]
    B3 -.->|Fact R4| F3["✓"]
```

**Final Answer:** `grandfather(tom, sara)` is **PROVED** with binding chain $Z = \text{bob}$ (4 marks for trace, 3 marks for graph).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Modus Ponens (forward) with Modus Tollens (backward).** Many students lose 2–3 marks by misnaming the inference rule.
> 2. **Always show the goal stack evolution step-by-step** — vague "and so on" answers fetch zero marks.
> 3. **AND-OR graphs must use clear edge labels** indicating which rule fired. Unlabelled graphs are penalized.
> 4. **Closed-World Assumption** is implicit in PROLOG — explicitly stating it in proofs earns a bonus mark.
> 5. For **failure traces**, students often forget to show the *backtrack path*. Always enumerate alternatives tried.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 10 high-yield points before your KTU exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** Backward chaining is a *goal-driven* inference mechanism working from conclusion to premises.
- [x] **Logic Foundation:** Uses **reverse modus ponens** on **Horn clauses** (definite clauses).
- [x] **Search Strategy:** **Depth-First Search (DFS)** with automatic **backtracking**.
- [x] **Primary Language:** **PROLOG** (Programming in Logic) — its interpreter is a backward chaining engine.
- [x] **Closed-World Assumption (CWA):** Anything not provable is assumed FALSE.
- [x] **Time Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ — exponential; mitigated by memoization/tabling.
- [x] **Space Complexity:** $O(d)$ — linear in proof tree depth.
- [x] **Best For:** **Diagnostic expert systems** (MYCIN-style), theorem provers, query answering.
- [x] **Contrast with Forward Chaining:** Forward = data-driven BFS; Backward = goal-driven DFS.
- [x] **Key Applications:** Medical diagnosis (MYCIN), chemical analysis (DENDRAL), legal reasoning, natural language QA systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Exam Finisher:** *"Backward chaining asks: 'What must be true for my hypothesis to hold?' — then recursively verifies each requirement, terminating only when every branch is grounded in a known fact."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning: Classical Planning, Algorithms for Classical Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning: Classical Planning, Algorithms for Classical Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Planning in Artificial Intelligence?

In the context of Artificial Intelligence, **Planning** is the deliberate, structured process of deciding *which actions to take, in what order, and under what conditions*, so that an intelligent agent can transform an initial world state into a desired goal state. Unlike reactive systems that simply map percepts to actions, a planning system reasons about the *future consequences* of its choices before executing them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Planning is the problem of finding a sequence of actions (a **plan**) that takes an agent from a given **initial state** to a state in which a **goal** is achieved, given a description of how the world evolves in response to actions.

Formally, a classical planning problem is a tuple:
$$
\mathcal{P} = \langle S, S_0, G, A, f, cost \rangle
$$
where:
- $S$ = finite set of world states
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = initial state
- $G \subseteq S$ = set of goal states
- $A$ = finite set of actions (or operators)
- $f: S \times A \rightarrow S$ = deterministic transition function
- $cost: A \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ = action cost function

## 1.2 What is "Classical" Planning?

**Classical Planning** refers to planning under the following simplifying assumptions (often called the **STRIPS assumptions**):

| # | Assumption | Real-World Meaning |
|---|------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Fully Observable** | The agent knows the complete initial state. |
| 2 | **Deterministic** | Every action has a single, predictable outcome. |
| 3 | **Finite** | A finite number of states, actions, and objects. |
| 4 | **Static** | No exogenous events change the world except the agent's own actions. |
| 5 | **Single Agent** | Only one decision-maker exists in the world. |
| 6 | **Implicit Time** | Actions are instantaneous; duration does not matter. |

> [!NOTE]
> These restrictions allow *compact representation* (the **factored representation**) where a state is described as a conjunction of ground atoms, instead of being enumerated explicitly. This is what makes classical planning *tractable to represent*, even though the underlying search problem is still PSPACE-hard in the worst case.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Planning a Road Trip

Imagine you are a student from **Kerala** planning a road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram to Delhi** on a Royal Enfield:

- **Initial State** — Bike is in Thiruvananthapuram with a full tank.
- **Goal State** — Bike is in Delhi, and you have the university admit letter.
- **Actions** — *Refuel, Ride, Eat, Sleep, Get Letter*.
- **Preconditions** — To *Ride*, the tank must not be empty; to *Get Letter*, you must be in a city with the embassy.
- **Effects** — *Ride* changes your location; *Refuel* changes tank level.
- **Plan** — A complete ordered sequence of actions (e.g., `Refuel → Ride → Sleep → … → Get Letter`).

A classical planner is exactly this trip-planner, but the "bike" is the *agent*, and the "actions" are the moves the agent can legally execute. The planner finds the *shortest correct itinerary* automatically, given the rules of the world.

## 1.4 Why Planning is Different from Other Search Problems

| Feature | Classical Search | Planning |
|---|---|---|
| State Representation | Atomic (black box) | **Factored** (conjunction of variables/atoms) |
| Actions | Abstract (e.g., "node 5") | **Structured** (preconditions + effects) |
| Heuristic Availability | Domain-specific only | **Domain-independent** heuristics from the action structure |
| Goal Test | A single goal node | A **partial** state (any world satisfying it) |

> [!TIP]
> The **structured action description** is the *key innovation* of classical planning. It lets us derive *general-purpose heuristics* without knowing the specific domain — this is why planning scaled up to industrial-sized problems (e.g., NASA Deep Space 1, Mars rovers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Language of Classical Planning — STRIPS & PDDL

### 2.1.1 Ground Atomic Representation (Fluent Based)

A state is a **conjunction of ground (variable-free) atomic literals** (also called **fluents**). Example:
$$
s = \text{At(Bike, Trivandrum)} \land \text{TankFull} \land \neg \text{Has(Letter)}
$$

### 2.1.2 Action Schema (Operator)

Each action is described by:
- **Action Name** with parameters (e.g., `Ride(?b, ?from, ?to)`)
- **Preconditions** — what must be true to *apply* the action
- **Effects** — what becomes true / false after applying it

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **STRIPS** convention, an effect is of the form `Add(a)` or `Delete(a)`. The closed-world assumption holds: anything not asserted true is false.

### 2.1.3 PDDL — Planning Domain Definition Language

PDDL (McDermott et al., 1998) was introduced to standardise planning languages. A PDDL problem has two files:
- **Domain file** — defines *types*, *predicates*, and *action schemas*.
- **Problem file** — defines *objects*, the *initial state*, and the *goal*.

KTU expects students to recognise the following PDDL keywords:

| Keyword | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `:requirements` | Feature flags | `:strips, :typing, :negative-preconditions` |
| `:types` | Inheritance hierarchy | `vehicle - object` |
| `:predicates` | Fluent declarations | `(at ?v - vehicle ?l - location)` |
| `:action` | Action header | `:action MOVE` |
| `:parameters` | Typed variables | `?v - vehicle ?from ?to - location` |
| `:precondition` | Required literals | `(and (at ?v ?from) (road ?from ?to))` |
| `:effect` | Outcome literals | `(and (at ?v ?to) (not (at ?v ?from)))` |

## 2.2 The Blocks World — Canonical KTU Example

A classic benchmark domain used in every KTU lecture slide:

- **Objects:** blocks A, B, C and the table.
- **Predicates:** `On(?x, ?y)`, `OnTable(?x)`, `Clear(?x)`, `Holding(?x)`, `ArmEmpty`.
- **Action:** `Stack(?x, ?y)` requires `Holding(?x)` and `Clear(?y)`, with effects `On(?x, ?y)`, `ArmEmpty`, and deletes `Holding(?x)`, `Clear(?y)`.

This tiny domain is *PSPACE-complete* in the general case but solvable in milliseconds with good heuristics.

## 2.3 Algorithms for Classical Planning — Taxonomy

The algorithms divide into **four families**:

| Family | Direction | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Forward (Progression) Search** | State → State | Apply actions whose preconditions are satisfied in current state. |
| 2. **Backward (Regression) Search** | Goal → Goal | Choose an action that achieves a sub-goal and regress sub-goals. |
| 3. **Planning Graph Methods** | Level-by-level graph | Build a polynomial-sized data structure, then extract a plan. |
| 4. **Partial-Order Planning** | Non-linear plans | Plan the *partial* ordering, then linearise later. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks the difference between **progression** and **regression**, and between **total-order** vs **partial-order** planners. Make sure to remember these.

## 2.4 The "Delete-Relaxation" Heuristic Family (Hoffmann 2001)

This is *the* foundational heuristic for modern planners (used in **Fast Downward**, the most widely used academic planner).

### 2.4.1 Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **relaxed problem**, we *delete* all delete-effects of every action. So an action only ever *adds* facts. This makes the relaxed problem a *monotonic* one — once true, a fact stays true.

In the relaxed world, the shortest plan to a goal is computable as the **layered graph** of facts and actions.

### 2.4.2 Two Cost Variants — KTU High-Yield Formulas

Let $g(f)$ denote the **layer at which fact $f$ first becomes reachable** in the relaxed planning graph, and $g(a)$ the layer at which action $a$ is first applicable.

**Heuristic 1 — $h_{\text{add}}$ (additive cost):**
$$
h_{\text{add}}(s) = \sum_{f \in G} g(f)
$$
Sum the cost of each goal fact *independently*. Overestimates — inadmissible but informative.

**Heuristic 2 — $h_{\text{max}}$ (max cost):**
$$
h_{\text{max}}(s) = \max_{f \in G} g(f)
$$
Take the *maximum* layer among all goal facts. **Admissible** (does not overestimate) and therefore safe for optimal search.

> [!TIP]
> $h_{\text{max}}$ is admissible because in the delete-relaxed world the hardest goal fact dominates the plan length. KTU has previously asked: *"Which is admissible: $h_{\text{add}}$ or $h_{\text{max}}$?"* — answer: **$h_{\text{max}}$**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{P} = \langle S, S_0, G, A, f \rangle$ | Planning problem tuple | All algorithms |
| $pre(a)$, $eff^+(a)$, $eff^-(a)$ | Precondition, add-list, delete-list of $a$ | STRIPS representation |
| $S_0 \models g$ | State $S_0$ satisfies goal $g$ | Goal test |
| $h_{\text{max}}(s)$ | Max-layer heuristic (admissible) | Delete-relaxation |
| $h_{\text{add}}(s)$ | Sum-layer heuristic (inadmissible) | Delete-relaxation |
| $h^+(s)$ | Optimal delete-relaxed plan cost | Upper bound reference |
| $s \vdash_a s'$ | Action $a$ applicable in $s$, yielding $s'$ | Transition relation |
| $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ | Plan — sequence of actions | Output of planner |
| $L_i$, $A_i$, $F_i$ | $i$-th fact / action / mutex layer | **Graphplan** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Planning Role |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (NASA)** | Autonomous spacecraft activity scheduling — Remote Agent (Deep Space 1, 1999) was the first AI planner flown in space. |
| **Robotics** | Mission planning for Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance). |
| **Logistics** | Container-yard, warehouse, and supply-chain planning (used by DHL, Maersk). |
| **Semiconductor Manufacturing** | Lithography tool scheduling at Intel, TSMC. |
| **Game AI** | Strategy game opponents — Total War, StarCraft II (Hierarchical Task Network planners). |
| **Cybersecurity** | Automated penetration testing — generates an *attack plan*. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Blocks World with Delete-Relaxation Heuristic

### Problem Statement

Consider the following **initial state** $S_0$ in a 3-block world (A, B, C):
$$
S_0 = \text{On}(C,A) \land \text{On}(A,\text{Table}) \land \text{On}(B,\text{Table}) \land \text{Clear}(B) \land \text{Clear}(C) \land \text{ArmEmpty}
$$
The **goal** is to achieve:
$$
G = \text{On}(A,B) \land \text{On}(B,C)
$$
The available actions are `Stack`, `Unstack`, `Pickup`, `Putdown` (classic STRIPS block actions).

### Step 1: Enumerate the Action Schemas

For brevity, we write:
- **$a_1$ = Unstack(C, A)** — $pre: \text{On}(C,A), \text{Clear}(C), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^+: \text{Holding}(C), \text{Clear}(A)$ — $eff^-: \text{On}(C,A), \text{ArmEmpty}$.
- **$a_2$ = Putdown(C)** — $pre: \text{Holding}(C)$ — $eff^+: \text{On}(C,\text{Table}), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^-: \text{Holding}(C)$.
- **$a_3$ = Pickup(B)** — $pre: \text{On}(B,\text{Table}), \text{Clear}(B), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^+: \text{Holding}(B)$ — $eff^-: \text{On}(B,\text{Table}), \text{ArmEmpty}, \text{Clear}(B)$.
- **$a_4$ = Stack(B, C)** — $pre: \text{Holding}(B), \text{Clear}(C)$ — $eff^+: \text{On}(B,C), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^-: \text{Holding}(B), \text{Clear}(C)$.
- **$a_5$ = Pickup(A)** — $pre: \text{On}(A,\text{Table}), \text{Clear}(A), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^+: \text{Holding}(A)$ — $eff^-: \text{On}(A,\text{Table}), \text{ArmEmpty}, \text{Clear}(A)$.
- **$a_6$ = Stack(A, B)** — $pre: \text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B)$ — $eff^+: \text{On}(A,B), \text{ArmEmpty}$ — $eff^-: \text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B)$.

### Step 2: Construct the Relaxed Planning Graph (Delete Effects Removed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **relaxed graph**, the delete-list $eff^-$ is discarded, so facts once added never disappear.

**Layer $F_0$ (Initial facts):**
$$
F_0 = \big\{\text{On}(C,A), \text{On}(A,\text{Tab}), \text{On}(B,\text{Tab}), \text{Clear}(B), \text{Clear}(C), \text{ArmEmpty}\big\}
$$

**Layer $A_1$ (Actions whose preconditions are all in $F_0$):**
- $a_1$ (Unstack C from A) ✓ — $pre \subseteq F_0$
- $a_3$ (Pickup B) ✓ — $pre \subseteq F_0$
- $a_5$ (Pickup A) — requires $\text{On}(A,\text{Tab})$ ✓ and $\text{Clear}(A)$ ✗ (A is under C) → **NOT applicable yet.**

So $A_1 = \{a_1, a_3\}$.

**Layer $F_1$ (Add facts of $A_1$, union $F_0$):**
$$
F_1 = F_0 \cup \{\text{Holding}(C), \text{Clear}(A), \text{Holding}(B)\}
$$

**Layer $A_2$:**
- $a_2$ (Putdown C) ✓ — needs Holding(C) ∈ $F_1$.
- $a_4$ (Stack B on C) — needs Holding(B) ✓ and Clear(C) ✓ → applicable!
- $a_5$ (Pickup A) — now needs Clear(A) ✓, On(A,Tab) ✓, ArmEmpty — but ArmEmpty is true in $F_0$ and never deleted in the relaxed world, so ✓.

So $A_2 = \{a_2, a_4, a_5\}$.

**Layer $F_2$:**
$$
F_2 = F_1 \cup \big\{\text{On}(C,\text{Tab}), \text{ArmEmpty}, \text{On}(B,C), \text{Holding}(A)\big\}
$$
Goal facts so far: $\text{On}(B,C) \in F_2$ ✓, but $\text{On}(A,B) \notin F_2$.

**Layer $A_3$:**
- $a_6$ (Stack A on B) — needs Holding(A) ∈ $F_2$ ✓ and Clear(B) ∈ $F_2$ ✓ (Clear(B) was true in $F_0$ and never deleted in the relaxed world).

So $A_3 = \{a_6\}$.

**Layer $F_3$:**
$$
F_3 = F_2 \cup \big\{\text{On}(A,B), \text{ArmEmpty}\big\}
$$
Both goal facts are present: $G = \{\text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,C)\} \subseteq F_3$.

### Step 3: Extract the Relaxed Plan

We trace back the dependencies:
- $\text{On}(A,B)$ was first added by $a_6$ at layer 3.
- $a_6$ needed $\text{Holding}(A)$ added by $a_5$ at layer 2.
- $a_5$ needed $\text{Clear}(A)$ added by $a_1$ at layer 1.
- $\text{On}(B,C)$ was first added by $a_4$ at layer 2.
- $a_4$ needed $\text{Holding}(B)$ added by $a_3$ at layer 1.

**Relaxed plan:** $\pi^+ = \langle a_1, a_3, a_4, a_5, a_6 \rangle$ — length **5**.

### Step 4: Compute $h_{\text{max}}$ and $h_{\text{add}}$

$$
h_{\text{max}}(S_0) = \max\!\big(g(\text{On}(A,B)),\ g(\text{On}(B,C))\big) = \max(3, 2) = 3
$$

$$
h_{\text{add}}(S_0) = g(\text{On}(A,B)) + g(\text{On}(B,C)) = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice $h_{\text{add}} = 5$ equals the actual relaxed plan length here, because the two sub-goals share no actions. $h_{\text{add}}$ overcounts (gives 5) while $h_{\text{max}}$ gives the correct lower bound of 3, which *coincidentally* matches the true optimal plan length. In general, $h_{\text{max}} \le h^+ \le h_{\text{add}}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Progression Search on a Logistics Problem

**Initial State:**
$$
S_0 = \{\text{At(Pkg, Kochi)},\ \text{At(Truck, Kochi)},\ \text{At(Plane, Kochi)}\}
$$
**Goal:** $\text{At(Pkg, Delhi)}$.

**Action $a$ — Load(Pkg, Truck, Kochi):**
$$
pre = \{\text{At(Pkg, Kochi)}, \text{At(Truck, Kochi)}\},\quad eff^+ = \{\text{In(Pkg, Truck)}\},\quad eff^- = \{\text{At(Pkg, Kochi)}\}
$$

**Action $b$ — Drive(Truck, Kochi, Delhi):**
$$
pre = \{\text{At(Truck, Kochi)}\},\quad eff^+ = \{\text{At(Truck, Delhi)}\},\quad eff^- = \{\text{At(Truck, Kochi)}\}
$$

**Action $c$ — Unload(Pkg, Truck, Delhi):**
$$
pre = \{\text{In(Pkg, Truck)}, \text{At(Truck, Delhi)}\},\quad eff^+ = \{\text{At(Pkg, Delhi)}\},\quad eff^- = \{\text{In(Pkg, Truck)}\}
$$

### Step 1 — Initial State $S_0$ Goal Test

$$
S_0 \models \text{At(Pkg, Delhi)}? \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{At(Pkg, Delhi)} \notin S_0
$$
Goal not satisfied. Expand.

### Step 2 — Forward Step

Actions applicable in $S_0$: only $a$ (Load). Apply it:
$$
S_1 = (S_0 \cup \{In(Pkg, Truck)\}) \setminus \{At(Pkg, Kochi)\} = \{At(Pkg, Kochi)\text{ is gone},\ In(Pkg, Truck),\ At(Truck, Kochi),\ At(Plane, Kochi)\}
$$

### Step 3 — Continue Forward Search

Applicable in $S_1$: $b$ (Drive). Apply:
$$
S_2 = \{In(Pkg, Truck),\ At(Truck, Delhi),\ At(Plane, Kochi)\}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Step

Applicable in $S_2$: $c$ (Unload). Apply:
$$
S_3 = \{At(Pkg, Delhi),\ At(Truck, Delhi),\ At(Plane, Kochi)\}
$$
Goal satisfied. **Plan found:** $\pi = \langle a,\ b,\ c\rangle$, length **3**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves this kind of trace. Always show: *precondition check → effect update → new state*. Examiners award partial credit for the state transition even if the final plan is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Planning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef def fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef algo fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff;
    classDef heur fill:#b45309,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff;
    classDef out  fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,color:#ffffff;

    A[Problem Specification: Domain PDDL and Problem PDDL]:::def
    B[Factored State Representation: Conjunction of fluents]:::def
    C{Planning Algorithm Selection}:::def
    D[Forward Progression Search]:::algo
    E[Backward Regression Search]:::algo
    F[Planning Graph: Graphplan]:::algo
    G[Heuristic Estimator: Delete Relaxation h_add, h_max]:::heur
    H[Conflict Resolution: Partial Order Planning]:::algo
    I[Search Engine: A Star, Greedy, Hill Climbing]:::algo
    J[Output Plan: Sequence of grounded actions]:::out

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> H
    G -.guides.-> D
    G -.guides.-> E
    G -.guides.-> F
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

    A1["ACTION LEVEL 1: a1 UnstackCA, a3 PickupB"]:::act
    F1["FACT LEVEL 1: HoldingC, ClearA, HoldingB + F0"]:::fact
    A2["ACTION LEVEL 2: a2 PutdownC, a4 StackBC, a5 PickupA"]:::act
    F2["FACT LEVEL 2: OnCTab, OnBC, HoldingA + F1"]:::fact
    A3["ACTION LEVEL 3: a6 StackAB"]:::act

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Classical Planning and Problem-Solving Search (e.g., A*).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **State representation** — Classical planning uses a *factored* representation (conjunction of variables/predicates), whereas classical A* uses an *atomic* (black-box) state. **(1 mark)**
- **Action representation** — Planning actions have an *explicit internal structure* (preconditions + add / delete effects), enabling derivation of *domain-independent heuristics*. Search actions are abstract labels. **(1 mark)**
- **Heuristic derivation** — In planning, heuristics like $h_{\text{max}}$ and $h_{\text{add}}$ are *automatically extracted from the action descriptions*. In search, heuristics are hand-crafted per problem. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the "delete relaxation" of a planning problem? Why is it useful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **delete relaxation** of a planning problem $\mathcal{P}$ is a new problem $\mathcal{P}^+$ in which the delete-list $eff^-(a)$ of every action $a$ is removed; actions can only *add* facts, never remove them. **(1 mark)**
- The relaxed problem is **monotonic** — once a fact becomes true, it stays true, so plan-length estimation is greatly simplified. **(1 mark)**
- It is useful because it yields *polynomial-time computable, domain-independent heuristics* $h_{\text{max}}$ and $h_{\text{add}}$ that guide a forward search engine (e.g., Fast Downward) toward the goal efficiently. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering planning trace questions, students *frequently lose 2–3 marks* by **not showing the state after each action**. Examiners expect a fully written $S_0 \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow S_G$ trace. Also, do **not** confuse **progression** (forward) with **regression** (backward) in your headings. Always **define preconditions and effects** before applying an action.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Forward Search + Heuristics

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]`
> **(a)** Consider a 3-block Blocks World with initial state:
> $S_0 = \{\text{On}(A,B), \text{On}(B,C), \text{On}(C,\text{Table}), \text{Clear}(A), \text{ArmEmpty}\}$
> Goal: $G = \{\text{On}(B,A), \text{On}(C,B)\}$.
> Using **Forward State-Space Search with the $h_{\text{max}}$ heuristic** (delete relaxation), show the first three expanded states and compute $h_{\text{max}}(S_0)$. State one admissible plan if found. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Graphplan algorithm** and its level-by-level expansion. Discuss the role of **mutual exclusion (mutex)** in the planning graph. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Identify applicable actions in $S_0$.**

The only action applicable is `Unstack(A, B)` (since `On(A,B)`, `Clear(A)`, `ArmEmpty` all hold). Call it $a_1$.

**Step 2 — Apply $a_1$ to get $S_1$.**
$$
S_1 = S_0 \cup \{\text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B)\} \setminus \{\text{On}(A,B), \text{ArmEmpty}\}
$$
$$
S_1 = \{\text{On}(B,C), \text{On}(C,\text{Tab}), \text{Clear}(A), \text{Clear}(B), \text{Holding}(A)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Applicable actions in $S_1$.**

- `Stack(A, C)` — needs `Clear(C)`. ❌ (C is not clear.)
- `Putdown(A)` — needs `Holding(A)`. ✓ → $a_2$.
- `Stack(A, Table)` — needs `Clear(Table)`. (Often allowed in simplified blocks world.)

Apply `Putdown(A)` to get $S_2$:
$$
S_2 = \{\text{On}(A,\text{Tab}), \text{On}(B,C), \text{On}(C,\text{Tab}), \text{Clear}(A), \text{Clear}(B), \text{ArmEmpty}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_{\text{max}}(S_0)$ via delete relaxation.**

In the relaxed graph, fact layer $F_0$ = $S_0$. Action layer $A_1 = \{a_1\}$. Fact layer $F_1$ gains `Holding(A)` and `Clear(B)`. Action layer $A_2$ contains `Stack(B,A)` (since `On(B,C)`, `Clear(B)`, `ArmEmpty` were true in $F_0$ and never deleted in the relaxed world; but `Holding(B)` is required — that comes from `Unstack(B,C)`, applicable at layer 2 once `Clear(B)` is true). Working through the layers:
- Layer 0 — $F_0$ given.
- Layer 1 — `$a_1$: Unstack(A,B)`. New facts: `Holding(A), Clear(B)`.
- Layer 2 — `$a_3$: Unstack(B,C)`. New facts: `Holding(B), Clear(C)`. Plus `$a_2$: Putdown(A)` adds `On(A,Tab), ArmEmpty`.
- Layer 3 — `$a_4$: Stack(B,A)` achievable (needs `Holding(B)`, `Clear(A)`; `Clear(A)` was in $F_0$).
- Layer 4 — `On(B,A)` reached.
- Layer 5 — For `On(C,B)`, need `Holding(C)` and `Clear(B)`. `Clear(B)` available; `Holding(C)` needs `Unstack(C,Tab)` which needs `On(C,Tab)`, `Clear(C)`, `ArmEmpty` — reachable by layer 4. So `On(C,B)` at layer 6.

$$
h_{\text{max}}(S_0) = \max(4, 6) = 6
$$

(Actual plan length in the un-relaxed world is 6 as well — `Unstack A, Putdown A, Unstack B, Stack B on A, Pickup C, Stack C on B`.)

**Mark split:** State trace — 3 marks; Relaxed graph + $h_{\text{max}}$ computation — 3 marks; Plan — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Graphplan Algorithm (Blum & Furst, 1995):**

1. Build a **planning graph** in two alternating layers: a **fact layer** $F_i$ and an **action layer** $A_i$.
2. $F_0$ = the initial state.
3. $A_i$ = all actions whose preconditions are in $F_i$ (plus *no-op* actions for persistence).
4. $F_{i+1}$ = $F_i$ ∪ (all add-effects of $A_i$).
5. **Mutex propagation:** at each layer, mark pairs of facts/actions as *mutually exclusive* (mutex) if they cannot both be true / happen simultaneously.
6. **Termination:** stop when $F_i$ contains all goal facts *and* no two of them are mutex — at that point, a plan *might* exist.
7. **Plan extraction:** search *backward* from the goal layer. If a chosen set of actions is consistent (no mutex between them), recurse one level back. If inconsistent, try another combination or **add a level** to the graph.

**Mutex rules** (two types for each layer):
- *Action mutex:* two actions $a, b$ at the same action layer are mutex if (i) one deletes a precondition of the other, (ii) their effects are mutex, or (iii) their preconditions are pairwise mutex.
- *Fact mutex:* two facts $p, q$ at the same fact layer are mutex if every action achieving $p$ is mutex with every action achieving $q$.

**Role of mutex:** It prunes the search space by *detecting impossible conjunctions early*, enabling polynomial-time plan *existence* checks. The graph is polynomial in size, and mutexes make the back-extraction phase tractable.

**Mark split:** Algorithm description — 3 marks; Layer expansion — 2 marks; Mutex rules — 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Backward Search + PDDL

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
> **(a)** Define the **STRIPS** representation. Write a complete PDDL-style definition of an action `Load(pkg, veh, loc)` and explain each component with an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Backward State-Space Search (Regression)** with a suitable example. State one key advantage and one disadvantage compared to forward search. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**STRIPS Representation:**

A STRIPS planning instance has:
- A set of **ground atomic predicates** (fluents) — e.g., `At(truck, kochi)`.
- An **initial state** — a set of fluents true at the start.
- A **goal state** — a conjunction of fluents.
- A set of **action schemas** with `precondition`, `add-list` (effects⁺), and `delete-list` (effects⁻).

**PDDL Definition of `Load`:**

```pddf
(define (domain logistics)
  (:requirements :strips :typing)
  (:types pkg vehicle location - object)
  (:predicates
    (at ?v - vehicle ?l - location)
    (in  ?p - pkg  ?v - vehicle)
    (at  ?p - pkg  ?l - location))

  (:action Load
    :parameters (?p - pkg ?v - vehicle ?l - location)
    :precondition (and (at ?p ?l) (at ?v ?l))
    :effect (and (in ?p ?v) (not (at ?p ?l)))))
```

**Component explanation:**

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `:parameters` | Typed variables bound at instantiation | `?p = Pkg1, ?v = Truck1, ?l = Kochi` |
| `:precondition` | Must hold for action to be applicable | Both `at Pkg1 Kochi` and `at Truck1 Kochi` must be true |
| `:effect (and …)` | Add list | `in Pkg1 Truck1` is added |
| `:effect (not …)` | Delete list | `at Pkg1 Kochi` is removed |

**Mark split:** STRIPS definition — 2 marks; PDDL syntax — 3 marks; Component explanation — 2 marks.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Backward State-Space Search (Regression):**

Regression works *from the goal backward toward the initial state*. For each goal fluent, we ask: *which action has this fluent in its add-list?* Pick such an action $a$, ensure $a$'s preconditions are not deleted by any other action chosen in the same step, and **regress** the goal: replace the goal fluent with $a$'s preconditions (minus the add-effect that became true, and minus anything $a$ deletes from the goal).

**Example** — Goal: `At(Pkg, Delhi)`, Action `Unload(Pkg, Truck, Delhi)` has add-effect `At(Pkg, Delhi)`. Regress the goal to the precondition:
$$
G_1 = \{\text{In(Pkg, Truck)}, \text{At(Truck, Delhi)}\}
$$
Now apply regression to `In(Pkg, Truck)`: action `Load(Pkg, Truck, Kochi)` adds it, so:
$$
G_0 = \{\text{At(Pkg, Kochi)}, \text{At(Truck, Kochi)}, \text{At(Truck, Delhi)}\}
$$
Continue regressing `At(Truck, Delhi)` via `Drive(Truck, Kochi, Delhi)` to get the initial state conditions.

**Advantage:** Lower effective branching factor because only *relevant* actions (those that achieve a sub-goal) are considered.

**Disadvantage:** Computing the regressed set is hard when actions have *delete effects* — they can clobber previously established sub-goals, requiring extra bookkeeping (the *clobberer / threatened-link* analysis).

**Mark split:** Regression concept — 2 marks; Worked example — 3 marks; Advantage + Disadvantage — 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (save this!)**

- ⭐ **Classical Planning** is factored, deterministic, fully observable, static, single-agent, finite.
- ⭐ A planning problem = $\langle S, S_0, G, A, f \rangle$ (ignore `cost` in unweighted case).
- ⭐ **STRIPS** action = `{name, parameters, precondition, add-list, delete-list}`.
- ⭐ **PDDL** is the standardised language (Domain file + Problem file).
- ⭐ **Progression** (forward): choose applicable action in current state → next state.
- ⭐ **Regression** (backward): choose an action that adds a goal fluent → regress goal to its preconditions.
- ⭐ **Graphplan** = polynomial-sized graph, fact/action layers, **mutex** propagation, backward plan extraction.
- ⭐ **Delete relaxation** removes all delete effects → monotonic relaxed problem.
- ⭐ $h_{\text{max}}$ = max goal-fact layer (admissible) ✓, $h_{\text{add}}$ = sum (inadmissible, informative).
- ⭐ $h_{\text{max}} \le h^+ \le h_{\text{add}}$ (true cost is bounded between them).
- ⭐ **Blocks World** is the canonical example — Stack, Unstack, Pickup, Putdown.
- ⭐ **Partial-Order Planning (POP)** uses *least commitment* — orders actions only when necessary.
- ⭐ **SATPlan** encodes the planning graph as a Boolean formula and uses a SAT solver.
- ⭐ Always **show full state trace** in exam answers — partial credit depends on it.
- ⭐ Always **define preconditions and effects** before applying any action in a trace.
- ⭐ Modern planners: **Fast Downward** (Helmert, 2006), **LPG**, **Optic**, **POPF**.

topics.title: Forward State-Space Search for Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forward State-Space Search for Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forward State-Space Search** (also called **Progression Search** or **Forward Planning**) is a fundamental planning technique in Artificial Intelligence where the search begins from the **initial state** of the world and applies applicable actions in a forward-chaining manner to progress toward the **goal state**. At each step, the algorithm selects an action whose **preconditions** are satisfied by the current state, applies the action's **effects** (add list and delete list) to generate a new state, and continues this process until a state is reached that satisfies all goal conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Forward state-space search is a form of planning in which the reasoning proceeds in the same direction as the arrow of time — from the present (initial state) toward a desired future (goal state) — by repeatedly applying ground actions whose preconditions are entailed by the current world state.*

The underlying state-transition model is formally defined as a 4-tuple $\langle S, A, \gamma, cost \rangle$ where:
- $S$ = set of all world states (each state is a set of ground fluents/atoms)
- $A$ = set of ground actions (operators)
- $\gamma(s, a)$ = deterministic transition function yielding the successor state
- $cost(s, a, s')$ = the cost of applying action $a$ in state $s$ to reach $s'$

The **STRIPS-style** (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver) representation of an action $a$ is:

$$\text{Action}(a) = \langle \text{Name}(a), \text{Pre}(a), \text{Eff}^+(a), \text{Eff}^-(a) \rangle$$

where:
- $\text{Pre}(a)$ = preconditions (must hold before execution)
- $\text{Eff}^+(a)$ = **add list** (fluents made true)
- $\text{Eff}^-(a)$ = **delete list** (fluents made false)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Cooking a Recipe Step-by-Step**
> 
> Imagine you are cooking dinner. Your **initial state** is "kitchen empty, raw ingredients in fridge". Your **goal state** is "Biryani cooked and served". You scan your recipe book (the **action library**) and pick the next action — say *chop onions* — only if its preconditions are met (you have onions, you have a knife, your hands are free). You perform the action, your kitchen state changes, and you repeat. **Forward state-space search is exactly this**: the AI agent is the cook, the world is the kitchen, and the plan is the sequence of steps it will take.

A second analogy: **GPS Navigation**. Your car is at the starting point (initial state). The GPS looks at the next legal turn (an action whose preconditions are satisfied: you're on this road, the turn is allowed). It executes the turn, updates your position, and continues until you reach the destination (goal state).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Reference Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glossary of Critical Terms (High-Frequency in KTU Exams)**
> 
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **State** | A complete assignment of truth values to all fluents (variables) in the world. |
> | **Fluent** | A time-varying property of the world (e.g., $\text{At(Robot, R1)}$). |
> | **Ground Action** | An action with all variables instantiated to specific constants. |
> | **Precondition** | A literal that must be true for the action to be applicable. |
> | **Effect** | The change (additions and deletions) an action makes to the world. |
> | **Plan** | A sequence (or partially ordered set) of ground actions transforming $S_0$ to $S_g$. |
> | **Relaxed Plan** | A plan computed in a *simpler* world where delete lists are ignored. |
> | **Heuristic $h(s)$** | An estimated cost from state $s$ to the nearest goal state. |
> | **Progression** | Synonym for forward search — moving forward in time. |

### 1.4 Why Forward Search is Significant

Forward search is the **most natural** and most widely deployed planning paradigm because:
1. It mirrors the physical flow of time and causality.
2. It is easy to implement and debug.
3. It directly leverages strong domain-independent heuristics (e.g., $h_{FF}$, $h_{add}$, $h_{max}$).
4. It is the foundation of modern **satisficing planners** like **Fast-Downward**, **LPG**, and **FF**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The **STRIPS** planner (Fikes & Nilsson, 1971) used forward search. The **Graphplan** system (Blum & Furst, 1995) and the **FF** planner (Hoffmann, 2001) revolutionized forward search by introducing the **planning graph** and **relaxed planning graph heuristics**, respectively. These advances made forward search competitive with — and often superior to — backward (regression) search.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The Forward Search Loop

The forward state-space search algorithm operates as a **state-by-state expansion loop**. The high-level procedure is:

1. **Initialize:** Open list contains the initial state $S_0$. Closed set is empty.
2. **Selection:** Pick a state $s$ from the open list (depending on the strategy — BFS, DFS, A*, Greedy, etc.).
3. **Goal Test:** If $s \models G$ (entails all goal propositions), return the plan (sequence of actions used to reach $s$).
4. **Expansion (Progression Step):** Generate all ground actions $a \in A$ whose preconditions $\text{Pre}(a)$ are satisfied in $s$.
5. **State Update:** For each applicable $a$, compute the successor state $s' = \gamma(s, a)$:
   - $s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$
6. **Insertion:** Add $s'$ to the open list (if not already visited).
7. **Loop:** Return to step 2 until goal found or open list is empty (failure).

### 2.2 Detailed Walk-Through of Each Step

#### Step 1: Why Start at $S_0$?
Forward search **must** start at the initial state because it cannot reason about preconditions the agent has not yet observed. Unlike humans who can mentally "work backward" from a goal, the agent must physically (or computationally) move from what it knows to what it wants.

#### Step 2: Why Select Strategically?
The order in which states are selected determines the **search efficiency**. Uninformed strategies:
- **BFS** guarantees shortest plan length but uses exponential memory.
- **DFS** uses less memory but can get stuck in deep, dead-end branches.
- **Uniform-cost search** is optimal for action costs.

Informed strategies (heuristic-guided):
- **Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS):** expand node with lowest $h(s)$.
- **A* search:** expand node with lowest $f(s) = g(s) + h(s)$.
- **Enforced Hill Climbing (EHC):** used in the famous **FF planner** — only accepts a successor with strictly lower $h$ than the current state.

#### Step 3: Goal Test
A state $s$ satisfies the goal $G$ if and only if:
$$s \models G \iff \forall g \in G, \; g \in s$$
That is, every goal fluent is a member of the state's fluent set. In set notation: $G \subseteq s$.

#### Step 4: Why Generate *All* Applicable Actions?
Forward search must consider every action whose preconditions are satisfied. **Missing even one applicable action** could cause the algorithm to miss the optimal plan. The set of applicable actions in state $s$ is:
$$\text{App}(s) = \{ a \in A \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s \}$$

#### Step 5: The State Transition
This is the **STRIPS update rule**:
$$s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Frame Problem:** The transition function assumes that anything *not* explicitly listed in $\text{Eff}^+(a) \cup \text{Eff}^-(a)$ remains unchanged. This is the famous **STRIPS Assumption** — a clean but sometimes unrealistic simplification. In KTU exams, mention this assumption explicitly for full credit.

#### Step 6: Closed-Set Check (Visited States)
To prevent infinite loops, we maintain a closed set of visited states:
$$C = \{ s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \}$$
A newly generated $s'$ is added to the open list only if $s' \notin C$.

#### Step 7: Termination
- **Success:** $G \subseteq s$ for some $s$ popped from the open list.
- **Failure:** The open list is empty before the goal is reached.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for Part A and Part B numerical/analytical questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Transition (STRIPS) | $s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$ | Updates state after action $a$ | Sets of fluents |
| 2 | Goal Satisfaction | $G \subseteq s$ | State $s$ satisfies goal $G$ | Boolean |
| 3 | Applicability | $\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s$ | Action $a$ applicable in $s$ | Boolean |
| 4 | A* Evaluation | $f(s) = g(s) + h(s)$ | Estimated total cost through $s$ | Numeric (cost) |
| 5 | Greedy Evaluation | $f(s) = h(s)$ | Heuristic-only | Numeric (cost) |
| 6 | Add Heuristic | $h_{add}(s) = \sum_{g \in G} C(s, g)$ | Sum of relaxed costs of goals | Numeric |
| 7 | Max Heuristic | $h_{max}(s) = \max_{g \in G} C(s, g)$ | Max of relaxed costs of goals | Numeric |
| 8 | FF Heuristic | $h_{FF}(s) = \mid \pi_{relax}(s) \mid$ | Length of relaxed plan | Numeric (integer) |
| 9 | Relaxed Cost Recursion | $C(s, p) = \begin{cases} 0 & p \in s \\ \min_{a: p \in \text{Eff}^+(a)} \left[ 1 + \max_{q \in \text{Pre}(a)} C(s, q) \right] & p \notin s \end{cases}$ | Cost of achieving proposition $p$ | Numeric |
| 10 | Search Complexity (BFS) | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | Time and space, branching $b$, depth $d$ | Big-O |
| 11 | Number of Successors | $\mid \text{App}(s) \mid$ | Branching factor of node $s$ | Integer |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

Forward state-space search powers several real-world AI systems:

- **Robotics & Autonomous Navigation:** Robots use forward search over discretized state spaces to plan collision-free paths. Examples include warehouse robots (Kiva/Amazon) and Mars rovers.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Companies like FedEx and DHL use forward planning to schedule package routing.
- **Game AI:** NPC behavior trees often use forward search to decide moves.
- **Manufacturing & Process Planning:** Industrial automation uses forward planning to schedule assembly sequences.
- **Satellite & Spacecraft Control:** NASA's Deep Space 1 mission used the **Remote Agent** planner, which employed forward search over a STRIPS-like representation.

### 2.5 Properties of Forward Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Properties to Memorize:**
> 1. **Soundness:** Any plan returned is valid (achieves the goal from the initial state).
> 2. **Completeness:** If a plan exists, the algorithm will eventually find it (assuming finite branching and a visited-state check).
> 3. **Optimality:** Achieved only with **A\*** and an **admissible** heuristic; not guaranteed for BFS unless all action costs are equal.
> 4. **Termination:** Guaranteed for finite state spaces; **not guaranteed** for infinite state spaces without visited-state tracking.
