## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: The Robot-and-Box Domain

Consider a simple planning problem with **two rooms** $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected by a doorway. A **Robot** $R$ and a **Box** $B$ can each occupy one room. The robot can pick up the box, put it down, and move between rooms.

#### 3.1.1 Formal STRIPS Specification

**Initial State $S_0$:**
$$S_0 = \{ \text{At}(R, R_1), \; \text{At}(B, R_1), \; \neg \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}$$

**Goal State $G$:**
$$G = \{ \text{At}(B, R_2) \}$$

**Actions (with full preconditions and effects):**

| Action | Preconditions | Add List | Delete List |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PickUp** | $\text{At}(R, r), \; \text{At}(B, r), \; \neg \text{Carrying}(R, B)$ | $\text{Carrying}(R, B)$ | $\text{At}(B, r)$ |
| **PutDown** | $\text{At}(R, r), \; \text{Carrying}(R, B)$ | $\text{At}(B, r)$ | $\text{Carrying}(R, B)$ |
| **Move**$(r_1, r_2)$ | $\text{At}(R, r_1)$ | $\text{At}(R, r_2)$ | $\text{At}(R, r_1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on PickUp:** For any $r \in \{R_1, R_2\}$, PickUp is parameterized by the room $r$ where the robot and box are co-located. So there are **two ground instances**: $\text{PickUp}(R_1)$ and $\text{PickUp}(R_2)$.

---

#### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Forward Search Trace

We now perform a step-by-step forward expansion of the state space.

**Step 0: Initial state.**
$$S_0 = \{ \text{At}(R, R_1), \; \text{At}(B, R_1), \; \neg \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}$$
Goal test: $\text{At}(B, R_2) \in S_0$? **No.** Continue.

**Identify applicable actions $\text{App}(S_0)$:**
- $\text{PickUp}(R_1)$: preconditions $\{ \text{At}(R, R_1), \text{At}(B, R_1), \neg \text{Carrying}(R, B) \} \subseteq S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$: precondition $\text{At}(R, R_1) \in S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{PutDown}$: requires $\text{Carrying}(R, B)$, not in $S_0$. **No ✗**

So $\text{App}(S_0) = \{ \text{PickUp}(R_1), \; \text{Move}(R_1, R_2) \}$.

---

**Branch 1: Apply $\text{PickUp}(R_1)$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= (S_0 \setminus \text{Eff}^-(\text{PickUp}(R_1))) \cup \text{Eff}^+(\text{PickUp}(R_1)) \\
    &= (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{At}(B, R_1) \}) \cup \{ \text{Carrying}(R, B) \} \\
    &= \{ \text{At}(R, R_1), \; \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}
\end{aligned}$$

Goal test: $\text{At}(B, R_2) \in S_1$? **No.** Continue.

Applicable actions in $S_1$:
- $\text{PutDown}(R_1)$: preconditions $\{ \text{At}(R, R_1), \text{Carrying}(R, B) \} \subseteq S_1$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$: precondition $\text{At}(R, R_1) \in S_1$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{PickUp}(R_1)$: requires $\neg \text{Carrying}$, but $\text{Carrying}$ is in $S_1$. **No ✗**

---

**Branch 2 (from $S_1$): Apply $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= (S_1 \setminus \text{Eff}^-(\text{Move}(R_1, R_2))) \cup \text{Eff}^+(\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)) \\
    &= (S_1 \setminus \{ \text{At}(R, R_1) \}) \cup \{ \text{At}(R, R_2) \} \\
    &= \{ \text{At}(R, R_2), \; \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}
\end{aligned}$$

Goal test: $\text{At}(B, R_2) \in S_2$? **No** (box is carried, not at location). Continue.

Applicable actions in $S_2$:
- $\text{PutDown}(R_2)$: preconditions $\{ \text{At}(R, R_2), \text{Carrying}(R, B) \} \subseteq S_2$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Move}(R_2, R_1)$: precondition $\text{At}(R, R_2) \in S_2$? **Yes ✓** (but unnecessary, backtracking)
- $\text{PickUp}$: requires $\neg \text{Carrying}$. **No ✗**

---

**Step 3: Apply $\text{PutDown}(R_2)$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_3 &= (S_2 \setminus \text{Eff}^-(\text{PutDown}(R_2))) \cup \text{Eff}^+(\text{PutDown}(R_2)) \\
    &= (S_2 \setminus \{ \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}) \cup \{ \text{At}(B, R_2) \} \\
    &= \{ \text{At}(R, R_2), \; \text{At}(B, R_2), \; \neg \text{Carrying}(R, B) \}
\end{aligned}$$

Goal test: $\text{At}(B, R_2) \in S_3$? **Yes ✓ — GOAL REACHED!**

---

**The successful plan is:**
$$\pi = \langle \text{PickUp}(R_1), \; \text{Move}(R_1, R_2), \; \text{PutDown}(R_2) \rangle$$

This plan has **length 3** (number of actions). Branch 1 (using $\text{Move}$ first) led to a dead-end, and the algorithm had to backtrack.

---

#### 3.1.3 Heuristic Computation (Optional Enhancement for KTU)

Suppose the heuristic $h_{FF}$ (length of relaxed plan, ignoring delete lists) is used. In the relaxed world, dropping the box is unnecessary once carried (since $\text{At}(B, R_2)$ is in the goal and is achieved once we Move while carrying). The relaxed plan is:
$$\pi_{relax}(S_0) = \langle \text{PickUp}(R_1), \; \text{Move}(R_1, R_2) \rangle$$

So $h_{FF}(S_0) = 2$. This is **admissible** (it underestimates the true cost of 3) and helps the search prioritize the correct branch.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forward vs. Backward Search

| Criterion | Forward Search (Progression) | Backward Search (Regression) |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Initial state $S_0$ | Goal state $S_g$ |
| **Direction** | Forward in time | Backward in time |
| **Branching factor** | High (many applicable actions) | Lower (relevant actions only) |
| **States explored** | All reachable states | Only relevant states (regression set) |
| **Heuristic availability** | Many (h_FF, h_add, h_max, CG) | Fewer (mostly causal graph heuristics) |
| **Suitability for partially observable worlds** | Poor (needs full state) | Better (can use belief states) |
| **Best for** | Large action libraries, unknown goal distance | Small state spaces, irrelevant-action-heavy |
| **Examples of planners** | FF, Fast-Forward, LAMA, Fast-Downward | GOAP, partial-order planners |
| **Memory usage** | Higher | Lower |

---

### 3.3 Branching Factor Reduction Techniques

The **branching factor** of forward search is the number of applicable actions at each state. To make it tractable, planners use:

1. **Reachability pruning:** Discard actions whose effects are already true in $s$.
2. **Helpful actions pruning (FF):** Only consider actions that achieve some unachieved proposition in the relaxed plan.
3. **Action schema instantiation:** Only instantiate action parameters with objects relevant to the current state.
4. **Disjunctive pruning:** Avoid generating equivalent states via commuting actions.

The effective branching factor is:
$$b_{eff} = \mid \text{App}(s) \mid - \text{irrelevant actions} \leq b_{raw}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Forward State-Space Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State S0] --> B[Open List Contains S0]
    B --> C{Select State s from Open List}
    C --> D{Goal Test: s entails G?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Return Plan and Terminate]
    D -- No --> F[Compute Applicable Actions App s]
    F --> G[Generate Successor States using STRIPS Update]
    G --> H{State s prime already visited?}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I[Insert s prime into Open List]
    I --> C
    C --> J{Open List Empty?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Search Failure Return Null]
    J -- No --> C
```

**Caption:** The high-level control flow of forward state-space search. The loop continues until either the goal is reached (success) or the open list is exhausted (failure).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram: Worked Example Search Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State 0: Robot and Box in R1] --> A1[Apply PickUp R1]
    S0 --> A2[Apply Move R1 to R2]
    A1 --> S1[State 1: Carrying Box in R1]
    A1 --> S3[State 1b: Robot in R2 Box in R1 Dead End]
    A2 --> S3
    S1 --> A3[Apply PutDown R1]
    S1 --> A4[Apply Move R1 to R2]
    A3 --> S4[State 3: Box Placed in R1 Wrong Location]
    A4 --> S2[State 2: Carrying Box in R2]
    S2 --> A5[Apply PutDown R2]
    A5 --> Sg[State Goal: Box in R2 GOAL REACHED]
    Sg --> End1[Plan: PickUp, Move, PutDown]
    S3 --> End2[Backtrack to S0]
    S4 --> End2
    End2 --> S0
```

**Caption:** The full search tree for the Robot-and-Box problem. Shaded branches (dead ends) are pruned by the closed-set check; the highlighted path from $S_0$ to $S_g$ is the optimal plan.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Planning System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        P[Planning Problem]
    end
    subgraph Engine
        Q[State Representation Module]
        H[Heuristic Estimator hFF]
        S[Search Controller]
    end
    subgraph Output
        O[Plan: Sequence of Actions]
    end
    P --> Q
    Q --> S
    H --> S
    S --> O
```

**Caption:** Functional block diagram of a generic forward-search planner. The **Search Controller** is the heart of the engine; it queries the **Heuristic Estimator** to prioritize states and uses the **State Representation Module** to apply the STRIPS update.

---

### 4.4 State Transition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    St[State s] -->|Apply Action a: Pre a in s| Stp[State s prime]
    Stp -->|Update: Add Eff Plus minus Del Eff Minus| St
    Stp --> G{Goal G satisfied?}
    G -- Yes --> Done[Return Plan]
    G -- No --> Stp
```

**Caption:** Local view of one transition step. The action's preconditions are checked against the current state; if satisfied, the state is updated via the STRIPS add/delete lists.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define forward state-space search in AI planning. What are its key components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Forward state-space search (also called progression search) is a planning technique in which the algorithm begins at the **initial state** of the world and iteratively applies actions whose **preconditions** are satisfied, moving forward in time until a state is reached that satisfies all **goal** propositions. (1.5 marks)

Its four key components are:
1. **Initial State $S_0$:** the starting configuration of the world. (0.5 marks)
2. **Action Schema / Operators:** STRIPS-style actions with preconditions, add lists, and delete lists. (0.5 marks)
3. **Goal State $G$:** a set of fluents that must be achieved. (0.25 marks)
4. **Search Strategy:** the method used to explore the state space (BFS, DFS, A*, Greedy, etc.). (0.25 marks)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the STRIPS representation of an action. Why is it called the "STRIPS assumption"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the STRIPS representation, each action $a$ is defined by a 4-tuple:
$$\text{Action}(a) = \langle \text{Name}(a), \text{Pre}(a), \text{Eff}^+(a), \text{Eff}^-(a) \rangle$$
where $\text{Pre}(a)$ is the precondition, $\text{Eff}^+(a)$ is the **add list** (fluents made true), and $\text{Eff}^-(a)$ is the **delete list** (fluents made false). (2 marks)

The **STRIPS Assumption** states that any fluent not mentioned in the action's effect list remains unchanged after the action is applied. This solves the **frame problem** by default — everything stays the same unless explicitly modified. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** *Explain the forward state-space search algorithm in detail. How does it differ from backward (regression) search? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of forward search. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Consider the following STRIPS planning problem:*
- *Initial State: $\{$ On(A, B), On(B, Table), On(C, Table), Clear(A), Clear(C) $\}$*
- *Goal: $\{$ On(C, B) $\}$*
- *Actions available:*
  - *Stack$(x, y)$: pre: Clear$(x)$, Clear$(y)$; add: On$(x, y)$, Clear$(x)$; del: Clear$(y)$*
  - *Unstack$(x, y)$: pre: On$(x, y)$, Clear$(x)$; add: Holding$(x)$, Clear$(y)$; del: On$(x, y)$, Clear$(x)$*

*Trace the forward state-space search step-by-step until the goal is reached. Show all applicable actions and the resulting states. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(a):**

The **forward state-space search algorithm** (also known as **progression search**) is a planning technique that operates in the direction of time. The algorithm can be summarized as follows:

**Step 1:** Initialize the open list with the initial state $S_0$.

**Step 2:** Pop a state $s$ from the open list (the selection strategy depends on the search variant — BFS, DFS, A*, etc.).

**Step 3:** Apply the **goal test**: if $G \subseteq s$, return the plan that led to $s$.

**Step 4:** **Generate applicable actions** $\text{App}(s) = \{a \in A \mid \text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s\}$.

**Step 5:** For each applicable action $a$, compute the successor state:
$$s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$$

**Step 6:** Add $s'$ to the open list (if not in the closed set).

**Step 7:** Loop until goal found or open list empty.

**Comparison with Backward (Regression) Search:**

| Aspect | Forward | Backward |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Start → Goal | Goal → Start |
| Branching | Many applicable actions | Only relevant actions |
| Heuristics | Many available (h_FF, h_add) | Fewer |
| Best for | Unknown goal distance | Highly constrained goals |

**Advantages of Forward Search (2 marks):**
1. Conceptually simple and natural.
2. Compatible with rich, domain-independent heuristics.
3. Suitable for problems where the goal is not well-understood in advance.
4. Can leverage strong pruning techniques (helpful actions, relevance pruning).

**Disadvantages of Forward Search (1 mark):**
1. High branching factor due to many applicable actions.
2. Generates many irrelevant states.
3. Can be memory-intensive for large state spaces.
4. Inefficient when the goal is narrow but the action space is broad.

*(7 marks total — full marks for covering algorithm steps, comparison, advantages, and disadvantages.)*

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A(b):**

**Step 0: Initial state.**
$$S_0 = \{ \text{On}(A, B), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(A), \; \text{Clear}(C) \}$$
Goal: $\text{On}(C, B)$. Not in $S_0$. Continue.

**Identify $\text{App}(S_0)$:**
- $\text{Unstack}(A, B)$: pre $\{$ On(A,B), Clear(A) $\} \subseteq S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Unstack}(C, \text{Table})$: pre $\{$ On(C,Table), Clear(C) $\} \subseteq S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Stack}(A, C)$: pre $\{$ Clear(A), Clear(C) $\} \subseteq S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Stack}(A, \text{Table})$: pre $\{$ Clear(A), Clear(Table) $\}$ — is $\text{Clear}(Table)$ in $S_0$? **No ✗** (Table is covered by B)
- $\text{Stack}(C, A)$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(A) $\} \subseteq S_0$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Stack}(C, B)$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(B) $\}$ — is $\text{Clear}(B)$ in $S_0$? **No ✗** (A is on B)
- $\text{Stack}(C, \text{Table})$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(Table) $\}$ — Clear(Table)? **No ✗**

So $\text{App}(S_0) = \{ \text{Unstack}(A,B), \; \text{Unstack}(C,Table), \; \text{Stack}(A,C), \; \text{Stack}(C,A) \}$.

---

**Branch A: Apply $\text{Unstack}(A, B)$.**
$$S_A = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{On}(A,B), \text{Clear}(A) \}) \cup \{ \text{Holding}(A), \text{Clear}(B) \}$$
$$S_A = \{ \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(C), \; \text{Clear}(B), \; \text{Holding}(A) \}$$
Goal: $\text{On}(C,B) \in S_A$? **No.** Continue.

$\text{App}(S_A)$:
- $\text{Stack}(A, B)$: pre $\{$ Clear(A), Clear(B) $\}$ — Clear(A)? No (Holding). **No.**
- $\text{Stack}(A, C)$: pre $\{$ Clear(A), Clear(C) $\}$ — Clear(A)? **No ✗**
- $\text{Stack}(A, \text{Table})$: pre $\{$ Clear(A), Clear(Table) $\}$ — Clear(A)? **No ✗**

**Branch A is a dead end! Backtrack.**

---

**Branch B: Apply $\text{Unstack}(C, \text{Table})$.**
$$S_B = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \text{Clear}(C) \}) \cup \{ \text{Holding}(C), \text{Clear}(\text{Table}) \}$$
$$S_B = \{ \text{On}(A, B), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(A), \; \text{Clear}(\text{Table}), \; \text{Holding}(C) \}$$
Goal: $\text{On}(C, B) \in S_B$? **No.** Continue.

$\text{App}(S_B)$:
- $\text{Stack}(C, B)$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(B) $\}$ — is Clear(C) in $S_B$? **No, we are Holding(C).** So this is **not applicable** in standard STRIPS.
- $\text{Stack}(C, A)$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(A) $\}$ — Clear(C)? **No ✗**
- $\text{Stack}(C, \text{Table})$: pre $\{$ Clear(C), Clear(Table) $\}$ — Clear(C)? **No ✗**

**Branch B is also a dead end! Backtrack.**

---

**Branch C: Apply $\text{Stack}(A, C)$.**
$$S_C = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{Clear}(C) \}) \cup \{ \text{On}(A, C), \text{Clear}(A) \}$$
$$S_C = \{ \text{On}(A, B), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(A, C), \; \text{Clear}(A) \}$$

Wait — there is a logical issue: $A$ cannot be both on $B$ and on $C$. STRIPS would handle this as $A$ being on $C$ (the latest effect). Let me re-examine. With the proper delete list:
$$S_C = \{ \text{On}(A, C), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(A) \}$$
(Here $\text{On}(A,B)$ is deleted, but in STRIPS we only delete from the add list, not the explicit delete list given. Using only the listed delete list, we get:)
$$S_C = \{ \text{On}(A, B), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(A), \; \text{On}(A, C) \}$$
which is inconsistent. In well-defined problems, this is impossible, so we consider this branch infeasible.

**Branch C fails due to inconsistency. Backtrack.**

---

**Branch D: Apply $\text{Stack}(C, A)$.**
$$S_D = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{Clear}(A) \}) \cup \{ \text{On}(C, A), \text{Clear}(C) \}$$
$$S_D = \{ \text{On}(A, B), \; \text{On}(B, \text{Table}), \; \text{On}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Clear}(C), \; \text{On}(C, A) \}$$

Similarly, $C$ cannot be both on Table and on $A$. **Inconsistent. Backtrack.**

---

**Conclusion:** With the given action set, **no plan exists** to achieve $\text{On}(C, B)$ from $S_0$ without first moving $A$ off $B$ to free up the precondition $\text{Clear}(B)$. The correct plan requires the actions to be defined to also handle the held-state transitions. In a fully specified version:

**The intended solution (with extended action set including PutDown/Hold transitions):**
$$\pi = \langle \text{Unstack}(A, B), \; \text{Stack}(A, \text{Table}), \; \text{Unstack}(C, \text{Table}), \; \text{Stack}(C, B) \rangle$$

The plan length is **4 actions**. *(7 marks for showing all 4 branches, identifying dead ends, and arriving at the correct plan structure.)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *What are the key heuristics used in forward state-space search? Explain the relaxed planning graph heuristic ($h_{FF}$) with an example. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For a logistics problem with the following STRIPS specification:*
- *Initial: $\{$ At(Package, Hub1), At(Truck, Hub1) $\}$*
- *Goal: $\{$ At(Package, Hub2) $\}$*
- *Actions:*
  - *Load$(pkg, tr, loc)$: pre At(pkg,loc), At(tr,loc); eff: In(pkg,tr), ¬At(pkg,loc)*
  - *Unload$(pkg, tr, loc)$: pre In(pkg,tr), At(tr,loc); eff: At(pkg,loc), ¬In(pkg,tr)*
  - *Drive$(tr, from, to)$: pre At(tr,from); eff: At(tr,to), ¬At(tr,from)*

*Show how forward state-space search with the **Greedy Best-First** strategy and heuristic $h$ = number of unsatisfied goals would expand the search. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(a):**

**Heuristics in Forward State-Space Search:**

Heuristics estimate the cost of reaching a goal from a given state. In STRIPS-style planning, three main families of heuristics are used:

**1. Delete-Relaxation Heuristics** (most popular, introduced by Hoffmann in FF planner, 2001):
These heuristics **ignore delete lists**, meaning fluents once true stay true forever. This makes the relaxed problem much easier (polynomial to solve) and provides an admissible and consistent estimate.

- **$h_{add}(s)$:** Sum over all goal propositions of the cost of achieving each one independently in the relaxed problem.
$$h_{add}(s) = \sum_{g \in G} C(s, g)$$
- **$h_{max}(s)$:** Maximum over all goal propositions of the cost of achieving each one.
$$h_{max}(s) = \max_{g \in G} C(s, g)$$
- **$h_{FF}(s)$:** Length of the **relaxed plan** (the set of actions extracted from the planning graph that achieves all goals).
$$h_{FF}(s) = \mid \pi_{relax}(s) \mid$$

**2. Critical Path Heuristics (Causal Graph Heuristics):** Use the **causal graph** (a directed graph of variable dependencies) to compute distances.

**3. Landmarks Heuristics:** Count the number of **landmarks** (propositions that must be true at some point in any plan) not yet achieved.

---

**Example of $h_{FF}$ computation:**

Consider the Robot-and-Box problem from Section 3.1.

In the **relaxed problem** (no delete lists), after $\text{PickUp}(R_1)$, the box stays at $R_1$ AND the robot carries it. After $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$, the robot is at $R_1$ AND $R_2$. So in the relaxed world, the goal $\text{At}(B, R_2)$ is achieved by the single action $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$ — no need for $\text{PickUp}$ or $\text{PutDown}$ because the box never "leaves" $R_1$ in the relaxed world.

Wait — re-examining: actually in the relaxed problem, the box is *already* at $R_1$ and stays there. We need it at $R_2$. The relaxed action $\text{Move}(R_1, R_2)$'s effect (in the relaxed sense) makes the robot at $R_2$, but doesn't move the box. So we still need to **Carry** the box, which still requires $\text{PickUp}$ and $\text{Move}$. Thus:
$$h_{FF}(S_0) = \mid \{ \text{PickUp}, \text{Move}, \text{PutDown} \} \mid = 3$$

This matches the true optimal plan length, making $h_{FF}$ very informative for this problem. *(7 marks for explanation, formulas, and worked example.)*

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B(b):**

The heuristic used is:
$$h(s) = \mid G \setminus s \mid = \text{number of unsatisfied goals in state } s$$

For our problem, $G = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub2)} \}$, so $h(s) \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Initial state $S_0$:**
$$S_0 = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub1)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub1)} \}$$
$$h(S_0) = \mid \{ \text{At(Package, Hub2)} \} \setminus S_0 \mid = 1$$

**Greedy Best-First** expands the state with the **lowest $h$** first. With $h$ binary, multiple states may tie. Tie-breaking order is shown below.

**Step 1: Open list = $\{ S_0 \}$. Pop $S_0$. $h(S_0) = 1$. Goal not reached.**

Applicable actions in $S_0$:
- $\text{Load(Package, Truck, Hub1)}$: preconditions satisfied? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Drive(Truck, Hub1, Hub2)}$: precondition $\text{At(Truck, Hub1)}$? **Yes ✓**
- $\text{Unload}$: requires $\text{In(Package, Truck)}$, not in $S_0$. **No ✗**

**Branch 1: Apply $\text{Load(Package, Truck, Hub1)}$.**
$$S_1 = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{At(Package, Hub1)} \}) \cup \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)} \}$$
$$S_1 = \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub1)} \}$$
$$h(S_1) = 1$$ (Package still not at Hub2)

**Branch 2: Apply $\text{Drive(Truck, Hub1, Hub2)}$.**
$$S_2 = (S_0 \setminus \{ \text{At(Truck, Hub1)} \}) \cup \{ \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$$
$$S_2 = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub1)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$$
$$h(S_2) = 1$$

**Open list = $\{S_1, S_2\}$. Pop the first (assume $S_1$).**

**Step 2: $S_1 = \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)}, \text{At(Truck, Hub1)} \}$. $h(S_1) = 1$.**

Applicable actions in $S_1$:
- $\text{Drive(Truck, Hub1, Hub2)}$: **Yes ✓**

**Branch 1a: Apply $\text{Drive}$.**
$$S_3 = \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$$
$$h(S_3) = 1$$

(No $\text{Load}$ since already loaded.)

**Open list = $\{S_2, S_3\}$. Pop the first ($S_2$).**

**Step 3: $S_2 = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub1)}, \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$. $h(S_2) = 1$.**

Applicable actions in $S_2$:
- $\text{Load(Package, Truck, Hub2)}$: requires $\text{At(Package, Hub2)}$? **No ✗**
- $\text{Drive(Truck, Hub2, Hub1)}$: **Yes ✓** (but backtracks)
- $\text{Unload}$: requires $\text{In(Package, Truck)}$? **No ✗**

**Branch 2a: Apply $\text{Drive(Truck, Hub2, Hub1)}$.**
$$S_4 = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub1)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub1)} \} = S_0$$ (already visited!)

**Open list = $\{S_3\}$. Pop $S_3$.**

**Step 4: $S_3 = \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)}, \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$. $h(S_3) = 1$.**

Applicable actions in $S_3$:
- $\text{Unload(Package, Truck, Hub2)}$: preconditions satisfied? **Yes ✓**

**Branch 3a: Apply $\text{Unload}$.**
$$S_5 = (S_3 \setminus \{ \text{In(Package, Truck)} \}) \cup \{ \text{At(Package, Hub2)} \}$$
$$S_5 = \{ \text{At(Package, Hub2)}, \; \text{At(Truck, Hub2)} \}$$
$$h(S_5) = 0 \longrightarrow \text{GOAL REACHED!}$$

**Final Plan:**
$$\pi = \langle \text{Load(Package, Truck, Hub1)}, \; \text{Drive(Truck, Hub1, Hub2)}, \; \text{Unload(Package, Truck, Hub2)} \rangle$$
**Plan length = 3 actions.** *(7 marks for full trace, heuristic values, and identifying the plan.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common places students lose marks on this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the closed-set (visited) check.** If you re-expand already visited states, your search becomes incorrect and inefficient. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Mishandling the STRIPS update.** Many students write $s' = s \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$ and forget to **remove** $\text{Eff}^-(a)$. Always use the full formula $s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$. Deducted: 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Confusing forward vs. backward search.** Writing regression-style "choose action that achieves a goal" inside a forward search question loses full conceptual credit.
> 
> 4. **Not listing *all* applicable actions in a state.** Examiners expect exhaustive enumeration. Missing actions = −1.5 marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting goal test results.** After computing $s'$, explicitly write "Goal test: G ⊆ s'? Yes/No". A full trace must include this.
> 
> 6. **In the heuristic question, confusing $h_{add}$ with $h_{max}$.** Memorize: $h_{add}$ is a **sum**; $h_{max}$ is a **maximum**.
> 
> 7. **Failing to state the STRIPS Assumption explicitly** when asked about frame problem. Always mention it for full marks.
> 
> 8. **Confusing "applicable action" with "useful action".** An applicable action may be useless (not contributing to the goal). Heuristic-guided search uses **helpful actions**, not all applicable ones.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Forward State-Space Search for Planning**

- [ ] **Definition:** Forward search = progression from initial state toward goal via applicable actions.
- [ ] **STRIPS Tuple:** $\langle \text{Name}, \text{Pre}, \text{Eff}^+, \text{Eff}^- \rangle$ — memorize the four components.
- [ ] **State Update Rule:** $s' = (s \setminus \text{Eff}^-(a)) \cup \text{Eff}^+(a)$.
- [ ] **Applicability:** Action $a$ applicable iff $\text{Pre}(a) \subseteq s$.
- [ ] **Goal Satisfaction:** State $s$ satisfies goal $G$ iff $G \subseteq s$.
- [ ] **Algorithm Steps:** Initialize → Select → Goal Test → Expand → Update → Closed-Set Check → Loop.
- [ ] **STRIPS Assumption:** Anything not in the effect lists is unchanged (**frame problem** solved by default).
- [ ] **Three Delete-Relaxation Heuristics:** $h_{add}$ (sum), $h_{max}$ (max), $h_{FF}$ (relaxed plan length).
- [ ] **A* Evaluation:** $f(s) = g(s) + h(s)$; Greedy: $f(s) = h(s)$.
- [ ] **Enforced Hill Climbing:** Used in the FF planner; only accepts strictly improving successors.
- [ ] **Forward vs. Backward:** Forward = many applicable actions, strong heuristics; Backward = fewer actions, harder heuristics.
- [ ] **Branching Factor:** Reduced by helpful actions, relevance pruning, and schema instantiation.
- [ ] **Search Complexity:** BFS/DFS in worst case $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$.
- [ ] **Famous Planners:** STRIPS (1971), Graphplan (1995), FF (2001), Fast-Downward (2006), LAMA (2008).
- [ ] **Real-World Uses:** Robotics, logistics, game AI, manufacturing, spacecraft control.
- [ ] **Key Properties:** Soundness, Completeness (with visited-check), Optimality (only with A* + admissible $h$).
- [ ] **Closed Set:** Mandatory to prevent infinite loops and redundant work.
- [ ] **Most Common Exam Pitfall:** Forgetting the delete step in the STRIPS update formula.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "trace forward state-space search" in a KTU exam, follow this exact structure for maximum marks: **(1)** Show $S_0$ explicitly. **(2)** Goal test. **(3)** List ALL applicable actions (in a table). **(4)** For each, compute $s'$ with the full update formula. **(5)** Goal test on each $s'$. **(6)** Continue until goal reached. **(7)** State the final plan and its length. Following this template guarantees full marks on any forward search trace question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backward Search for Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Backward Search for Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backward Search (also called Regression Search)** is a planning strategy in which the search begins from the **Goal State** and proceeds *backwards* toward the **Initial State** by repeatedly selecting an action whose **add-effect** satisfies a current sub-goal, then replacing that sub-goal with the action's **preconditions**.

In the formal STRIPS-style notation, if $G_k$ is the current regression (sub-goal) state and $a$ is the chosen action, the next regression state is computed as:

$$G_{k+1} \;=\; \bigl(G_k \;-\; \text{Add}(a)\bigr) \;\cup\; \text{Pre}(a) \;\cup\; \bigl(G_k \;\cap\; \text{Del}(a)\bigr)$$

This is the foundational mechanism used in **Partial-Order Planning (POP)** and **regression-based planners**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Solving a Maze from the Exit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are standing at the *entrance* of a maze (Initial State) and want to reach the *exit* (Goal). 
> - **Forward Search** = Walk forward, trying every possible path until you find the exit. You explore the *future*. 
> - **Backward Search** = Stand at the exit, and ask: *"What was the room I must have come from?"* Then ask: *"What was the room before that?"* — until you trace back to the entrance. 
> 
> This reverse-tracing is **Regression Search**.

Because planning problems often have many possible *future* states (branching factor of forward search is very large) but only a *few* relevant sub-goals at any time, working from the goal is usually much more focused.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Regression State** | The set of sub-goals (literals) that must be true just before an action executes. |
| **Relevant Action** | An action whose **add-list** contributes a literal to the current sub-goal, and which does not **clobber** (delete) anything needed. |
| **Clobbering** | When an action deletes a literal that is still required by another open sub-goal. |
| **STRIPS Operator** | Tuple $\langle \text{Name, Pre, Add, Del} \rangle$ describing a planning action. |
| **Established Literal** | A literal made true in the plan and never violated afterwards. |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4) specifically requires students to understand *how regression states are constructed*, *how relevant actions are identified*, and *why backward search is often preferred over forward search* in classical planning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Regression Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The algorithm proceeds through a tight loop of decisions:

1. **Start** with $G_0 = \text{Goal State}$.
2. **Pick a sub-goal** literal $g \in G_k$ that is *not yet* true in the Initial State.
3. **Choose a relevant action** $a$ such that:
   - $g \in \text{Add}(a)$  
   - $\text{Add}(a) \cap G_k \setminus \{g\} \neq \emptyset$ does not introduce redundancy without need (preferably each add should match exactly).
   - $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$ (the action must **not clobber** any open sub-goal).
   - $\text{Pre}(a) \not\subseteq G_k$ (otherwise the action is irrelevant — its preconditions are already assumed).
4. **Compute the new regression state**:
   
$$G_{k+1} = (G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \;\cup\; \text{Pre}(a)$$

5. **Insert** $a$ at the *front* of the partial plan.
6. **Repeat** until $G_k \subseteq \text{Initial State}$.
7. If no relevant action exists for some sub-goal, **backtrack** and try a different action.

## 2.2 Conditions for an Action to be "Relevant"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An action $a$ is **relevant** to sub-goal $g$ in regression state $G_k$ if **all** of the following hold:
> 1. $g \in \text{Add}(a)$ — the action *achieves* the sub-goal.
> 2. $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$ — the action does *not* delete any needed literal.
> 3. $\text{Pre}(a) \not\subseteq G_k$ — the action is not trivially satisfied; it introduces *new* work.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Mathematical Form | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regression state update | $G_{k+1} = (G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$ | New sub-goal set after applying $a$ backwards | Core recursion in POP, Graphplan |
| Clobber test | $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$ | Detects threat to an open sub-goal | Used in plan verification |
| Relevance test | $g \in \text{Add}(a)$ and $\text{Pre}(a) \not\subseteq G_k$ | Identifies candidate actions | Prunes search space |
| Branching factor bound | $\vert B_{\text{back}} \vert \leq \vert B_{\text{forward}} \vert$ | Backward search is no wider than forward in goal-driven domains | Justifies choice in SAT/SMT planners |
| Plan size complexity | $O(\vert G_0 \vert \cdot \vert A \vert)$ where $A$ = action set | Worst-case number of expansions | Theoretical planner bounds |

> [!TIP]
> The notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ above denotes the *cardinality* (set size). In markdown tables the vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table grid.

## 2.4 Why Backward Search is Often Preferred

- **Focused search space:** Only sub-goals matter, not every world fact.
- **Smaller branching factor:** Typically $\vert B_{\text{back}} \vert \ll \vert B_{\text{forward}} \vert$ in goal-driven problems.
- **Causal reasoning:** Naturally builds **causal links** $a \xrightarrow{g} a'$ (used in POP).
- **Industry relevance:** Forms the mathematical backbone of modern planners like **Fast Downward**, **LPG**, and the regression module of **Graphplan**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Problem Setup (STRIPS-Style)

Consider a simple **Delivery Robot** domain. The robot must reach the market and have drinks, starting from home with a banana.

### 3.1.1 Initial State
$$I \;=\; \{\, \text{At(Home)},\; \text{Have(Banana)} \,\}$$

### 3.1.2 Goal State
$$G \;=\; \{\, \text{At(Market)},\; \text{Have(Drinks)} \,\}$$

### 3.1.3 Action Schemas (STRIPS Notation)

| Action | Preconditions | Add-Effects | Delete-Effects |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Go}(x, y)$ | $\text{At}(x)$ | $\text{At}(y)$ | $\text{At}(x)$ |
| $\text{Buy}(x)$ | $\text{At(Market)}$ | $\text{Have}(x)$ | $\emptyset$ |

> In our world, $x, y \in \{\text{Home}, \text{Market}\}$.

## 3.2 Backward Search Execution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

### ▶ Iteration 0 — Initial Regression State

$$G_0 \;=\; \{\, \text{At(Market)},\; \text{Have(Drinks)} \,\}$$

Both literals are *not* in $I$, so we proceed.

---

### ▶ Iteration 1 — Pick Sub-Goal $\text{Have(Drinks)}$

**Step 1.1** — Find relevant action.  
$\text{Have(Drinks)} \in \text{Add}(\text{Buy(Drinks)})$, so $\text{Buy(Drinks)}$ is a candidate.  

**Step 1.2** — Clobber test: $\text{Del}(\text{Buy(Drinks)}) \cap G_0 = \emptyset \cap G_0 = \emptyset$. ✅ Passes.  

**Step 1.3** — Pre-condition redundancy: $\text{Pre}(\text{Buy(Drinks)}) = \{\text{At(Market)}\} \not\subseteq G_0$. ✅ Passes.  

**Step 1.4** — Compute next regression state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 \;&= \; (G_0 \setminus \text{Add}(\text{Buy(Drinks)})) \;\cup\; \text{Pre}(\text{Buy(Drinks)}) \\
      \;&= \; \bigl(\{\text{At(Market)}, \text{Have(Drinks)}\} \setminus \{\text{Have(Drinks)}\}\bigr) \;\cup\; \{\text{At(Market)}\} \\
      \;&= \; \{\text{At(Market)}\} \;\cup\; \{\text{At(Market)}\} \\
      \;&= \; \{\text{At(Market)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.5** — Partial plan so far: $P_1 = \langle\,\text{Buy(Drinks)}\,\rangle$.

---

### ▶ Iteration 2 — Pick Sub-Goal $\text{At(Market)}$

**Step 2.1** — Find relevant action.  
$\text{At(Market)} \in \text{Add}(\text{Go(Home, Market)})$, so this action is relevant.  

**Step 2.2** — Clobber test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Del}(\text{Go(Home, Market)}) \cap G_1 
&= \{\text{At(Home)}\} \cap \{\text{At(Market)}\} \\
&= \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Passes.  

**Step 2.3** — Pre-condition redundancy: $\text{Pre}(\text{Go(Home, Market)}) = \{\text{At(Home)}\} \not\subseteq G_1 = \{\text{At(Market)}\}$. ✅ Passes.  

**Step 2.4** — Compute next regression state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_2 \;&= \; (G_1 \setminus \text{Add}(\text{Go(Home, Market)})) \;\cup\; \text{Pre}(\text{Go(Home, Market)}) \\
      \;&= \; \bigl(\{\text{At(Market)}\} \setminus \{\text{At(Market)}\}\bigr) \;\cup\; \{\text{At(Home)}\} \\
      \;&= \; \emptyset \;\cup\; \{\text{At(Home)}\} \\
      \;&= \; \{\text{At(Home)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.5** — Partial plan so far: $P_2 = \langle\,\text{Go(Home, Market)},\; \text{Buy(Drinks)}\,\rangle$.

---

### ▶ Iteration 3 — Termination Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_2 \subseteq I 
&\iff \{\text{At(Home)}\} \subseteq \{\text{At(Home)}, \text{Have(Banana)}\} \\
&\iff \text{True}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ All sub-goals are satisfied by the Initial State. **Algorithm terminates with success.**

## 3.3 Final Extracted Plan

$$
\boxed{\;\Pi \;=\; \bigl[\;\text{Go(Home, Market)},\;\; \text{Buy(Drinks)}\;\bigr]\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The plan is read **forward in time**: first move from home to market, then buy drinks. The order is preserved by *prepending* each chosen action to the plan list during the backward search.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Iteration $k$ | Sub-Goal $g$ | Chosen Action $a$ | Clobber Test | New $G_{k+1}$ | Plan $\Pi$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | — | — | — | $\{ \text{At(Market)}, \text{Have(Drinks)} \}$ | [] |
| 1 | $\text{Have(Drinks)}$ | $\text{Buy(Drinks)}$ | Pass | $\{ \text{At(Market)} \}$ | $[\text{Buy(Drinks)}]$ |
| 2 | $\text{At(Market)}$ | $\text{Go(Home, Market)}$ | Pass | $\{ \text{At(Home)} \}$ | $[\text{Go(Home, Market)}, \text{Buy(Drinks)}]$ |
| 3 | — | — | — | $\subseteq I$ — Done | Final |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backward Search Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Goal State G0] --> B[Set Regression State RS to G0]
    B --> C[Initialize Plan P as empty list]
    C --> D{RS subset of Initial State I?}
    D -- Yes --> Z[RETURN Plan P and exit]
    D -- No  --> E[Select a sub-goal literal g from RS]
    E --> F[Search action set for candidate a where g is in Add a]
    F --> G{Clobber test: Del a intersect RS equals empty?}
    G -- No  --> F
    G -- Yes --> H{Pre a subset RS? If yes, action is trivial}
    H -- Yes --> F
    H -- No  --> I[Compute new RS equals RS minus Add a union Pre a]
    I --> J[Prepend action a to Plan P]
    J --> D
```

## 4.2 Regression State Evolution (Robot Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["RS0: At Market AND Have Drinks"] -->|Buy Drinks| N1["RS1: At Market"]
    N1 -->|Go Home Market| N2["RS2: At Home"]
    N2 -->|Matches Initial State| N3["STOP: Plan Found"]
    N0 -.Goal.-> N0
    N3 -.Final Plan.-> P["Go Home Market, Buy Drinks"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Regression Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN1[Input Layer]
        I1[Initial State]
        I2[Goal Description]
        I3[Action Schemas]
    end

    subgraph CORE1[Regression Engine]
        R1[Sub-Goal Selector]
        R2[Relevant Action Finder]
        R3[Clobbering Detector]
        R4[Regression State Updater]
        R5[Plan Builder Stack]
    end

    subgraph OUT1[Output Layer]
        O1[Ordered Action Sequence]
        O2[Plan Verification Log]
    end

    I1 --> R1
    I2 --> R1
    I3 --> R2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R1
    R5 --> O1
    R3 --> O2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The architecture above is a **block-level functional map**. In real systems (e.g., Graphplan, Fast Downward), each box maps to a dedicated module handling heuristic computation, causal-link tracking, and threat resolution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Q, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]`

**Define backward search in planning. State one key advantage it has over forward search.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Backward search, also called **regression search**, is a planning technique that begins at the **goal state** and works backwards to the **initial state**. At each step, an action is selected whose **add-effect** satisfies a chosen sub-goal, and its **preconditions** become the new sub-goals. Formally, $G_{k+1} = (G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$.
>
> **Advantage (1 mark):** The branching factor is typically much smaller than forward search because only actions relevant to the current sub-goal are considered, leading to a more focused and efficient search — especially in domains with large initial-state spaces.

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Q, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]`

**What is a "relevant action" in backward search? List the two essential conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **relevant action** is one that can be used to *achieve* a sub-goal in the current regression state. Two essential conditions:
>
> 1. **Achiever condition:** The sub-goal literal $g$ must be in the action's add-list, i.e. $g \in \text{Add}(a)$. (1.5 marks)
> 2. **Non-clobbering condition:** The action's delete-list must not contain any literal currently in the regression state, i.e. $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$. (1.5 marks)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ✅ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the **backward search algorithm** for classical planning in detail. Use STRIPS notation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **relevant actions** and **clobbering** with suitable examples. Show how a wrong choice of action leads to plan failure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ▶ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Backward Search Algorithm

**Algorithm Outline (3 marks):**

The algorithm operates as a regression loop:

1. Initialise $G_0 = \text{Goal}$, Plan $P = \langle \rangle$.
2. While $G_k \not\subseteq I$ (Initial State):
   - Pick sub-goal $g \in G_k$.
   - Choose relevant action $a$.
   - Update $G_{k+1} = (G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$.
   - Prepend $a$ to $P$.
3. If a relevant action cannot be found, **backtrack** and pick an alternative.
4. On success, return $P$.

**Formal Update Rule (2 marks):**

$$
G_{k+1} = \bigl(G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)\bigr) \;\cup\; \text{Pre}(a) \;\cup\; \bigl(G_k \cap \text{Del}(a)\bigr)
$$

The third term is added only to *track* threats; in pure regression we usually enforce $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$.

**Complexity (2 marks):** In the worst case, complexity is exponential in $\vert G_0 \vert$, but in practice heuristic pruning (e.g., $h_{add}$, $h_{max}$) reduces it to polynomial for many benchmarks.

---

#### ▶ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Relevant Actions & Clobbering

**Definition (2 marks):** A relevant action $a$ for sub-goal $g$ satisfies:
- $g \in \text{Add}(a)$
- $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$ (no clobbering)
- $\text{Pre}(a) \not\subseteq G_k$ (introduces new work)

**Clobbering Example (3 marks):** Consider $G_k = \{\text{At(Market)}, \text{Have(Banana)}\}$. If we choose action $\text{Go(Home, Market)}$ to achieve $\text{At(Market)}$, it does **not** delete $\text{Have(Banana)}$, so it is safe. But if instead we had an action $\text{Buy(Milk)}$ that had $\text{Del}(\text{Buy(Milk)}) = \{\text{Have(Banana)}\}$, applying it would **clobber** the sub-goal $\text{Have(Banana)}$. The algorithm must therefore reject such an action.

**Plan Failure Illustration (2 marks):** Selecting a clobbering action forces the algorithm to add a *re-achievement* step, increasing plan length and possibly causing loops. If no non-clobbering action exists, the planner must backtrack to a previous decision point.

---

### ✅ **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Compare **forward search** and **backward search** in planning. Under what conditions is backward search clearly preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **backward search algorithm** to the following STRIPS problem and obtain the plan. **(7 marks)**

> - **Initial State:** $\{ \text{At(Home)},\; \text{Have(Milk)} \}$
> - **Goal State:** $\{ \text{At(Market)},\; \text{Have(Drinks)} \}$
> - **Actions:** $\text{Go}(x,y)$ with pre $\text{At}(x)$, add $\text{At}(y)$, del $\text{At}(x)$; $\text{Buy}(x)$ with pre $\text{At(Market)}$, add $\text{Have}(x)$.

---

#### ▶ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Comparison

| Aspect | Forward Search | Backward Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Start point** | Initial State $I$ | Goal State $G$ |
| **Direction** | Forward chaining | Regression |
| **Branching factor** | $\vert \text{Actions} \vert$ (often huge) | $\vert \text{Relevant actions} \vert$ (small) |
| **State representation** | Full world state | Set of sub-goals (literals) |
| **Preferred when** | Many goals, small initial state | Few goals, large initial state |
| **Used in** | State-space planners (e.g., BFS, A*) | POP, Graphplan, SAT planners |

**Conditions where backward is preferred (3 marks):**
- Goal has only a *few* literals.
- Initial state is large (many irrelevant facts).
- Many actions exist, but few are relevant to a given sub-goal.
- Causal structure is more important than world-trajectory.

---

#### ▶ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Worked Backward Search

**Step 0:** $G_0 = \{\text{At(Market)}, \text{Have(Drinks)}\}$

**Step 1:** Sub-goal $\text{Have(Drinks)}$ → action $\text{Buy(Drinks)}$.

$$
G_1 = (G_0 \setminus \{\text{Have(Drinks)}\}) \cup \{\text{At(Market)}\} = \{\text{At(Market)}\}
$$

**Step 2:** Sub-goal $\text{At(Market)}$ → action $\text{Go(Home, Market)}$.

$$
G_2 = (G_1 \setminus \{\text{At(Market)}\}) \cup \{\text{At(Home)}\} = \{\text{At(Home)}\}
$$

**Step 3:** $G_2 = \{\text{At(Home)}\} \subseteq I = \{\text{At(Home)}, \text{Have(Milk)}\}$ ✅

**Final Plan:**

$$
\Pi = \bigl[\, \text{Go(Home, Market)},\; \text{Buy(Drinks)} \,\bigr]
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the clobber test:** Many students apply an action without checking $\text{Del}(a) \cap G_k = \emptyset$. This loses **2 marks** per occurrence in Part B. 
> 2. **Wrong plan order:** The plan must be built by *prepending* — many students write the actions in *reverse* order, losing **1–2 marks**. 
> 3. **Stopping too early:** Termination requires $G_k \subseteq I$, not equality. Missing this loses **1 mark**. 
> 4. **Mixing up regression state with world state:** Regression state is a *set of sub-goals*, not a complete world snapshot. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. 
> 5. **Not stating relevance conditions explicitly:** In 7-mark questions, listing the three conditions (achieve, non-clobber, non-trivial pre) is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Backward search = Regression Search** — starts at the goal, ends at the initial state.
- ✅ The **regression update rule** is $G_{k+1} = (G_k \setminus \text{Add}(a)) \cup \text{Pre}(a)$.
- ✅ An action is **relevant** if it achieves a sub-goal, doesn't clobber, and has non-trivial preconditions.
- ✅ **Clobbering** = an action deleting a needed literal in the current regression state.
- ✅ Backward search has a **smaller effective branching factor** than forward search in goal-driven domains.
- ✅ It is the **mathematical foundation of Partial-Order Planning (POP)** and Graphplan.
- ✅ Plan is built by **prepending** actions during backward iteration.
- ✅ Termination occurs when $G_k \subseteq I$ (not just when $G_k = I$).
- ✅ Always **state all three relevance conditions** in 7-mark answers for full credit.
- ✅ Use **STRIPS notation** $\langle \text{Name, Pre, Add, Del} \rangle$ in all derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning as Boolean Satisfiability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning as Boolean Satisfiability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Planning as Boolean Satisfiability (SAT-based Planning)** is a paradigm in **Classical AI Planning** in which a **STRIPS-style planning problem** $\Pi = \langle \Sigma, s_0, G, A \rangle$ is reduced (compiled) into a **propositional Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problem** $\Phi_k$ parameterized by a fixed plan horizon $k$. A SAT solver is then invoked to find a model (truth assignment). If $\Phi_k$ is **SATISFIABLE**, the satisfying assignment is decoded back into a valid sequential or parallel plan of length $\leq k$. The process is iterated with $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until either a plan is found (optimal in the number of time steps) or a maximum bound is reached.

The encoding converts the planning problem into a **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** Boolean formula — a conjunction of disjunctive clauses — over two families of propositional variables:
- **State (fluent) variables** $F^{t}$ — a fluent $F$ true at time step $t$.
- **Action variables** $A^{t}$ — an action $A$ executed at time step $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard SATPLAN formulation is credited to **Henry Kautz and Bart Selman (1992, 1996)**. It established that general-purpose SAT solvers (e.g., DPLL, CDCL, WalkSAT, MiniSAT) can outperform hand-crafted planning heuristics in many domains, making SAT the **dominant underlying engine** for several award-winning planners.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Lights and Switches" Puzzle**
> Imagine you are locked in a room with five unlabeled light switches and five lamps, and you have exactly **three minutes** to figure out which switch controls which lamp. You cannot enter the room more than once. The classical brute-force approach is hopeless.
>
> However, suppose you are allowed to **build a giant Boolean formula** in which every variable represents a question like *"Did I touch switch #3 before entering the room?"* or *"Is lamp #2 ON right now?"* — and every clause represents a logical constraint such as *"If I touched switch #3, then lamp #2 must be ON."* A SAT solver is then a super-fast **automatic puzzle-solver** that finds a consistent combination of TRUE/FALSE answers to all variables.
>
> **Planning as SAT works exactly this way.** Each "question" is *"Is fluent $F$ true at time $t$?"* or *"Is action $A$ executed at time $t$?"* Each "constraint" is a logical rule of the planning domain (preconditions, effects, persistence). The SAT solver, instead of performing a slow state-space search, **collapses the entire planning problem into a single logical formula** and uses clever deduction to find a satisfying truth assignment — i.e., a valid plan.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|:-----|:--------|:-------|
| **Fluent** | A Boolean ground fact (a state variable) | $F$ |
| **Planning Horizon** | Maximum number of time steps assumed | $k$ |
| **CNF** | Conjunctive Normal Form: AND of ORs of literals | $\Phi_k$ |
| **Clause** | A disjunction of literals in the CNF | $(l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \ldots \vee l_m)$ |
| **Literal** | A Boolean variable or its negation | $F^t$ or $\neg F^t$ |
| **Frame Axiom** | Logical rule saying "what is not changed remains" | $A \rightarrow (F^{t+1} \leftrightarrow F^t)$ |
| **SAT Solver** | Algorithm that decides Boolean satisfiability | MiniSAT, Glucose, etc. |
| **STRIPS** | Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver — action language | — |

### 1.4 Why Convert Planning to SAT? — Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Motivation**
> 1. **Performance:** Modern SAT solvers (CDCL-based) routinely handle millions of clauses — encoding planning exploits decades of SAT solver engineering.
> 2. **Generality:** SAT is **domain-independent**; the same solver handles logistics, blocks-world, scheduling, hardware verification, etc.
> 3. **Theoretical Elegance:** Planning becomes *just another NP-complete problem* (in the bounded-length case $k$ fixed), enabling reuse of the vast Boolean reasoning literature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of the SAT Encoding

A complete SAT encoding $\Phi_k$ of a planning problem with horizon $k$ is the **conjunction of six families of axioms**. Each axiom family constrains a different physical/logical aspect of the world evolution.

#### 2.1.1 Initial-State Axioms (Time Step 0)

For every fluent $F$:
- If $F \in s_0$, assert: $F^0$
- If $F \notin s_0$, assert: $\neg F^0$

> These axioms **freeze** the world at its starting configuration.

#### 2.1.2 Goal Axioms (Time Step $k$)

For every fluent $F$ in the goal $G$:
- Assert: $F^k$

> These axioms **declare** what must hold at the end of the plan.

#### 2.1.3 Precondition Axioms

For every action $A$ and every time step $t \in [0, k-1]$, for every precondition fluent $P \in \text{pre}(A)$:
- Assert: $A^t \rightarrow P^t$

In CNF (clause form): $(\neg A^t \vee P^t)$

> **Meaning:** An action cannot fire unless its preconditions are satisfied.

#### 2.1.4 Effect Axioms

For every action $A$, time $t$, and every fluent $F \in \text{eff}^+(A)$ (add-list) and $F \in \text{eff}^-(A)$ (delete-list):
- **Add effect:** $A^t \rightarrow F^{t+1}$  ⇒  $(\neg A^t \vee F^{t+1})$
- **Delete effect:** $A^t \rightarrow \neg F^{t+1}$  ⇒  $(\neg A^t \vee \neg F^{t+1})$

> **Meaning:** If the action executes, its consequences must appear in the next time step.

#### 2.1.5 Frame Axioms (Persistence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame Axioms are the most subtle and the most crucial component.** They state that *fluents not modified by an action remain unchanged* — the famous **"Frame Problem"** of classical AI.

**Forward frame axiom** — "if a fluent becomes true, some action made it true":
$$F^{t+1} \rightarrow F^t \;\vee\; \bigvee_{A : F \in \text{eff}^+(A)} A^t$$

**Backward frame axiom** — "if a fluent becomes false, some action made it false":
$$\neg F^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg F^t \;\vee\; \bigvee_{A : F \in \text{eff}^-(A)} A^t$$

In CNF:
- $(\neg F^{t+1} \vee F^t \vee a_1^t \vee \ldots \vee a_m^t)$
- $(F^{t+1} \vee \neg F^t \vee d_1^t \vee \ldots \vee d_n^t)$

#### 2.1.6 Action-Exclusion Axioms (Mutual Exclusion)

> For every pair of actions $(A_i, A_j)$ that interfere (one deletes a precondition or effect of the other, or has incompatible preconditions), and for every time $t$:
> $$\neg(A_i^t \wedge A_j^t) \equiv (\neg A_i^t \vee \neg A_j^t)$$

For a **sequential plan** (one action per time step), assert:
$$(\bigvee_{i} A_i^t)$$  for each $t$ (exactly-one encoded via pairwise exclusions and an at-least-one clause).

For a **parallel plan**, allow multiple non-interfering actions simultaneously.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Axiom Family | Propositional Form | CNF Clause |
|:-:|:-------------|:-------------------|:-----------|
| 1 | **Initial State** | $F \in s_0 \Rightarrow F^0$  ;  $F \notin s_0 \Rightarrow \neg F^0$ | $F^0$  or  $\neg F^0$ (unit) |
| 2 | **Goal State** | $\forall F \in G : F^k$ | $F^k$ (unit) |
| 3 | **Precondition** | $A^t \rightarrow P^t$ | $(\neg A^t \vee P^t)$ |
| 4 | **Add Effect** | $A^t \rightarrow F^{t+1}$ | $(\neg A^t \vee F^{t+1})$ |
| 5 | **Delete Effect** | $A^t \rightarrow \neg F^{t+1}$ | $(\neg A^t \vee \neg F^{t+1})$ |
| 6 | **Forward Frame** | $F^{t+1} \rightarrow F^t \vee \bigvee_{A \in \text{Add}(F)} A^t$ | $(\neg F^{t+1} \vee F^t \vee a_1^t \vee \ldots)$ |
| 7 | **Backward Frame** | $\neg F^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg F^t \vee \bigvee_{A \in \text{Del}(F)} A^t$ | $(F^{t+1} \vee \neg F^t \vee d_1^t \vee \ldots)$ |
| 8 | **Action Exclusion** | $\neg(A_i^t \wedge A_j^t)$ | $(\neg A_i^t \vee \neg A_j^t)$ |
| 9 | **At-Least-One Action** | $\bigvee_i A_i^t$ | $(A_1^t \vee A_2^t \vee \ldots)$ |
| 10 | **Total Variables** | $n_F$ fluents, $n_A$ actions, horizon $k$ | $k \cdot (n_F + n_A)$ props |

### 2.3 The SATPLAN Iterative Deepening Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **SATPLAN = Iterative SAT Solving over Increasing Horizons**

The algorithm works as follows:
1. Set $k = 0$.
2. Build the SAT formula $\Phi_k$ (using all six axiom families).
3. Hand $\Phi_k$ to a SAT solver.
4. If $\Phi_k$ is **SATISFIABLE**, decode the model → return the plan.
5. Else, increment $k$ and go to Step 2.
6. If $k$ exceeds a user-defined bound → return "no plan exists within bound."

> [!TIP]
> **Optimality Guarantee:** Since the algorithm tries $k = 1, 2, \ldots$ in order, the first plan found is **shortest in number of time steps** (parallel-plan optimality). For sequential plans, additional action-exclusion clauses enforce exactly-one-action-per-step.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | SAT-Planning Application |
|:-------|:------------------------|
| **Spacecraft Operations** | Autonomous scheduling of NASA Deep Space Network antenna tasks |
| **Robotic Manipulation** | High-MPC motion and manipulation planning for warehouse robots |
| **VLSI Design** | Routing and placement as Boolean satisfiability (closely related) |
| **Software Verification** | Model checking encoded as bounded SAT (BMC — Bounded Model Checking) |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Optimal routing of trucks, drones, packages |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Maneuver planning under traffic rules and dynamic constraints |
| **Cybersecurity** | Attack-graph generation for penetration testing |

### 2.5 Complexity of SAT-based Planning

| Aspect | Complexity |
|:-------|:-----------|
| Deciding $\Phi_k$ satisfiability | **NP-complete** (for fixed $k$) |
| Optimal planning (unbounded) | **PSPACE-complete** |
| Size of $\Phi_k$ | Polynomial in $k$, $n_F$, $n_A$ |
| Solver time (worst-case) | Exponential in $k$ (practical sub-exponential with modern CDCL) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The "Toggle-Domain" Problem

> **Problem Setup (Toy Domain):**
> - **Fluents:** $A$, $B$ (Boolean state variables)
> - **Initial State:** $s_0 = \{A, \neg B\}$
> - **Goal State:** $G = \{B\}$
> - **Actions:**
>   - $X$: pre = $\{A\}$, eff = $\{\neg A, B\}$
>   - $Y$: pre = $\{\neg A\}$, eff = $\{A, \neg B\}$
> - **Task:** Encode the problem into a SAT formula $\Phi_2$ for horizon $k = 2$ (sequential plan).

> [!NOTE]
> The intuitive plan is: execute $X$ at $t=0$, then execute $Y$ at $t=1$ (this brings $A$ back so the system is in the initial state at $t=2$). Alternatively, the plan may terminate at $t=1$ if $B^1$ holds.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Variables

We need state variables and action variables for each time step.

- **State variables:** $A^0, A^1, A^2$ and $B^0, B^1, B^2$  → **6 state variables**
- **Action variables:** $X^0, X^1$ and $Y^0, Y^1$  → **4 action variables**
- **Total propositional variables:** $6 + 4 = \mathbf{10}$

#### Step 2 — Write Initial-State Axioms

From $s_0 = \{A, \neg B\}$:

$$A^0 \quad \text{and} \quad \neg B^0$$

In CNF: unit clauses $(A^0)$ and $(\neg B^0)$.

#### Step 3 — Write Goal Axioms (at time $k = 2$)

From $G = \{B\}$:

$$B^2$$

In CNF: unit clause $(B^2)$.

#### Step 4 — Write Precondition Axioms

For each action and time step, every precondition must hold *before* the action fires.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X^0 &\rightarrow A^0  \quad \text{(X at t=0 needs A true at t=0)} \\
X^1 &\rightarrow A^1  \quad \text{(X at t=1 needs A true at t=1)} \\
Y^0 &\rightarrow \neg A^0  \quad \text{(Y at t=0 needs A false at t=0)} \\
Y^1 &\rightarrow \neg A^1  \quad \text{(Y at t=1 needs A false at t=1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

CNF form:

$$
(\neg X^0 \vee A^0), \;\; (\neg X^1 \vee A^1), \;\; (\neg Y^0 \vee \neg A^0), \;\; (\neg Y^1 \vee \neg A^1)
$$

#### Step 5 — Write Effect Axioms

Every effect propagates to the *next* time step.

For action $X$ — pre $= \{A\}$, eff $= \{\neg A, B\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X^0 &\rightarrow \neg A^1 \quad \text{and} \quad X^0 \rightarrow B^1 \\
X^1 &\rightarrow \neg A^2 \quad \text{and} \quad X^1 \rightarrow B^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For action $Y$ — pre $= \{\neg A\}$, eff $= \{A, \neg B\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y^0 &\rightarrow A^1 \quad \text{and} \quad Y^0 \rightarrow \neg B^1 \\
Y^1 &\rightarrow A^2 \quad \text{and} \quad Y^1 \rightarrow \neg B^2
\end{aligned}
$$

CNF clauses:

$$
(\neg X^0 \vee \neg A^1), \; (\neg X^0 \vee B^1), \; (\neg X^1 \vee \neg A^2), \; (\neg X^1 \vee B^2)
$$

$$
(\neg Y^0 \vee A^1), \; (\neg Y^0 \vee \neg B^1), \; (\neg Y^1 \vee A^2), \; (\neg Y^1 \vee \neg B^2)
$$

#### Step 6 — Write Frame Axioms (Critical Section)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame axioms ensure that fluents not changed by any active action stay the same.**

##### 6a. Forward Frame Axiom for fluent $A$

$A^{t+1}$ is true if and only if $A^t$ was true and no action deleted $A$, or some action added $A$.

$$
A^{t+1} \rightarrow A^t \vee Y^t
$$

The only action that **adds** $A$ is $Y$. CNF:

$$
(\neg A^{t+1} \vee A^t \vee Y^t)
$$

For $t = 0, 1$:

$$
(\neg A^1 \vee A^0 \vee Y^0), \quad (\neg A^2 \vee A^1 \vee Y^1)
$$

##### 6b. Backward Frame Axiom for fluent $A$

$\neg A^{t+1}$ holds if and only if $A^t$ was false and no action added $A$, or some action deleted $A$.

$$
\neg A^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg A^t \vee X^t
$$

The only action that **deletes** $A$ is $X$. CNF:

$$
(A^{t+1} \vee \neg A^t \vee X^t)
$$

For $t = 0, 1$:

$$
(A^1 \vee \neg A^0 \vee X^0), \quad (A^2 \vee \neg A^1 \vee X^1)
$$

##### 6c. Forward Frame Axiom for fluent $B$

The only action that **adds** $B$ is $X$.

$$
(\neg B^{t+1} \vee B^t \vee X^t)
$$

For $t = 0, 1$:

$$
(\neg B^1 \vee B^0 \vee X^0), \quad (\neg B^2 \vee B^1 \vee X^1)
$$

##### 6d. Backward Frame Axiom for fluent $B$

The only action that **deletes** $B$ is $Y$.

$$
(B^{t+1} \vee \neg B^t \vee Y^t)
$$

For $t = 0, 1$:

$$
(B^1 \vee \neg B^0 \vee Y^0), \quad (B^2 \vee \neg B^1 \vee Y^1)
$$

#### Step 7 — Action-Exclusion Axioms (Sequential Plan)

We want exactly one action per time step.

- **At-Least-One clause** at each $t$:

$$
(X^t \vee Y^t) \quad \text{for } t = 0, 1
$$

- **Mutual-Exclusion clause:**

$$
(\neg X^t \vee \neg Y^t) \quad \text{for } t = 0, 1
$$

Together, $(X^t \vee Y^t) \wedge (\neg X^t \vee \neg Y^t) \equiv (X^t \oplus Y^t)$ — exactly one action at time $t$.

#### Step 8 — Assemble the Full CNF Formula

$$
\Phi_2 \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{N} C_i
$$

where the clauses $C_i$ are all the unit and binary/ternary clauses derived above.

> [!TIP]
> **In a real SAT solver (e.g., MiniSAT),** the CNF is fed as a DIMACS file. The solver applies unit-propagation, conflict-learning, and CDCL to find a satisfying assignment in milliseconds for small problems.

#### Step 9 — Solve and Decode

For the given problem, a valid satisfying assignment is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^0 &= \text{True}, \quad B^0 = \text{False} \\
A^1 &= \text{False}, \quad B^1 = \text{True} \\
A^2 &= \text{True}, \quad B^2 = \text{False}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
X^0 = \text{True}, \quad Y^0 = \text{False}, \quad X^1 = \text{False}, \quad Y^1 = \text{True}
$$

**Decoded plan:** Execute $X$ at $t=0$, execute $Y$ at $t=1$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Axiom Family is Essential

| Axiom | What it Prevents | What Happens if Omitted |
|:------|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| **Initial-State** | Inconsistent starting state | Solver might "cheat" by setting any initial fluent |
| **Goal-State** | Plans that do not achieve $G$ | Plans terminate arbitrarily early |
| **Precondition** | Firing an action when its requirements are unmet | Illegal actions accepted as valid |
| **Add-Effect** | Failure of postconditions to appear | Plans claim effects that never happen |
| **Delete-Effect** | Stale positive fluents persisting | Deleted facts reappear magically |
| **Forward-Frame** | Spontaneous generation of positive fluents | New true fluents appear from nowhere |
| **Backward-Frame** | Spontaneous generation of negative fluents | False fluents become true magically |
| **Action-Exclusion** | Multiple conflicting actions at same time | Plans execute impossible parallel actions |

### 3.3 Optimization Variants Discussed in KTU Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Important SAT-Encoding Variants**

**1. Kautz-Selman (Original) Encoding:**
Uses full frame axioms (forward + backward). Clause count is large but solver propagation is strong.

**2. Lin-Jang / Compact Encoding:**
Merges forward and backward frame axioms into a single equivalence clause per fluent per time step, reducing clause count by ~50%.

**3. Plan-Graph-Based Encoding (GraphSAT):**
Uses the *plan graph* (from Graphplan) to identify which fluents are reachable, dramatically reducing irrelevant variables and clauses — especially effective in large domains.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Modern SAT planners like **Satplan06, MAXPLAN, CpSAT, and Picat-SAT** use the plan-graph-based encoding because it eliminates "dead" variables before the SAT solver even starts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SATPLAN — Iterative Deepening Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Planning Problem P]) --> B[Initialize horizon k = 0]
    B --> C[k = k + 1]
    C --> D["Build SAT formula Phi_k from P"]
    D --> E[Invoke SAT Solver]
    E --> F{SAT Solver Verdict}
    F -->|SATISFIABLE| G[Decode Truth Assignment]
    G --> H[Extract Action Sequence]
    H --> I([Return Plan of length k])
    F -->|UNSATISFIABLE| J{k exceeds max_bound?}
    J -->|No| C
    J -->|Yes| K([Return: No Plan Exists])
```

### 4.2 Encoding Pipeline — From Planning Problem to CNF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Planning Problem P] --> P1[State Fluents F_0 ... F_k]
    P0 --> P2[Action Variables A_0 ... A_k-1]
    P1 --> P3[Axiom Generator]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Initial State Axioms]
    P3 --> P5[Goal State Axioms]
    P3 --> P6[Precondition Axioms]
    P3 --> P7[Effect Axioms]
    P3 --> P8[Frame Axioms]
    P3 --> P9[Action Exclusion Axioms]
    P4 --> P10[CNF Formula Phi_k]
    P5 --> P10
    P6 --> P10
    P7 --> P10
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[SAT Solver e.g. MiniSAT]
    P11 --> P12{Model Found?}
    P12 -->|Yes| P13[Valid Plan]
    P12 -->|No| P14[Increase k and Retry]
```

### 4.3 Time-Stepped World Evolution View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0  s0 = A, not B"] --> actX[Action X fires]
    actX --> t1["t = 1  not A, B"]
    t1 --> actY[Action Y fires]
    actY --> t2["t = 2  A, not B"]
    t2 --> goal([Goal B achieved at t=1])
```

### 4.4 Frame-Axiom Mechanism — Schematic View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F["Fluent F at time t"] -->|unchanged by any action| F1["Fluent F at time t+1"]
    A1[Action A1: adds F] -->|fires at t| F1
    A2[Action A2: deletes F] -->|fires at t| F2["Fluent not F at time t+1"]
    F --> A1
    F --> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above represent the **logical flow** of information in a SAT-based planner. Notice how each planning-domain element (state, action, axiom) flows through a single pipeline, converging into a CNF formula that a generic SAT solver can process. This **compiler-style architecture** is what makes SAT-based planning both elegant and powerful.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

*Define **Planning as Boolean Satisfiability**. Mention the role of a SAT solver in this approach.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Planning as Boolean Satisfiability is a technique in classical AI planning where a planning problem is reduced (compiled) into a propositional Boolean formula in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF), parameterized by a plan horizon $k$. The formula contains variables representing fluents at each time step and action occurrences at each time step, along with logical axioms encoding the planning semantics (initial state, goal, preconditions, effects, frame, and action exclusion).

**Role of the SAT solver (1 mark):** A SAT solver (e.g., MiniSAT, Glucose) is invoked to determine whether the CNF formula is satisfiable. If satisfiable, the satisfying truth assignment is decoded into a valid plan of length $\leq k$. If unsatisfiable, the horizon is incremented and the process repeats.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

*List the **six families of axioms** used in the SAT encoding of a planning problem. State the purpose of the **Frame Axioms** in one sentence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six axiom families are:

1. **Initial-State Axioms** — specify fluents true/false at $t = 0$.
2. **Goal-State Axioms** — specify fluents that must be true at $t = k$.
3. **Precondition Axioms** — every action's preconditions must hold when the action fires.
4. **Effect (Add/Delete) Axioms** — every action's effects are realized in the next time step.
5. **Frame Axioms** — fluents not modified by any active action persist unchanged.
6. **Action-Exclusion Axioms** — conflicting actions cannot occur at the same time step.

**Purpose of Frame Axioms (1 mark):** Frame axioms solve the *Frame Problem* by ensuring that fluents unaffected by any active action retain their truth value across time steps.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **SATPLAN algorithm** in detail. Discuss how the *plan length parameter $k$* is determined and why *iterative deepening* is used.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the following planning problem:
- Fluents: $P$, $Q$
- Initial state: $s_0 = \{P, \neg Q\}$
- Goal: $G = \{Q\}$
- Action $A$: pre = $\{P\}$, eff = $\{\neg P, Q\}$

Encode this problem into a **CNF SAT formula $\Phi_1$** for horizon $k = 1$. Show all six axiom families. State the satisfying assignment and the decoded plan.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Frame Problem** in classical AI planning. Describe how the *forward* and *backward* frame axioms solve it. Use proper logical notation in your answer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Plan-Graph-Based SAT Encoding (GraphSAT)** approach. How does it improve upon the original Kautz-Selman encoding in terms of *variable count* and *clause count*?

---

### 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — SATPLAN Algorithm [7 marks]:**

The **SATPLAN algorithm** (Kautz & Selman, 1992) converts a planning problem into a Boolean SAT problem and uses an off-the-shelf SAT solver to find a satisfying assignment, which is then decoded into a plan. The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Input:** A planning problem $\Pi = \langle \Sigma, s_0, G, A \rangle$ and an upper bound on plan length $k_{max}$.

2. **Iteration over horizons:** For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, k_{max}$:
   - **Step 1: Construct CNF formula $\Phi_k$** by generating all six axiom families for the chosen horizon $k$.
   - **Step 2: Invoke a SAT solver** on $\Phi_k$.
   - **Step 3: Branch on result:**
     - If $\Phi_k$ is **SATISFIABLE**, decode the truth assignment into a plan of length $\leq k$ and return it.
     - If $\Phi_k$ is **UNSATISFIABLE**, increment $k$ and continue.

3. **Termination:** If all $k$ from 0 to $k_{max}$ yield UNSAT, report "no plan exists within the bound."

**Why iterative deepening over $k$? [2 marks]**
- It guarantees the **first plan found is optimal** in number of time steps (shortest).
- It avoids committing to a possibly-too-large $k$, which would generate a huge SAT instance.
- Unsatisfiable short horizons give the solver **learned clauses** that speed up the next, longer instance.
- It mirrors the BFS-like behaviour of state-space search but with a much stronger pruning mechanism (Boolean propagation and conflict learning).

**Determination of $k$:** $k$ is not known a priori; SATPLAN increments it from 0 until either a plan is found or the bound is reached. Heuristic lower bounds (e.g., from relaxed-plan graphs) can be used to skip small $k$ values.

---

**Part (b) — CNF Encoding for $k = 1$ [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Variables:**
- State variables: $P^0, P^1, Q^0, Q^1$
- Action variables: $A^0$

**Step 2 — Initial-State Axioms (1 mark):**

$$
P^0, \quad \neg Q^0
$$

**Step 3 — Goal-State Axioms (1 mark):**

$$
Q^1
$$

**Step 4 — Precondition Axiom (1 mark):**

$$
A^0 \rightarrow P^0 \;\; \equiv \;\; (\neg A^0 \vee P^0)
$$

**Step 5 — Effect Axioms (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^0 \rightarrow \neg P^1 \;\; &\equiv \;\; (\neg A^0 \vee \neg P^1) \\
A^0 \rightarrow Q^1 \;\; &\equiv \;\; (\neg A^0 \vee Q^1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Frame Axioms (2 marks):**

For fluent $P$ (only action $A$ deletes $P$):
- Forward: $P^1 \rightarrow P^0 \;\; \equiv \;\; (\neg P^1 \vee P^0)$
- Backward: $\neg P^1 \rightarrow \neg P^0 \vee A^0 \;\; \equiv \;\; (P^1 \vee \neg P^0 \vee A^0)$

For fluent $Q$ (only action $A$ adds $Q$):
- Forward: $Q^1 \rightarrow Q^0 \vee A^0 \;\; \equiv \;\; (\neg Q^1 \vee Q^0 \vee A^0)$
- Backward: $\neg Q^1 \rightarrow \neg Q^0 \;\; \equiv \;\; (Q^1 \vee \neg Q^0)$

**Satisfying Assignment and Decoded Plan (1 mark):**

$$
A^0 = \text{True}, \quad P^0 = \text{True}, \quad P^1 = \text{False}, \quad Q^0 = \text{False}, \quad Q^1 = \text{True}
$$

**Decoded plan:** Execute action $A$ at $t = 0$. (1-step plan that achieves $Q$.)

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — The Frame Problem and its SAT Solution [7 marks]:**

The **Frame Problem**, identified by John McCarthy and Patrick Hayes in 1969, is the problem of representing the *non-effects* of actions — i.e., specifying what *does not* change when an action is performed. In STRIPS-style planning, the world is described by a set of fluents, and each action only explicitly specifies a few fluents that change. Yet the planner must implicitly know that **all other fluents remain unchanged** across the action.

Without frame axioms, the planner would have no way of reasoning about persistence, leading to incorrect or unrealizable plans.

**SAT Solution via Forward and Backward Frame Axioms [5 marks]:**

For every fluent $F$ and every time step $t \in [0, k-1]$:

**1. Forward Frame Axiom (Persistence of Truth):**
If $F$ is true at time $t+1$, then either it was true at $t$ and no action deleted it, OR some action added it:

$$
F^{t+1} \rightarrow F^t \vee \bigvee_{A \in \text{Add}(F)} A^t
$$

> *Meaning:* A fluent cannot spontaneously become true; it must persist or be made true by an action.

**2. Backward Frame Axiom (Persistence of Falsity):**
If $F$ is false at time $t+1$, then either it was false at $t$ and no action added it, OR some action deleted it:

$$
\neg F^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg F^t \vee \bigvee_{A \in \text{Del}(F)} A^t
$$

> *Meaning:* A fluent cannot spontaneously become false; it must persist or be made false by an action.

Together, these two axioms **completely solve the frame problem** by exhaustively accounting for every possible way a fluent can change (or remain) value.

**Example [2 marks]:**
For a fluent $B$ with add-list action $X$ and delete-list action $Y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward:} \quad & B^{t+1} \rightarrow B^t \vee X^t \quad \equiv \quad (\neg B^{t+1} \vee B^t \vee X^t) \\
\text{Backward:} \quad & \neg B^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg B^t \vee Y^t \quad \equiv \quad (B^{t+1} \vee \neg B^t \vee Y^t)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Plan-Graph-Based SAT Encoding (GraphSAT) [7 marks]:**

The **GraphSAT** approach (Smith & Weld, 1998; Kautz & Selman, 1996) integrates the *plan graph* of Graphplan (Blum & Furst, 1995) into the SAT encoding. The plan graph is a polynomial-sized, leveled structure that encodes:

- **Proposition levels:** Which fluents can be true at each time step.
- **Action levels:** Which actions can be executed at each time step.
- **Mutual-exclusion relations:** Pairs of fluents or actions that cannot both be true/executed at the same level.

**Improvements over Kautz-Selman (KS) encoding [5 marks]:**

| Aspect | Kautz-Selman | GraphSAT |
|:-------|:-------------|:---------|
| **Variable count** | Considers *all* fluents $\times k$ steps | Considers only fluents *reachable* in plan graph |
| **Clause count** | Includes all frame axioms for all fluents | Frame axioms restricted to reachable fluents |
| **Mutex reasoning** | Not explicitly used | Mutex relations prune action pairs early |
| **Encoding size** | Polynomial in $\vert\Sigma\vert \cdot k$ | Polynomial in $\vert\text{PlanGraph}\vert$ — much smaller in practice |
| **Solver performance** | Good for small domains | Often 10–100$\times$ faster in large domains |

**Mechanism [2 marks]:**
1. Build the plan graph up to level $k$ (this is polynomial-time).
2. Mark every fact and action level as "possible" if it is *not* mutually exclusive.
3. Generate SAT variables and clauses **only for these possible** fluents/actions.
4. Add a final layer of mutex constraints between action and fact levels.

**Result:** Many "impossible" branches of the state space are eliminated *before* the SAT solver even sees the formula, dramatically reducing problem size and improving solver efficiency.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Them)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Frame Axioms (Loses 3–4 marks!):** A large number of students write only the initial-state, goal, precondition, and effect axioms but omit the frame axioms. The SAT encoding is then **incomplete**, and the decoded plan may contain fluents that magically appear or disappear. **Always include both forward and backward frame axioms.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Effect Time:** A frequent error is writing $A^t \rightarrow F^t$ (effect at the *same* time step) instead of $A^t \rightarrow F^{t+1}$ (effect at the *next* time step). Remember: **the world transitions to time $t+1$ after the action at $t$.**
>
> 3. **Mixing Up Add vs. Delete Lists:** A fluent cannot be in both the add-list and the delete-list of the *same* action. The preconditions are also not effects — they are constraints, not transitions.
>
> 4. **Sequential vs. Parallel Confusion:** In sequential plans, only one action executes per time step (use action-exclusion clauses). In parallel plans, multiple *non-interfering* actions may execute — the action-exclusion clauses are then conditional on mutex relations.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Decoding Step:** When asked to encode, many students forget to **decode the satisfying assignment back into an English plan**. Always end with: "*Decoded plan: Action X at t=0, Action Y at t=1, …*"
>
> 6. **Incorrect CNF Conversion:** Converting an implication $P \rightarrow Q$ to CNF is $(\neg P \vee Q)$, not $(P \vee Q)$. A common conversion error.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
