> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Planning as Boolean Satisfiability**

- [x] **Planning as SAT** = Reducing a planning problem to a CNF Boolean formula and using a SAT solver.
- [x] **SATPLAN** is the canonical algorithm; it iteratively increases horizon $k$ until a plan is found.
- [x] **Six Axiom Families:** Initial-State, Goal-State, Precondition, Effect (Add/Delete), Frame (Forward/Backward), Action-Exclusion.
- [x] **Implication to CNF rule:** $P \rightarrow Q \equiv (\neg P \vee Q)$.
- [x] **Effect axiom timing:** Action at $t$ affects the world at $t+1$.
- [x] **Frame Problem:** Solved by forward and backward frame axioms.
- [x] **Forward Frame:** $F^{t+1} \rightarrow F^t \vee (\text{add actions of } F)^t$
- [x] **Backward Frame:** $\neg F^{t+1} \rightarrow \neg F^t \vee (\text{delete actions of } F)^t$
- [x] **Action Exclusion:** For sequential plans, exactly one action per time step (at-least-one + pairwise mutex).
- [x] **Bounded planning is NP-complete; unbounded planning is PSPACE-complete.**
- [x] **Encoding size is polynomial in $k$, number of fluents, and number of actions.**
- [x] **Plan-Graph-Based Encoding (GraphSAT)** is a more efficient variant that prunes unreachable fluents and uses mutex relations.
- [x] **The first plan found by iterative deepening is optimal in number of time steps.**
- [x] **Kautz & Selman (1992, 1996)** are credited with establishing SAT-based planning as a mainstream approach.
- [x] **Modern SAT solvers** (MiniSAT, Glucose, CaDiCaL) implement **CDCL** (Conflict-Driven Clause Learning), making SAT-based planning practically very fast.
- [x] **Decoding step:** Always convert the truth assignment of action variables back to an English-language plan.
- [x] **Sequential vs. parallel plans:** Determined by the action-exclusion clauses (mutex vs. none).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristics for Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heuristics for Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning Heuristic (Academic Definition):**
> A *heuristic function* $h(s)$ for a classical planning problem is an **estimate of the cost (number of actions) required to reach a goal state from a given state $s$**. Domain-independent heuristics for planning are derived *automatically* from the planning task description (PDDL specification) itself, without requiring hand-coded domain knowledge.

In STRIPS-style planning, a heuristic is said to be **safe** (or **admissible**) if it never overestimates the true optimal cost $h^*(s)$, and **goal-aware** if $h(s) = 0$ exactly when $s$ satisfies the goal. Good heuristics must trade off **informativeness** (closeness to $h^*$) against **computational tractability** (polynomial-time evaluation).

**Boundary Condition:** A heuristic is *consistent* (monotone) if for every action $a$ applicable in state $s$:

$$h(s) \leq \text{cost}(a) + h(\text{result}(s,a))$$

This guarantees admissibility is preserved along search paths — a property heavily exploited by KTU-style problems involving greedy best-first and $A^*$ planners.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber's Altimeter

Imagine you are a climber at the **base camp** (initial state $s_0$) and need to reach the **summit** (goal $G$). You cannot see the summit directly, but you have an **altimeter** that tells you your *estimated* elevation gain still required.

- The **true distance** to the summit ($h^*(s)$) is unknown — that is the exact plan you are searching for.
- A **heuristic** $h(s)$ is your altimeter reading: a rough, cheap estimate.
- A **good heuristic** is like an accurate altimeter that always reads *less than or equal to* the true elevation (admissible) — so when it reads 0, you *know* you are at the summit.
- A **bad heuristic** could read 1000m when the summit is only 500m away — this overestimation makes $A^*$ lose optimality.

In classical planning, the "altimeter" is automatically synthesized from the *delete-relaxation* or *critical-path* analysis of the operator schemata.

## 1.3 Why Are Heuristics Needed in Planning?

The forward state space of a planning problem with $n$ state variables has up to $2^n$ states. For $n = 50$, this is $1.12 \times 10^{15}$ states — far beyond reach. Blind search (BFS, uniform-cost) is **exponential and impractical**. Heuristics *prune* this space by:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Roles of a Planning Heuristic:**
> 1. **Guidance:** Direct search towards goal-relevant states.
> 2. **Termination proof:** Identify unsolvable/over-constrained subgoals early.
> 3. **Admissible guidance (with $A^*$):** Preserve optimality guarantees.

## 1.4 Categories of Planning Heuristics

| Category | Source of Estimate | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain-independent** | Auto-derived from PDDL | $h_{add}$, $h_{max}$, $h_{FF}$, landmark heuristic |
| **Domain-dependent** | Hand-coded per task | Manhattan distance in 8-puzzle |
| **Critical-path based** | Operator causal structure | $h^m$ (level-$m$ cost partition) |
| **Abstraction-based** | State-space projection | Merge-and-shrink, PDBs |
| **Landmark-based** | Sub-goal decomposition | $h_L$, $h_{LA}$ |

This module focuses on **domain-independent heuristics**, the heart of modern KTU AI curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Planning Heuristic Pipeline

The design of a planning heuristic proceeds through four logical stages:

- **Stage 1 — Task Formalisation:**
  The PDDL planning task $\Pi = \langle V, s_0, s_*, O \rangle$ is reduced to a set of propositional (ground) operators and propositional facts. Each operator $a$ has the tuple $\langle \text{pre}(a), \text{add}(a), \text{del}(a) \rangle$.

- **Stage 2 — Relaxation:**
  The original problem is *simplified* into a tractable surrogate. The most celebrated relaxation is the **delete-relaxation $\Pi^+$**, in which $\text{del}(a) = \emptyset$ for every action $a$. *Why?* Because deletes are the source of *interference* between subgoals; ignoring them makes subgoals independent, enabling polynomial estimation.

- **Stage 3 — Estimation:**
  Solve (approximately) the relaxed problem and use the cost of its solution as $h(s)$.

- **Stage 4 — Search Coupling:**
  Plug $h(s)$ into a best-first framework (Greedy, $A^*$, Weighted $A^*$, Enforced Hill-Climbing).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Delete-Relaxation Heuristic Family** (Bonet & Geffner, 1999; Hoffmann, 2001) is the foundation of the Fast-Forward (FF) planner — and is a **recurring KTU exam favourite**.

## 2.2 Building the Relaxed Planning Graph

The **relaxed planning graph** (RPG) is a layered graph:

$$\text{RPG} = (P_0, A_1, P_1, A_2, P_2, \ldots, A_k, P_k)$$

where:
- $P_0 = s$ (current state propositions).
- $A_i = \{a \in O^+ \mid \text{pre}(a) \subseteq P_{i-1}\}$ — *no-op* included implicitly.
- $P_i = P_{i-1} \cup \text{add}(A_i)$ — propositions reachable by level $i$.

The first level $k$ such that $G \subseteq P_k$ is the **relaxed plan length**. Constructing the RPG is polynomial — each level adds only *new* facts, guaranteeing termination in $\leq \vert V \vert$ levels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $h^*(s)$ | True optimal cost from $s$ | — |
| $h(s)$ | Heuristic estimate | $0 \leq h(s) \leq h^*(s)$ (admissible) |
| $h^+(s)$ | Cost of optimal relaxed plan | Computed via RPG |
| $h_{add}(p)$ | Additive heuristic for fact $p$ | $h_{add}(p) = 0$ if $p \in s$; else $\min_a \big[ h_{add}(\text{pre}(a)) + 1 \big]$ |
| $h_{max}(p)$ | Max heuristic for fact $p$ | $h_{max}(p) = 0$ if $p \in s$; else $\min_a \max_{q \in \text{pre}(a)} h_{max}(q) + 1$ |
| $h_{FF}(s)$ | FF heuristic | Length of relaxed plan extracted from RPG |
| $h_L(s)$ | Landmark count heuristic | Number of unmet landmarks from $s$ |
| $L_{ag}$ | Action landmark | An action that *must* appear in every valid plan |
| $L_f$ | Fact landmark | A fact that *must* hold in every path to goal |
| Consistency | $h(s) \leq c(a) + h(s')$ | Required for $A^*$ re-expansion safety |
| RPG layers | $k = \min\{i \mid G \subseteq P_i\}$ | $h^+(s) = k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield):**
> $h_{max} \leq h^+ \leq h_{add}$ in terms of informativeness, and $h_{max}$ is the only **admissible** one of the three. $h_{add}$ is **inadmissible but very informative** — used in Greedy and FF.

## 2.4 The $h_{add}$ and $h_{max}$ Operators (Engineering Insight)

Define a cost vector $C : F \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ over facts. The **Bellman-style** backward propagation on the RPG is:

$$C_i(p) = \begin{cases} 0 & p \in s \\ \min\limits_{a : p \in \text{add}(a)} \left[ 1 + \sum\limits_{q \in \text{pre}(a)} C_{i-1}(q) \right] & \text{(additive)} \\ \min\limits_{a : p \in \text{add}(a)} \left[ 1 + \max\limits_{q \in \text{pre}(a)} C_{i-1}(q) \right] & \text{(max)} \end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** $h_{add}$ is the default heuristic inside the **Fast-Downward** planner, the workhorse of IPC (International Planning Competition). It is **inadmissible** but extremely fast to evaluate, and empirically solves 1000+ action benchmarks within seconds.

## 2.5 Critical-Path Heuristic ($h^m$ / Cost Partitioning)

The **$h^m$ family** of heuristics (Haslum et al., 2005) partitions the goal set into $m$ subsets and sums their optimal costs, computed by multiple calls to $h_{add}$ or $h_{max}$. As $m \to \vert G \vert$, $h^m \to h^+$. Cost-partitioning ensures **admissibility** even when adding sub-heuristics.

## 2.6 Landmark Heuristic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landmark Definition (Hoffmann et al., 2004):**
> A *fact landmark* $L_f$ is a proposition that is true in *every* solution plan. An *action landmark* $L_{ag}$ is an action executed in *every* solution plan.

Landmarks can be computed greedily from the RPG by checking **necessary ordering** relations. The number of *unachieved* landmarks from $s$ gives a coarse but very fast heuristic $h_L(s) = \vert \{L \mid L \text{ unachieved in } s\} \vert$.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Heuristic Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Robotic task planning** | $h_{FF}$ | Real-time, informative |
| **NASA mission sequencing** | $h_{add}$ + landmarks | Explainability, safety bounds |
| **Logistics / warehouse bots** | $h_{max}$ in $A^*$ | Optimality required |
| **Automated program synthesis** | $h^m$ (cost partitioned) | Strong guidance on long-horizon tasks |
| **Game AI (real-time strategy)** | Domain-dependent + landmarks | Microsecond latency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Logistics Domain

We now derive $h_{add}$, $h_{max}$, and $h_{FF}$ on a standard KTU-style example. This is the kind of trace that earns full marks in a 14-mark KTU question.

### 3.1.1 Task Specification

**State Variables (ground facts):**
- Cargo: $c_1, c_2$
- Plane: $p_1, p_2$
- Locations: $L_1, L_2$

**Initial State $s_0$:**
$$s_0 = \{\, At(c_1,L_1),\ At(c_2,L_1),\ At(p_1,L_1),\ At(p_2,L_1) \,\}$$

**Goal $G$:**
$$G = \{\, At(c_1,L_2),\ At(c_2,L_2) \,\}$$

**Operators (STRIPS, action costs = 1):**

| Action | Precondition | Add | Delete |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Load(c,p,l)$ | $\{At(c,l), At(p,l)\}$ | $\{In(c,p)\}$ | $\{At(c,l)\}$ |
| $Unload(c,p,l)$ | $\{In(c,p), At(p,l)\}$ | $\{At(c,l)\}$ | $\{In(c,p)\}$ |
| $Fly(p, from, to)$ | $\{At(p,from)\}$ | $\{At(p,to)\}$ | $\{At(p,from)\}$ |

### 3.1.2 Construction of the Relaxed Planning Graph (RPG)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 0 — Level 0 propositions $P_0$:**
> $P_0 = s_0 = \{At(c_1,L_1), At(c_2,L_1), At(p_1,L_1), At(p_2,L_1)\}$

**Step 1 — Identify applicable actions $A_1$ (no deletes considered):**
- $Load(c_1, p_1, L_1)$: pre $\{At(c_1,L_1), At(p_1,L_1)\} \subseteq P_0$. ✓ Adds $In(c_1,p_1)$.
- $Load(c_1, p_2, L_1)$: ✓ Adds $In(c_1,p_2)$.
- $Load(c_2, p_1, L_1)$: ✓ Adds $In(c_2,p_1)$.
- $Load(c_2, p_2, L_1)$: ✓ Adds $In(c_2,p_2)$.
- $Fly(p_1, L_1, L_2)$: pre $\{At(p_1,L_1)\} \subseteq P_0$. ✓ Adds $At(p_1,L_2)$.
- $Fly(p_2, L_1, L_2)$: ✓ Adds $At(p_2,L_2)$.

**Step 2 — Level 1 propositions $P_1$:**
$$P_1 = P_0 \cup \{In(c_1,p_1), In(c_1,p_2), In(c_2,p_1), In(c_2,p_2), At(p_1,L_2), At(p_2,L_2)\}$$

Goal not yet reached ($At(c_1,L_2) \notin P_1$).

**Step 3 — Level 2 actions $A_2$:**
- $Unload(c_1, p_1, L_2)$: pre $\{In(c_1,p_1), At(p_1,L_2)\} \subseteq P_1$. ✓ Adds $At(c_1,L_2)$.
- $Unload(c_2, p_1, L_2)$: ✓ Adds $At(c_2,L_2)$.
- (Similarly with $p_2$.)

**Step 4 — Level 2 propositions $P_2$:**
$$P_2 = P_1 \cup \{At(c_1,L_2), At(c_2,L_2)\}$$

Goal $G \subseteq P_2$ — **first goal level $k = 2$**.

$$\boxed{\,h^+(s_0) = 2\,}$$

### 3.1.3 Computing $h_{add}$ — Additive Heuristic

Backward propagation. For each fact $p$, $h_{add}(p)$ is the minimum over achievers of $1 + \sum_{q \in \text{pre}(a)} h_{add}(q)$.

**Level 0 (base facts in $s_0$):**
$$h_{add}(At(c_1,L_1)) = h_{add}(At(c_2,L_1)) = h_{add}(At(p_1,L_1)) = h_{add}(At(p_2,L_1)) = 0$$

**Level 1 (inverted evaluation, propositions added in $P_1$):**
$$h_{add}(In(c_1,p_1)) = 1 + h_{add}(At(c_1,L_1)) + h_{add}(At(p_1,L_1)) = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$$
$$h_{add}(In(c_2,p_1)) = 1$$
$$h_{add}(At(p_1,L_2)) = 1 + h_{add}(At(p_1,L_1)) = 1$$
$$h_{add}(At(p_2,L_2)) = 1$$

**Level 2 (goal propositions):**
$$h_{add}(At(c_1,L_2)) = 1 + h_{add}(In(c_1,p_1)) + h_{add}(At(p_1,L_2)) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$
$$h_{add}(At(c_2,L_2)) = 1 + h_{add}(In(c_2,p_1)) + h_{add}(At(p_1,L_2)) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$

**Aggregate heuristic for the goal set:**
$$h_{add}(s_0, G) = h_{add}(At(c_1,L_2)) + h_{add}(At(c_2,L_2)) = 3 + 3 = 6$$

### 3.1.4 Computing $h_{max}$ — Max Heuristic

**Base facts:**
$$h_{max}(At(c_1,L_1)) = h_{max}(At(c_2,L_1)) = h_{max}(At(p_1,L_1)) = h_{max}(At(p_2,L_1)) = 0$$

**Level 1:**
$$h_{max}(In(c_1,p_1)) = 1 + \max(0, 0) = 1$$
$$h_{max}(At(p_1,L_2)) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Level 2:**
$$h_{max}(At(c_1,L_2)) = 1 + \max(1, 1) = 2$$
$$h_{max}(At(c_2,L_2)) = 1 + \max(1, 1) = 2$$

**Aggregate:**
$$h_{max}(s_0, G) = \max(2, 2) = 2$$

### 3.1.5 The $h_{FF}$ Heuristic — Extracting a Relaxed Plan

A **relaxed plan** is a set of actions from the RPG that achieves $G$. Standard extraction (Hoffmann 2001, "helpful actions" pruning):

| Step | Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Load(c_1, p_1, L_1)$ | Achieves pre of $Unload(c_1,p_1,L_2)$ |
| 2 | $Load(c_2, p_1, L_1)$ | Achieves pre of $Unload(c_2,p_1,L_2)$ |
| 3 | $Fly(p_1, L_1, L_2)$ | Achieves pre of both Unloads |
| 4 | $Unload(c_1, p_1, L_2)$ | Achieves $At(c_1,L_2)$ |
| 5 | $Unload(c_2, p_1, L_2)$ | Achieves $At(c_2,L_2)$ |

$$\boxed{\,h_{FF}(s_0) = 5\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $h_{max} = 2 \leq h_{FF} = 5 \leq h_{add} = 6$ — the admissibility-informativeness hierarchy is preserved.

### 3.1.6 Landmark Computation

A *fact landmark* must be true on every solution path. By inspecting the RPG:
- $At(p_1, L_2)$ is a landmark (no other way to deliver cargo).
- $In(c_1, p_1)$ and $In(c_2, p_1)$ are ordered landmarks (load must precede unload).

$$h_L(s_0) = 3 \text{ (unachieved landmarks)}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Heuristic | Computed Value | Admissible? | Consistent? | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{max}$ | **2** | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | Polynomial in operators |
| $h^+$ | **2** | ✓ Yes (optimal relaxed) | ✗ Not always | NP-hard in general |
| $h_{FF}$ | **5** | ✗ No | ✗ No | Polynomial |
| $h_{add}$ | **6** | ✗ No | ✗ No | Polynomial |
| $h_L$ (landmarks) | **3** | ✗ No | ✗ No | Polynomial (approximate) |
| $h^*$ (true optimum) | **5** | — | — | NP-hard to compute |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Pitfall):** For the above example, the *true* optimal plan cost is 5. Therefore, $h_{add} = 6$ is an *overestimate* — inadmissible. Students who claim $h_{add}$ is always admissible **lose 2 marks immediately** on KTU papers.

## 3.3 Worked Search Trace (Greedy Best-First + $h_{add}$)

Suppose we run GBFS with $h_{add}$ from $s_0$. The search expands states in order of *descending* cost (we are minimising):

| Expanded State | Achieves | $h_{add}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_0$ | baseline | 6 |
| After $Load(c_1,p_1,L_1)$ | $In(c_1,p_1)$ gained | 5 |
| After $Load(c_2,p_1,L_1)$ | both cargos loaded | 4 |
| After $Fly(p_1,L_1,L_2)$ | plane relocated | 2 |
| After $Unload(c_1,p_1,L_2)$ | one goal fact | 1 |
| After $Unload(c_2,p_1,L_2)$ | **goal** | **0** |

The planner reaches the goal in 5 steps, matching the optimum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Heuristic Search Planner

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **block-level functional architecture** of a heuristic-driven classical planner. Note: per protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed; all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[PDDL Task Input] --> B1[Grounding Module]
    B1 --> C1[Relaxed Planning Graph Builder]
    C1 --> D1[Heuristic Evaluator]
    D1 --> E1[Best First Search Engine]
    E1 --> F1{Goal Reached}
    F1 -- YES --> G1[Output Plan]
    F1 -- NO --> H1[Successor Generator]
    H1 --> E1
    D1 -. provides h of s .-> E1
    C1 -. feeds facts .-> D1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Relaxed Planning Graph Builder** (centre) supplies a tractable surrogate world; the **Heuristic Evaluator** scores each candidate state $s$; the **Best-First Search Engine** picks the lowest-$h$ state and loops back via the **Successor Generator**.

## 4.2 Layered Structure of the Relaxed Planning Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Level 0 Propositions in s0] -->|achieve via| L1A[Level 1 Actions]
    L1A -->|add effects| L1[Level 1 Propositions]
    L1 -->|achieve via| L2A[Level 2 Actions]
    L2A -->|add effects| L2[Level 2 Propositions]
    L2 -->|achieve via| L3A[Level 3 Actions]
    L3A -->|add effects| L3[Level 3 Propositions]
    L3 -->|achieve via| L4A[Level 4 Actions]
    L4A -->|add effects| L4[Level 4 Propositions]
    L4 --> GOAL{Goal Subset}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **first level $k$** at which the goal is fully contained in $P_k$ gives $h^+(s) = k$. The **logistics example** in Section 3 reaches the goal at $k = 2$.

## 4.3 Decomposition of the Heuristic Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Planning Heuristic h of s] --> H1A[Domain Independent]
    H1 --> H1B[Domain Dependent]
    H1A --> H1A1[Delete Relaxation Family]
    H1A --> H1A2[Critical Path h to the m]
    H1A --> H1A3[Abstraction Family]
    H1A --> H1A4[Landmark Family]
    H1A1 --> H1A1A[h plus optimal relaxed]
    H1A1 --> H1A1B[h add additive]
    H1A1 --> H1A1C[h max max]
    H1A1 --> H1A1D[h FF Fast Forward]
    H1A2 --> H1A2A[h to the 1]
    H1A2 --> H1A2B[h to the m sum of partitions]
    H1A3 --> H1A3A[Pattern Databases PDB]
    H1A3 --> H1A3B[Merge and Shrink]
    H1A4 --> H1A4A[h L landmark count]
    H1A4 --> H1A4B[h LA landmark cost partitioning]
```

## 4.4 Detailed Trace: Forward Search State Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State s0 h add 6] --> S1[State after Load c1 h add 5]
    S0 --> S2[State after Fly p1 h add 4]
    S1 --> S3[State after Load c2 h add 4]
    S1 --> S4[State after Fly p1 h add 3]
    S2 --> S5[State after Load c1 h add 3]
    S2 --> S6[State after Load c2 h add 3]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S7[State after Unload c1 h add 1]
    S4 --> S8[State after Unload c2 h add 1]
    S7 --> GOAL[Goal State h add 0]
    S8 --> GOAL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the trace:** Greedy best-first with $h_{add}$ expands the lowest-estimated-cost state. Notice that the search is *focused* — irrelevant actions (e.g., loading cargo on $p_2$) are never expanded because they have higher $h_{add}$ values. This focus is the practical engine of FF.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Delete-Relaxation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[Original Operator a] --> REL[Relaxed Operator a plus]
    REL --> DROP[Delete List Discarded]
    DROP --> RP[Relaxed Pre Add Effects]
    RP --> SOL[Relaxed Solution Extracted]
    SOL --> VAL[Value Used as h of s]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, concept-testing, expecting 3–4 sentence answers. No derivations; definitions and comparative analysis only.

---

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] [3 Marks]

**Q: Define a planning heuristic. Differentiate between an admissible heuristic and a consistent heuristic with an example from the Blocks World domain.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **planning heuristic** $h(s)$ is a function that estimates the cost of reaching a goal state from state $s$ in a classical planning problem, derived *automatically* from the PDDL task description.

An **admissible** heuristic never overestimates the true optimal cost: $h(s) \leq h^*(s)$. The **Blocks World** example: if three blocks are stacked in the wrong order, the admissible heuristic is the *minimum number of moves* required to reach the goal configuration; for instance, $h_{max} = 2$ (move top block aside, then move target block).

A **consistent** (monotone) heuristic additionally satisfies $h(s) \leq \text{cost}(a) + h(s')$ for every applicable action $a$. In Blocks World, the *manhattan-style* block-displacement distance is consistent because each move can only reduce the remaining distance by at most one. Consistency guarantees $A^*$ does not need to re-expand states, which is critical for KTU problems on search efficiency. *(3 marks awarded: 1 for definition, 1 for admissibility distinction, 1 for consistency example.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] [3 Marks]

**Q: What is a relaxed planning graph? State the role of delete-relaxation in computing the $h^+$ heuristic.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **relaxed planning graph (RPG)** is a layered, level-by-level structure of proposition and action sets, $P_0, A_1, P_1, A_2, \ldots$, constructed under the assumption that no action deletes any fact.

**Delete-relaxation** is the simplification step where, for every operator $a$, the delete list is discarded ($\text{del}(a) = \emptyset$). This makes the relaxed problem *monotone* — once a fact is achieved, it can never be removed — and hence *tractable*. The cost of the optimal solution to this relaxed problem is $h^+(s)$, an admissible heuristic. Crucially, the delete-relaxation converts a hard planning problem (PSPACE-complete in general) into a polynomial-time solvable estimation, enabling $A^*$ and GBFS to scale to problems with thousands of facts. *(3 marks: 1 for RPG definition, 1 for delete-relaxation role, 1 for admissibility + tractability claim.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two internal choices (Q-A and Q-B), each with two sub-parts carrying 7 marks apiece. Full derivations and traces are expected.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] [14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the Blocks World instance below, compute the values of $h_{add}$, $h_{max}$, and $h_{FF}$ from the initial state.

- **Initial state $s_0$:** $On(A,B)$, $OnTable(B)$, $OnTable(C)$, $Clear(A)$, $Clear(C)$.
- **Goal $G$:** $On(B,C)$, $On(A,B)$, $Clear(A)$.

- **Operators:**
  - $Stack(x,y)$: pre $\{Holding(x), Clear(y)\}$; add $\{On(x,y), Clear(x), \neg Holding(x)\}$; del $\{Holding(x), Clear(y)\}$.
  - $Unstack(x,y)$: pre $\{On(x,y), Clear(x), \neg Holding(\cdot)\}$; add $\{Holding(x), Clear(y)\}$; del $\{On(x,y), Clear(x)\}$.
  - $Pickup(x)$: pre $\{OnTable(x), Clear(x), \neg Holding(\cdot)\}$; add $\{Holding(x)\}$; del $\{OnTable(x), Clear(x)\}$.
  - $Putdown(x)$: pre $\{Holding(x)\}$; add $\{OnTable(x), Clear(x)\}$; del $\{Holding(x)\}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the admissibility and informativeness of $h_{max}$, $h_{add}$, and $h_{FF}$. Under what conditions is each preferred in a planner?

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Build the RPG:**

- $P_0 = \{On(A,B), OnTable(B), OnTable(C), Clear(A), Clear(C)\}$.
- Achievable actions in $A_1$:
  - $Unstack(A,B)$: pre $\subseteq P_0$ ✓ adds $\{Holding(A), Clear(B)\}$.
  - $Putdown(A)$: needs $Holding(A)$ → not yet.
  - $Stack(C,B)$: needs $Holding(C)$, $Clear(B)$ → not yet.
- $P_1 = P_0 \cup \{Holding(A), Clear(B)\}$.
- $A_2$: $Putdown(A)$ ✓ (adds $OnTable(A), Clear(A)$); $Pickup(C)$ ✓ (adds $Holding(C)$); $Stack(A,B)$ needs $Holding(A)$ — now available ✓.
- $P_2 = P_1 \cup \{OnTable(A), Clear(A), Holding(C)\}$.
- $A_3$: $Putdown(C)$ ✓ (adds $OnTable(C), Clear(C)$); $Stack(C, B)$? Pre $\{Holding(C), Clear(B)\}$ ✓ — adds $On(C,B), Clear(C)$.
- $P_3 = P_2 \cup \{On(C,B)\}$. But goal wants $On(B,C)$, not $On(C,B)$ — so the *stack direction* is wrong.

**Step 2 — Re-plan with correct ordering:**

- We need $Holding(B)$ to stack $B$ on $C$. So $Unstack(A,B)$ first, then $Pickup(B)$, then $Stack(B,C)$. Then re-stack $A$ on $B$.

**Step 3 — Compute $h_{max}$:**

$$h_{max}(Holding(B)) = 1 + \max(h_{max}(On(A,B)), h_{max}(Clear(A))) = 1 + 0 = 1$$
$$h_{max}(On(B,C)) = 1 + \max(h_{max}(Holding(B)), h_{max}(Clear(C))) = 1 + \max(1, 0) = 2$$
$$h_{max}(On(A,B)) = 0 \text{ (already true)}$$

**Aggregate $h_{max}(s_0, G) = \max(2, 0) = 2$.**

**Step 4 — Compute $h_{add}$:**

$$h_{add}(Holding(B)) = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$$
$$h_{add}(On(B,C)) = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2$$

**Aggregate $h_{add}(s_0, G) = 2 + 0 = 2$.**

**Step 5 — Compute $h_{FF}$:** The relaxed plan is:
1. $Unstack(A,B)$
2. $Pickup(B)$
3. $Stack(B,C)$
4. $Unstack(A,B)$ (to free A; but in relaxed world, this is free) → $Pickup(A)$, $Stack(A,B)$ — total **5 actions**.

$$\boxed{\,h_{add} = 2,\quad h_{max} = 2,\quad h_{FF} = 5\,}$$

*(Full marks: 2 for RPG construction, 2 for $h_{add}$, 2 for $h_{max}$, 1 for $h_{FF}$.)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

| Property | $h_{max}$ | $h_{add}$ | $h_{FF}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Admissible? | **Yes** (always) | **No** (overestimates) | **No** |
| Consistent? | Yes (with care) | No | No |
| Informativeness | Weakest | Strong (overestimates) | Strong |
| Computation | Polynomial | Polynomial | Polynomial |
| Preferred in | $A^*$ for *optimal* plans | Greedy / FF for *fast* plans | GBFS + helpful actions pruning |
| Empirical win | IPC optimal tracks | IPC agile tracks | Many IPC-2000 wins |

> [!TIP]
> **Synthesis:** $h_{max}$ is preferred when *optimality* is required (IPC optimal track), $h_{add}$ when *scalability* is paramount, and $h_{FF}$ when both speed and reasonable plan quality matter.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] [14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **landmark heuristic** in planning. Given the following planning task, identify all fact landmarks and compute $h_L(s_0)$.

- **Initial state $s_0$:** $\{At(rob, kitchen), Closed(door), OnTable(cup), LightOff\}$.
- **Goal $G$:** $\{At(rob, living-room), Open(door), Holding(cup), LightOn\}$.
- **Operators (informal):** $Move(from, to)$, $Open(door)$, $Pickup(cup)$, $TurnOnLight$. Each requires appropriate preconditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **$h^m$ heuristic family**. Show how $h^1$ and $h^2$ differ, and explain the trade-off with respect to admissibility vs. informativeness.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Landmark Definition (recap):** A *fact landmark* is a proposition that must be true in *every* solution plan.

**Landmark Identification (backward from goal):**

- $Holding(cup)$: requires $At(rob, living-room)$ (so that rob can pick up the cup if it's in the LR; assume cup is there) — actually, $OnTable(cup)$ is the precondition. So $OnTable(cup)$ is a landmark (already true).
- $Open(door)$: requires the door to be closed initially (true in $s_0$). So $Open(door)$ is a landmark.
- $At(rob, living-room)$: requires $At(rob, kitchen)$ (true) and a *Move* action.
- $LightOn$: requires $LightOff$ (true) and a $TurnOnLight$ action with no precondition beyond being in the room (assume so).

**Landmark set:**
$$LM = \{Open(door),\ At(rob, living-room),\ LightOn,\ Holding(cup)\}$$

(All four goal facts are themselves landmarks because they appear in $G$.)

**Computing $h_L(s_0)$:** $s_0$ satisfies none of the goal landmarks.

$$\boxed{\,h_L(s_0) = 4\,}$$

*(Marks: 2 for landmark definition, 3 for identification, 2 for $h_L$ computation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Definition of $h^m$:** Partition the goal $G$ into $m$ disjoint subsets $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_m$, compute the optimal relaxed cost $h^+(G_i)$ for each, and sum:

$$h^m(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} h^+_i(s)$$

where each $h^+_i$ is computed on a *copy* of the relaxed problem in which the cost of facts outside $G_i$'s causal support is set to 0 (cost partitioning).

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | $h^1$ | $h^2$ | $h^{\vert G \vert}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partition | 1 subset (whole goal) | 2 subsets | Each goal fact alone |
| Informativeness | Weakest | Stronger | Equals $h^+$ |
| Admissibility | Admissible | Admissible | Admissible |
| Computation | 1 call to $h^+$ | 2 calls to $h^+$ | $\vert G \vert$ calls |

**Why $h^1$ vs. $h^2$:** $h^1$ treats all subgoals as a single unit, so causal interactions are *over-counted* (interference is maximised) — yields weak guidance. $h^2$ separates subgoals so each is solved in isolation, eliminating *negative* interactions and increasing heuristic value. The cost-partitioning trick guarantees the sum is still a *lower bound* on $h^+$, hence admissible.

**Trade-off:** As $m$ grows, $h^m$ approaches $h^+$, becoming more informative but more expensive. KTU planners typically cap at $m = 2$ or $m = 3$ in practice.

*(Marks: 2 for definition, 2 for $h^1$ vs. $h^2$ differentiation, 3 for admissibility-informativeness trade-off with cost-partitioning justification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Heuristics for Planning" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing admissibility with informativeness.** $h_{add}$ and $h_{FF}$ are *inadmissible* — claiming otherwise costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the delete list in RPG construction.** Every action in the relaxed problem has $\text{del}(a) = \emptyset$. Students who carry over deletes into the RPG lose the entire question.
> 3. **Summing $h_{max}$ values instead of taking the maximum.** $h_{max}$ is the *worst-case* (max) over preconditions; summing converts it into $h_{add}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the level-0 base case.** Every fact already in $s_0$ must have $h(p) = 0$; missing this propagates errors everywhere.
> 5. **Skipping helpful-action justification in $h_{FF}$.** KTU examiners award 1 mark for the "why" — the justification of why an action is selected, not just the action itself.
> 6. **Treating $h_L$ as admissible.** Landmark count heuristics are *inadmissible* and inconsistent — they are coarse counts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Heuristics for Planning**

- ✅ A **planning heuristic** $h(s)$ estimates the cost from state $s$ to the goal.
- ✅ **Admissible** heuristics satisfy $h(s) \leq h^*(s)$; **consistent** ones additionally satisfy $h(s) \leq c(a) + h(s')$.
- ✅ **Delete-relaxation** discards $\text{del}(a)$, making subgoals independent and the problem tractable.
- ✅ The **Relaxed Planning Graph (RPG)** is built level-by-level; first goal-reaching level $k$ equals $h^+(s)$.
- ✅ **$h_{add}$** sums the minimum cost chain over preconditions — *inadmissible but informative*.
- ✅ **$h_{max}$** takes the maximum — *admissible* and the foundation of $A^*$ optimal planning.
- ✅ **$h_{FF}$** extracts a relaxed plan from the RPG — basis of the famous FF planner.
- ✅ **Landmarks** are facts/actions in every solution; $h_L$ counts unmet ones — *fast but coarse*.
- ✅ **$h^m$** partitions the goal and sums $m$ admissible sub-heuristics; increases informativeness while preserving admissibility via cost partitioning.
- ✅ Hierarchy: $h_{max} \leq h^+ \leq h_{FF} \leq h_{add}$ (informativeness); $h_{max}, h^+, h^m$ are admissible.
- ✅ Domain-independent heuristics are auto-derived from PDDL — no hand-coding required.
- ✅ FF and Fast-Downward (using $h_{add}$) are the most influential planners in IPC history.
- ✅ Admissible + consistent → $A^*$ guarantees optimal plans without re-expansion.
- ✅ Inadmissible + informative → GBFS finds near-optimal plans quickly, dominant in IPC agile tracks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domain-Independent Pruning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Domain-Independent Pruning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Domain-Independent Pruning** (also called *Constraint Propagation* or *Inference*) refers to a family of systematic techniques used in Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs) to eliminate values from the domains of variables — *without using any problem-specific knowledge*. The pruning is **domain-independent** because the same algorithmic machinery works on graphs, scheduling, Sudoku, map coloring, or cryptarithmetic without modification. Only the **structure of the constraints** is exploited.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCMT402, Module 4):**
> *"Domain-independent pruning methods (e.g., node consistency, arc consistency, path consistency, k-consistency) use the structure of the constraint graph to reduce the search space of a CSP, prior to or during backtracking search."*

### 1.2 Mathematical Formulation of a CSP
A Constraint Satisfaction Problem is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\} \quad \text{(set of variables)} \\
D &= \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\} \quad \text{(domains, where } D_i \text{ is the set of values for } X_i\text{)} \\
C &= \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\} \quad \text{(set of constraints)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **constraint** $C_i$ on variables $(X_{i_1}, X_{i_2}, \dots, X_{i_k})$ is a subset of the Cartesian product $D_{i_1} \times D_{i_2} \times \cdots \times D_{i_k}$ that specifies the allowed tuples.

The **goal of pruning** is to shrink each $D_i$ (or remove entire values) such that no solution is lost — i.e., every tuple in the solution set still has a valid representative in the reduced domains.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sudoku Detective
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Detective Who Crosses Out Suspects"**
>
> Imagine a detective has a list of **100 suspects** for a crime, but the crime requires **two accomplices** who must have been in the **same city** on a given night. The detective doesn't know who did it (no domain-specific clue), but she checks every **pair**: *"If Suspect A was in Paris, then no one in Paris fits — so Suspect A is ruled out."* She systematically eliminates suspects by looking only at the **structural rule** ("two people in the same city"), never at the content of the crime itself. This elimination of impossibilities, driven purely by the constraint's structure, is **domain-independent pruning**.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Pruning?
- **Exponential blow-up**: A naive CSP on $n$ variables each with domain size $d$ has $d^n$ possible assignments. Pruning cuts this to manageable size.
- **Early failure detection**: Many partial assignments can never lead to a full solution; pruning catches this before backtracking wastes effort.
- **Guarantee of completeness**: Unlike heuristic search, constraint propagation never destroys solutions.
- **Tractability transformation**: Some CSPs that are exponential in the general case become polynomial after sufficient propagation.

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)
- **Time Complexity** of the original CSP: $O(d^n)$.
- **Space Complexity** for storing domains: $O(nd)$.
- **Standard metrics**: $n$ = number of variables, $d$ = maximum domain size, $e$ = number of arcs in the constraint graph, $k$ = arity of constraint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Consistency

Pruning strategies form a strict hierarchy from weakest to strongest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Node Consistency} \;\subset\; \text{Arc Consistency} \;\subset\; \text{Path Consistency} \;\subset\; k\text{-Consistency}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each higher level subsumes (implies) the lower levels. The stronger the consistency, the more pruning is performed, but the more expensive the algorithm.

---

### 2.2 Level 1 — Node Consistency (NC)

**Definition**: A variable $X_i$ is **node-consistent** if every value in $D_i$ satisfies all **unary constraints** on $X_i$.

$$
\forall v \in D_i, \quad v \text{ satisfies every unary constraint } C(X_i)
$$

If a value $v$ violates a unary constraint, it is **deleted** from $D_i$.

**Complexity**: $O(nd)$ — just one pass over all domain values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unary constraints** involve a *single* variable, e.g., $X_1 \neq 3$. Since they involve no other variable, they can be removed entirely after pruning — the variable's domain is permanently reduced.

---

### 2.3 Level 2 — Arc Consistency (AC)

**Definition**: A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ (written $X_i \to X_j$) if for every value $v_i \in D_i$, there exists some value $v_j \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied.

$$
\forall v_i \in D_i, \; \exists v_j \in D_j : (v_i, v_j) \in C_{ij}
$$

A CSP is **arc-consistent** if every variable is arc-consistent with respect to every other variable it shares a constraint with.

**Operational meaning**: A value $v_i$ that has *no supporting partner* in the neighboring variable's domain is **pruned** (deleted).

#### 2.3.1 The AC-3 Algorithm (Mackworth, 1977)
AC-3 is the most-taught algorithm in KTU. It maintains a **queue of arcs** to be checked:

1. Initialize queue $Q$ with all arcs $(X_i, X_j)$ in the constraint graph.
2. Pop an arc $(X_i, X_j)$ from $Q$.
3. **Revise** the domain of $X_i$ with respect to $X_j$:
   - For each $v_i \in D_i$, check if $\exists v_j \in D_j$ such that $(v_i, v_j) \in C_{ij}$.
   - If no such $v_j$ exists, **delete** $v_i$ from $D_i$.
4. If $D_i$ was modified, add **all neighbors** $X_k$ of $X_i$ (except $X_j$) back into $Q$ as $(X_k, X_i)$.
5. Repeat until $Q$ is empty.

**Complexity of AC-3**: $O(e \cdot d^3)$ in the worst case, where $e$ is the number of arcs and $d$ is the max domain size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "domain-independent"?** The revision step uses *only* the binary relation $C_{ij}$. It does **not** know *what* the variables represent (colors, cities, numbers, etc.). This is what makes it universally applicable.

---

### 2.4 Level 3 — Path Consistency (PC)

**Definition**: A pair of variables $(X_i, X_j)$ is **path-consistent** with respect to a third variable $X_m$ if for every assignment $(v_i, v_j)$ that satisfies $C_{ij}$, there exists a value $v_m \in D_m$ such that both $(v_i, v_m) \in C_{im}$ and $(v_j, v_m) \in C_{jm}$.

$$
\forall v_i \in D_i, \forall v_j \in D_j : (v_i, v_j) \in C_{ij} \implies \exists v_m \in D_m : (v_i, v_m) \in C_{im} \wedge (v_j, v_m) \in C_{jm}
$$

- Operates on **pairs** of variables, not single variables.
- Adds new constraints called **path constraints** between $X_i$ and $X_j$.
- **Complexity**: $O(n^3 \cdot d^3)$ — generally too expensive for large problems.

---

### 2.5 Level 4 — k-Consistency

**Definition**: A CSP is **k-consistent** if, for any set of $k-1$ variables and any consistent assignment to them, there exists a value for any $k$-th variable that extends the assignment consistently.

- **1-consistency** = Node Consistency.
- **2-consistency** = Arc Consistency.
- **3-consistency** = Path Consistency.
- **Strong k-consistency**: the CSP is j-consistent for all $j \le k$.

> [!NOTE]
> A CSP that is **strongly $n$-consistent** (where $n$ is the number of variables) is solvable in $O(n^2 d)$ with no backtracking — a huge win, but achieving full $n$-consistency is itself exponential.

---

### 2.6 Forward Checking (FC) — Lightweight Pruning

Forward checking is a **simple, online** form of arc consistency enforced *during* backtracking search:

- When a variable $X_i$ is assigned value $v_i$, examine every unassigned neighbor $X_j$.
- Delete from $D_j$ any value $v_j$ that is **inconsistent** with $v_i$.
- If any $D_j$ becomes empty, **backtrack immediately**.

**Complexity**: $O(d)$ per assignment, $O(nd)$ per branch — much cheaper than full AC-3, but less powerful.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Complexity | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | CSP representation | $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ | — | Triple: variables, domains, constraints |
| 2 | Cartesian search space | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \mid D_i \mid$ | $O(d^n)$ | Naive brute-force |
| 3 | Node Consistency | $\forall v \in D_i : v \models C(X_i)$ | $O(nd)$ | Unary constraints only |
| 4 | Arc Consistency | $\forall v_i \in D_i, \exists v_j \in D_j : (v_i,v_j) \in C_{ij}$ | $O(e \cdot d^3)$ | AC-3 algorithm |
| 5 | Path Consistency | $\forall (v_i, v_j) \in C_{ij}, \exists v_m \in D_m$ supporting | $O(n^3 d^3)$ | Adds implied constraints |
| 6 | k-Consistency | Extends $(k-1)$ consistent assignment to $k$-th var | $O(d^k)$ per check | $k=n \Rightarrow$ backtrack-free |
| 7 | Forward Checking | Prunes $D_j$ when $X_i$ assigned | $O(d)$ per var | Online, weak |
| 8 | AC-4 Algorithm | Directional arc consistency with counters | $O(e \cdot d^2)$ | Optimal worst-case |
| 9 | Look-ahead cost | Tree-structured CSP solvable in $O(nd^2)$ | — | No pruning needed if tree |
| 10 | Cycle-cut bound | Cutset size $c \Rightarrow O(d^c \cdot n)$ | — | Cycle cutset method |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: These algorithms are the backbone of:
> - **Industrial scheduling** (job-shop, crew rostering).
> - **Compiler register allocation** (graph coloring CSP).
> - **Bioinformatics** (protein structure prediction).
> - **Hardware verification** (SAT/CSP solving in chip design).
> - **Network configuration** and **cryptographic key distribution**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Map Coloring Australia

**Problem**: Color the map of Australia with 3 colors $\{R, G, B\}$ such that no two adjacent states share a color. Variables: $\{WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T\}$. Tasmania (T) is isolated, so it can take any color.

**Adjacencies**: WA–NT, WA–SA, NT–SA, NT–Q, SA–Q, SA–NSW, SA–V, Q–NSW, NSW–V.

#### Step 1 — Initial State
Each variable has full domain $D_i = \{R, G, B\}$. Arc queue contains all 9 arcs (constraints).

#### Step 2 — Apply AC-3: First Revise (WA, SA)

Check every value in $D_{WA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } v = R \in D_{WA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{SA} = \{R,G,B\} \text{ with } v \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes (e.g., } G\text{)} \\
\text{For } v = G \in D_{WA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{SA} \text{ with } v \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes} \\
\text{For } v = B \in D_{WA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{SA} \text{ with } v \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**No pruning** — $D_{WA}$ unchanged. Continue with arc (SA, WA).

#### Step 3 — Revise (SA, WA) — Critical Step

South Australia (SA) is the most constrained. Check each value in $D_{SA} = \{R, G, B\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } v = R \in D_{SA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{WA} \text{ with } R \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes (G or B)} \\
\text{For } v = G \in D_{SA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{WA} \text{ with } G \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes} \\
\text{For } v = B \in D_{SA}: & \quad \exists v' \in D_{WA} \text{ with } B \neq v'? \quad \text{Yes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Still no pruning yet — but this is misleading. The interaction of SA with **all three** neighbors (WA, NT, Q) is what bites.

#### Step 4 — Revise (SA, Q)

For SA to take value $R$, Q must take a different value. Check:
- $R \in D_{SA} \Rightarrow$ supported by $G$ or $B$ in $D_Q$? **Yes**.
- $G \in D_{SA} \Rightarrow$ supported by $R$ or $B$ in $D_Q$? **Yes**.
- $B \in D_{SA} \Rightarrow$ supported by $R$ or $G$ in $D_Q$? **Yes**.

No pruning yet. AC-3 is conservative — it only prunes on direct evidence.

#### Step 5 — Re-revise After Cascading

When $D_{SA}$ was unchanged, no neighbors of SA are re-added. But when we check (NT, SA):

For $v = R \in D_{NT}$: need a value in $D_{SA}$ different from $R$ → exists (G or B). ✓
For $v = G \in D_{NT}$: need a value in $D_{SA}$ different from $G$ → exists. ✓
For $v = B \in D_{NT}$: need a value in $D_{SA}$ different from $B$ → exists. ✓

**Result after full AC-3**: In this specific 3-color Australia problem, AC-3 alone **does not prune any value**! All 3 colors are still viable for every state. AC-3 proves only that the problem is *locally* consistent — global consistency requires search.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic KTU exam trap**: Students assume AC-3 always reduces domains. It does not. It only removes values that have *zero* support. To actually solve Australia, you need **backtracking search combined with AC-3 (MAC — Maintaining Arc Consistency)** or **forward checking**.

---

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Why AC-3 is $O(e \cdot d^3)$

#### Total Number of Arc Revisions
- A constraint graph with $e$ arcs, max domain size $d$.
- Each arc can be added to the queue **at most $d$ times** (because each time, at least one value is deleted from the source variable's domain, and there are at most $d$ values).
- Total arc insertions into queue: $O(e \cdot d)$.

#### Cost Per Revision
- For arc $(X_i, X_j)$: test each $v_i \in D_i$ by scanning all $v_j \in D_j$.
- Cost per revision: $O(\mid D_i \mid \cdot \mid D_j \mid) = O(d^2)$.

#### Total Complexity
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \underbrace{O(e \cdot d)}_{\text{revisions}} \times \underbrace{O(d^2)}_{\text{per revision}} \\
     &= O(e \cdot d^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

For AC-4 (the optimal algorithm), the bound tightens to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AC4}(n) &= O(e \cdot d^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

achieved by maintaining **support counters** for each (variable, value) pair — when a counter hits zero, the value is deleted exactly once.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Method | Strength | Weakness | Recommended Use Case |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Node Consistency** | Trivially fast | Handles only unary constraints | Initial cleanup pass |
| **AC-3** | Easy to implement | $O(e d^3)$ worst case | Pre-processing or **MAC** search |
| **AC-4** | Optimal $O(e d^2)$ | Complex counter bookkeeping | Hard, dense CSPs |
| **Path Consistency** | Strong pruning | $O(n^3 d^3)$ — very expensive | Small, tightly constrained problems |
| **Forward Checking** | Cheap, online | Misses future constraints | First-time learners, weak pruning needs |
| **MAC (AC-3 + backtrack)** | Powerful | Costly per search node | Default for serious CSP solvers |
| **Cycle Cutset** | Linear in $n$ | Exponent in cutset size | Tree-like graphs with small feedback set |

---

### 3.4 Proof Sketch: AC-3 Soundness

**Claim**: AC-3 never deletes a value that participates in a solution.

**Proof**:
1. Suppose for contradiction AC-3 deletes $v \in D_i$ but some complete solution $S$ uses $(X_i = v, X_j = w)$ for every neighbor $X_j$.
2. When $v$ was deleted, the revise step found **no** $w \in D_j$ such that $(v, w) \in C_{ij}$ at that moment.
3. Values are only deleted, never added. So at the time of deletion, $D_j$ was a *superset* of its final content.
4. Yet $S$ contains $w$ that *should* have been in $D_j$ if the algorithm were correct. Contradiction with how revise works.

Therefore, AC-3 cannot destroy solutions. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soundness holds**; **completeness does not**. AC-3 is *not* a complete solver — it only prunes. After AC-3, you still need search (often backtracking) to find the actual solution.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Pruning Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CSP Input: X, D, C] --> B[Node Consistency Pass]
    B --> C[Unary Constraints Removed]
    C --> D[Arc Consistency AC-3]
    D --> E{Domain Wiped?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Inconsistent CSP - Backtrack]
    E -- No --> G[Path Consistency]
    G --> H{Implied Constraints Added?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Re-run AC-3]
    I --> D
    H -- No --> J[Apply k-Consistency]
    J --> K{k = n?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Solution Found Directly]
    K -- No --> M[Backtracking Search with MAC]
    M --> N[Final Solution]
    F --> O[FAIL]
    L --> N
```

### 4.2 AC-3 Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start AC-3]) --> initQ[Initialize Queue Q with all arcs]
    initQ --> checkQ{Q is empty?}
    checkQ -- Yes --> done([AC-3 Complete - Domains Reduced])
    checkQ -- No --> popArc[Pop arc X_i, X_j from Q]
    popArc --> revise[REVISE X_i w.r.t. X_j]
    revise --> changed{D_i changed?}
    changed -- No --> checkQ
    changed -- Yes --> addNeighbors[Add arcs X_k, X_i to Q for all neighbors X_k of X_i except X_j]
    addNeighbors --> checkQ
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Constraint Propagation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        VARS[Variables X]
        DOM[Domains D]
        CON[Constraints C]
    end

    subgraph NodeNC[Node Consistency Module]
        NC1[Unary Filter]
        NC2[Domain Cleaner]
    end

    subgraph ArcAC[Arc Consistency Module]
        ACQ[Arc Queue Manager]
        REV[Revise Operator]
        CTR[Support Counter Engine]
    end

    subgraph PathPC[Path Consistency Module]
        PC1[Pair Validator]
        PC2[Implied Constraint Generator]
    end

    subgraph SearchLayer[Search Layer]
        FC[Forward Checker]
        MAC[MAC Backtracker]
        SOL[Solution Extractor]
    end

    VARS --> NC1
    DOM --> NC1
    CON --> NC1
    NC1 --> NC2
    NC2 --> ACQ
    ACQ --> REV
    REV --> CTR
    CTR --> PC1
    PC1 --> PC2
    PC2 --> FC
    FC --> MAC
    MAC --> SOL
    SOL --> OUT([Final Assignment])
```

### 4.4 Consistency Strength Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Weakest[Weakest Pruning]
        NC[Node Consistency - O of n times d]
    end
    subgraph Mid[Medium Pruning]
        AC[Arc Consistency AC-3 - O of e times d cubed]
    end
    subgraph Strong[Stronger Pruning]
        PC[Path Consistency - O of n cubed times d cubed]
    end
    subgraph Strongest[Strongest Pruning]
        KC[k-Consistency for large k - O of d to power k]
    end
    NC -->|subsumes| AC
    AC -->|subsumes| PC
    PC -->|subsumes| KC
```

### 4.5 Worked Trace: AC-3 on a 3-Node Path (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Variable A: Domain dA] -->|Arc A,B| B[Variable B: Domain dB]
    B -->|Arc B,C| C[Variable C: Domain dC]
    A -->|Arc A,C| C

    subgraph QueueOps[Queue Operations in Order]
        Q1[Step 1: Push A,B]
        Q2[Step 2: Push B,A]
        Q3[Step 3: Push B,C]
        Q4[Step 4: Push C,B]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define domain-independent pruning. Why is it called "domain-independent"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Domain-independent pruning refers to a set of techniques used in Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs) to systematically reduce the search space by eliminating values from variable domains, using only the **structural properties of the constraints** and not any problem-specific knowledge (3 marks).

It is called "domain-independent" because the same algorithms (e.g., arc consistency, path consistency) work uniformly across **diverse problem domains** such as map coloring, scheduling, Sudoku, or cryptarithmetic — they only manipulate the constraint relations, not the semantics of the variables.

**Mark Distribution**:
- Definition: 2 marks
- Explanation of "domain-independent": 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between node consistency and arc consistency with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Node Consistency | Arc Consistency |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Scope | Single variable, unary constraints | Two variables, binary constraints |
| Condition | Every value satisfies all unary constraints | Every value has at least one supporting partner in neighbor's domain |
| Complexity | $O(nd)$ | $O(e \cdot d^3)$ via AC-3 |
| Example | "Variable $X$ must be $> 0$" — removes 0 from $D_X$ | "Binary constraint $X \neq Y$" — checks cross-support |

**Example**: Let $X, Y \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with constraint $X < Y$.
- **Node consistency**: $X=4$ is removed (no $Y > 4$ exists). Result: $D_X = \{1,2,3\}$, $D_Y = \{2,3,4\}$.
- **Arc consistency**: Further, $X=3$ is supported by $Y=4$; $X=1$ by $Y \in \{2,3,4\}$; but $Y=2$ is supported by $X=1$, and so on — no further pruning needed in this case.

**Mark Distribution**:
- Tabular difference: 2 marks
- Example: 1 mark

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]*

**Q3a.** Define Constraint Satisfaction Problem. Explain arc consistency with a formal definition. State the AC-3 algorithm and analyze its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Q3b.** Consider the following CSP:
- Variables: $X_1, X_2, X_3$
- Domains: $D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$
- Constraints: $C_{12}$: $X_1 < X_2$; $C_{23}$: $X_2 < X_3$; $C_{13}$: $X_1 \neq X_3$

Apply the AC-3 algorithm step-by-step. Show the final reduced domains. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q3a (7 marks)

**Definition of CSP (1 mark)**:
A CSP is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where $X$ is a finite set of variables, $D$ is a set of domains (one per variable), and $C$ is a set of constraints specifying allowed combinations of values.

**Arc Consistency — Formal Definition (2 marks)**:
A variable $X_i$ is arc-consistent with respect to $X_j$ if:

$$
\forall v_i \in D_i, \quad \exists v_j \in D_j \text{ such that } (v_i, v_j) \text{ satisfies } C_{ij}
$$

A CSP is arc-consistent if every variable is arc-consistent with respect to every variable it is constrained with.

**AC-3 Algorithm (3 marks)**:

```
AC-3(CSP):
  Q = queue of all arcs (X_i, X_j) in CSP
  while Q is not empty:
    (X_i, X_j) = Q.pop()
    if REVISE(X_i, X_j):
      if D_i is empty: return failure
      for each X_k in NEIGHBORS[X_i] \ {X_j}:
        Q.push((X_k, X_i))
  return success

REVISE(X_i, X_j):
  revised = false
  for each v_i in D_i:
    if no v_j in D_j satisfies (v_i, v_j) in C_{ij}:
      delete v_i from D_i
      revised = true
  return revised
```

**Time Complexity (1 mark)**:
Each arc can be re-enqueued at most $d$ times (since each time at least one value is pruned from $D_i$, and $|D_i| \le d$). There are $e$ arcs. Each REVISE call costs $O(d^2)$. Total:

$$
T(n) = O(e \cdot d \cdot d^2) = O(e \cdot d^3)
$$

---

### Model Solution to Q3b (7 marks)

**Initial State (1 mark)**:
$D_1 = D_2 = D_3 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Queue initially contains 6 arcs: $(X_1, X_2), (X_2, X_1), (X_2, X_3), (X_3, X_2), (X_1, X_3), (X_3, X_1)$.

**Step 1: REVISE(X_1, X_2) using $C_{12}: X_1 < X_2$ (1 mark)**:

- $v_1 = 1$: supported by $v_2 = 2 \in D_2$. Keep.
- $v_1 = 2$: supported by $v_2 = 3$. Keep.
- $v_1 = 3$: supported by $v_2 = 4$. Keep.
- $v_1 = 4$: needs $v_2 > 4$. None exists. **Delete 4**.

Result: $D_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$. Re-enqueue $(X_3, X_1)$.

**Step 2: REVISE(X_2, X_1) using $C_{12}$ (1 mark)**:

- $v_2 = 4$: needs $v_1 < 4$. $D_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ supports. Keep.
- Other values trivially supported.

No change. Continue.

**Step 3: REVISE(X_2, X_3) using $C_{23}: X_2 < X_3$ (1 mark)**:

- $v_2 = 4$: needs $v_3 > 4$. None. **Delete 4**.

Result: $D_2 = \{1, 2, 3\}$.

**Step 4: REVISE(X_3, X_2) using $C_{23}$ (1 mark)**:

- $v_3 = 4$: needs $v_2 < 4$. Supported by $D_2 = \{1,2,3\}$. Keep.

**Step 5: REVISE(X_1, X_3) using $C_{13}: X_1 \neq X_3$ (1 mark)**:

With $D_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ and $D_3 = \{1,2,3,4\}$:
- Every $v_1 \in \{1,2,3\}$ has a $v_3 \in \{1,2,3,4\} \setminus \{v_1\}$. No pruning.

**Step 6: REVISE(X_3, X_1) using $C_{13}$ (0.5 marks)**:

$D_3 = \{1,2,3,4\}$, $D_1 = \{1,2,3\}$:
- $v_3 = 4$: supported by any $v_1 \in \{1,2,3\}$. Keep.
- $v_3 = 1,2,3$: each supported by some $v_1 \in \{1,2,3\} \setminus \{v_3\}$. Keep.

**Final Reduced Domains (0.5 marks)**:

$$
D_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}, \quad D_2 = \{1, 2, 3\}, \quad D_3 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}
$$

Original search space: $4^3 = 64$. Reduced: $3 \times 3 \times 4 = 36$. **~44% reduction** with no backtracking.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]*

**Q4a.** Explain path consistency and k-consistency. Discuss their relationship with arc consistency. **(7 marks)**

**Q4b.** Consider a graph coloring problem with 4 variables $\{A, B, C, D\}$, each with domain $\{1, 2, 3\}$. Constraints: $A \neq B$, $B \neq C$, $C \neq D$, $A \neq D$, $A \neq C$. Apply node consistency, then arc consistency, and report final domains. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q4a (7 marks)

**Path Consistency Definition (2.5 marks)**:
A pair of variables $(X_i, X_j)$ is path-consistent with respect to a third variable $X_m$ if for every consistent assignment $(v_i, v_j)$ that satisfies the binary constraint $C_{ij}$, there exists at least one value $v_m$ in the domain of $X_m$ such that both $(v_i, v_m) \in C_{im}$ and $(v_j, v_m) \in C_{jm}$.

**Mathematical Formulation**:

$$
\forall (v_i, v_j) \in C_{ij} \implies \exists v_m \in D_m \text{ s.t. } (v_i,v_m) \in C_{im} \wedge (v_j,v_m) \in C_{jm}
$$

Path consistency adds **new constraints** between $X_i$ and $X_j$ (called *implied* or *path constraints*). Complexity: $O(n^3 \cdot d^3)$.

**k-Consistency Definition (2.5 marks)**:
A CSP is **k-consistent** if, given any consistent assignment to $k-1$ variables, there exists a value for any $k$-th variable that extends this assignment consistently. The hierarchy:
- 1-consistent $\equiv$ Node consistent
- 2-consistent $\equiv$ Arc consistent
- 3-consistent $\equiv$ Path consistent
- Strongly $n$-consistent $\Rightarrow$ backtrack-free solution

**Relationship (2 marks)**:
Arc consistency is a special case of path consistency (k=2), which itself is a special case of k-consistency. Higher k means stronger pruning but exponentially more expensive. The trade-off determines practical use:
- Arc consistency (AC-3) is the workhorse.
- Path consistency is rarely used standalone; mostly in constraint *tightening* pre-processing.
- Full $n$-consistency is theoretical — exponential in $n$.

---

### Model Solution to Q4b (7 marks)

**Initial State (1 mark)**: $D_A = D_B = D_C = D_D = \{1, 2, 3\}$.

**Step 1 — Node Consistency (1 mark)**:
No unary constraints given, so node consistency pass leaves all domains unchanged.

**Step 2 — Identify All Arcs (1 mark)**:
Constraint graph edges: $(A,B), (B,C), (C,D), (A,D), (A,C)$. With both directions: 10 arcs.

**Step 3 — Apply AC-3 (3 marks)**:

*REVISE(B, A) using $A \neq B$*:
$D_B = \{1,2,3\}$, $D_A = \{1,2,3\}$. Each $v_B$ has support in $D_A$. No pruning.

*REVISE(A, B)*, *REVISE(C, B)*, *REVISE(B, C)*: symmetric. No pruning yet.

*REVISE(C, A) using $A \neq C$*: No pruning.

*REVISE(D, C) using $C \neq D$*: No pruning.
*REVISE(C, D)*: No pruning.

*REVISE(D, A) using $A \neq D$*: No pruning.

**Key Observation**: For each arc, every value in any domain has a partner in the neighbor's domain (since both domains contain all of $\{1,2,3\}$ and the constraint is just inequality). **AC-3 cannot prune**.

**Final Domains (1 mark)**:

$$
D_A = D_B = D_C = D_D = \{1, 2, 3\}
$$

Search space remains $3^4 = 81$. The problem is solvable (e.g., $A=1, B=2, C=3, D=1$), but AC-3 alone cannot detect that. **MAC (Maintaining Arc Consistency during backtracking) is needed**.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks)**: This example illustrates the **limitation of pure AC-3** — it provides a *necessary* but not *sufficient* condition for solvability. Domain-independent pruning reduces the search space but does not replace search.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Domain-Independent Pruning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pruning with solving**: AC-3 is *not* a complete solver. Writing "AC-3 finds the solution" will cost you 2–3 marks. Always say *"AC-3 prunes the domain; backtracking is then required."*
>
> 2. **Forgetting cascading re-queueing**: When a value is deleted from $D_i$, **all neighbors** of $X_i$ (except the one that caused the deletion) must be re-enqueued. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing arc and path consistency**: Students frequently say "path consistency is faster than arc consistency" — **false**. Path consistency is exponentially more expensive.
>
> 4. **Wrong direction of arc**: Arc $(X_i, X_j)$ means "revise $X_i$ w.r.t. $X_j$", not the other way around. The source variable's domain is pruned.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "domain-independent" qualifier**: Examiners specifically test whether you understand *why* these methods generalize. A bare algorithm walkthrough without the "domain-independent" justification loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Time complexity slip-up**: Writing $O(n \cdot d^3)$ instead of $O(e \cdot d^3)$ is a common error. $e$ is the number of arcs, not variables.
>
> 7. **Not showing each REVISE step explicitly**: Examiners want a **trace table** or step-by-step. A single line "AC-3 runs and reduces the domain" earns zero marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Domain-Independent Pruning**

- **CSP triple**: $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ — variables, domains, constraints.
- **Naive search space**: $d^n$ — exponential, hence need pruning.
- **Node Consistency**: Removes values violating **unary** constraints. Complexity: $O(nd)$. Trivial.
- **Arc Consistency**: For every value in $D_i$, there must exist a supporting value in $D_j$. **AC-3** is the canonical algorithm. Complexity: $O(e \cdot d^3)$.
- **AC-3 procedure**: Initialize queue with all arcs → pop arc → REVISE → if revised, re-enqueue neighbor arcs → repeat until empty.
- **AC-4**: Optimal $O(e \cdot d^2)$ using support counters.
- **Path Consistency**: Operates on **pairs** of variables, adds implied constraints. Complexity: $O(n^3 d^3)$. Rare in practice due to cost.
- **k-Consistency**: Generalization; $k$-consistency $\Rightarrow$ $(k-1)$-consistency. Strongly $n$-consistent CSPs are backtrack-free.
- **Forward Checking**: Lightweight, online pruning. $O(d)$ per assignment. Weaker than AC-3.
- **MAC** (Maintaining Arc Consistency): The gold standard — re-runs AC-3 after every assignment.
- **Sound but not complete**: Pruning never destroys solutions, but does not *find* them alone.
- **Tree-structured CSPs**: Solvable in $O(n d^2)$ with no backtracking — a special case where AC alone suffices.
- **Cycle cutset method**: For graphs with small feedback sets, condition on the cutset, then solve the remaining tree.
- **Domain-independent**: The algorithms use **only constraint structure**, not problem semantics — this is what makes them universal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Abstraction in Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Abstraction in Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of classical AI planning, a **planning problem** is a tuple $\Pi = \langle V, A, I, G \rangle$, where:
- $V$ is a finite set of state variables (propositions or multi-valued variables),
- $A$ is a finite set of actions (each with preconditions and effects),
- $I \subseteq V$ is the initial state,
- $G \subseteq V$ is the goal condition.

A **State Abstraction** is formally defined as a function
$$\alpha : S \rightarrow S_{\alpha}$$
that maps each *concrete state* $s \in S$ of the original (ground) planning problem to an *abstract state* $\hat{s} \in S_{\alpha}$ of an abstract planning problem $\Pi_{\alpha} = \langle V_{\alpha}, A_{\alpha}, I_{\alpha}, G_{\alpha} \rangle$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** State abstraction is the process of *collapsing* multiple concrete states into a single abstract state by ignoring irrelevant variables, thereby producing a smaller, computationally tractable planning problem that preserves certain structural properties of the original problem.

Mathematically, $\alpha$ induces an **equivalence relation** $\sim_{\alpha}$ over the concrete state space:
$$s_1 \sim_{\alpha} s_2 \iff \alpha(s_1) = \alpha(s_2)$$

The equivalence classes of $\sim_{\alpha}$ form the abstract state space:
$$S_{\alpha} = S / \sim_{\alpha}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "GPS Zoom Level" View

Imagine planning a road trip from **Kochi to Delhi**.

| Zoom Level | What You See | Variable Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0 (Full Detail)** | Every lane, pothole, traffic signal, speed-breaker | $\approx 10^7$ details |
| **Level 1 (City View)** | Streets, junctions, landmarks | $\approx 10^4$ details |
| **Level 2 (State View)** | National Highways (NH-44, NH-48) | $\approx 10^2$ details |
| **Level 3 (Country View)** | North → South → East → West zones | $\approx 4$ details |

At each higher zoom level, you *group* thousands of concrete roads into a single abstract node (e.g., "NH-44 corridor"). The route you compute at Level 2 is a *valid skeleton* — once you descend back to Level 0, you can still drive the trip, because every Level-2 node *refines into* a network of Level-0 roads.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is exactly what state abstraction does in AI planning:** it groups concrete states into abstract "super-states," solves the (much smaller) abstract problem, and then *refines* the abstract solution back into concrete actions. The key challenge — and what makes abstraction an *exam-worthy* topic — is guaranteeing that the abstract plan is *actually executable* and *actually solves* the original problem.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of a Valid Abstraction

For an abstraction $\alpha$ to be useful, it must satisfy:

1. **Well-definedness:** Every concrete state maps to *exactly one* abstract state.
2. **Soundness:** Any plan that works in the abstract problem *must refine* to a plan that works in the concrete problem.
3. **Usefulness:** The abstract problem must be *strictly smaller* (in state-space size) than the concrete one — otherwise abstraction is pointless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Projection Abstraction:** The most common abstraction in KTU textbooks is **projection** (variable-dropping). Given a subset $V' \subseteq V$, the projected state is $\alpha(s) = s \upharpoonright_{V'}$ (i.e., $s$ restricted to variables in $V'$). This single mechanism underlies landmark heuristics, causal graph heuristics, and merge-and-shrink abstractions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a State Abstraction

A state abstraction operates through the following structured pipeline:

### Step 1 — Choose the Abstraction Function $\alpha$
The designer decides *which details to forget*. Formally:
$$\alpha : S \rightarrow S_{\alpha}, \quad |S_{\alpha}| \ll |S|$$

### Step 2 — Compute Abstract Initial State and Goal
$$I_{\alpha} = \alpha(I), \quad G_{\alpha} = \alpha(G)$$

### Step 3 — Derive Abstract Actions
Each concrete action $a \in A$ has an abstract counterpart $\hat{a} = \alpha(a)$ such that:
$$\text{pre}_{\alpha}(\hat{a}) = \alpha(\text{pre}(a))$$
$$\text{eff}_{\alpha}(\hat{a}) = \alpha(\text{eff}(a))$$

### Step 4 — Solve Abstract Problem
Run any planner (BFS, $A^*$, Graphplan) on $\Pi_{\alpha}$. Since $|S_{\alpha}| \ll |S|$, this is exponentially cheaper.

### Step 5 — Refine Abstract Solution
Map each abstract action $\hat{a}$ back to the concrete action $a$ that produced it (or choose from the set of concrete actions $a$ with $\alpha(a) = \hat{a}$).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why Step 1 matters:** A bad $\alpha$ either loses too much information (causing the abstract plan to fail in the concrete world) or preserves too much (defeating the purpose of abstraction).
- **Why Step 3 is critical:** The abstract action set must be a *conservative over-approximation* — every concrete action must have a *corresponding* abstract action, but not vice-versa. This is the **homomorphism condition**.
- **How Step 5 works:** Refinement may fail; in that case, we must *re-plan* with a refined abstraction (iterative abstraction, used in systems like **SHOP** and **FF** with abstraction).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Abstraction Function | $\alpha : S \rightarrow S_{\alpha}$ | Total function, well-defined |
| 2 | Equivalence Relation | $s_1 \sim_{\alpha} s_2 \iff \alpha(s_1) = \alpha(s_2)$ | Reflexive, symmetric, transitive |
| 3 | Projection Abstraction | $\alpha(s) = s \upharpoonright_{V'},\ V' \subseteq V$ | Most-used in practice |
| 4 | Abstract State Count | $\vert S_{\alpha}\vert \leq \prod_{v \in V'} \vert \text{dom}(v)\vert$ | Bounds explosion |
| 5 | Homomorphism (Actions) | $\forall a \in A : \alpha(\text{pre}(a)) \subseteq \text{pre}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a))$ | Soundness condition |
| 6 | Effect Preservation | $\alpha(\text{eff}(a)) = \text{eff}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a))$ | Determinism |
| 7 | Soundness Theorem | $\pi_{\alpha} \text{ solves } \Pi_{\alpha} \Rightarrow \text{refine}(\pi_{\alpha}) \text{ solves } \Pi$ | Requires homomorphism |
| 8 | Lossy Refinement | $\pi_{\alpha}$ may *not* refine to a valid concrete plan | Triggers re-planning |
| 9 | Causal Graph Height | $h(\Pi_{\alpha}) \leq h(\Pi)$ | Abstraction never *increases* $h$-value |
| 10 | Bisimulation (strong) | $\alpha(s_1) = \alpha(s_2) \Rightarrow \gamma(s_1, a) \sim \gamma(s_2, a)$ | Strongest correctness condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Warning for Table:** The above table uses `\vert` for cardinality bars to prevent breaking markdown rendering. **Do NOT use `|` for absolute value inside KTU table cells — it will destroy the column structure.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** A robot vacuum abstracts a 2D occupancy grid into room-level topological maps ("kitchen", "living room") for fast path planning.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Waymo's planner uses hierarchical abstraction — lane-level → road-level → region-level — to plan across cities in real-time.
- **Industrial Automation:** Siemens factory planners use abstraction to schedule thousands of machines by first solving a "factory-floor" plan, then refining each cell.
- **Game AI (StarCraft, Chess engines):** AlphaZero-style systems use state abstraction over massive board spaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Concrete Problem to Abstract Problem

Let the concrete planning problem be:
$$\Pi = \langle V, A, I, G \rangle, \quad V = \{p, q, r\}$$

with the concrete state space being all subsets of $V$, i.e., $\vert S \vert = 2^3 = 8$.

**Step 1 — Choose abstraction variable subset.**
Let $V' = \{p, q\} \subseteq V$. (We "forget" $r$.)

**Step 2 — Define $\alpha$ as projection.**
$$\alpha : S \rightarrow S_{\alpha}, \quad \alpha(s) = s \cap V'$$

**Step 3 — Enumerate concrete states and their abstract images.**

| Concrete State $s$ | Abstract State $\alpha(s)$ |
|---|---|
| $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $\{p\}$ | $\{p\}$ |
| $\{q\}$ | $\{q\}$ |
| $\{r\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $\{p, q\}$ | $\{p, q\}$ |
| $\{p, r\}$ | $\{p\}$ |
| $\{q, r\}$ | $\{q\}$ |
| $\{p, q, r\}$ | $\{p, q\}$ |

**Step 4 — Compute abstract state space size.**
$$\vert S_{\alpha} \vert = 2^{\vert V'\vert} = 2^2 = 4$$
The states $\emptyset$, $\{p\}$, $\{q\}$, $\{p,q\}$ are the four abstract states.

**Step 5 — Group concrete states by equivalence class.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
{[\emptyset]}_{\sim_{\alpha}} &= \{\emptyset,\ \{r\}\} \\
{[\{p\}]}_{\sim_{\alpha}} &= \{\{p\},\ \{p, r\}\} \\
{[\{q\}]}_{\sim_{\alpha}} &= \{\{q\},\ \{q, r\}\} \\
{[\{p, q\}]}_{\sim_{\alpha}} &= \{\{p, q\},\ \{p, q, r\}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define abstract actions.**
Suppose the concrete action $a$ is: $\text{pre}(a) = \{q\},\ \text{eff}(a) = \{p, \neg r\}$.
Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{pre}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a)) &= \alpha(\{q\}) = \{q\} \\
\text{eff}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a)) &= \alpha(\{p, \neg r\}) = \{p\}
\end{aligned}
$$
The abstract action ignores the $r$-effect, which is exactly what abstraction means.

**Step 7 — Verify homomorphism condition (soundness).**
For each concrete action $a$, we must check:
$$\alpha(\text{pre}(a)) \subseteq \text{pre}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a))$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha(\text{pre}(a)) &= \alpha(\{q\}) = \{q\} \\
\text{pre}_{\alpha}(\alpha(a)) &= \{q\}
\end{aligned}
$$
Since $\{q\} \subseteq \{q\}$, the condition holds. ✓

**Step 8 — Solve abstract problem.**
With $\vert S_{\alpha} \vert = 4$ instead of $8$, the search tree is roughly **half the size**, demonstrating the computational savings of abstraction.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Abstraction Properties vs. Plan Quality

| Abstraction Type | State Reduction | Plan Optimality | Soundness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Projection ($V' \subset V$)** | Exponential in $\vert V \setminus V'\vert$ | May lose optimality | Guaranteed if homomorphic | Heuristic functions |
| **Merge-and-Shrink** | Polynomial | Preserves distance approx. | Guaranteed | $h^{\text{MS}}$ heuristic |
| **Domain Abstraction** (merge values) | Variable | Often lossy | Guaranteed | Fast Downward planner |
| **Counter-Example Guided (CEGAR)** | Adaptive | Iteratively refined | Iterative | Model checking, planning |
| **Relaxation (delete-relaxation)** | Removes delete-effects | Heuristic only | Admissible $h^+$ | FF, Fast Downward |
| **Landmark Abstraction** | Polynomial | Sound | Yes | LM-cut, RHWP |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Soundness Theorem (Statement + Sketch)

> **Theorem (Soundness of State Abstraction):**
> Let $\alpha$ be a homomorphism from $\Pi$ to $\Pi_{\alpha}$. If $\pi_{\alpha}$ is a plan for $\Pi_{\alpha}$, then the refinement $\pi$ obtained by replacing each $\hat{a}_i$ in $\pi_{\alpha}$ with some concrete $a_i \in \alpha^{-1}(\hat{a}_i)$ is a valid plan for $\Pi$ from any concrete initial state $I$ with $\alpha(I) = I_{\alpha}$ to any concrete goal $G$ with $\alpha(G) = G_{\alpha}$.

**Proof sketch (by induction on plan length $n$):**

*Base case ($n=0$):* Trivially, if $I_{\alpha} \models G_{\alpha}$, then by $\alpha(G) = G_{\alpha}$ and monotonicity of $\alpha$, we have $I \models G$. ✓

*Inductive step:* Assume after $k$ actions, the concrete state $s_k$ satisfies $\alpha(s_k) = \hat{s}_k \models \hat{g}$ for all $\hat{g} \in G_{\alpha}$. Action $\hat{a}_{k+1}$ has $\text{pre}_{\alpha}(\hat{a}_{k+1}) \subseteq \hat{s}_k$. By homomorphism:
$$\alpha(\text{pre}(a_{k+1})) \subseteq \text{pre}_{\alpha}(\hat{a}_{k+1}) \subseteq \hat{s}_k = \alpha(s_k)$$
By the *concrete-to-abstract* direction (which is total), we conclude $\text{pre}(a_{k+1}) \subseteq s_k$. Thus $a_{k+1}$ is applicable. Effects then propagate via $\alpha(\text{eff}(a_{k+1})) = \text{eff}_{\alpha}(\hat{a}_{k+1})$. By induction, the abstract plan terminates successfully in $\Pi_{\alpha}$, and the concrete plan terminates successfully in $\Pi$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: State Abstraction Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["CONCRETE PROBLEM Pi"]
    nodeB["State Space S, size 2^n"]
    nodeC["Abstraction Function alpha"]
    nodeD["ABSTRACT PROBLEM Pi_alpha"]
    nodeE["State Space S_alpha, size 2^k, k less than n"]
    nodeF["Abstract Planner BFS or A-star"]
    nodeG["Abstract Plan pi_alpha"]
    nodeH["Refinement Function beta"]
    nodeI["CONCRETE PLAN pi"]
    nodeJ["Execution in Real World"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ

    nodeC -. equivalence relation .-> nodeB
    nodeH -. inverse mapping alpha inverse .-> nodeG
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The *downward* path (left → right) is **abstraction** (concrete → abstract).
- The *upward* path (right → left, bottom row) is **refinement** (abstract → concrete).
- The dotted arrows represent the *mathematical coupling*: $\alpha$ induces equivalence, $\beta = \alpha^{-1}$ performs refinement.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Equivalence-Class Partitioning of Concrete States

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph EQ1["Class 1: alpha equals empty"]
        st1["s1 equals empty"]
        st2["s2 equals only r"]
    end

    subgraph EQ2["Class 2: alpha equals only p"]
        st3["s3 equals only p"]
        st4["s4 equals p and r"]
    end

    subgraph EQ3["Class 3: alpha equals only q"]
        st5["s5 equals only q"]
        st6["s6 equals q and r"]
    end

    subgraph EQ4["Class 4: alpha equals p and q"]
        st7["s7 equals p and q"]
        st8["s8 equals p and q and r"]
    end

    EQ1 --> AS1["Abstract State 1: empty"]
    EQ2 --> AS2["Abstract State 2: only p"]
    EQ3 --> AS3["Abstract State 3: only q"]
    EQ4 --> AS4["Abstract State 4: p and q"]
```

This block diagram directly visualizes the equivalence classes derived in **Section 3.1, Step 5**. Each subgraph is one abstract state, and the concrete states inside it are "collapsed" by $\alpha$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Refinement Failure & Re-Planning Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    spA["Abstract Plan pi_alpha computed"]
    spB["Attempt Refinement via beta"]
    spC{"Refinement succeeds?"}
    spD["Concrete Plan pi, return to executor"]
    spE["Refinement fails, action mismatch"]
    spF["Refine abstraction alpha prime"]
    spG["Re-solve pi_alpha prime"]
    spH["Loop limit reached?"}
    spI["Return failure to caller"]

    spA --> spB
    spB --> spC
    spC -->|"Yes"| spD
    spC -->|"No"| spE
    spE --> spF
    spF --> spG
    spG --> spB
    spB --> spH
    spH -->|"No"| spE
    spH -->|"Yes"| spI
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The above loop is the operational essence of **Counter-Example Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR)** — a high-yield concept for Part B questions. The "loop" structure shows *why* pure abstraction can fail and *how* iterative refinement salvages correctness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define state abstraction in the context of classical planning. How does it differ from action abstraction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
