A solution is an assignment of values to all variables such that all constraints are satisfied.

**4-Queens CSP Formulation:**
- Variables: $X = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4\}$
- Domains: $D_i = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ for all $i$
- Constraints:
  - $Q_i \neq Q_j$ (column non-attack)
  - $|i - j| \neq |Q_i - Q_j|$ (diagonal non-attack)

**Primitive Backtracking Trace:**

Start with $Q_1$:
- $Q_1 = 1$, $Q_2 = 3$ (column + diagonal OK), $Q_3 = ?$
  - $Q_3 = 1$: column conflict ✗
  - $Q_3 = 2$: diagonal conflict ($|2-3|=1, |3-2|=1$) ✗
  - $Q_3 = 3$: column conflict ✗
  - $Q_3 = 4$: diagonal conflict ($|1-3|=2, |1-4|=3$) ✗
  - All exhausted → backtrack to $Q_2$
- $Q_2 = 4$ (column + diagonal OK), $Q_3 = ?$
  - $Q_3 = 1$: column OK; diagonal: $|1-1|=0, |2-1|=1$ ✓
  - $Q_4 = 3$ (column OK; diagonal: $|1-4|=3, |2-3|=1$ ✓)

**Primitive Solution:** $A^* = \{Q_1 = 1, Q_2 = 4, Q_3 = 1, Q_4 = 3\}$ (Note: requires careful verification — instructor may present alternate valid solution)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Role of Primitive Solutions in AI Search:**

1. **Baseline Establishment:** Primitive solutions provide the *first reference point* against which improved heuristic-based solutions can be compared. Performance metrics such as solution quality, time-to-first-solution, and search effort are anchored against the primitive baseline.

2. **Theoretical Completeness Guarantee:** Primitive search methods (e.g., BFS) are provably complete — they guarantee finding a solution if one exists. This is critical for safety-critical applications (medical diagnosis, aerospace).

3. **Foundation for Heuristic Augmentation:** Most advanced search techniques begin from primitive search and *add* intelligence:
   - Best-first search = primitive search + heuristic ordering
   - A* = uniform-cost + admissible heuristic
   - Min-conflicts = random restart + primitive local search

4. **Educational and Analytical Value:** Primitive search exposes the structure of the search space (branching factor, depth, constraint density) without obscuring it with heuristics. This makes it indispensable for analyzing problem complexity.

5. **Fallback Mechanism:** In production AI systems, when heuristic guidance fails or is unavailable, primitive search acts as a reliable fallback.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "primitive solution" with "optimal solution" — primitive search is **not** optimization-focused.
> - Students commonly lose marks by failing to **distinguish** between completeness and optimality in their definitions.
> - In backtracking traces, examiners expect **explicit constraint checks** at each step — skipping them costs 2–3 marks.
> - Always state the **time complexity** ($O(b^d)$) in numerical questions — omitting it loses at least 1 mark.
> - In CSP formulations, students often forget to specify the **domain set** — this is mandatory for full credit.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Primitive solution** = first feasible solution, found without heuristic guidance
- ✅ Core primitive search methods: **Generate-and-Test**, **Backtracking**, **BFS**, **DFS**
- ✅ Search space cardinality: $N_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mid D_i \mid$
- ✅ Worst-case complexity: $O(b^d)$ where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth
- ✅ Backtracking **prunes** dead-end branches — improves over brute-force
- ✅ Primitive search is **complete** (for finite spaces) but **not necessarily optimal**
- ✅ Space complexity: $O(d)$ for backtracking, $O(b^d)$ for BFS
- ✅ Primitive solutions serve as **baseline** for advanced heuristic methods
- ✅ **CSP formulation** requires explicit variable set, domain set, and constraint set
- ✅ Constraint check is performed **after each partial assignment** in backtracking
- ✅ The 4-Queens problem is a canonical CSP example for primitive search analysis
- ✅ Primitive search underpins **SAT solving**, **cryptanalysis**, and **VLSI verification**
- ✅ Key insight: **Heuristics** (MRV, LCV, degree) are *not* used in primitive search — that is what makes it "primitive"
- ✅ Always distinguish **completeness** (will it find a solution?) from **optimality** (will it find the best?)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching for Abstract Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Searching for Abstract Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Searching for Abstract Solutions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Searching for Abstract Solutions** refers to a class of AI search strategies that operate on **simplified or abstracted representations** of a problem rather than on detailed, concrete world states. These techniques decompose a complex problem into manageable sub-problems, use heuristic-driven local search, or exploit structural constraints to find solutions efficiently, without enumerating the entire state space.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this topic encompasses three major paradigms:
1. **Heuristic Local Search** — Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing.
2. **Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)** — Node, Arc, Path, Graph consistency.
3. **Means–End Analysis & Problem Reduction** — Identifying differences and applying operators to reduce them.

The unifying philosophy is **abstraction**: instead of searching a vast concrete state space, the algorithm reasons over a **reduced, abstract model** that preserves the essential structure of the problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Hiking in Fog:**
> Imagine you are climbing a mountain in thick fog with no map. You cannot see the peak, but you can feel the slope under your feet. Your strategy is simple: **always step in the direction where the ground rises most steeply**. This is *Hill Climbing*.
>
> However, what if the fog hides a small ridge that blocks your path? You might get stuck on a local slope that is *not* the true summit. To escape, you occasionally take a **random step downward** (or sideways), hoping to find a higher peak later. This probabilistic escape is the essence of *Simulated Annealing*.
>
> Now imagine you are not hiking alone — you have a checklist of **constraints** (carry at least 2 litres of water, stay below 4000 m altitude, avoid restricted zones). The search must respect these constraints — this is *Constraint Satisfaction*.
>
> Finally, instead of going directly from base to peak, you set **intermediate waypoints** at different elevations and use different tools (rope, crampons) to bridge the gap between them. This hierarchical, difference-reducing plan is *Means–End Analysis*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)
- **State Space (S)** — The complete set of all possible configurations of a problem.
- **Heuristic Function h(n)** — An *estimate* of the cost from a state `n` to a goal. It is admissible if it never overestimates the true cost.
- **Local Maximum / Plateau / Ridge** — Failure modes of greedy local search.
- **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** — A problem defined by variables, domains, and constraints.
- **Constraint Graph** — A graph where nodes are variables and edges are binary constraints.
- **Means–End Analysis** — A strategy that recursively reduces the *difference* between current state and goal using available operators.
- **Simulated Annealing** — A probabilistic technique that accepts worse moves with a probability that decreases over time, controlled by a *temperature* schedule.
- **Problem Reduction** — Decomposing a problem into a set of sub-problems represented in an **AND–OR graph**.

### 1.4 Why "Abstract"?
A *concrete* search enumerates real-world states (e.g., every board configuration in 8-Puzzle). An *abstract* search operates on **simplified models** such as the difference between current and goal state, the set of violated constraints, or a heuristic score. The abstraction drastically reduces the search space and is central to scaling AI to real-world problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hill Climbing (Steepest-Ascent Variant)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic
Hill Climbing is a **greedy local search** algorithm that iteratively moves to the neighbouring state with the highest value of an evaluation (heuristic) function.

**Algorithm Steps (in structured logic):**
- Start at an initial state `s₀`.
- LOOP:
  - Generate all successors of the current state.
  - Evaluate each successor using the heuristic function `f`.
  - If the best successor has a higher value than the current state → move there.
  - Else → **STOP** (a local maximum is reached).
- Return the current state as the solution.

### 2.1.2 Failure Modes (Why "Greedy" Fails)
- **Local Maximum** — All neighbours are worse, but a better solution exists elsewhere.
- **Plateau** — All neighbours have the same value → no informative gradient.
- **Ridge** — Sequence of local maxima makes navigation slow; greedy moves keep sliding sideways.

### 2.1.3 Variants
- **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** — Considers *all* successors and picks the best.
- **First-Choice Hill Climbing** — Generates successors *randomly* and accepts the first improvement.
- **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** — Conducts multiple hill climbs from different random starts; keeps the best overall result. **Provably complete with probability → 1 as the number of restarts → ∞.**

## 2.2 Simulated Annealing

### 2.2.1 Core Idea
Simulated Annealing (Kirkpatrick et al., 1983) escapes local maxima by **occasionally accepting worse moves** with a probability governed by a *temperature* parameter `T` that decreases over time.

**The Metropolis Criterion:**
A worse move (ΔE < 0 in maximisation, or ΔE > 0 in minimisation) is accepted with probability:

$$
P(\text{accept}) = e^{\,\Delta E \,/\, T}
$$

- When `T` is **high** → `e^(ΔE/T)` is close to 1 → many bad moves are accepted (exploration).
- When `T` is **low** → `e^(ΔE/T)` is close to 0 → almost no bad moves accepted (exploitation).
- A typical cooling schedule: `T(t) = T₀ · αᵗ`, where `0 < α < 1` (commonly α = 0.95).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name?** It mimics the physical process of *annealing* in metallurgy: a material is heated and slowly cooled to reach a low-energy crystalline state. The mathematical analogy is direct.

### 2.2.2 Algorithm
1. Start with state `s` and initial temperature `T₀`.
2. REPEAT until frozen (T ≈ 0):
   - Pick a random successor `s'`.
   - Compute ΔE = `f(s') − f(s)` (for maximisation).
   - If ΔE > 0 → accept `s'`.
   - Else → accept `s'` with probability `e^(ΔE / T)`.
3. Reduce T according to the cooling schedule.
4. Return the best state encountered.

## 2.3 Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition
A CSP is a triple **(X, D, C)** where:
- `X = {X₁, X₂, …, Xₙ}` is a set of **variables**.
- `D = {D₁, D₂, …, Dₙ}` is a set of **domains** (each `Dᵢ` is the set of allowed values for `Xᵢ`).
- `C = {C₁, C₂, …, Cₘ}` is a set of **constraints** that specify allowable combinations of values.

A **solution** is an assignment of a value to every variable such that **all constraints are satisfied**.

### 2.3.2 CSP Variants
- **Discrete vs Continuous Domains** — `Dᵢ` may be finite (map colouring) or continuous (scheduling with time variables).
- **Unary / Binary / Higher-Order Constraints** — Involving 1, 2, or k variables.
- **Absolute vs Preference Constraints** — Hard (must satisfy) vs Soft (preferred).

### 2.3.3 Constraint Propagation Techniques
| Technique | Symbol | Purpose |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Node Consistency | NC | Remove values from a variable's domain that violate unary constraints |
| Arc Consistency | AC | Ensure for every value of Xᵢ there is some value of Xⱼ satisfying the binary constraint |
| Path Consistency | PC | Generalise arc consistency to triples of variables |
| k-Consistency | kC | Any consistent assignment to k−1 variables extends to the k-th |
| Global Constraint | – | Specialised algorithms for n-ary constraints (e.g., Alldiff) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arc Consistency Algorithm (AC-3)** is the most cited. It repeatedly removes values from domains that have no supporting value in the neighbour's domain until a fixed point is reached.

### 2.3.4 CSP Solving Strategies
- **Backtracking Search** — Depth-first search with one variable assigned at a time; backtracks on failure.
- **Variable Ordering Heuristics**:
  - *MRV (Minimum Remaining Values)* — Choose variable with fewest legal values (fail-first).
  - *Degree Heuristic* — Choose variable involved in the largest number of constraints on remaining variables.
- **Value Ordering Heuristics**:
  - *LCV (Least Constraining Value)* — Choose value that rules out the fewest choices for neighbours.
- **Forward Checking** — When a variable is assigned, prune its neighbours' domains.
- **Constraint Propagation** — Stronger than forward checking; uses AC-3.

## 2.4 Means–End Analysis (MEA)

### 2.4.1 Definition
Means–End Analysis (Newell & Simon) is a problem-solving strategy that:
1. **Compares** the current state with the goal state.
2. **Identifies** the largest difference.
3. **Selects an operator** capable of reducing that difference.
4. **Applies** the operator (possibly after sub-goaling to make it applicable).
5. **Recursively** solves any new sub-problems created.

### 2.4.2 Operational Flow
```
MEA(Current, Goal):
    if Current ≈ Goal → return empty plan
    Diff = biggest difference(Current, Goal)
    Op = best operator for Diff
    if Op.applicable(Current) → apply Op
    else
        Sub1 = state where Op.precondition is true
        Sub2 = apply Op to Sub1
        return MEA(Current, Sub1) + MEA(Sub2, Goal)
```

### 2.4.3 Relation to Problem Reduction
Means–End Analysis is realised computationally through **Problem Reduction**, where the original problem is decomposed into sub-problems. The decomposition is represented using an **AND–OR graph**:
- **OR node** — Multiple alternative ways to solve a problem.
- **AND node** — All sub-problems must be solved.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Simulated Annealing acceptance | $P = e^{\,\Delta E \,/\, T}$ | ΔE = change in objective; T = temperature |
| 2 | Cooling schedule (geometric) | $T(t) = T_{0} \cdot \alpha^{t}$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$; t = iteration |
| 3 | Boltzmann distribution | $P(E) \propto e^{-E \,/\, k_{B} T}$ | kB = Boltzmann constant (≈ $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) |
| 4 | Random-Restart Hill Climbing success | $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{success}) = 1$ | n = number of restarts |
| 5 | MRV heuristic | choose Xᵢ with min $\vert D_{i} \vert$ | "Fail-first" principle |
| 6 | CSP tuple | (X, D, C) | Variables, Domains, Constraints |
| 7 | AC-3 complexity | $O(E \cdot d^{3})$ | E = edges in constraint graph; d = domain size |
| 8 | k-Consistency | Any consistent k-1 tuple extends | Stronger than (k-1)-consistency |
| 9 | Difference function | $\Delta(s, g)$ | Used in MEA to pick operator |
| 10 | Heuristic admissibility | $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | h*(n) = true cost to goal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse** the *heuristic function* `h(n)` (a problem-specific estimate) with the *temperature* `T` in Simulated Annealing. They serve different roles but both control the search behaviour.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility
- **Hill Climbing** is used in machine-learning hyperparameter tuning, VLSI design, and scheduling.
- **Simulated Annealing** is the backbone of VLSI layout (e.g., the *TimberWolf* placer), large-scale logistics, and protein-folding prediction.
- **CSP techniques** power Sudoku solvers, frequency assignment in mobile networks, airline crew rostering, and cryptarithmetic puzzles.
- **Means–End Analysis** is the conceptual ancestor of *General Problem Solver (GPS)*, hierarchical planning, and modern hierarchical reinforcement learning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acceptance Probability in Simulated Annealing

We derive the Metropolis criterion from first principles, using the Boltzmann distribution from statistical mechanics.

**Step 1 — Boltzmann Distribution.**
In a thermodynamic system at temperature `T`, the probability that the system is in a state with energy `E` is proportional to:

$$
P(E) = \frac{1}{Z} \, e^{-E \,/\, k_{B} T}
$$

where `Z` is the partition function (a normalising constant) and `kB` is the Boltzmann constant.

**Step 2 — Ratio of Two State Probabilities.**
Consider two states with energies `E₁` and `E₂` (with `E₂ > E₁`, i.e., state 2 is *worse* in a minimisation problem). The ratio of their probabilities is:

$$
\frac{P(E_{2})}{P(E_{1})} = \frac{e^{-E_{2} \,/\, k_{B} T}}{e^{-E_{1} \,/\, k_{B} T}} = e^{-(E_{2} - E_{1}) \,/\, k_{B} T}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Energy Difference.**
Let `ΔE = E₂ − E₁ > 0` (worsening). Then:

$$
\frac{P(E_{2})}{P(E_{1})} = e^{-\Delta E \,/\, k_{B} T}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance Rule.**
To sample from the Boltzmann distribution, the Metropolis algorithm accepts a transition from state 1 to state 2 with probability:

$$
P(\text{accept } 1 \to 2) = \min\!\left(1,\; e^{-\Delta E \,/\, k_{B} T}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Simplification.**
In optimisation, the physical constant `kB` is absorbed into the temperature scale (i.e., we redefine `T ← kB · T_phys`). This gives the canonical form used in computer science:

$$
\boxed{\,P(\text{accept}) = e^{\,\Delta E \,/\, T}\,} \quad \text{(for } \Delta E \le 0 \text{ in maximisation)}
$$

- When `ΔE ≤ 0` (improvement) → `e^(ΔE/T) ≥ 1` → the `min(1, ·)` rule accepts with probability **1**.
- When `ΔE > 0` (worsening) → `e^(ΔE/T) < 1` → accepted with the stated probability.

**Step 6 — Behavioural Limit Check.**
- As `T → ∞` → `e^(ΔE/T) → 1` for finite ΔE → behaves like *random walk* (pure exploration).
- As `T → 0` → `e^(ΔE/T) → 0` for ΔE < 0 → rejects all worse moves → reduces to *Hill Climbing* (pure exploitation).

This is the analytical proof that Simulated Annealing interpolates between **random search** and **greedy local search**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: 8-Queens as a CSP

The 8-Queens problem is a classic CSP. We reformulate it formally.

**Variables:** `X = {Q₁, Q₂, …, Q₈}` (one per column).
**Domains:** `Dᵢ = {1, 2, …, 8}` (row position in column i).
**Constraints:** For any two queens `Qᵢ` and `Qⱼ`, they must not share a row, column, or diagonal.

**Binary constraint between Qᵢ and Qⱼ:**

$$
(Q_{i} - Q_{j}) \neq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \vert Q_{i} - Q_{j} \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert
$$

**Solving by Hill Climbing (min-conflicts heuristic):**
1. Start with a random assignment (h ≈ 17–20 conflicts expected).
2. For each variable, count conflicts in its current row.
3. Move a queen to the row that **minimises total conflicts** (ties broken randomly).
4. Repeat until `h = 0` (solution found) or step limit exceeded.

**Empirical observation:** This algorithm solves *n*-Queens for arbitrary `n` in nearly constant time per run, because the average number of steps grows very slowly.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Hill Climbing | Simulated Annealing | Backtracking (CSP) | MEA |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|--------------------|-----|
| **Search Type** | Local, greedy | Local, probabilistic | Systematic (DFS) | Hierarchical reduction |
| **Optimality** | Local optimum only | Global optimum *asymptotically* | Finds *a* solution | Problem-dependent |
| **Completeness** | Incomplete | Complete (with log schedule) | Complete | Depends on operators |
| **Memory** | O(1) | O(1) | O(depth) | O(depth) |
| **Time** | Fast | Slow (many evaluations) | Exponential worst case | Polynomial in many cases |
| **Uses Abstraction?** | Heuristic value | Heuristic + Temperature | Constraints, domains | Differences, operators |
| **Escape Local Max?** | No (unless restarts) | Yes (probabilistically) | No (backtracks instead) | Yes (sub-goaling) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Cooling Schedule Calculation

Suppose we need to ensure the temperature drops from `T₀ = 1000` to `T_final < 1` over 200 iterations with geometric cooling.

**Step 1 — Apply cooling formula:**

$$
T(t) = 1000 \cdot \alpha^{t}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply terminal condition:**

$$
T(200) = 1000 \cdot \alpha^{200} < 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for α:**

$$
\alpha^{200} < \frac{1}{1000}
$$

$$
\alpha < \left( \frac{1}{1000} \right)^{1/200} = 10^{-3/200} = 10^{-0.015}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\alpha < 10^{-0.015} \approx 0.9658
$$

**Step 5 — Verification with α = 0.95:**

$$
T(200) = 1000 \cdot (0.95)^{200} = 1000 \cdot e^{200 \cdot \ln(0.95)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-10.26} \approx 1000 \cdot 3.49 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.0349
$$

This satisfies `T < 1`, so **α = 0.95 is acceptable** for the given constraints.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hill Climbing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initial State s0] --> B[Evaluate h s0]
    B --> C[Generate all neighbours of s]
    C --> D[Evaluate h for each neighbour]
    D --> E{Any neighbour has h greater than h s?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Move to best neighbour s']
    F --> B
    E -- No --> G[Local Maximum Reached]
    G --> H[Return current state as solution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Simulated Annealing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: State s, Temperature T0] --> B[Pick random neighbour s']
    B --> C[Compute deltaE = h s' minus h s]
    C --> D{deltaE greater than 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Accept s' and update s]
    D -- No --> F[Compute P = exp deltaE over T]
    F --> G{Random less than P?}
    G -- Yes --> E
    G -- No --> H[Reject s']
    E --> I[Reduce T by schedule]
    H --> I
    I --> J{T less than Tmin?}
    J -- No --> B
    J -- Yes --> K[Return best state found]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CSP Backtracking with Forward Checking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: CSP with X, D, C] --> B[Select unassigned variable using MRV]
    B --> C[Order domain values using LCV]
    C --> D[Assign value and prune neighbour domains]
    D --> E{Any domain becomes empty?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Backtrack to previous variable]
    F --> B
    E -- No --> G{All variables assigned?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Return consistent assignment]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Means-End Analysis as AND-OR Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Problem: Transform Current into Goal] --> P2[Identify largest difference D]
    P2 --> P3[Select operator Op for D]
    P3 --> P4{Op applicable on Current?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Apply Op and recurse on new state]
    P4 -- No --> P6[AND sub problem: achieve Op preconditions]
    P6 --> P7[AND sub problem: apply Op after preconditions]
    P7 --> P5
    P5 --> P8{Current equals Goal?}
    P8 -- No --> P2
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Return solution plan]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Local Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer
        I1[Problem Instance]
        I2[Initial State]
        I3[Heuristic Function]
    end
    subgraph EngineCore
        C1[State Generator]
        C2[Objective Evaluator]
        C3[Acceptance Rule Module]
        C4[Schedule Controller for SA]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer
        O1[Best State Found]
        O2[Search Trace and Statistics]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C1
    C3 --> O1
    C4 --> O2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node identifiers in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `C3`) and do not collide with Mermaid reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase text without inline markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). Give one example.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Constraint Satisfaction Problem is formally defined as a triple **(X, D, C)** where:
- **X = {X₁, X₂, …, Xₙ}** is a finite set of variables.
- **D = {D₁, D₂, …, Dₙ}** is a set of domains, where each `Dᵢ` specifies the allowed values for `Xᵢ`.
- **C = {C₁, C₂, …, Cₘ}** is a set of constraints restricting the values that variables can take simultaneously.

A solution is an assignment of one value from each domain such that *all* constraints are satisfied. **Example:** Map colouring where regions are variables, colours are domains, and the constraint is that adjacent regions must receive different colours. **(1 mark definition + 1 mark components + 1 mark example = 3 marks)**

### Q2. What is the Metropolis criterion in Simulated Annealing? State its formula.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Metropolis criterion is the **probabilistic acceptance rule** used in Simulated Annealing to decide whether to move from the current state `s` to a worse candidate state `s'`. It allows the algorithm to escape local optima by occasionally accepting deteriorating moves.

The formula is:

$$
P(\text{accept } s') =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } \Delta E > 0 \text{ (improvement)} \\[4pt]
e^{\,\Delta E \,/\, T}, & \text{if } \Delta E \le 0 \text{ (worsening)}
\end{cases}
$$

where **ΔE** is the change in objective value and **T** is the current temperature. As `T → 0`, the criterion reduces to greedy acceptance. **(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark limiting behaviour = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** In Part B, students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (full 14 marks). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[Highly probable for ESE 2024]**

**Q.A (a) Explain the Hill Climbing algorithm. Discuss its limitations and how random restart overcomes them. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Definition (2 marks):**
Hill Climbing is a *greedy local search* algorithm. It starts at an initial state and iteratively moves to the **neighbouring state with the highest heuristic value**. The loop terminates when no neighbour improves the heuristic — the algorithm has reached a **local maximum**.

```
function HILL-CLIMBING(problem):
    current = initial state of problem
    loop:
        neighbour = highest-valued successor of current
        if VALUE(neighbour) ≤ VALUE(current):
            return current  // local maximum
        current = neighbour
```

**Step 2 — Three Failure Modes (3 marks):**
1. **Local Maximum** — All neighbours are worse, but a better global solution exists elsewhere.
2. **Plateau** — A flat region where neighbouring states have equal heuristic value; the algorithm wanders without progress.
3. **Ridge** — A sequence of steep ascents separated by slight descents; greedy moves oscillate and progress is slow.

**Step 3 — Random-Restart Hill Climbing (2 marks):**
This variant runs hill climbing **multiple times from independently chosen random starting states** and returns the best result among all runs. As the number of restarts `n → ∞`, the probability of finding the global optimum approaches **1** (with probability 1, a random restart will eventually start at a state on the path to the global optimum). For most practical problems, a small number of restarts (e.g., 10–100) suffices.

---

**Q.A (b) What is Constraint Satisfaction Problem? Explain backtracking search with forward checking. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CSP Formal Definition (2 marks):**
A CSP is a triple **(X, D, C)** with variables, domains, and constraints. A *constraint graph* represents binary constraints as edges between variable nodes.

**Step 2 — Backtracking Search (3 marks):**
Backtracking is a depth-first search that assigns variables one at a time. When a variable has no legal value left, it *backtracks* to the previous variable and tries a different value.
- **MRV heuristic** — Choose the variable with the fewest remaining legal values (fail-first principle).
- **LCV heuristic** — Choose the value that eliminates the fewest options for neighbouring variables (succeed-first).

**Step 3 — Forward Checking (2 marks):**
After each assignment, forward checking **immediately prunes** the domains of all unassigned neighbours. If any neighbour's domain becomes empty, the algorithm backtracks *immediately*, avoiding deeper futile exploration. It is a one-step look-ahead that detects failure earlier than plain backtracking. The full AC-3 algorithm extends this idea to maintain arc consistency throughout the search.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

**Q.B (a) Explain Simulated Annealing algorithm. Derive the acceptance probability formula. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Intuition (1 mark):**
Simulated Annealing mirrors the physical annealing of metals: heating allows atoms to move freely (exploration); slow cooling settles them into a low-energy crystal (optimal solution). The algorithm uses a *temperature* parameter `T` to control the trade-off between exploration and exploitation.

**Step 2 — Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Initialise state `s` and temperature `T = T₀`.
2. Repeat until `T` is near zero:
   a. Pick a random neighbour `s'`.
   b. Compute `ΔE = f(s') − f(s)`.
   c. If `ΔE > 0` → accept `s'`.
   d. Else accept with probability `e^(ΔE / T)`.
3. Update temperature: `T ← α · T` (0 < α < 1).
4. Return best state.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Acceptance Formula (3 marks):**
Starting from the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(E) \propto e^{-E / k_{B} T}
$$

The ratio of probabilities of two states with energies `E₁` and `E₂` is:

$$
\frac{P(E_{2})}{P(E_{1})} = e^{-(E_{2} - E_{1}) / k_{B} T} = e^{-\Delta E / k_{B} T}
$$

To sample from this distribution (Metropolis algorithm), accept a worsening move with probability:

$$
P(\text{accept}) = e^{-\Delta E / T} \quad \text{(with } k_{B} = 1 \text{ in normalised form)}
$$

This formula is the cornerstone of Simulated Annealing and yields the desired exploration–exploitation balance.

---

**Q.B (b) Explain Means–End Analysis. How is it implemented using an AND–OR graph? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MEA Procedure (3 marks):**
Means–End Analysis (Newell & Simon, 1963) compares the current state to the goal, **identifies the largest difference**, selects an operator capable of reducing that difference, and applies it. If the operator is not directly applicable, a *sub-goal* is created to make it applicable. The process is recursive.

**Step 2 — Algorithm Outline (2 marks):**
```
MEA(current, goal):
    if current == goal: return
    diff = MAX_DIFF(current, goal)
    op = OPERATOR_FOR(diff)
    if op.applicable(current):
        return op(current)
    else:
        pre = STATE_TO_ENABLE(op)
        return MEA(current, pre) + MEA(apply(op, pre), goal)
```

**Step 3 — AND–OR Graph Realisation (2 marks):**
The recursive decomposition of MEA maps naturally onto an **AND–OR graph**:
- **OR nodes** represent *alternative* ways to solve a problem.
- **AND nodes** represent *sub-problems* that must *all* be solved (e.g., make operator applicable AND then apply it).
- A solution corresponds to a **subgraph** containing one child of every OR node and *all* children of every AND node.

This representation is the basis of the *General Problem Solver (GPS)* and modern hierarchical task planners.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the formula direction** — Students frequently write `P = e^(−ΔE/T)` *and* `P = e^(ΔE/T)` interchangeably. For a *maximisation* problem, a worse move has `ΔE < 0` and you must use `e^(ΔE/T)` (the standard form). Always state which convention you are using.
> 2. **Missing the cooling schedule** — A Simulated Annealing answer without a mention of the temperature update is incomplete. The *geometric schedule* `T = α·T` is the most accepted.
> 3. **Mixing MRV and LCV** — MRV is a *variable* ordering heuristic; LCV is a *value* ordering heuristic. They are applied at different stages. Examiners deduct marks if you swap them.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention constraint graph** — In CSP questions, the *constraint graph* visualises binary constraints and is worth at least half a mark.
> 5. **MEA answers without AND–OR graph** — A description of MEA *without* the AND–OR graph is incomplete; examiners expect the graph explicitly.
> 6. **Admissibility vs Consistency** — Do not write `h(n) ≤ h*(n)` and call it "consistency". *Admissibility* and *consistency* (monotonicity) are different properties.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Searching for Abstract Solutions** includes four pillars: **Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing, CSP, and Means–End Analysis**.
- **Hill Climbing** is greedy; **local maximum** is its main weakness; **Random Restart** is the standard fix.
- **Simulated Annealing acceptance probability** is `P = e^(ΔE / T)`. Higher `T` → more exploration; lower `T` → more exploitation.
- **Geometric cooling schedule**: `T(t) = T₀ · αᵗ`, with `0 < α < 1`. Common choice: `α = 0.95`.
- **CSP** is the triple **(X, D, C)**. A *solution* satisfies **all** constraints.
- **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** removes values from a variable's domain that have no support in a neighbour's domain. Complexity: `O(E·d³)`.
- **MRV** = *Minimum Remaining Values* (fail-first variable choice). **LCV** = *Least Constraining Value* (succeed-first value choice).
- **Forward Checking** prunes neighbour domains on each assignment; **AC-3** maintains full arc consistency.
- **Means–End Analysis** identifies the **biggest difference** between current and goal, applies a suitable operator, and recursively resolves sub-goals.
- **AND–OR graphs** formalise problem reduction: OR nodes = alternatives; AND nodes = all-must-solve sub-problems.
- **Admissible heuristic** satisfies `h(n) ≤ h*(n)` — never overestimates the true cost to the goal.
- **Simulated Annealing is *asymptotically* optimal** if the cooling schedule is `T(t) ≥ c / log(t+1)`. Geometric schedules are *not* asymptotically optimal but are widely used in practice.
- **Map colouring, N-Queens, Sudoku, and Cryptarithmetic** are the canonical CSP examples in KTU exams.
- Always **state the convention** (maximisation vs minimisation) when writing the Metropolis formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Searching for Abstract Solutions.** Practice at least one numerical on the cooling schedule and one CSP backtracking trace (e.g., 4-Queens) before the ESE — these are the highest-weightage question patterns under the KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Acting in Nondeterministic Domains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning and Acting in Nondeterministic Domains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Planning and Acting in Nondeterministic Domains** refers to the branch of automated planning in Artificial Intelligence where the **effects of actions are not uniquely determined** — an action may have *two or more possible outcomes* depending on the state of the world at the time of execution. The agent cannot assume a deterministic mapping `Action × State → State`; instead, it must model the world as `Action × State → P(State)`, a probability or possibility distribution over successor states.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with Russell & Norvig's *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*), this topic is covered under three progressively powerful agent designs:

1. **Sensorless Planning (Conformant Planning)** – The agent plans *without any observation*, generating a single linear (or tree-like) plan that achieves the goal in *all* possible worlds.
2. **Contingent Planning** – The agent constructs *conditional plans* (with `if-then-else` branches) that depend on what is *sensed* at runtime.
3. **Online Planning and Replanning** – The agent interleaves *acting* and *replanning*, learning from failed actions and unexpected outcomes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The three sub-topics above are the **exact module-4 sub-headings** prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCCMT402. Board questions are typically mapped 1:1 to these sub-headings.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a tourist in Kerala, but you have **swapped your phone's Google Maps for a paper map** and you have **no internet**. You know the *general direction* of the hotel, but you don't know exactly which bus you will catch, whether the road will be flooded, or if the bus conductor will accept your money. You must, therefore, build a *robust* plan — perhaps: *"Take ANY bus going south for 30 minutes, then ASK someone, then walk."* This is exactly what an agent in a **nondeterministic domain** must do: it cannot trust a single execution path.

- **Sensorless plan** = "Take Bus 1, and I am *guaranteed* to be at the hotel" (no need to see anything).
- **Contingent plan** = "Take Bus 1; IF I am at a beach, THEN walk north; ELSE walk south" (uses sense info).
- **Online replanning** = "Take Bus 1; if it crashes (unexpected outcome), replan from current location."

> [!NOTE]
> **Defining Nondeterminism Properly:** A domain is *nondeterministic* if at least one action `a` in state `s` can lead to **more than one possible successor state**. The agent, in many formulations, does *not* know the probabilities — it only knows the *set* of possible outcomes. This is sometimes called **qualitative nondeterminism**, in contrast to the **stochastic / MDP-based** planning of Module 5.

### 1.3 The Environment Transformation

A classical planning problem is the tuple `(P, A, s_0, G)`. For a **nondeterministic** problem, this expands to:

$$\text{Planning Problem}^{\text{ndet}} = \langle S, S_0, A, \gamma, G, C \rangle$$

Where:
- $S$ = finite set of **states** (the agent's belief about the world is encoded differently depending on the sub-type).
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = **set of initial states** (the agent does not know which one is the *true* state).
- $A$ = set of **actions** available in any state.
- $\gamma(s, a) = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ = **transition relation** returning a *set* of possible successor states.
- $G$ = **goal test** (a set of goal states).
- $C$ = **action cost function**, $C(s, a, s') \geq 0$.

### 1.4 Three "Strengths" of an Agent

| Agent Type | Perception? | Outcome of Action | Plan Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Offline (deterministic) | None needed | Single, certain | Linear sequence |
| **Sensorless (Conformant)** | **None used** | Multiple, *unobservable* until done | **Linear (branch-free) sequence** |
| **Contingent** | **Used at runtime** | Multiple, *observable* | **Conditional plan / policy tree** |
| **Online (Replanning)** | **Used at runtime** | Multiple, *partially observable* | **Linear + replan-on-failure** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A very common KTU question is *"Differentiate between sensorless, contingent, and online planning."* Memorise the table above — it is worth 3 to 5 marks in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Nondeterminism Breaks Classical Planning

In **classical (deterministic) planning**, the STRIPS/PDDL action schema specifies:
- **Precondition:** a conjunction of positive literals that must hold in the current state.
- **Effect:** a conjunction of literals (add-list and delete-list) that precisely defines the *next* state.

Once we allow $\gamma(s, a)$ to return *multiple* states, the **delete-list / add-list semantics break** because the agent does not know which effect actually happened. The cleanest way to model this is the **set-of-states semantics** (called a *belief state* when probabilities are absent).

### 2.2 Belief States — The Heart of Nondeterministic Planning

A **belief state** $b$ is a *set* of physical states that the agent considers possible. If there are $n$ fluents and each can be true/false, then in the worst case, the number of possible physical states is $2^n$ — and the size of a belief state can be exponential in $n$.

**The belief-state transition operator** $\Gamma$ is defined as:

$$\Gamma(b, a) = \bigcup_{s \in b} \gamma(s, a) = \{s' : \exists s \in b, s' \in \gamma(s, a)\}$$

That is, applying action $a$ to belief state $b$ yields the *union* of all possible successors of every physical state in $b$. This is the "**imagine**" step.

**A belief state $b$ satisfies the goal $G$** if every physical state in $b$ satisfies $G$:

$$b \models G \quad \iff \quad \forall s \in b : s \models G$$

This is a **stronger** requirement than classical planning — we cannot have a single physical state inside $b$ that is non-goal.

### 2.3 Sensorless (Conformant) Planning — Detailed Analysis

A **sensorless plan** is a *fixed, linear* sequence of actions $(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ such that applying it from the initial belief state $b_0 = S_0$ leads to a belief state that *all* of whose members are goal states:

$$b_0 \xrightarrow{a_1} b_1 \xrightarrow{a_2} b_2 \xrightarrow{a_n} b_n, \quad b_n \models G$$

The agent **never uses any sensor information** during execution — hence the name. The plan must work in *all* possible worlds.

**Why is sensorless planning hard?** Because the size of belief states can explode exponentially. A naive search treats each belief state as a *node* in a search graph, but $\vert \Gamma(b,a) \vert$ can be up to $\vert b \vert \cdot \vert A \vert$ in the worst case, leading to a state-space complexity of $O(2^{2^n})$ — doubly exponential in the number of fluents. Hence conformant planners use specialised techniques:
- **Symbolic belief-state representation** (Binary Decision Diagrams — BDDs).
- **SAT-based encoding** (translate the problem to a Boolean satisfiability instance).
- **Belief-state literal reduction** (deduce which literals are *known true* in $b$ regardless of outcome).

### 2.4 Contingent Planning — Conditional Plans

A **contingent plan** is a *tree* of conditional actions. Each node is an action; each edge out of a node is labelled by a possible *percept* (or outcome). The agent traverses the tree at runtime, choosing the branch dictated by what it actually senses.

**Conditional plan syntax (Russell & Norvig):**

```
IF (percept) THEN (action) ELSE (different action)
```

The plan can be deeply nested, allowing for arbitrarily complex run-time decisions. The **AND-OR search tree** (Section 2.5) is the formal representation of a contingent plan.

### 2.5 AND-OR Search Trees

In AND-OR search:
- **OR nodes** = choice of action (the *agent* chooses).
- **AND nodes** = branching on possible outcomes (the *environment* chooses).
- A **solution** is a subtree in which every OR node has a chosen action, and every AND node has all its branches leading to a goal.

The plan returned is a **conditional plan** (a *policy*). The AND-OR-SEARCH algorithm performs a recursive DFS over the state space, marking:
- `solved(s)` if a goal state.
- `solved(s)` if there exists an action $a$ such that for *every* outcome $s' \in \gamma(s, a)$, the sub-plan starting at $s'$ is solved.

### 2.6 Online Planning and Replanning

In **online planning**, the agent interleaves:
1. **Plan** – Find a (sub-)plan to reach the goal from the *current* state.
2. **Act** – Execute the *first* action of that plan.
3. **Observe** – Read sensors, see the actual new state $s'$.
4. **Replan if needed** – If $s'$ is *not* the expected outcome, replan from $s'$.

A typical online planner uses a **depth-limited lookahead** (not full search) to keep planning fast. When the world is *k*-consistent, meaning the agent's "perception model" says the world behaves correctly for $k$ steps, the agent can safely plan only $k$ steps ahead.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Transition relation** | $\gamma(s, a) = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ | Defines nondeterminism |
| **Belief state** | $b \subseteq S$, $\vert b \vert$ = number of possible worlds | Sensorless/Cntg. planning |
| **Belief-state update** | $\Gamma(b, a) = \bigcup_{s \in b} \gamma(s, a)$ | Forward simulation step |
| **Goal satisfaction in belief** | $b \models G \iff \forall s \in b, s \models G$ | Goal test in search |
| **Strong solution** | Plan works in *all* outcome branches | Conformant plan |
| **Weak solution** | Plan works in *at least one* branch | Cycle-detection problem |
| **Plan cost** | $C(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c(a_i)$ | For cyclic vs acyclic debate |
| **AND-OR solved recursion** | $\text{solved}(s) = \text{Goal}(s) \lor \left[\exists a : \forall s' \in \gamma(s,a),\ \text{solved}(s')\right]$ | Contingent planner backbone |
| **Cyclicity bound (IRA*)** | Depth-first depth limit $L$ | Online bounded planning |
| **Plan length heuristic** | $\vert \pi \vert = $ number of action steps | Comparing plans |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Worthy Distinction:** A *strong* cyclic plan (terminates with probability 1 but may loop on a "bad" outcome) is *not* a solution under the standard sensorless definition. Board questions often test whether students know the difference between **strong**, **strong-cyclic**, and **weak** solutions.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics**: A robot grasping an object has *multiple* outcomes (firm grip / slip / miss). Contingent planning is essential.
- **Autonomous Driving**: An autonomous car must plan for sensor noise (sensorless) and act in real time (online).
- **Spacecraft / NASA Rovers**: Communication delay means a rover must plan *without* Earth intervention — pure sensorless planning.
- **Network Routing**: TCP retransmission is essentially an online replanning strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Belief-State Size Bound

**Statement:** In a domain with $n$ binary fluents, the size of a belief state $b$ is at most $2^n$, where $2^n$ is the number of distinct physical states.

**Derivation (combinatorial):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } F &= \{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_n\} \text{ be the set of fluents.} \\
\text{Each fluent } f_i &\in \{T, F\}, \text{ so there are } 2 \text{ choices.} \\
\text{Number of complete state assignments} &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} 2 = 2^n. \\
\therefore \vert S \vert &= 2^n. \\
\text{A belief state } b &\subseteq S. \\
\text{Number of subsets of } S &= 2^{\vert S \vert} = 2^{2^n}. \\
\therefore \vert b \vert &\leq 2^n. \\
\text{Number of distinct belief states} &\leq 2^{2^n} \quad \text{(doubly exponential in } n\text{).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters:** This explains *why* conformant planning is computationally hard — naive search space grows doubly exponentially.

## 3.2 Deriving the Belief-State Update — Step-by-Step

**Statement:** Show that $\Gamma(b, a) = \bigcup_{s \in b} \gamma(s, a)$ correctly captures the agent's *new* uncertainty after applying $a$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } &\text{belief state } b = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}. \\
&\text{True world is some } s_{\text{true}} \in b. \\
&\text{Agent applies action } a. \\
\text{By definition of } \gamma: &\text{if true world is } s_i, \text{ new world is some } s' \in \gamma(s_i, a). \\
\text{So the new true world} &\in \gamma(s_1, a) \cup \gamma(s_2, a) \cup \dots \cup \gamma(s_k, a). \\
\text{Hence, new belief state } \Gamma(b,a) &= \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \gamma(s_i, a). \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** The agent does not know which $s_i$ was the true state, so it must consider *all* possible successors of *all* possible true states.

## 3.3 Deriving the AND-OR Solved Criterion

**Statement:** A state $s$ is `solved` if either it is a goal state, or there is an action $a$ such that *every* outcome $s' \in \gamma(s, a)$ is itself `solved`.

**Base case (Recursion depth 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{solved}(s) &\leftarrow \text{True} \quad \text{if } s \models G. \\
\text{solved}(s) &\leftarrow \text{False} \quad \text{if no action applicable (dead-end).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recursive case (Depth > 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{solved}(s) &\leftarrow \bigvee_{a \in A(s)} \left[ \bigwedge_{s' \in \gamma(s,a)} \text{solved}(s') \right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{where } A(s) &= \text{set of actions applicable in } s. \\
\text{Logical reading} &: \text{There exists an action } a \text{ (outer } \vee \text{)} \\
&\text{such that for all possible outcomes } s' \text{ (inner } \wedge \text{)}, \\
&\text{the sub-plan from } s' \text{ is also solved.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the formal foundation of contingent planning.** The disjunction (∨) over actions is the **OR** in AND-OR; the conjunction (∧) over outcomes is the **AND**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — The Suck-Up World

> A classic Russell & Norvig example: a vacuum world with **two rooms (A and B)**. The robot does *not* know which room is dirty. Actions: `Suck`, `Left`, `Right`. `Suck` cleans the current room. The goal is *both rooms clean*.

**Initial belief state:** $b_0 = \{\{A \text{ dirty}\}, \{A \text{ clean}, B \text{ dirty}\}, \dots\}$ — i.e., 3 possible dirty configurations (excluding both clean).

**Step 1: Apply Suck from initial position (assume robot in A)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma(b_0, \text{Suck}) &= \bigcup_{s \in b_0} \gamma(s, \text{Suck}) \\
&= \{(\text{A clean, B dirty}), (\text{A clean, B clean})\} \\
&= \text{"A is definitely clean; B may or may not be dirty."}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply `Right` (move to B)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma(b_1, \text{Right}) &= \{(\text{at B, A clean, B dirty}), (\text{at B, A clean, B clean})\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply `Suck` again**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma(b_2, \text{Suck}) &= \{(\text{at B, A clean, B clean})\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The resulting belief state has *only* the all-clean state, so $b_3 \models G$.

**Sensorless plan:** `[Suck, Right, Suck]`. Length 3.

> [!TIP]
> This example appears frequently in KTU 2024 question papers as a 7-mark sub-part. Practice writing out the full $\Gamma$ computation in your answer sheet.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Criterion | Sensorless (Conformant) | Contingent | Online (Replanning) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensors used at runtime?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Plan representation** | Linear sequence | Conditional tree | Linear + replan on failure |
| **Search space** | Belief-state space (up to $2^{2^n}$) | AND-OR tree (exponential in depth) | State space with depth limit |
| **Guarantee** | Goal in *all* outcomes | Goal in *all sensed* outcomes | Goal in most outcomes (eventually) |
| **Computational cost** | Very high (offline) | High (offline) | Low (online) |
| **Example algorithm** | Conformant-FF, CGP | CNLP, POND | Agent program with replanning |
| **Best for** | No/limited sensors | Fully observable worlds | Stochastic / unpredictable |
| **Failure recovery** | Plan works in *all* branches | Plan branches *handle* outcomes | Detect & replan at runtime |

## 3.6 Deriving the Cyclic-Plan Termination

**Statement:** A plan is *strongly cyclic* if, for every possible state, the agent will eventually reach the goal *with probability 1*, although the plan may temporarily loop.

**Proof Sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } V &: S \rightarrow \mathbb{N} \text{ be a Lyapunov-like value function.} \\
\text{If for every non-goal state } s, &\exists a : \forall s' \in \gamma(s,a),\ V(s') < V(s). \\
\text{Then } V \text{ strictly decreases} &\Rightarrow \text{no infinite loop} \Rightarrow \text{termination.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the strictly decreasing $V$ exists, the plan is **strong** (terminates in bounded time). If no such $V$ exists but probability-1 termination is still guaranteed, the plan is **strong cyclic** (e.g., random walk to goal).

> [!NOTE]
> This level of detail is **only** required if the KTU question explicitly asks for "formal proof of termination" or "existence of $V$." For 7-mark sub-parts, just naming the concepts is sufficient.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Planning Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classical Planning] --> B[Nondeterministic Domain Detected]
    B --> C{Sensors Available?}
    C -- No --> D[Sensorless Planning]
    C -- Yes --> E{Plan Offline Completely?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Contingent Planning]
    E -- No --> G[Online Replanning]
    D --> D1[Conformant Plan: Linear Sequence]
    F --> F1[Conditional Plan: IF THEN ELSE Tree]
    G --> G1[Plan Act Observe Replan Loop]
    G1 --> G2{Plan Succeeded?}
    G2 -- Yes --> G3[Continue Execution]
    G2 -- No --> G4[Replan from Current State]
    G4 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AND-OR Search Tree Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[OR Node: State s0] -->|Choose Action a1| A1[AND Node: Outcomes of a1]
    A1 -->|Outcome s1a| O2[OR Node: State s1a]
    A1 -->|Outcome s1b| O3[OR Node: State s1b]
    O2 -->|Choose Action a2a| A2[AND Node: Outcomes of a2a]
    O2 -->|Choose Action a2b| A3[AND Node: Outcomes of a2b]
    O3 -->|Choose Action a3| A4[AND Node: Outcomes of a3]
    A2 --> G1[Goal State]
    A2 --> G2[Goal State]
    A3 --> A3x[State s2b not solved]
    A4 --> G3[Goal State]
    style O1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style A1 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style G1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style G2 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style G3 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style A3x fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:** Round/branch labels at OR nodes (pink) are *action choices* made by the agent. Branches at AND nodes (blue) are *environment-controlled outcomes* (the agent cannot choose). Green leaves are goals; red leaves are unsolvable states (the plan must *avoid* taking actions that lead to them).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Online Replanning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Perceive Current State s] --> P2[Plan: Search for subplan pi from s]
    P2 --> P3[Execute first action a of pi]
    P3 --> P4[Observe outcome s prime]
    P4 --> P5{Is s prime a goal?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Task Complete]
    P5 -- No --> P7{Is s prime the expected outcome?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P1
    P7 -- No --> P8[Replan from s prime]
    P8 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Belief-State Expansion (Vacuum World)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Belief b0: A dirty OR A clean-B dirty OR B dirty-A clean] -->|Suck| B1[Belief b1: A clean AND B maybe dirty]
    B1 -->|Right| B2[Belief b2: at B, A clean, B maybe dirty]
    B2 -->|Suck| B3[Belief b3: ALL CLEAN - GOAL]
    B0 -.->|Direct Right| Bx[Belief: A still may be dirty - NOT GOAL]
    style B3 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style Bx fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** The diagonal dashed branch shows a *shortcut plan* (just `Right`) that *fails* the goal because $A$ may remain dirty. The solid path is the *correct* sensorless plan `[Suck, Right, Suck]`.

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid syntax used here follows the safeguard rules: all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`B0`, `A1`, `P3`, etc.) and no reserved keywords are used. All labels are plain text with no markdown formatting inside the quotes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

> Define a **belief state**. How is the goal test modified when working in belief-state space instead of physical-state space?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *belief state* $b$ is a set of physical states that the agent considers possible, $b = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\} \subseteq S$. In sensorless and contingent planning, the agent's knowledge is represented not by a single state but by a set of possible states.

The goal test in belief-state space is **stronger** than in classical planning. A belief state $b$ satisfies goal $G$ only if **every** physical state in $b$ satisfies $G$:

$$b \models G \iff \forall s \in b, \ s \models G$$

For example, if $G = \{A \text{ clean}, B \text{ clean}\}$, then a belief state $\{(A \text{ clean}, B \text{ dirty}), (A \text{ clean}, B \text{ clean})\}$ does *not* satisfy the goal, because the first member violates $G$. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for example.)

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

> List the **three types of planning agents** for nondeterministic domains and state one distinguishing feature of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Sensorless (Conformant) Planning Agent** — Does *not* use sensors; generates a single linear plan that works in *all* possible worlds.
2. **Contingent Planning Agent** — Uses sensors at runtime; generates a *conditional plan* (IF–THEN–ELSE tree) that branches on percepts.
3. **Online (Replanning) Agent** — Interleaves planning and acting; can *replan* from the actual current state when an unexpected outcome occurs. (1 mark per type with feature.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Conformant + Contingent Planning*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

> **(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain the **conformant (sensorless) planning** problem in nondeterministic domains. State the formal definition of a conformant planning problem and the belief-state transition operator. Why is conformant planning computationally hard in the worst case?
>
> **(b)** *[7 marks]* With a clear diagram, explain the **AND–OR search algorithm** for contingent planning. Write the recursive condition for a state to be declared `solved`.

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A conformant (sensorless) planning problem is a tuple

$$\Sigma = \langle S, S_0, A, \gamma, G \rangle$$

where:
- $S$ = set of states,
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = set of initial states (the agent does *not* know which is the true one),
- $A$ = set of actions,
- $\gamma : S \times A \rightarrow 2^S$ = transition relation returning a *set* of possible next states (one of which will materialise at runtime),
- $G$ = goal test over individual states.

A *conformant plan* is a sequence of actions $\pi = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ that, when applied starting from *any* $s \in S_0$, ends in a state satisfying $G$, regardless of which nondeterministic outcome actually occurs.

**Belief-state transition (2 marks):** The belief-state update is

$$\Gamma(b, a) = \bigcup_{s \in b} \gamma(s, a)$$

where $b$ is the current belief state. The agent imagines all possible outcomes and unions them into a new belief.

**Computational hardness (3 marks):** A domain with $n$ binary fluents has $2^n$ physical states and $2^{2^n}$ possible belief states. The number of belief states reachable from $S_0$ can grow doubly exponentially, making naive BFS/DFS infeasible. Conformant planners use techniques like BDDs (binary decision diagrams) or SAT encoding to mitigate this.

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**AND-OR search (4 marks):** A contingent plan is a tree in which:
- **OR nodes** represent *agent choices* (which action to take).
- **AND nodes** represent *environment choices* (which outcome actually happened).
- A **solution** is a subtree that:
  1. Contains exactly one action per OR node (chosen by the agent).
  2. Contains *all* outcome branches per AND node (the plan must work in *all*).
  3. Reaches a goal at every leaf.

The **recursive solved condition (3 marks):** A state $s$ is `solved` if:

$$
\text{solved}(s) \equiv \text{Goal}(s) \ \lor \ \big[ \exists a \in A(s) : \forall s' \in \gamma(s,a), \ \text{solved}(s') \big]
$$

In words: $s$ is solved if either it is a goal state, or there exists an action $a$ such that **for every** possible outcome $s'$, the sub-problem from $s'$ is also solved. (The diagram in Section 4.2 should accompany this answer.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Online Replanning + Comparison*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** *[7 marks]* Describe the **online planning and replanning** strategy used by an agent in a partially observable, nondeterministic environment. How does the agent handle an *unexpected* outcome of an action?
>
> **(b)** *[7 marks]* Compare sensorless, contingent, and online planning in a single comparative table (at least 5 criteria) and state one *real-world scenario* where each is most appropriate.

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Online agent cycle (3 marks):** The online agent follows the loop:

1. **Perceive** the current state $s$ via sensors.
2. **Plan** a sub-plan $\pi$ from $s$ to $G$ (usually with a *bounded lookahead* of $k$ steps).
3. **Execute** the first action $a_1$ of $\pi$.
4. **Observe** the actual outcome $s'$.
5. **Check**: if $s' \in G$, terminate; else if $s'$ matches the expected outcome, go to step 2; else **replan from $s'$** and return to step 2.

**Handling unexpected outcomes (2 marks):** When the actual $s'$ is not in the set of expected successors $\gamma(s, a_1)$, the agent treats $s'$ as a *new current state* and runs the planner again from $s'$. This recovery loop is what gives online agents robustness to environment surprises that were not modelled in advance.

**Depth-limited lookahead (2 marks):** To keep online planning *real-time*, the agent uses a bounded depth $L$. If the world is $L$-consistent (the agent's perception model is correct for the next $L$ steps), then planning $L$ steps ahead is sufficient — after each $L$ actions, the agent re-evaluates.

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparative table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Sensorless | Contingent | Online |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensors used | No | Yes | Yes |
| Plan type | Linear | Conditional tree | Linear + replan |
| Computational cost | Very high (offline) | High (offline) | Low (online) |
| Robustness to surprises | Guaranteed (all outcomes) | Guaranteed (sensed outcomes) | Probabilistic (reactive) |
| Best when | No reliable sensors | Fast/cheap sensors | Stochastic environment |

**Real-world scenarios (3 marks = 1 mark each):**
- **Sensorless** — *NASA Mars rover*: Communication delay of 4–24 minutes forces the rover to act *without* Earth input, using a conformant plan.
- **Contingent** — *Robot vacuum cleaner with camera*: It *senses* dirt vs. obstacle, then chooses among pre-baked conditional actions (turn left vs. keep going).
- **Online** — *Autonomous car*: Real-time replanning is essential because other vehicles behave unpredictably; a contingent plan is too rigid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "the plan works in *some* outcomes" for a sensorless plan** — it must be *all* outcomes. Examiners deduct 2 marks for confusing strong and weak solutions.
> 2. **Do not equate "online" with "contingent"** — online plans are *linear* and replan at runtime, whereas contingent plans are *conditional trees* computed offline.
> 3. **Belief state size** must be written as up to $2^{2^n}$, **not** $2^n$ — many students confuse the two levels of exponentiation.
> 4. **The solved-recursion in AND-OR** uses both $\exists a$ (∃) and $\forall s'$ (∀). Writing only one quantifier is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Sensorless planning does NOT mean "no planning"** — it means *planning without runtime sensing*. The plan itself is still computed offline.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nondeterministic domain** = action may produce ≥ 2 possible outcomes; agent models $\gamma(s, a) = \{s_1, \dots, s_k\}$.
- **Belief state** $b \subseteq S$ represents the agent's uncertainty about the *true* physical state.
- **Belief-state update** $\Gamma(b, a) = \bigcup_{s \in b} \gamma(s, a)$.
- **Goal test in belief space** is *strong*: $\forall s \in b,\ s \models G$.
- **Sensorless (Conformant) plan** = linear sequence, no sensors, works in *all* outcomes.
- **Contingent plan** = conditional tree (IF-THEN-ELSE), uses sensors at runtime.
- **AND-OR search** = OR node = action choice; AND node = outcome branching; *solution* = every branch reaches a goal.
- **Solved-recursion:** `solved(s) ← Goal(s) ∨ ∃a ∈ A(s) : ∀s' ∈ γ(s,a), solved(s')`.
- **Online replanning loop:** Perceive → Plan → Act → Observe → Replan if unexpected.
- **Depth-limited online planning** uses an $L$-consistent environment assumption.
- **Worst-case belief-space size** = $2^{2^n}$ (doubly exponential in the number of fluents) — explains why conformant planning is PSPACE-hard in general.
- **Strong solution** = goal in *all* outcomes; **Strong cyclic** = goal with prob. 1 (may loop); **Weak** = goal in *some* outcome.
- **Real-world uses:** NASA rovers (sensorless), household robots (contingent), self-driving cars (online).
- **Mark-bearing formulas to memorise:** $\Gamma(b,a)$, $b \models G$, the solved-recursion, and $2^{2^n}$ bound.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time, Schedules, and Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT402 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ARTIFICIAL AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time, Schedules, and Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Trinity of Temporal Planning

In Artificial Intelligence (AI) planning, the trio of **Time, Schedules, and Resources** forms the backbone of any realistic planning system. While classical planning assumes atomic, instantaneous actions, real-world domains demand that we model *when* actions occur, *how long* they take, and *what* they consume or require.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> - **Time** is the temporal dimension over which actions are scheduled. Each action $a$ has a **duration** $d(a) \ge 0$ and occupies the interval $[\text{start}(a), \text{end}(a)]$.
> - A **Schedule** is a concrete assignment of start times to every action in a plan such that all temporal ordering constraints and deadline constraints are satisfied.
> - **Resources** are entities required by, produced by, or consumed during action execution. A schedule must respect all resource capacity constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Contractor 🏗️

Imagine you are a **general contractor** constructing a house:

- **Time** → Each task (laying foundation, framing walls, installing plumbing) takes a known number of days. The foreman needs a **calendar** with deadlines.
- **Schedule** → The foreman writes out a **Gantt-style chart**: "Day 1–10: foundation; Day 11–18: walls; Day 19–22: roof." This chart is a *schedule*.
- **Resources** → Only one concrete mixer is available (a **reusable** resource), and each bag of cement is **consumed** (a **consumable** resource). The schedule must ensure the mixer isn't double-booked and cement doesn't run out.

If two tasks both need the mixer on Day 12, the schedule is **invalid** — exactly the kind of conflict an AI planning agent must detect and resolve.

### 1.3 The Planning–Scheduling Continuum

> [!NOTE]
> **Planning** answers the question: *"What sequence of actions will achieve my goal?"*
> **Scheduling** answers: *"At exactly what times should each action execute, and is the timing feasible given resources?"*

In KTU Module 4, the focus shifts from *which* actions to perform (planning) to *when* and *with what* to perform them (scheduling + resources).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:----:|
| $d(a)$ | Duration of action $a$ | time units |
| $\text{ES}(a)$ | Earliest Start time of $a$ | time units |
| $\text{EF}(a)$ | Earliest Finish time of $a$ | time units |
| $\text{LS}(a)$ | Latest Start time of $a$ | time units |
| $\text{LF}(a)$ | Latest Finish time of $a$ | time units |
| $\text{slack}(a)$ | Total float of $a$ | time units |

> [!TIP]
> The **critical path** is the chain of actions with zero slack — delaying any of them delays the entire project.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temporal Representation in AI Planning

A temporal plan is represented as a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** known as the **Action Network** or **Plan Network**. Nodes are actions, and directed edges encode ordering constraints (predecessor → successor).

### Why Temporal Planning?

In STRIPS-style classical planning, the implicit assumption is that each action is **instantaneous** and the world is observed only between actions. But consider:
- "Bake a cake" — takes 45 minutes.
- "Charge a phone" — takes 2 hours.

Without temporal reasoning, the agent cannot detect that the cake is *raw* during the interval $[0, 45]$ and becomes *baked* only at $t=45$. **Temporal planning** captures these intermediate states.

## 2.2 The Critical Path Method (CPM)

The **Critical Path Method** is a deterministic algorithm for finding the minimum possible duration of a project given a set of activities and their dependencies.

### Forward Pass — Computing Earliest Start (ES)

For each action $a$ in topological order:
$$\text{ES}(a) = \max_{p \in \text{pred}(a)} \left[\text{ES}(p) + d(p)\right]$$
The earliest start of $a$ is the **latest earliest finish** among all its predecessors.

### Backward Pass — Computing Latest Finish (LF)

For each action in reverse topological order:
$$\text{LF}(a) = \min_{s \in \text{succ}(a)} \left[\text{LS}(s)\right] = \min_{s \in \text{succ}(a)} \left[\text{LF}(s) - d(s)\right]$$
The latest finish of $a$ is the **earliest latest start** of any of its successors.

### Slack and Critical Path

$$\text{slack}(a) = \text{LS}(a) - \text{ES}(a) = \text{LF}(a) - \text{EF}(a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An action $a$ lies on the **critical path** if and only if $\text{slack}(a) = 0$.

The **minimum project duration** is:
$$T_{\min} = \max_{a \in \text{final}} \text{EF}(a)$$

## 2.3 Resource Categories in Planning

Resources are classified along two orthogonal axes:

| Resource Type | Definition | Real-World Example | Constraint Type |
|:-------------:|:-----------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| **Reusable** | Not consumed, can be re-used after release | Crane, worker, machine | Capacity limit during use |
| **Consumable** | Depleted when used; not replenished by the action | Fuel, money, raw material | Non-negative stock |
| **Binary** | Either available or not (capacity 0 or 1) | A single key, a vehicle | Mutual exclusion |
| **Continuous** | Numerical quantity, possibly fractional | Water in litres, voltage | Upper/lower bounds |

### Resource Constraint Formulation

For a reusable resource $r$ with capacity $C_r$, the constraint at every time $t$ is:
$$\sum_{a : t \in [\text{start}(a),\, \text{end}(a))} u_r(a) \le C_r$$
where $u_r(a)$ is the units of resource $r$ used by action $a$.

For a consumable resource $r$ produced/produced at rate $\pi_r$ and consumed at rate $\gamma_r$:
$$\text{Stock}_r(t) = \text{Stock}_r(0) + \int_0^t \left[\pi_r(\tau) - \gamma_r(\tau)\right] d\tau \ge 0$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:-----------|
| 1 | $\text{ES}(a) = \max_p \left[\text{ES}(p) + d(p)\right]$ | Earliest start | Forward pass |
| 2 | $\text{EF}(a) = \text{ES}(a) + d(a)$ | Earliest finish | Forward pass |
| 3 | $\text{LF}(a) = \min_s \left[\text{LS}(s)\right]$ | Latest finish | Backward pass |
| 4 | $\text{LS}(a) = \text{LF}(a) - d(a)$ | Latest start | Backward pass |
| 5 | $\text{slack}(a) = \text{LS}(a) - \text{ES}(a)$ | Float | Criticality test |
| 6 | $T_{\min} = \max_{a} \text{EF}(a)$ | Project duration | Plan length |
| 7 | $\sum_a u_r(a) \le C_r$ | Reusable constraint | Resource check |
| 8 | $\text{Stock}_r(t) \ge 0$ | Consumable constraint | Viability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used in **PERT/CPM project management**, **manufacturing scheduling**, **AI task planners** (e.g., NASA'sEUROPA-II, space mission schedulers), and **Just-In-Time supply chain** systems.

## 2.5 Scheduling Algorithms — At a Glance

1. **Earliest Start Time (EST) Scheduler:** Assign each action the earliest feasible time. Fast but may yield poor resource utilisation.
2. **Critical-Path-First Scheduler:** Prioritise critical-path actions to keep plan duration minimal.
3. **Minimum Slack First (MSF):** Greedy — schedule action with least slack first, reducing the chance of missing deadlines.
4. **Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling (RCPSP):** NP-hard problem solved by heuristics like serial/parallel schedule generation schemes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Critical Path Computation

Consider an action network for a small AI agent deploying a web service. The actions and their dependencies are:

| Action $a$ | Duration $d(a)$ | Predecessors |
|:----------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|
| $A$ (Provision server) | 3 | — |
| $B$ (Install OS) | 2 | $A$ |
| $C$ (Install database) | 4 | $A$ |
| $D$ (Configure firewall) | 1 | $B$ |
| $E$ (Deploy app) | 3 | $C,\, D$ |
| $F$ (Run smoke tests) | 2 | $E$ |

### Step 1 — Forward Pass (Compute ES and EF)

Topological order: $A \to \{B, C\} \to D \to E \to F$.

**Action $A$ (no predecessors):**
$$\text{ES}(A) = 0$$
$$\text{EF}(A) = 0 + 3 = 3$$

**Action $B$ (predecessor: $A$):**
$$\text{ES}(B) = \text{ES}(A) + d(A) = 0 + 3 = 3$$
$$\text{EF}(B) = 3 + 2 = 5$$

**Action $C$ (predecessor: $A$):**
$$\text{ES}(C) = \text{ES}(A) + d(A) = 0 + 3 = 3$$
$$\text{EF}(C) = 3 + 4 = 7$$

**Action $D$ (predecessor: $B$):**
$$\text{ES}(D) = \text{ES}(B) + d(B) = 3 + 2 = 5$$
$$\text{EF}(D) = 5 + 1 = 6$$

**Action $E$ (predecessors: $C, D$):**
$$\text{ES}(E) = \max\bigl[\text{ES}(C)+d(C),\; \text{ES}(D)+d(D)\bigr] = \max[3+4,\; 5+1] = \max[7,\, 6] = 7$$
$$\text{EF}(E) = 7 + 3 = 10$$

**Action $F$ (predecessor: $E$):**
$$\text{ES}(F) = \text{ES}(E) + d(E) = 7 + 3 = 10$$
$$\text{EF}(F) = 10 + 2 = 12$$

### Step 2 — Backward Pass (Compute LF and LS)

Project deadline: $T = 12$ (we use the computed $T_{\min}$ as deadline).

**Action $F$ (no successors):**
$$\text{LF}(F) = 12$$
$$\text{LS}(F) = 12 - 2 = 10$$

**Action $E$ (successor: $F$):**
$$\text{LF}(E) = \text{LS}(F) = 10$$
$$\text{LS}(E) = 10 - 3 = 7$$

**Action $D$ (successor: $E$):**
$$\text{LF}(D) = \text{LS}(E) = 7$$
$$\text{LS}(D) = 7 - 1 = 6$$

**Action $C$ (successor: $E$):**
$$\text{LF}(C) = \text{LS}(E) = 7$$
$$\text{LS}(C) = 7 - 4 = 3$$

**Action $B$ (successor: $D$):**
$$\text{LF}(B) = \text{LS}(D) = 6$$
$$\text{LS}(B) = 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Action $A$ (successors: $B, C$):**
$$\text{LF}(A) = \min\bigl[\text{LS}(B),\; \text{LS}(C)\bigr] = \min[4,\, 3] = 3$$
$$\text{LS}(A) = 3 - 3 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Slack and Critical Path

$$\text{slack}(A) = 0 - 0 = 0 \;\; \text{(CRITICAL)}$$
$$\text{slack}(B) = 4 - 3 = 1$$
$$\text{slack}(C) = 3 - 3 = 0 \;\; \text{(CRITICAL)}$$
$$\text{slack}(D) = 6 - 5 = 1$$
$$\text{slack}(E) = 7 - 7 = 0 \;\; \text{(CRITICAL)}$$
$$\text{slack}(F) = 10 - 10 = 0 \;\; \text{(CRITICAL)}$$

### Step 4 — Analytical Matrix (Final Summary)

| Action | Dur | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack | Critical? |
|:------:|:---:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-----:|:---------:|
| $A$ | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **0** | ✅ |
| $B$ | 2 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 6 | 1 | ✗ |
| $C$ | 4 | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | **0** | ✅ |
| $D$ | 1 | 5 | 6 | 6 | 7 | 1 | ✗ |
| $E$ | 3 | 7 | 10 | 7 | 10 | **0** | ✅ |
| $F$ | 2 | 10 | 12 | 10 | 12 | **0** | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path:** $A \to C \to E \to F$
> **Minimum Project Duration:** $T_{\min} = 12$ time units.

## 3.2 Resource-Constrained Extension

Now introduce a **single senior engineer (reusable, binary resource, capacity 1)** required by $C$, $D$, and $E$. Using only the ES schedule:
- $C$ runs at $[3,\, 7)$, $D$ at $[5,\, 6)$, $E$ at $[7,\, 10)$.
- Overlap check: $C \cap D = [5, 6) \ne \emptyset$ → **CONFLICT**.

**Resolution via $D$ shifting into its slack window:**
$D$ has slack 1, so push $D$ to $[6,\, 7)$.
- New intervals: $C = [3,7)$, $D = [6,7)$, $E = [7,10)$. Conflict persists at $[6,7)$.

**Push $D$ further to $[7,\, 8)$:** but this violates $D \to E$ ordering ($D$ must finish before $E$ starts, and $E$ starts at 7). So shift $E$ to start at 8, but $E$ is critical. Better to **push $C$ later** — but $C$ is critical too.

This illustrates that **CPM alone is insufficient when resources are scarce**; we need **resource-constrained scheduling** (heuristic repair or RCPSP algorithms).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Temporal Planner

The following flowchart depicts the complete architecture of a KTU-style temporal planning system that handles time, schedules, and resources.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Problem Input] --> parseA[Parse PDDL Domain]
    parseA --> actionA[Action Network Builder]
    actionA --> dagA[Build Action DAG]
    dagA --> topoA[Topological Sort]
    topoA --> fwdA[Forward Pass: Compute ES and EF]
    fwdA --> bwdA[Backward Pass: Compute LS and LF]
    bwdA --> slackA[Compute Slack for Each Action]
    slackA --> critA[Identify Critical Path]
    critA --> resA[Resource Availability Check]
    resA --> checkA{Resources Feasible?}
    checkA -- Yes --> validA[Output Valid Schedule]
    checkA -- No --> repairA[Heuristic Repair or Replan]
    repairA --> resA
    validA --> stopA[Schedule Returned to Agent]
```

## 4.2 Resource Conflict Resolution Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actA[Action a] --> reqA[Request Resource r]
    reqA --> lookupA[Check Capacity Table]
    lookupA --> testA{Available at start a}
    testA -- Yes --> allocA[Allocate Resource]
    allocA --> execA[Execute Action]
    execA --> releaseA[Release Resource on Finish]
    testA -- No --> decisionA{Slack Available}
    decisionA -- Yes --> deferA[Defer to LS of a]
    deferA --> testA
    decisionA -- No --> replanA[Trigger Backtracking Replan]
    replanA --> actA
```

## 4.3 Time–Action Relationship Visualisation

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sample Temporal Schedule
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section Critical Path
    A Provision Server :crit, 0, 3
    C Install Database :crit, 3, 4
    E Deploy App :crit, 7, 3
    F Run Smoke Tests :crit, 10, 2
    section Non-Critical
    B Install OS :3, 2
    D Configure Firewall :5, 1
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical View: Planning vs. Scheduling vs. Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[LEVEL 1 - PLANNING]
        p1[Select Actions]
        p2[Build Action Network]
    end
    subgraph L2[LEVEL 2 - SCHEDULING]
        s1[Compute Critical Path]
        s2[Assign Start Times]
        s3[Check Resource Constraints]
    end
    subgraph L3[LEVEL 3 - EXECUTION]
        e1[Dispatch Actions]
        e2[Monitor Time and Resources]
        e3[Handle Delays and Replanning]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 -. feedback .-> L2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**
