**Q1.** Differentiate between **planning** and **scheduling** in AI. Why is scheduling considered a refinement of planning?

**Model Answer:**
Planning is the process of selecting a *sequence* of actions (qualitative aspect) that transforms an initial state into a goal state. Scheduling is the process of assigning *concrete start times and resources* (quantitative aspect) to the actions of an already-derived plan. Scheduling is a refinement of planning because it imposes temporal and resource constraints on a plan, ensuring that the plan is *realizable* in the real world, not merely *logically valid*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1b]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **reusable** and **consumable** resources with one example each. Which one requires a non-negativity stock constraint?

**Model Answer:**
A **reusable resource** is one that is not depleted by use and becomes available again after the action completes (e.g., a CNC machine, a worker, a vehicle). A **consumable resource** is depleted when used and is not returned at the end of the action (e.g., fuel, money, raw material). The **consumable** resource requires a non-negativity stock constraint: $\text{Stock}_r(t) \ge 0 \;\;\forall t$.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024, Q7 Choice A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following action network for a manufacturing process. Compute **Earliest Start (ES), Earliest Finish (EF), Latest Start (LS), Latest Finish (LF), and Slack** for every action. Identify the **critical path** and **minimum project duration**.

| Action | Duration | Predecessors |
|:------:|:--------:|:------------:|
| $P_1$ | 4 | — |
| $P_2$ | 3 | $P_1$ |
| $P_3$ | 2 | $P_1$ |
| $P_4$ | 5 | $P_2$ |
| $P_5$ | 6 | $P_2,\, P_3$ |
| $P_6$ | 3 | $P_4,\, P_5$ |

**Model Solution:**

**Forward Pass:**

$P_1$: $\text{ES}=0, \text{EF}=4$

$P_2$: $\text{ES}=0+4=4, \text{EF}=4+3=7$

$P_3$: $\text{ES}=0+4=4, \text{EF}=4+2=6$

$P_4$: $\text{ES}=4+3=7, \text{EF}=7+5=12$

$P_5$: $\text{ES}=\max(7,\, 6)=7, \text{EF}=7+6=13$

$P_6$: $\text{ES}=\max(12,\, 13)=13, \text{EF}=13+3=16$

**Project duration** $T_{\min}=16$.

**Backward Pass (from $T=16$):**

$P_6$: $\text{LF}=16, \text{LS}=13$

$P_5$: $\text{LF}=13, \text{LS}=7$

$P_4$: $\text{LF}=13, \text{LS}=8$

$P_3$: $\text{LF}=7, \text{LS}=5$

$P_2$: $\text{LF}=\min(8,\, 7)=7, \text{LS}=4$

$P_1$: $\text{LF}=\min(4,\, 5)=4, \text{LS}=0$

**Slack values:**

| Action | $d$ | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack | Critical |
|:------:|:---:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-----:|:--------:|
| $P_1$ | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✅ |
| $P_2$ | 3 | 4 | 7 | 4 | 7 | **0** | ✅ |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 5 | 7 | 1 | ✗ |
| $P_4$ | 5 | 7 | 12 | 8 | 13 | 1 | ✗ |
| $P_5$ | 6 | 7 | 13 | 7 | 13 | **0** | ✅ |
| $P_6$ | 3 | 13 | 16 | 13 | 16 | **0** | ✅ |

**Critical Path:** $P_1 \to P_2 \to P_5 \to P_6$, **Duration = 16**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of **resources** in AI planning. Discuss how each type is represented in the **PDDL 2.1** language.

**Model Answer:**

In PDDL 2.1, resources fall into four classes:

1. **Reusable Resources** — Declared with `:typing` and predicates that indicate availability, e.g., `(available ?machine - machine)`. The action precondition asserts availability; the effect removes it during execution (`(not (available ?machine))`) and re-adds it on completion (`(available ?machine)`). Capacity is implicitly 1 per object.
2. **Consumable Resources** — Modelled as **numeric fluents** in PDDL 2.1 Level 2, e.g., `(fuel-level ?truck - truck)`. The action effect decrements the level: `(decrease (fuel-level ?truck) (fuel-use ?truck))`. The constraint $\text{fuel-level} \ge 0$ is a numeric invariant.
3. **Continuous Resources** — Same as consumable but with a continuous real-valued level; modelled with `:fluents` and rate-based effects.
4. **Discrete Capacity Resources** — Numeric fluent with capacity limit, e.g., `(slots-used ?factory - factory)`.

The action network must then be checked against these constraints at every time point — this is the essence of **resource-constrained scheduling**.

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024, Q7 Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **schedule** in AI planning? Explain with an example how **deadline constraints** and **action durations** are incorporated into temporal planning using the **Simple Temporal Network (STN)** formalism.

**Model Answer:**

A **schedule** is a mapping $\sigma : \text{Actions} \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ assigning a start time to each action such that:
1. All precedence constraints are satisfied: $\sigma(s) + d(s) \le \sigma(a)$ for every edge $s \to a$.
2. All deadline constraints are satisfied: $\sigma(a) + d(a) \le D$ for terminal actions.
3. All resource capacity constraints are satisfied.

A **Simple Temporal Network (STN)** represents these constraints as a weighted directed graph with **time-point nodes** $T_0, T_1, \ldots, T_n$ and weighted edges encoding $T_j - T_i \le w_{ij}$. For example, if action $A$ has duration 3 and must precede action $B$, we add the edge $T_B - T_A \le 0$ (B starts no earlier than A ends) and the duration constraint is captured by introducing a "start" and "end" time-point for each action, with $T_{a,\text{end}} - T_{a,\text{start}} \le d(a)$. A **deadline** $D$ on the whole plan is added as $T_{\text{final}} - T_0 \le D$. The STN is feasible iff the graph contains no negative cycle, checked in $O(n^3)$ by Bellman-Ford. A consistent schedule is extracted by topological ordering or shortest-path propagation (Floyd-Warshall).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Solve the following **resource-constrained scheduling problem**:

A factory has **two machines ($M_1, M_2$)**, each of capacity 1 (reusable). Three jobs must be processed:

| Job | Duration | Needs |
|:---:|:--------:|:-----:|
| $J_1$ | 4 | $M_1$ |
| $J_2$ | 3 | $M_1$ and $M_2$ |
| $J_3$ | 2 | $M_2$ |

Deadline: complete all jobs by $T = 8$. Find a valid schedule and compute the total resource conflict resolution steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Try EST schedule:**
- $J_1$ on $M_1$ at $[0, 4)$.
- $J_2$ needs both machines: $M_1$ free at 4, $M_2$ free at 0. So $J_2$ on $M_1$ at $[4, 7)$, $M_2$ at $[0, 3)$ — but $J_3$ also needs $M_2$.
- $J_3$ on $M_2$ at $[3, 5)$ — conflict with $J_2$ on $M_2$? $J_2$ uses $M_2$ at $[0, 3)$, $J_3$ at $[3, 5)$. **No overlap** (3 is the end of $J_2$ on $M_2$).
- $J_2$ uses $M_1$ at $[4, 7)$, $J_3$ needs only $M_2$ — no conflict.

**Final schedule:**

| Job | Start | End | $M_1$ | $M_2$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $J_1$ | 0 | 4 | $[0, 4)$ | — |
| $J_3$ | 0 | 2 | — | $[0, 2)$ |
| $J_2$ | 4 | 7 | $[4, 7)$ | $[4, 7)$ |
| $J_3$ (shifted) | 2 | 4 | — | $[2, 4)$ |

Revised: $J_3$ on $M_2$ at $[2, 4)$, $J_2$ on $M_2$ at $[4, 7)$. **No conflicts. Total time = 7 ≤ 8. Schedule feasible.**

**Resource-conflict resolution steps:**
1. Initial assignment: $J_2$ tries to start at 0 — fails ($M_1$ occupied by $J_1$).
2. Push $J_2$ to start at 4.
3. Initial $J_3$ at $[0, 2)$ on $M_2$ — okay.
4. $J_2$ on $M_2$ at $[4, 7)$ — okay.

**Minimum makespan = 7** (1 time unit slack before deadline).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EF and ES formulas.** Some students write $\text{ES}(a) = \text{ES}(\text{pred}) + d(a)$ instead of $+d(\text{pred})$. **Memorise: the predecessor's duration, not the current action's.**
> 2. **Forgetting the `max` in the forward pass.** When an action has multiple predecessors, ES is the **max** of all predecessor finish times, not the min or any one of them.
> 3. **Forgetting the `min` in the backward pass.** LF is the **min** of successor LS values.
> 4. **Not propagating the deadline.** If a global deadline is given, propagate it to all terminal actions in the backward pass.
> 5. **Treating resource constraints as a separate step.** In KTU valuation, resource feasibility must be **explicitly checked and shown** with overlap intervals. Simply stating "schedule is feasible" will cost 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Misidentifying the critical path.** A common error is to take the path with the most nodes, not the one with the **longest total duration**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for Exam Day!):**

- **Time** in planning = **durations** of actions, modelled as $\text{end} = \text{start} + d(a)$.
- **Schedule** = assignment of start times to all actions respecting ordering and resource constraints.
- **Critical Path Method (CPM)** finds the **minimum project duration** via:
  - **Forward pass** (ES, EF) using `max` of predecessor finish times.
  - **Backward pass** (LS, LF) using `min` of successor start times.
  - **Slack = LS − ES**; critical actions have **slack = 0**.
- **Reusable resource** ↔ binary or capacity-limited; constraint: $\sum u_r \le C_r$ at every $t$.
- **Consumable resource** ↔ numeric level; constraint: $\text{Stock}_r(t) \ge 0$.
- **Simple Temporal Network (STN)** = graph of time-points with weighted edges; feasible iff no negative cycle.
- **PDDL 2.1** is the standard language to express durations, deadlines, and numeric resources.
- **RCPSP (Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling Problem)** is **NP-hard**; use heuristics (EST, MSF, critical-path-first) for practical solutions.
- **Examiner keywords to use**: "temporal constraint", "earliest start", "critical path", "resource feasibility", "feasible schedule", "negative-cycle-free STN".

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Time, Schedules, and Resources**
